## Thread: Way to find the trailing blanks — 17 this month / 20 total

_Thread started [2001-12](2001-12.md#t-22510c470f)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Way to find the trailing blanks

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-01T02:02:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hN8Y7.106980$m05.9483611@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com> <6D0Y7.104601$m05.9037176@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com> wrote

I ran the tests on PIC X(80) VALUE 'HELLO WORLD' for 10 million iterations
of each example on a 900MHz desktop:

Results follow:

>
> 1. If you're using the newest version of Fujitsu's compiler on a Win
> machine, you can use:
>
> COMPUTE NUMCHAR = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(MYLINE)

(note difference in function name from original post)

4.59 seconds for 10 million iterations.

>
> 2. A common way is:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> where 'bb' is a double space.

8.75 seconds for 10 million iterations.

>
> 3. Another way:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    END-PERFORM.

18.72 seconds for 10 million iterations

>
> 4. Another way:
> UNSTRING MYLINE INTO JUNK DELIMITED 'bb' COUNT IN LEN.

5.72 seconds for 10 million iterations.


Your results may vary. Still, sixteen seconds variation in technique for
10,000,000 passes is, I believe, virtually inconsequential. Interestingly,
relative addressing takes the most time and UNSTRING (whoda thunk it?) is
pretty swift.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Way to find the trailing blanks

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-01-01T09:44:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0201010944.217cffcd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com> <6D0Y7.104601$m05.9037176@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <hN8Y7.106980$m05.9483611@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
A good exposition, however only the perform varying, which takes the
longest, does the actual job asked, since a character string could
contain an indeterminate number of spaces between characters and it is
only the trailing blanks that are of interest.

I am sorry that my example was incorrect in that it did not cover the
case where the character string was all blank.
```

```
"Robert Jones" <robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:fcd09c56.0201010944.217cffcd@posting.google.com...
> A good exposition, however only the perform varying, which takes the
> longest, does the actual job asked, since a character string could
> contain an indeterminate number of spaces between characters and it is
> only the trailing blanks that are of interest.
>

Sure. Good practice would squeeze out multiple blanks in the original field
before looking for trailing spaces. However, one could:

01  TESTARG.
   02  MYFIELD   PIC X(80).
   02  BLANK80   PIC X(80) VALUE SPACE.

...

INSPECT TESTARG TALLYING LEN FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE BLANK80.

or

UNSTRING TESTARG DELIMITED BY BLANK80 INTO JUNK COUNT IN LEN.

etc.
```

```
The problem in answering these kinds of questions is the way they�re presented:

�How to efficiently find a trailing blanks in a particular field.�  

To what end?  Do you want to know the # of trailing blanks?  Do you want to know
the length of the complement data?  Do you want to move the non-blank data? Are
you planning to create a string , of which this is a part?  Do you want to use
the trailing blanks for some purpose?  Do you want to overlay the trailing
blanks with other data? 

I�m sure there are other possibilities.  The �most efficient� solution depends
on what he intends to do when he gets it, and therefore, can't be given (just
presumed) until the ques is rephrased.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Way to find the trailing blanks

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T08:22:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3333B2.4AB5164@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com> <6D0Y7.104601$m05.9037176@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <hN8Y7.106980$m05.9483611@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse,
Could you try using subscripting as well as reference modification in the
PERFORM loop?  Also, you did not show how you defined the variables LEN and
MAXLEN.  At least on some platforms, this can have a tremendous effect on
efficiency.  Presumably, USAGE COMP with an "S" in the PICTURE would be about
the best, since COMP is allowed to be implementor defined for efficiency.  On
the IBM mainframe, the number of digits in the PICTURE can also affect
efficiency, with 1 - 4 denoting a 16 bit (halfword) item, 5 - 8 denoting a 32
bit (fullword) item, 9 digits also fullword, but supposedly inefficiently
handled, and 10 - 18 digits being 64 bits (doubleword), and requiring a
library routine.

On an IBM mainframe, I have reduced CPU time over 50% for expensive programs
without ever changing logic, but only changing the definitions of numeric data
items.

Finally, how about testing the INSPECT and UNSTRING with FUNCTION REVERSE
being applied to the input field?  That looks like the "other way" to get the
desired result.
=====
JerryMouse wrote:

> "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com> wrote

…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> pretty swift.
```

```
news:3C3333B2.4AB5164@ibm.net...
> JerryMouse,

> PERFORM loop?  Also, you did not show how you defined the variables LEN
and
> MAXLEN.  At least on some platforms, this can have a tremendous effect on
> efficiency.  Presumably, USAGE COMP with an "S" in the PICTURE would be
about
> the best, since COMP is allowed to be implementor defined for efficiency.
On
> the IBM mainframe, the number of digits in the PICTURE can also affect
> efficiency, with 1 - 4 denoting a 16 bit (halfword) item, 5 - 8 denoting a
32
> bit (fullword) item, 9 digits also fullword, but supposedly inefficiently
> handled, and 10 - 18 digits being 64 bits (doubleword), and requiring a
> library routine.
>
> On an IBM mainframe, I have reduced CPU time over 50% for expensive
programs
> without ever changing logic, but only changing the definitions of numeric
data
> items.

On subscripting vs relative addressing, we've visited this before - no
difference. Likewise with indexing.

The test program variables were defined as S9(7) COMP-5.

My contention was that tuning for micro-efficiency is, in most cases,
inefficient. And while I don't doubt that it's possible to reduce runtimes
by 50% with some tuning, the question remains: 50% of what? Half of a
one-minute job is insignificant; half of a ten-hour job is a big deal.


>
> Finally, how about testing the INSPECT and UNSTRING with FUNCTION REVERSE
> being applied to the input field?  That looks like the "other way" to get
the
> desired result.

Two excellent possibilities. There are probably several other methods of
getting the same result (don't you just love a robust language?). Thing is,
I can't see any of them making much difference. One second per million
iterations is plenty good - at least in my part of the universe.


> =====
> JerryMouse wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I ran the tests on PIC X(80) VALUE 'HELLO WORLD' for 10 million
iterations
> > of each example on a 900MHz desktop:
> >
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…

Interestingly,

is
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ A hard taskmaster...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T16:07:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C33A0D5.6C5C116D@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com> <6D0Y7.104601$m05.9037176@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <hN8Y7.106980$m05.9483611@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C3333B2.4AB5164@ibm.net> <dtLY7.320697$C8.23626248@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Aw heck, you're gonna make me write the code and run it myself, aren't you?
(Grin)

By the way, we have gone on efficiency kicks periodically for more than a
decade.  the 2001 exercise resulted in annual savings in excess of $600,000US.
Earlier exercises saved well over $1 million.

What galls me is that I figured out changes resulting in more than half these
savings, and what do I get?  Plaques and "Thank you's", not cash!

COBOL in large enterprises is often used to process huge volumes of data, making
optimization efforts well worth undertaking.  We search for likely candidates by
looking for batch job steps that are the most expensive, and we rarely are
disappointed when we start looking.

It is equally true that in other applications, efficiency is of little
importance, because doing "it" in half the time means you finish in only half
the blink of an eye, instead of a full blink, and what can you do with the time
saved?  Your eyes aren't even open!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A hard taskmaster...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T01:27:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qsOY7.434086$uB.41213903@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3C33A0D5.6C5C116D@attglobal.net...
> Aw heck, you're gonna make me write the code and run it myself, aren't
you?
> (Grin)
>
> By the way, we have gone on efficiency kicks periodically for more than a
> decade.  the 2001 exercise resulted in annual savings in excess of
$600,000US.
> Earlier exercises saved well over $1 million.
>
> What galls me is that I figured out changes resulting in more than half
these
> savings, and what do I get?  Plaques and "Thank you's", not cash!
>
> COBOL in large enterprises is often used to process huge volumes of data,
making
> optimization efforts well worth undertaking.  We search for likely
candidates by
> looking for batch job steps that are the most expensive, and we rarely are
> disappointed when we start looking.

Sure. I once cut a ten-hour job to a two-hour job by adding three lines of
code (if the account you want is the same as the account you've already got,
don't read the account file). That was worth digging out. Another time, my
company invested seven man-years in the rewrite of a single program (Fortran
and ALC). Of course this program was a good candidate, taking up 70% of
three mainframes 24/7. Cutting its processing time by 50% saved bags and
bags of money.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Way to find the trailing blanks

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T05:52:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bkSY7.7232$ZQ1.1742208156@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com> <6D0Y7.104601$m05.9037176@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <hN8Y7.106980$m05.9483611@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
    This was my solution in populating a VARCHAR DB2 column:

               10  WS-NBR-OF-TRAILING-BLANKS   PIC S999  COMP-3.

           10  WS-PLCM-T-POLICY-COMMENT.
               49  WS-PLCM-T-COMMENT-LEN   PIC S9(4)       USAGE COMP.
               49  WS-PLCM-T-COMMENT-TEXT  PIC X(462).

           INITIALIZE                      WS-NBR-OF-TRAILING-BLANKS.
           INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE (WS-PLCM-T-COMMENT-TEXT)
               TALLYING WS-NBR-OF-TRAILING-BLANKS FOR LEADING SPACE.
           COMPUTE DPLCM-T-COMMENT-LEN
               =   LENGTH OF WS-PLCM-T-COMMENT-TEXT
               -   WS-NBR-OF-TRAILING-BLANKS.
           IF DPLCM-T-COMMENT-LEN = ZERO
               MOVE +0001                  TO DPLCM-T-COMMENT-LEN
           END-IF.
           MOVE WS-PLCM-T-COMMENT-TEXT (1:DPLCM-T-COMMENT-LEN)
             TO DPLCM-T-COMMENT-TEXT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Way to find the trailing blanks

- **From:** hebisch@math.uni.wroc.pl (Waldek Hebisch)
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T19:28:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2hq5e$h4s$1@panorama.wcss.wroc.pl>`
- **References:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com> <6D0Y7.104601$m05.9037176@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <hN8Y7.106980$m05.9483611@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse (jerrymouse@invalid.com) wrote:

: "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com> wrote

: I ran the tests on PIC X(80) VALUE 'HELLO WORLD' for 10 million iterations
: of each example on a 900MHz desktop:

: Results follow:

: >
: > 1. If you're using the newest version of Fujitsu's compiler on a Win
: > machine, you can use:
: >
: > COMPUTE NUMCHAR = FUNCTION STORED-CHAR-LENGTH(MYLINE)

: (note difference in function name from original post)

: 4.59 seconds for 10 million iterations.

: >
: > 3. Another way:
: >
: > PERFORM VARYING LEN FROM MAXLEN BY -1 UNTIL LEN < 1
: >     OR MYLINE(LEN:1) NOT = SPACE
: >    END-PERFORM.

: 18.72 seconds for 10 million iterations

Just little comparison: A C program doing the same as 3 on 
an 800 Mhz Athlon takes 3.69s. So I would say that Fujitsu 
builtin fuctions are quite fast, while the compiler is not 
so good at optimising (or optimisation was turned off?).
```

```
Or at least optimizing this bizarre code. Point was, it doesn't make any
difference in the real world; 18 seconds, 3 seconds, whatever, for 10
million iterations is chump-change.

I did the following also:

MOVE 1.23 TO EDIT-FIELD

Where EDIT-FIELD was defined as PIC $$$$$9.99 (that is, float the dollar
sign) in C for 10 million iterations. I let it run on my laptop as I left
San Diego. I'm now in Ft Worth and it's still running.
```

```
> > : "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com> wrote
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > : Results follow:

If your application needs to remove trailing blanks from a character string
10 million times, I think you need to redesign the application.

Seriously, one-call-unreal-number-of-iteration 'performance' tests like this
are dopey and don't mean jack in the Real World.

As Mr. Hebisch pointed out, whatever time you are spending removing blank
spaces from strings is "chump change."

MCM
```

```
Yet another way that requires bound checking to be turned off as myline(0:1)
is
considered valid. I use this method within my 'string functions' library on
my old
MF WB compiler. I presume 900MHZ desktop is also an Athlon ?

...

01 myline-group.
 03 PIC X(01) VALUE x"FF".
 03 myline PIC X(80).

...

PERFORM VARYING LEN FROM MAXLEN BY -1 UNTIL MYLINE(LEN:1) NOT = SPACE
END-PERFORM

....
```

```
COBOL is used for business applications where I/O time is the only (should
be) thing that matters. In other words, it is a poor idea to code anything
but business applications in COBOL. On the other hand, C and C++ are great
for CPU bound applications. Though, but many consider C and C++ a poor
choice for business applications.

Please don't flame me. I personally like C and C++, and they can do any
application very well. Regardless of its complexity and quirks, many
business application programmers find COBOL easier to understand than C and
C++.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-01T02:10:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dU8Y7.178182$Zd.16439107@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<a0hf7v$gkt$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <20011231173013.05958.00001056@mb-fh.aol.com>`

```

"Sff5ky" <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123>
>
> I have not found a 'good' way of doing this same thing on the PC platform.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> file requirements.

We've got a way, making use of the CBL_READ_FILE routines. In summary, it
works like this:

Set BEGIN to 0

LOOP:
Read file starting at BEGIN for maximum record length into buffer
Figure out length of record and move that many bytes from buffer to work
area
<process record>
add length to BEGIN
go to LOOP

I can send you a copy of the code if you like.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-01T12:59:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<poiY7.184649$Zd.16790939@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<dU8Y7.178182$Zd.16439107@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <20020101014459.00964.00000785@mb-ft.aol.com>`

```

"Sff5ky" <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123>
>
> This sounds much like some of the 'big block' logic we have where
> we physically read a block as a record and manually process each
> logical record as an occurrence within the physical record.

Sure. We have an additional refinement.

Each "word" in each record of our file is part of a key in an ISAM file and
coupled with the beginning byte of the record.  So, if "DON'T EAT FEATHERS"
is a phrase in a record beginning at offset 1234567 bytes into the file, the
ISAM entries look like:

DONT          1234567
EAT             1234567
FEATHERS 1234567

The program looks up, say, "EAT," retrieves the file offset from the ISAM
file, then reads the data file starting at the supplied offset.
```

```
I think we're over-complicating Siri's problem. If he defines 2 files:
255-file and 356-file and uses SEQ-NUM to decide which one to process, the
problem becomes trivial, e.g.:

if seq-num = '01' open input 255-file else open input 356-file  

if seq-num = '01' read 255-rec else read 356-rec

etc.
```

```
I suppose that we could very well be over-complicating the issue,
but at what point are we able to determine the value of seq-num?
If seq-num is part of the record being read, it would seem that we
must have an initialization process to open the file and read the first
record to decide which file will be processed in mainline.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T03:36:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fvY7.117$cD4.137@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<1zpY7.1211$wp1.502@www.newsranger.com> <20020101193024.05670.00000680@mb-fb.aol.com>`

```
If I'm reading Sri correctly, he gets the SEQ-NUM from the Linkage Section, not
the file I/P itself. He also mentions that he codes the FD for the file. This
leads me to believe he must be opening and reading the file in toto as a result
of one call from a higher level module that passes him the record type (i.e.
length). 

My take is that the SEQ-NUM fld serves to tell the CALLed module what FILE (not
record) to use as input.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T15:27:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a0v8ma$7iq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<24df60cb.0112272007.46c45a1e@posting.google.com>`

```
When I started programming in 1969, I used variable length records quite a
bit.  But over the years they got rarer and rarer, even though I did
contract work and switched jobs enough to see quite a few shops.

At first they got replaced with files with header and detail records. 
Variable numbers of detail records where handled programmatically.  Later
on, database records did the same thing.

I'm not sure why all these shops stopped using variable length records - but
believe this is a variation of KISS.
```

```
Lorne,

Have you actually done this?

I am doing a fair bit of CGI and ISAPI programming and the interface is
nowhere near as simple as you describe.

If I have missed something, I'd really like to know...

In the past, Richard Plinston has posted something similar, but I believe
that Richard has investigated the lower levels of the CGI interface and
knows which ports to accept and display to in his environment. (Please
correct me if this wrong, Richard) I note that Richard has posted to this
thread qualifying your statement also.

My understanding is that the CGI interface is slightly different between
Windows and UNIX based systems and, at least as far as Fujitsu COBOL is
concerned, you certainly CAN'T do it with a simple ACCEPT and DISPLAY. My
newly installed Fujitsu V6.1 has comprehensive facilities for CGI
programming but it is done by calls to an interface. (I am still trying it
and it seems to be excellent).

If you know something I don't, Lorne, please let us all in on it...<G>

Pete.
```

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@rogers.com>

```
You have to be able to read from STDIN and write to STDOUT. It helps if you can also reference environment variables. MF COBOL supports reading from STDIN using ACCEPT, and writing STDOUT using DISPLAY but there are caveats.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HTTP Calls

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T04:38:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39wY7.283$DY1.51552@rwcrnsc54>`
- **References:** `<pSlY7.261$wU4.225628@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-LKj4qKnKcrdI@thishost> <3c3243c4_9@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> I am doing a fair bit of CGI and ISAPI programming and the interface is
> nowhere near as simple as you describe.

Using Micro Focus Net Express...

Same code works for CGI, ISAPI & NSAPI (you can choose your poison at
compile time).

Gazaloo.

==>start of cobolcgi source
identification division.

working-storage section.
01 input-page is external-form.
   03  name-field pic x(30) identified by "Name".
   03  phone-no   pic x(30) identified by "Phone".
   03  email      pic x(30) identified by "Email".

01 output-page is external-form identified by "output".
   03  name-field pic x(30) identified by "Name".
   03  phone-no   pic x(30) identified by "Phone".
   03  email      pic x(30) identified by "Email".

procedure division.

    accept input-page
    move corresponding input-page to output-page
    display output-page
    .
==>end of cobolcgi source

==>start of input.htm
<HTML>
<HEAD>
<TITLE>Sample Form</TITLE>
</HEAD>
<BODY>

<P>Input Form

<FORM METHOD="POST" ACTION="/cgi-bin/cobolcgi.exe"></P>
<PRE>
Name     <INPUT TYPE="TEXT" NAME="Name" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="30" >
Email id <INPUT TYPE="TEXT "NAME="Email" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="30" >
Phone    <INPUT NAME="Phone" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="12">
<INPUT TYPE="SUBMIT" VALUE="Send Form"><INPUT TYPE="RESET">
</PRE>
</FORM>

</BODY>
</HTML>
==>end of input.htm

==>start of output.htm
<HTML>

<P>*** SAMPLE OUTPUT FORM ***</P>
<P>Dear :name-field</P>
<P>blah blah blah</P>

</HTML>
==>end of output.htm
```

```
On Tue, 1 Jan 2002 23:07:18 UTC, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" 
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> Lorne,
> 
> Have you actually done this?
> 

Yes back about 6 or 7 years ago on the IBM Web server (now Lotus 
Domino web server) on an OS/2 machine using MF COBOL version 
3.something or other

The accept is from STDIN and the CGI handling in the web server pipes 
the form data to the program on that file handle. The program has to 
parse the xxxx=value stuff from the form and construct the HTML for 
the output, which is done with the display verb that ends up on STDOUT
where the web server grabs it and sends it off to the client.

> I am doing a fair bit of CGI and ISAPI programming and the interface is
> nowhere near as simple as you describe.
> 
> If I have missed something, I'd really like to know...

You can do it with any language as long as you can get to STDIN and 
STDOUT. REXX works the same way (with different syntax of course).

The form has to be POST (or maybe GET) I do not remember which method 
had to be used....

Lorne

> 
> In the past, Richard Plinston has posted something similar, but I believe
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com
```

```
Gazaloo...thanks Gary, I received your private mail)).

It looks like Net Express IS doing it through ACCEPT and DISPLAY, but uses
vendor extensions.

This is fine, and simplifies some of the code.

I am now finding that the Fujitu interface provides all the facilities
(including substitution of COBOL variables in the output HTML stream...very
useful), and (like most things) once you use it a few times it becomes
second nature.

Nevertheless, I learned something from the above posts (and it wasn't just
that there is more than one way to skin a cat <G>)

Thanks to all concerned.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HTTP Calls

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T09:01:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C341E01.11306328@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<pSlY7.261$wU4.225628@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-LKj4qKnKcrdI@thishost> <3c3243c4_9@Usenet.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-lQsvvS1JiTTZ@thishost>`

```
lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote:
 
> You can do it with any language as long as you can get to STDIN and
> STDOUT. REXX works the same way (with different syntax of course).
 
> The form has to be POST (or maybe GET) I do not remember which method
> had to be used....

POST gives input in stdin.  One problem is that some languages or
versions, such as Cobol, will not complete the ACCEPT until the variable
is full or an enter key which may have it hang on input. Note that
switch S5 is required to be on in MF Cobol for it to use stdin/stdout.

Using a GET requires that the environment variable QUERY_STRING is read.

> > In the past, Richard Plinston has posted something similar, but I believe
> > that Richard has investigated the lower levels of the CGI interface and
> > knows which ports to accept and display to in his environment. (Please
> > correct me if this wrong, Richard) I note that Richard has posted to this
> > thread qualifying your statement also.

Windows is the problem, or in fact, several problems.  Some Web Servers
use 'WinCGI' which avoids stdin/stdout and provides a file interface. 
With Windows it is necessary (in my experience) to set up a message loop
for the CGI program, though it doesn't do anything with it, in other
words it needs to be a Windows API program (YMMV).

Fortunately I did it all some time ago and it sorts itself out at run
time deciding how it takes its input and sends its output.  The
Windows/Xitami version has to link in a different main module for the
message loop.

> > > > Does anyone have a simple demo/explanation of a COBOL Program making an
> > HTTP Call? 

We still haven't determined what was meant by 'HTTP Call'.  He may be
wanting to send a URL and get a page back in return.  I have done this
with Python as there is a nice http parsing class which makes it really
easy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HTTP Calls

- **References:** `<pSlY7.261$wU4.225628@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-LKj4qKnKcrdI@thishost>`

```
lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote:
> 
> Is the COBOL program running in a CGI environment? If so ACCEPT and
> DISPLAY can be used to obtain the data for the program and to output
          ^^^^^^^^^^^
> the HTML that is passed back to the web server.

_might_ be able to be used depending on compiler, version, OS
environment, web server used, and how the form has been sent (ie GET or
POST).
```

<a id="t-2935eed135"></a>
## Thread: Indexed Files — 35 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Indexed Files

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2002-01-01T23:38:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Is it possible for an index to refers to more than one field? For 
example?  I would like to have a record that contains two or more names 
(e.g. husband and wife) associated with the remainder of record details 
(e.g. address).  The record might look something like this:

	01 Account-Holder.
		03 Account-Owner                  pic X(30).
		03 Account-Spouse                 pic X(30).
		03 Account-Address                pic X(30).
		03 Account-City                   pic X(20).
		03 Account-State                  pic X(2).
		03 Account-Zip                    pic X(5).
		03                                pic X value '-'.
		03 Account-Zip-Plus4              pic X(4).

It would be nice to have the first two fields included in the same index.

I can do this using a database - not sure if there is a simple solution in 
COBOL.  

Thanks for any tips.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-01T16:50:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C32596B.1533@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:
> 
> Is it possible for an index to refers to more than one field? For
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for any tips.

Probably the first thing you need is a UNIQUE key to the record.  You'll likely find 
that you have more than one "John Smith" and maybe more than one "John Smith" with a 
wife called "Mary".

Trying to make an existing field act as the primary key in these circumstances might get 
you more grief.  Most database software allows you to have a primary key which is 
assigned automatically (autoincremented) when the record is created and is used only as 
a unique identifier.  All of the other indexing is done as secondary indices which use 
the primary index to find the record (or records) which match the value in field you are 
searching on.
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T13:33:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b%DY7.315349$C8.23249902@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```

"Floyd Johnson" <floydj@netins.net> wrote in message
news:a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net...
> Is it possible for an index to refers to more than one field? For
> example?  I would like to have a record that contains two or more names
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL.

There's almost always a simple solution in COBOL. If ALL you want is to
combine fields for ISAM, the trivial way is:

01  Account-Holder.
   03  Account-Owner-and-Spouse
      05  Account-Owner
      05  Account-Spouse
   03  Account-Address
....
Where the SELECT statement would read:
   SELECT MYISAM-FILE ASSIGN TO (something)
     RECORD KEY IS Account-Owner-and-Spouse
     etc

To get all the "SMITH" people:

START MYISAM KEY NOT < "SMITH"
READ MYISAM-FILE NEXT.
PERFORM UNTIL Account-Owner NOT = "SMITH"
   IF Account-Owner = "SMITH"
      (process record)
      READ MYISAM-FILE NEXT
      END-IF
END-PERFORM.

Of course this fails miserably in the real world because:
1. One family may have multiple addresses,
2. Two or more families may have the same names, or
3. There may be multiple renditions of the same family names.
SMITH, JOHN            MARY
SMITH, J                    MARY
SMITH, JOHN            MARY LOUISE
SMITH, JOHN            M.L.
SMITH, JOHN            MIRY
etc.

Other observations:
1. A single field for street address is insufficient. Many people need two
or more lines (Apt 123, Suite A, Accounts Payable Department, etc.).
2. Your "State" and "ZIP" fields won't handle other country's postal codes.
You don't have a field for Country.
3. There is no need to include the dash ('-') (or any other constant) when
storing data.
4. "ZIP" is an acronym and should be in all caps. These can be inserted at
execution time, thereby saving storage on external media.
5. You defined your ZIP code storage as "X" (alphanumeric). This is
Good(tm). A sensible rule to follow is: If you can't do arithmetic on a
field, the field should almost never be defined as numeric (like account
codes, social security numbers, area codes, etc.).
6. ISAM files must have at least one unique key; you are permitted ALTERNATE
KEY WITH DUPLICATES, but the main key must be unique. We use fingerprints.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T09:47:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3347AA.1BA7@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net> <b%DY7.315349$C8.23249902@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 6. ISAM files must have at least one unique key; you are permitted ALTERNATE
> KEY WITH DUPLICATES, but the main key must be unique. We use fingerprints.

I'd agreed with everything you said up to this one.
1. Not everybody is allowed to use fingerprints (privacy concerns).
2. Not everybody has the means to generate whatever fingerprint hash number you use.
3. They aren't a guaranteed unique key.  How do you handle double-hand amputees? (I once 
worked for a disability organization and that would have given US several synonym keys 
of NULL).

NEVER try to use something as a unique key which wasn't designed as such. Eventually 
you'll hit a null or a synonym (or it'll change) and have to kludge around it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T21:43:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iaLY7.126359$m05.11332842@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net> <b%DY7.315349$C8.23249902@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C3347AA.1BA7@paralynx.com>`

```

"Ed Guy" <ed_guy@paralynx.com> wrote in message
news:3C3347AA.1BA7@paralynx.com...
> JerryMouse wrote:
> > 6. ISAM files must have at least one unique key; you are permitted
ALTERNATE
> > KEY WITH DUPLICATES, but the main key must be unique. We use
fingerprints.
>
> I'd agreed with everything you said up to this one.
> 1. Not everybody is allowed to use fingerprints (privacy concerns).
> 2. Not everybody has the means to generate whatever fingerprint hash
number you use.
> 3. They aren't a guaranteed unique key.  How do you handle double-hand
amputees? (I once
> worked for a disability organization and that would have given US several
synonym keys
> of NULL).


It was a joke. But there's no reason you couldn't use fingerprints. For a
double amputee, he could stick something else in the reader. (That, too, is
a joke.)

First I've heard of fingerprints being a privacy concern. Fact is, I've
wondered why the FBI fingerprint database isn't online. Arrest records are
public documents.
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T18:14:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C334EF5.1F2A8C4E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```


Floyd Johnson wrote:

> Is it possible for an index to refers to more than one field? For
> example?  I would like to have a record that contains two or more names
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Floyd,

You've already had several answers to this, but let's get back to basics of
design, not programming. Back in ye olden dayes you used card indexes to do
the above, and these also developed into systems where you could poke a
knitting needle through pre punched holes at the top of cards to select those
that met your criteria.

What you are attempting to do wouldn't work with those cards, and as
illustrated by others you run into all sorts of duplication on names. Further,
I would question can you do it satisfactorily even with a DB.

As a practical example, I can think of a local charity, folks taking their
cats and dogs into hospitals/nursing homes (Pet Assisted Therapy). Given some
400 volunteers, approx. some 15 - 30% could be family members. They want to
distribute a quarterly Newsletter to all members, but given 'families',
restrict the mailing to one copy per family. I'm not aware of current details,
but there is an annual membership fee of $10, and using record linking, this
fee is reduced for family members.

The DB, (originally Paradox, but now Clipper I believe), has a record per
volunteer with ID cross-references to other family members. Only one record
has an active flag 'Send Newsletter'. (Sure they can goof and set the Active
flag for other family members, but this is minor).

The only safe way, COBOL files or DBs, is to have a unique record ID,
cross-referencing records where appropriate. I doubt the application you are
thinking of warrants it, but another alternative would be :-

- A Person record (e.g. name info, DOB, SSN etc.)
- An Address Record

with the first containing a 'key' reference to the second.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
As I finished my semester, I am looking long range - to next fall.  I 
remember a PL/1 assignment that I received 20+ years ago.  I have neither 
used or had access to PL/1 since that time, but I would like to consider 
such an assisgnment for my COBOL class.

I would expect to have an account number as the primary key - though it 
was omitted from the original record description.  I would then 
like to have the owner and spouse indexed as sequential keys.  The 
original assignment allowed 1 to four lines of addresses with a tag on 
each line indicating the type of data it contains.

Right now, when my COBOL is current, I am at the brainstorming stage.  Any 
feedback or ideas are appreciated.

Thanks for the comments.

Floyd Johnson



Floyd Johnson (floydj@netins.net) wrote:
: Is it possible for an index to refers to more than one field? For 
: example?  I would like to have a record that contains two or more names 
: (e.g. husband and wife) associated with the remainder of record details 
: (e.g. address).  The record might look something like this:

: 	01 Account-Holder.
: 		03 Account-Owner                  pic X(30).
: 		03 Account-Spouse                 pic X(30).
: 		03 Account-Address                pic X(30).
: 		03 Account-City                   pic X(20).

: 		03 Account-Zip                    pic X(5).

: 		03 Account-Zip-Plus4              pic X(4).

: It would be nice to have the first two fields included in the same index.

: I can do this using a database - not sure if there is a simple solution in 
: COBOL.  

: Thanks for any tips.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T21:05:21+00:00

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:

> As I finished my semester, I am looking long range - to next fall.  I
> remember a PL/1 assignment that I received 20+ years ago.  I have neither
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Floyd,

One thing I didn't mention in my previous message was the following problem,
(again related to those volunteers) - we have Dad (Bob Smith), Mom (Adrienne
Smith) and daughter Judy Smith. It gets real 'hairy' when you try to cover the
following :-

- Original setup :-

Name    Address        Home Phone    Work Phone    email

Bob        123 etc.        271-2789        272-3038        bobsmith@home.com
Adrienne    ,,                    ,,
none                        ,,
Judy        ,,                    ,,
none                        ,,

Judy isn't working at this point, (student).

Now Dad keeps his private e-mail (OK for use my Mom and Judy) but introduces his
work e-mail bobSmith@petrocanda.ca.

Now Judy gets a job - so her e-mail becomes judySmith@chevroncanada.ca and she
has a work phone - 255-5685

Judy 'spreads her wings' and moves out to her own apartment - new address and
new home phone ! (Might even get her own PC and decide to direct mail to her
home, rather than the office  - judySmith@shaw.ca).

Then Judy gets married and changes her last name - and now she uses 'his' e-mail
!

KISS ! Separate address record for each.

In commerce, about the only way it happens is as follows - assume Kraft
delivering to Safeway.: They have an account for Safeway S102-233-44-55 and for
each branch or Safeway distribution centre :-

S102-233-44-55/0001  = Branch 1 in LA
S102-233-44-55/0002  = Branch 2 in LA
S102-233-44-55/0003  = Branch 3 in LA etc...

A/C S102-233-44-55, pulls all transactions together for Accounts Receivable, and
individual branch numbers are queried for statistics. But note - for each of
those branches they maintain a separate Address record, (to show where the
products were delivered).

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T22:46:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S4MY7.1212$cD4.2799@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Always together or not? Doesn't really matter -- it can be done. 

Put a group level above those two - make it the key.

Then make the other two individually alternate keys.

Note: Some compilers will not allow an alternate key to be a subset starting at
the same starting point as the primary key.  Thinking about this that's really
not a problem - just start with the first part filled and the second part blank.

Then just make the second part an alternate key.

…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T20:32:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
It really depends.  I think a primary and one alterate will do what you want,
but I'm not sure because I don't really understand yet what you want.

Tell me what fields you want to lookup with?

Your message says "The first two fields in one key".

That would be 

03  Name-Key 
05 Account-Owner....
05 Account-Spouse....

Then NAME-KEY is the key field and you need only one.  I was assuming that you
actually want something different - a lookup by either owner or spouse - in that
case make Owner the primary key and Spouse the alternate.
```

```
Thane:

Sorry for the delay in getting back to you.  I missed your reply on my
ISP's newserver.  I found it when replying to the question about
replacing newsranger [:(].

I would like the Account-Owner and the Account-Spouse to be part of
the same index.  In a DBMS (e.g. SQL Server) this would not be
difficult - but am not sure how to do this in the COBOL environment.

Something you (or somebody) said earlier suggested that all alternate
keys were stored in the same index.  This would mean, that once I had
a primary key set up, I could store Account-Owner and Account-Spouse
as alternate keys and they would be accessible from the same index.

If this is not possible, then every name would have to be checked in
two different alternate key indexes - one for the owner and one for
the spouse.  Or there may be some other way to solve this problem.

Thanks again for your comments.

Floyd Johnson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T20:37:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3i3Z7.2609$cD4.5243@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net> <S4MY7.1212$cD4.2799@www.newsranger.com> <a10j8o$asv$2@ins22.netins.net>`

```
In the real world here is how I would design this structure using COBOL.

01  Account-Record.
03  Record-Handle Pic 9(18).  (Primary Key).
03  Account-Number Pic ...whatever ... (Alternate Key no duplicates)
03  Account-Spouse-1  Pic X(whatever) (Alternate Key with duplicates)
03  Account-Spouse-2  Pic X(whatever) (Alternate key with duplicates)

Additionally when adding a record I would place the record handle (unique
number) in the spouse field for any spouse that is "missing".  This helps
performance on alternate keys when duplicates are allowed.

No split key is necessary.  

I use a record handle that is unique and fixed for the life of a record -
anything else can change - reasign account number - no big deal - etc etc.


In article <a10j8o$asv$2@ins22.netins.net>, Floyd Johnson says...
>
>As I finish my semester, I am looking long range - to next fall.  I 
…[87 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** "Charlie" <cgoodman@imi.mb.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T06:56:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AgTY7.22956$L4.2444934@news2.calgary.shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
COBOL does not allow more than one field to be a key for an ISAM file.
However some compilers allow split keys.

From MF help: The normal syntax allows a single data name following RECORD
KEY IS.
  RECORD KEY IS data-name-5
The extension to the language:
   RECORD KEY IS split-key-name-1 = data-name-6 [data-name-7 ] ...

RULES:
9. Split-key-name is a concatenation of one or more data items within a
record associated with that file-name. It can be referenced only in START
and READ statements.
10. The data items referenced by data-name-5 and data-name-8 and any
data-names referenced by split-key-name-1 and split-key-name-2 must be
defined within a record description entry associated with the file-name.

These data items can be defined as any category. However, the key is treated
as an alphanumeric data item by input and output statements for the file
connector referenced by file-name.


---Charlie

> example?  I would like to have a record that contains two or more names
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T17:54:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uV0Z7.2373$cD4.4228@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
On 1 Jan 2002 23:38:08 GMT, in article <a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net>, Floyd
Johnson stated 
>
>Is it possible for an index to refers to more than one field? For 
>example?  I would like to have a record that contains two or more names 
>(e.g. husband and wife) associated with the remainder of record details 
>(e.g. address). 

This is possible, but it would not be advisable to use two 30 char fields as a
key.  Smaller keys can be searched faster.  Even in a database you wouldnt use a
person's name as a key.  There would be duplicates.

Not all people have a spouse.  It might make more sense to put the
spouse/children in a file/table by themselves, or use multiple file record
types.  You run into problems of people having children with multiple
partners/spouses. Sometimes the spouse doesnt live at the same address as the
employee or whatever.  

Using an ID # for each person would work faster.  For a file if you can have
multiple keys for one file you may want to have one key with a to field key and
one key with a one field key.  If both people use the same account then just use
the account number plus a one or two digit key for each additional person.

Example  Account #      Holder-#
1234-456-2348    01
The key would be '1234456234801'  as text or number.  
(large number may cause trouble.)

That way 01 is the primary account holder and you can add more people.

The record might look something like this:
>
>	01 Account-Holder.
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>+-----------------------------------------------------------+
```

```
handle the fact that one social security number might be used by more than
one person.  (It was a criminal database).   Designing a system around
people who don't follow the rules poses unique challenges.
```

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> correctly.

One essential function of a system is to allow the company to operate in
spite of any or all rules.  The first system that I ever designed 3
decades ago was specified by the office manager of a company. 
Fortunately I insisted on talking to the lower levels where they
actually did the work without the manager being present.  What they did
to keep the company working was quite different to the manager's grasp
of it.  Naturally there was some delay while the specification was
'redeveloped'.

A computer system is only a tool.  It can be a tool to get the job done,
or it can be a tool to impose the management's authority, often not both
at the same time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed Files

- **From:** esr@davinci-systems.es (Esteve Soler)
- **Date:** 2002-01-06T20:16:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c38b091.36650768@news.filnet.es>`
- **References:** `<a0th90$u9$1@ins22.netins.net> <3C34E477.6AE79382@freeuk.com>`

```
Yes, this is possible.

When defining a logical file (DDS) on the AS/400 there is the keyword
RENAME for doing this.

Using the keyword RENAME for the spouse-name the OS/400 interprets
like if there was two records for this logical file, one record for
the husband-name and other record for the spouse-name so we can do a
COBOL/400 program that retrieves both names sorted.


But unfortunately I think other cobol compilers MF, ACU, etc, can't do
this.


Regards,

Esteve Soler (Barcelona, Spain)

>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>>                 03 Account-Zip-Plus4              pic X(4).
```

## Thread: DLL SUPPORT FOR V2R10. — 11 msgs

### DLL SUPPORT FOR V2R10.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T04:22:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0201020422.2d8cc74f@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
             As we know on V2R10 version of OS/390 support for DLL
call from COBOL is not available.We also know that compiling a source
code of C with DLL option makes it a DLL and C++ code on OS/390 is
always DLL.Based on these facts following are our doubts.
              1. If C++ code on OS/390 is DLL then how is it
executable?
              2. we tried calling a C program dynamically from
COBOL.The C program is using a DLL and compiled with DLL option(we
tried without DLL option but it was unsuccessful in LINKEDIT giving
message that the required DLL fuction can not be included from
designated call library) so theoretically call should fail but our
call is successful?
              3. Is there any difference between DLL on Os/390 and on
WINDOWS?

Thanks and warm Regards.
shyam nivas
```

#### ↳ Re: DLL SUPPORT FOR V2R10.

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-02T14:04:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020102090442.28300.00002189@mb-mo.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201020422.2d8cc74f@posting.google.com>`

```
shyam nivas writes ...

>Hi,
>             As we know on V2R10 version of OS/390 support for DLL
>call from COBOL is not available.

You are wrong. It is not the level of the operating system here, but the level
of your COBOL compiler. If you use COBOL for OS/390 & VM (2.1) or later, COBOL
programs can call DLLs.



>We also know that compiling a source
>code of C with DLL option makes it a DLL 

You are wrong. Compiling a C source with the DLL option tells the compiler to
make all dynamic calls DLL-style calls.


>and C++ code on OS/390 is
>always DLL.Based on these facts following are our doubts.
>              1. If C++ code on OS/390 is DLL then how is it
>executable?

Again, C++ code is just a program; it just happens that all its dynamic calls
use DLL linkages instead of traditional MVS dynamic linkages. Just run the
program.


>              2. we tried calling a C program dynamically from
>COBOL.The C program is using a DLL and compiled with DLL option(we
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>call is successful?

Again, the C program does not have to be compiled with the DLL option, as long
as you compile with option EXPORTALL or (Much Better) include a

       #pragma export(name)

statement for each function and variable you are exposing. Also, the C DLL
should include a 

      #pragma(function_name,FETCHABLE)

for the name of the C function itself. Be sure to compile with RENT and to bind
with RENT, DYNAM(DLL), AMODE=31, RMODE=ANY, and UPCASE(YES); also, point your
SYSLMOD DD statement to a PDSE and your SYSDEFSD DD statement to a member of a
PDS or PDSE. [Note: SYSLMOD can be a PDS in some cases, but PDSEs are supported
for all cases.]

(note, you will need to compile with DLL option if your C program in turn makes
dynamic calls to lower level functions).

Now, for your COBOL calling program, first be sure you are using a current
COBOL compiler.

Compile your calling program with these options: DLL, RENT, NOEXPORTALL,
NODYNAM, and possibly PGMNAME(LONGMIXED).

Your calls to DLL functions must be of the type "call identifier", such as:

          move 'dll_function_name' to pgm-name
          call pgm-name using arg1, arg2, ...

where "pgm-name" is defined as a character string in working-storage with
length long enough to contain the name of the function you are calling.

When you bind (link edit) your COBOL program, you need to include this
statement:

      IMPORT CODE,dll_member_name,'dll_function_name'

alternatively, you can supply a DD statement, say SYSDEFSD, that points to the
library used in your compile and bind of the C DLL, and then pass this
statement into the binder:

     INCLUDE SYSDEFSD(dll_name)

this will bring in the IMPORT statements generated at bind time by your bind of
the C DLL.


<ad>
All this, and much more, is discussed in great detail in our course
"Inter-Language Communication in OS/390". Details at:

http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220descr.htm

Nothing like some formal training to learn quickly how to be productive,
instead of stumbling around in the dark. We do the stumbling for you, learn
what works, and why,  and show you how to get practical tasks accomplished.

</ad>


>              3. Is there any difference between DLL on Os/390 and on
>WINDOWS?

Haven't a clue on this one. Sorry.


Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DLL SUPPORT FOR V2R10.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T03:49:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0201030349.1dc64e72@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201020422.2d8cc74f@posting.google.com> <20020102090442.28300.00002189@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
Hi Steve,
              The COBOL compiler we are using is of level 2.2.Today we
tried your suggestion and results are as follows.
     1. We have created a DLL written in C language and a C program
which uses DLL fuctions and called by COBOL program but we got error
messages which are as follows.

   a).   In linkedit step:(when option PGMNAME(LONGMIXED) is not used)
          "UNABLE TO SAVE ENTRY CLASS C_CODE IN OUTPUT MODULE." 
            "UNABLE TO SAVE TEXT CLASS B_LIT IN OUTPUT MODULE."  
              

   b). In cobol compile step:(when option PGMNAME(LONGMIXED) is used)
      A user-defined word was found as a "PROGRAM-ID" name under the
"PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)" cpmpiler option.when "PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)" is in
effect, a literal is expected for the "PROGRAM-ID" name.The name was
accepted in its uppercased format.
         We also tried with LOADMOD as PDSE but the error messages are
same.
******************************************************************************

 Following is source code and JCL used by us:
 1.DLL source code:
******************************************************************************
 #pragma export(badd)          
 #pragma export(bmultiply)     
 #pragma export(bdivide)       
    int badd(int x, int y)        
    {                             
        int z = x + y;            
        return(z);                
    }                             
    int bmultiply(int x, int y)   
    {                             
        int z = x*y;              
        return(z);                
    }                             
    int bdivide(int x, int y)     
    {                             
        int z = x/y;  
        return(z);          
 }                       
 #pragma export(berror)  
 int berror;  
*****************************************************************************
   This code is compiled(CBCDRVR )with option
EXPORTALL,DLL,prelinked(EDCPRLK) and linkedit(HEWL) and loadmod is
saved as DLLLRUN.
*****************************************************************************
 2.C source code.       
              

#include<stdio.h>                                      
#include<math.h>                                       
#pragma export(test)                                   
void test()                                            
{                                                      
     printf("this is a test function");                     
}                                                      
void main()                                            
{                                                      
     int a,b,c,d;                                           
     a=10;                                                 
     b=20;                                                 
     c=badd(a,b);                                          
     printf("result of addition is %d",c);                 
     d=bmultiply(a,b);                                     
     printf("result of multiplication is %d",d);           
}
*****************************************************************************
This code is compiled(CBCDRVR )with option
EXPORTALL,DLL,prelinked(EDCPRLK) and linkedit(HEWL) and loadmod is
saved as DLLFINAL
****************************************************************************
 2.COBOL source code:
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.        
 PROGRAM-ID. COBOL1.             
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.           
 DATA DIVISION.                  
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.        
 01 X PIC X9(9) VALUE 'DLLFINAL'.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.             
       CALL X.                   
       GOBACK. 
****************************************************************************
JCL for COBOL compile and linkedit:

//SWI6078$ JOB MSGCLASS=X,MSGLEVEL=(1,1),CLASS=B,                
 //         REGION=5M,NOTIFY=&SYSUID                              
 //STEP1   EXEC IGYWCL,

         INCLUDE SYSLIB(DLLRUN)

   "swi6078.test.load(DLLRUN)" contains the defintion sidedeck of the
imported fuction.
      In this COBOL compile and linkedit step we got error as
mentioned above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DLL SUPPORT FOR V2R10.

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T15:34:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020103103448.12656.00002281@mb-mt.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201030349.1dc64e72@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi, Shyam,

Hmm. It seems you've changed the problem slightly, but it should be solvable
anyway.

Just as a first comment, you no longer need the prelinker (at least if your
stuff goes to PDSEs) since the binder has been updated to do all the prelinker
stuff. Also, you will need to use binder option COMPAT(PM3) if that is not your
default.


1. In your DLL source, you must include

         #pragma linkage(badd,FETCHABLE)
         #pragma linkage(bmultiply,FETCHABLE)
         #pragma linkage(bdivide,FETCHABLE)

(I made an error in format in my earlier note); you do not need to compile this
with EXPORTALL, since you have #pragma exports for your variable and function
names, and you don't need the option DLL since this is a leaf routine and does
not do any calling itself.



2. In your C code, you will need 

          #pragma linkage(test,FETCHABLE)

Ahh, there it is: your C program cannot be a main!

A DLL is a collection of subroutines. In the LE world, you may only have one
main program in an enclave, and that role is played by your COBOL routine.

It looks like you have a C program to test your DLL and a COBOL program to test
your DLL; but your COBOL program cannot call your C test program: it's main
calling main.



3. Also, in your COBOL program you are calling DLLFINAL - you can't call a DLL:
you can only call functions in the DLL.



So the main thing seems to be you need to rethink your program structure.

Good luck

again, <ad>
 All this, and much more, is discussed in great detail in our course
 "Inter-Language Communication in OS/390". Details at:
 
 http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220descr.htm
 
 Nothing like some formal training to learn quickly how to be productive,
 instead of stumbling around in the dark. We do the stumbling for you, learn
 what works, and why,  and show you how to get practical tasks accomplished.
 
 </ad>


Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
facing a major problem which is as follows:
******************************************************************************
(a).DLL source code:
  #pragma linkage(BADD,FETCHABLE)    
  #pragma linkage(BMULTI,FETCHABLE)  
  #pragma linkage(BDIVIDE,FETCHABLE) 
  #pragma export(BADD)               
  #pragma export(BMULTI)             
  #pragma export(BDIVIDE)            
  int BADD(int x, int y)             
  {                                  
      int z = x + y;                 
      return(z);                     
  }                                  
  int BMULTI(int x, int y)           
  {                                  
      int z = x*y;                   
      return(z);                     
  }                                  
  int BDIVIDE(int x, int y)           
  {                                   
      int z = x/y;                    
      return(z);                      
  }                                   
******************************************************************************
(b).COBOL program calling above DLL:
   IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                                      
   PROGRAM-ID. COBOL1.                                           
   ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                                         
   DATA DIVISION.                                                
   WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                      
   01 WS-FIRST1 PIC S9(9) USAGE IS BINARY.                       
   01 WS-FIRST2 PIC S9(9) USAGE IS BINARY.                       
   01 WS-FIRST3 PIC S9(9) USAGE IS BINARY.

   01 WS-SUBRTN2 PIC X(6) VALUE 'BMULTI'.                      
   01 WS-SUBRTN3 PIC X(7) VALUE 'BDIVIDE'.

       CALL WS-SUBRTN2 USING BY VALUE 5, 5 RETURNING WS-FIRST2.

       CALL WS-SUBRTN3 USING BY VALUE 5, 5 RETURNING WS-FIRST3.     
       DISPLAY 'THIRD RETURN' WS-FIRST3.                            
       STOP RUN.   
******************************************************************************
 (c.)Definition sidedeck:
 IMPORT CODE 'MDLL'     BADD        
 IMPORT CODE 'MDLL'     BDIVIDE     
 IMPORT CODE 'MDLL'     BMULTI   
******************************************************************************
    Problem1.From the definition sidedeck it is clear that loadmod of
DLL is saved as member of PDSE as MDLL but at runtime of cobol we got

definition sidedeck and in linkedit map it shows the definition

The module BMULTIPL was not found."

follows:

 IMPORT CODE 'BADD'     BMULTI  
********************************************************************************
         so final conclusion is we are compelled to save loadmod name
same as subroutine name but with single subroutine in DLL it is OK
whereas with multiple subroutine in a DLL we are in problem.

*****************************************************************************
Thanks and Regards.
shyam nivas
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DLL SUPPORT FOR V2R10.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T14:24:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020112092456.22336.00000942@mb-mm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201112113.5ab4ac52@posting.google.com>`

```
Shyam Nivas writes ...

>Hi,
>       Calling DLL from cobol problem is solved but we are still
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>  #pragma linkage(BDIVIDE,FETCHABLE)

Shyam,

sorry, I was not precise enough about this. You need a #pragma linkage just for
the module, not each entry point in the module. So in your case, it looks like
the three statements above should be replace with:

#pragma linkage(MDLL<FETCHABLE)
 

>  #pragma export(BADD)               
>  #pragma export(BMULTI)             
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>sidedeck.


I think the correction suggested above will fix the problem


>*****************************************************************************
> problem2.If we save DLL load as BADD then output is as follows:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>*************************************************************************
*******
>         so final conclusion is we are compelled to save loadmod name
>same as subroutine name but with single subroutine in DLL it is OK
>whereas with multiple subroutine in a DLL we are in problem.

No, you just need to make a distinction between the module name and function
names. You are off on the right path, just got diverted a little.

When you bind your C DLL be sure to include a NAME statement as the last binder
control statement:

      NAME MDLL(R)

Hope this helps.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DLL SUPPORT FOR V2R10.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T00:50:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020114195008.10301.00003790@mb-fl.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201132121.5cb89fa1@posting.google.com>`

```
Shyam,

Well, I took your code and uploaded it to a mainframe running OS/390 2.10, and
here is what I found:

You need to put each C DLL function in a separate source module (so, BADD,
BMULTI, and BDIVIDE each contained just that function).

Each of these has to have a #pragma linkage(funct_name,FETCHABLE). Compile and
link each module.

Then build your DLL using the program binder using this input:

   INCLUDE SYSLMOD(BADD,BMULTI,BDIVIDE)
   INCLUDE SYSDEFSD(BADD,BMULTI,BDIVIDE)
   NAME MDLL(R)

this bind step should have at least RENT, DYNAM(DLL), AMODE=31, and RMODE=ANY

Compile and bind COBOL1 as you have it, and in the link step include:

//LKED.SYSIN  DD  *
   INCLUDE SYSDEFSD(MDLL)

This works like a champ.

I could have sworn I've put multiple functions in a C source that I built into
a DLL and then invoked from a COBOL program, but I couldn't get it to work. I
have accessed exported variables from C DLLs from COBOL code (in a variety of
ways), and I have included multiple functions in a C source module that I built
into a DLL and was able to access all the functions from a C program.

Good luck.

-Steve
Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

## Thread: Questions about COBOL... sorta... — 21 msgs

### Questions about COBOL... sorta...

- **From:** Ed Stoudenmire <estoude1@tampabay.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T17:54:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com>`

```
I've been programming in COBOL for about 20 years, mostly on MVS
systems (IBM and other), but also HP, Unisys, and some Unix and PC
dialects (RM, MF, etc).

I'm running into requirements today for LINC on a Unisys A-Series.
Who dat?

I've also seen requirements for Tandem SCOBOL, and for ADABAS/NATURAL.

Can't find any useful information about any of these.

ANY and ALL help will be most appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Questions about COBOL... sorta...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T20:03:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6O2Z7.1002$ex5.413816@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com>`

```
Ed Stoudenmire <estoude1@tampabay.rr.com> wrote in message
news:cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com...
>
> I'm running into requirements today for LINC on a Unisys A-Series.
> Who dat?

LINC is a Burroughs/Unisys 4GL product which generates COBOL code (which of
course, is recommended one edit "only" at the "officially safe" points, if
at all). Been around forever.

LINC systems used to have a (well-earned) reputation as real dogs, but I
haven't seen any LINC systems for at least 13 years, so that may well no
longer be true.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Questions about COBOL... sorta...

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T14:17:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a12l96$1n5b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com> <6O2Z7.1002$ex5.413816@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
The current name for LINC is Enterprise Application Environment, or EAE, it
is alive and well, has proven a very popular product, and it has indeed
evolved significantly in the last thirteen years!

The current version (EAE 3.1, AKA LINC 17) is available on a number of
platforms (NT/2000, MCP/AS, OS2200, UNIX that I know of).

The rest of this discussion is limited to the MCP/AS environment, which is
the one I am most familiar with, and my familiarity with LINC is limited to
its interaction with the various COBOL compilers over the last
decade-and-a-half.  .

In general, I find generated COBOL code hard to follow, and I think that's
as true of the code generated by LINC in the past as for any other COBOL
source generator from non-COBOL input.  Another "wrinkle" is that older
versions of LINC were designed to generate monolithic programs, and for
large LINC environments these single-source programs sometimes got HUGE
(exceeding half-a-million lines).

Although previous versions of EAE/LINC generated code compatible with the
COBOL74 compiler (the latest ones also allowed use of COBOL85), the COBOL
code that EAE 3.1 generates is COBOL85 source code exclusively, makes use of
many of the features of that language, and tends to avoid generating immense
monolithic COBOL programs in favor of a more modular approach, binding the
modules as subprograms in a larger ALGOL host file.

Yes, generated source can be difficult to follow sometimes.  But in general
I believe the EAE/LINC approach is to use COBOL as an "internal tool";
corrections and changes are made at that level rather than at the COBOL
source level; users are not encouraged to maintain multiple sets of changes
to the software (one to the LINC specifications, one to the COBOL source
programs, one to each of the associated data bases, etc.).   We believe the
application development tools available with EAE make that sort of
after-generation "tuning" unnecessary.   The fundamental idea is that the
EAE/LINC developer need not be aware of the fact that the environment is
generating COBOL code at all, much less be concerned with its intricacies.
The user interface has been vastly improved over LINC's lifetime, making
this a much more practical approach to application system design than once
it may have been.

I found the EAE Info Center at  http://www.unisys.es/sw/app_dev/eae/  .
Hope the information there proves useful; there are some other links that
also might be useful via a search on "eae" from www.unisys.com .

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Questions about COBOL... sorta...

- **From:** Richard Riehle <richard@adaworks.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T13:09:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C34C884.B591E0B1@adaworks.com>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com>`

```
Ed Stoudenmire wrote:

> I've also seen requirements for Tandem SCOBOL, and for ADABAS/NATURAL.

Tandem, unfortunately, is now part of COMPAQ.    SCOBOL is a specialized
language
used under the Pathway Facility of the Guardian Operating system for
developing
interactive applications.    It is somewhat the equivalent of CICS but
without all the
byzantizine complexity of CICS.    SCOBOL is COBOL-like in its syntax.
The reason
for this seems to be the notion that it would be easier for COBOL
programmers to
learn it using COBOL syntax.

You are right that there are not very many places to learn SCOBOL outside
COMPAQ's
own training programs.

Incidently, when it was still Tandem, they had one of the best COBOL 85
compilers
available on any system.   Credit for this probably should go to Dr.
Nelson, the
resident COBOL expert at Tandem.

I currently use Ada more than COBOL (by preference) and used the Tandem
Ada
compiler for some programming.   That was a much less capable product
since
Tandem management never took it seriously.   Ultimately, given the
advanced
design of the Tandem hardware and software architecture, Ada would have
been
a better match than COBOL.

Richard Riehle
richard@adaworks.
```

```
Tandem SCOBOL and COBOL85 are still alive and doing well under Compaq
management. However, many Tandem shops are using client-server technology,
which means they don't use SCOBOL. The shop I work for has never used
SCOBOL. Instead, it uses Realia Cobol running under PC DOS (legacy system),
and our newer Visual Basic GUI client upgrades us to the 21st century.
Tandem is our database engine, and it gives us 100% uptime, except for
scheduled downtime.
```

#### ↳ Re: Questions about COBOL... sorta...

- **From:** ookpik1@yahoo.co.uk (Dean Powell)
- **Date:** 2002-01-04T07:40:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f7686d4.0201040740.d518f12@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com>`

```
Ed Stoudenmire <estoude1@tampabay.rr.com> wrote in message news:<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com>...
[snip]
> 
> I've also seen requirements for Tandem SCOBOL, and for ADABAS/NATURAL.
> 
> Can't find any useful information about any of these.
> 

ADABAS is a database system that runs on IBM mainframes, among other
platforms.  It's similar, I suppose, to DB2.  I used it a few years
back -- quite a low-level interface,  but very, very fast.  As with
DB2, you can access information in an ADABAS database from a COBOL
program with embedded SQL.  ADABAS was developed by Software AG in
Germany.  A joke I heard floating around was that the AG stood for
"God Awful" backwards.  I didn't mind using it, although we accessed
it using assembler programs, so I can't say how good the COBOL
interfaces were.

Natural is a 4GL that Software AG developed specifically for ADABAS. 
I used this as well way back.  It had quite a COBOL feel to it.  When
I was using it (1991-1992) it was a pretty popular product in my neck
of the woods, but I haven't heard much about it since.  I'm not really
a big fan of 4GLs, but Natural was OK.

I hope this helps.

Regards,
Dean Powell
Edmonton, Canada
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Questions about COBOL... sorta...

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T12:11:48+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c363914_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com> <1f7686d4.0201040740.d518f12@posting.google.com>`

```

Dean Powell <ookpik1@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message >
> ADABAS is a database system that runs on IBM mainframes, among other
> platforms.  It's similar, I suppose, to DB2.  I used it a few years
> back -- quite a low-level interface,  but very, very fast.  As with
> DB2, you can access information in an ADABAS database from a COBOL
> program with embedded SQL.

This is interesting. I worked with ADABAS in the 70s and it was NOT a
Relational Database (they hadn't been invented then), so it couldn't be
accessed by SQL. From your post , Dean, it looks as though the system has
had a "face lift" and a Relational interface has been bolted to it so it can
support SQL (The most popular form of Database access.) While it may be
"similar to DB2" from the User point of view, the similarity ends there.
Unless they have completely rewritten it using the Relational model (and I
don't believe they have for a number of reasons, one of which is that it
performs very well and you noted how fast it is...), it is based on the
Network database model which utilises linked Master/Slave Sets. This is
completely different to DB2 which is based on the Relational model.

What your post illustrates is the ubiquity of the Relational model.  Even
things that are not Relational are made to look as if they are, because
that's what the programmer of today knows and understands.

I wonder how long it will be before we see IMS databases accessed using
embedded SQL <G>...

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
Everything to be known about this subject over powers my mind. It is
insteresting to me that
ADABASE followed the network structure.  That's what happens when one gets
old and out
of the main stream.

However, one point made by the author above reminds me of the effort to add
data
base features to COBOL  Without the details, note that it took seven years
to introduice
a CODASYL spec never standardized to my knowledge.  Meanwhile, the
"inventor" or
Relational (Date I belive) continued his research and IBM 's market place
put the Relational Model
in the work place by in large.

This was very funny to me for prior to this time, IBM published a "book" on
how to grow your
own data base I/O as there were too many different requirements. At least
that was the word some of us got.  Meanwhile, IDS users who had to move to
IBM hardware because of centralized
management decisions, wrote their own IDS look alike so that they could
continue to use
the method they had been using on GE hardware.  Following the INTRODUCTION
of Relational
data base C. Bachman produced the first such system using the network model.

I fully understand the desire to make the system operate using math, but
from my reading of
this newsgroup over the past five years, it seems that what Relational makes
up in computer
resources to obtain answers, it hampers the users needs to deside what is
needed and go directly
to the information without a great deal of investment.  While this is
speculation on my part,
the fact that there are network data base systems in the market gives me the
feeling that
people do not understand just what it is they are trying to do, and what
they pay for to get it.

Warren Simmons
and instead sells lots of memory, and software.
```

```
William, I recall the term "inverted file."

AFAIK, one can use this definition:
A  file with records indexed on one key is a single key inverted file.
etc.
A file with records indexed on ALL fields (keys) is  a fully inverted file.
As popularily said in these parts, you pick the inversion to fit the
problem.

Warren Simmons
```
