###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Inverted files WAS:Questions about COBOL... sorta...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T13:48:03+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c38f6b3_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com> <1f7686d4.0201040740.d518f12@posting.google.com> <3c363914_8@Usenet.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gph8mn0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <f6IZ7.326949$W8.12566349@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I wasn't going to go into this, Warren, but what the Hell...<G>


warren.simmons <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:f6IZ7.326949$W8.12566349@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> William, I recall the term "inverted file."
>
> AFAIK, one can use this definition:
> A  file with records indexed on one key is a single key inverted file.
> etc.

Er, yes... superficially.

The action of simply indexing does not ensure inversion, at least not in the
sense that a DBA would understand it. (It is more properly called "binary
inversion" but convention allows us to drop the "binary"... it is fully
explained below.)

Inverted indexes (and consequently, the "inverted files" they refer to)
first made their appearance in the late sixties as disk based technology
took hold. There was still a substantial "residue" of Assembler Programming
around and "everybody knew" that Binary was best. (The advent of High Level,
byte oriented Languages, like COBOL, slowly changed this perception until it
disappeared and today's Programmers are happy to deal with bytes, rather
than bits.)

Consider the following (Clear your mind of the beloved Indexed Sequential
model, and try to think in terms of RAW DATA, with no structure, sequence or
organization. Long ago, at the dawn of cybertime, when the first disk
actuators began clicking across the ferric oxide, this was what we dealt
with <G>...)

A file....each record has 3 fields. We'll call them "ID", "SIZE", and
"COLOUR". Note that the ID is unique, but the other fields are not. In fact,
it doesn't matter whether ANY key is unique or not in an inverted file (it
is MUCH more efficient if it ISN'T unique!), as we shall see in a
moment...(I did it this way because this is what most COBOL programmers are
familiar with.)

Record     ID     SIZE     COLOUR
(position)
1             7a1        6            blue
2             3b5        8            blue
3             7a3        8            red
4             7a4        10          blue
5             7a2        8            blue
6             7a6        10          red
7             7a5        6            red
8             3b3        10          blue

There are a few things we can immediately note about this data:

1.  "7a" objects are represented in SIZE 6, 8, and 10, and COLOURs red and
blue.
2.  "3b" objects are represented in SIZE 8 and 10, and COLOUR blue.
3.  It is NOT possible to derive the SIZE or COLOUR from the ID by a
consistent mathematical process.
4.  The IDs are unique.
4.  There is NO intended order or sequence to any of the data.

Because the IDs are unique we may decide to build an index on them and call
it a "primary key".  It (the index) looks like this:

Record     ID
(position)
1             7a1
2             3b5
3             7a3
4             7a4
5             7a2
6             7a6
7             7a5
8             3b3

We can sort it to provide the records in ID sequence. This is NOT an INDEXED
SEQUENTIAL organisation...(actually, Burroughs (the half of Unisys which was
NOT Sperry <G>), used to refer to it as INDEXED RANDOM which is quite a good
name for it...)

But what about the data? Well, we want to be able to search VERY quickly
without reading physical records (we will ensure that any indexes we create
are small enough to be in memory) so the best technique for this is called
FILE INVERSION. Here's how it works...

Let's build an inverted index for the SIZE. Start with SIZE 6.

There are 8 records on the file so we need a bit string 8 bits long. It
looks like this:

10000010

What this tells us is that record 1 and record 7 on the base file have a
SIZE attribute equal to "6".

Here's SIZE 8...

01101000

and SIZE 10...

00010101

Now we can do a check that all records have been accounted for by ORing the
3 indexes together, like this...
10000010
01101000
00010101
-----------
11111111

If the result is x'FF' (HIGH-VALUES, if you prefer...) then we have indeed
accounted for every record and our system has integrity.

Building COLOUR indexes is carried out the same way...

BLUE index
11011001

RED index
00100110

Note that in this example, we have organised ALL of the information (apart
from the ID), in ALL of the base records, in a total of 5 bytes!

The information in the base file has been "inverted" in the sense that we
can now search for any value and get back to a record, instead of the normal
approach of reading records to find a value.

So I want to find a "red" size 10 object...   I can AND the RED index and
the SIZE 10 index.

RED         00100110
SIZE 10   00010101
                ------------
                00000100

Immediately, I find there is only one, and it is in record 6.

Remember that to do this I used 2 bytes and one of the fastest instructions
in any machine's instruction set. (Logical instructions on bits are
typically MANY times faster than equivalent operations on bytes, if you care
about that sort of thing...<G>).

Databases which implement inverted files are typically blazingly fast. The
best one I ever saw was called "system 2000". Sadly, it has fallen victim to
the unstoppable onset of the Relational Model and I don't know of anyone who
is using it today. It was a Networked DB (like IDMS), with all sets
inverted. I saw it search a database of Ford motor parts in the
early 70s. It searched over 2,000,000 components in under 5 seconds and, at
the time, that was just awesome. An IMS database to do the same job would
have taken several minutes.

 (Willem noted elsewhere in this thread that he believed ADABAS was inverted
and NOT Networked. It was common practice for databases at the time to
establish inverted indexes to the underlying data, but the mechanism for
storing the underlying data was based on either the Network or Hierarchic
Database Models.)

So when Warren says a file with a primary index is a "single key inverted
file", he is not exactly wrong, because the key data HAS been "inverted",
inasmuch as we can get back to the base record from it. However, most DBAs
will consider an "inverted" file to be one which is referenced by inverted
indexes of the type described above. (i.e. binary inverted indexes)

By now you will have realised that if a key is UNIQUE, it is NOT  a good
candidate for binary inversion. It would mean having an index with a single
bit set, for every key value. However, back in the 60s there were systems
that did that. Where the file size is, say, not more than 4096 records
(index requires 512 bytes; easily stored in memory without requirement for
paging, even on a small system) it was considered feasible to index unique
values in this way. Today we wouldn't (and don't <G>) do it; we just opt for
a non binary unique index, or (preferably, in my opinion) move to RDB.

> A file with records indexed on ALL fields (keys) is  a fully inverted
file.
> As popularily said in these parts, you pick the inversion to fit the
> problem.
>
Yes, that's right, but it's not just the keys. EVERY field is a KEY if it is
represented by a binary inverted index. The criteria you use are:

1. Are there a number of unique fields? (Bad candidate for binary inversion;
may be good for Indexed Sequential with Alternate indexes (remember,
alternate indexes don't HAVE to be non-unique...)

2. How important is incredibly (as opposed to just fairly...<G>) fast
access, and what about available storage?

3. How big are the files? (you need 1 bit of storage in the index for every
record on the base file, and an index for each unique value of a data
field...)

I hope the above may stimulate some of the more adventurous to try some of
this as a departure from the Relational model <G> (this is not an implied
criticism of the Relational Model, which has achieved its place because it
is the best "all round" model for a data repository... but programmers need
to have some fun occasionally, and building indexed random, binary inverted
databases, for small (non critical) systems is not only fun, it is immensely
satisfying...<G>)

Pete.





 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Inverted files WAS:Questions about COBOL... sorta...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T02:26:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fH7_7.31217$fe1.532470@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com> <1f7686d4.0201040740.d518f12@posting.google.com> <3c363914_8@Usenet.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gph8mn0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <f6IZ7.326949$W8.12566349@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3c38f6b3_3@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter, when I first heard about inverted files, T. Wm. Olle who had created
a package for one of
the early Main Frame vendors explained it simple by telling us about an
application for as an example an Automobile Manufacture who not only wanted
to find all v8's built on such and such a date, but needed the color
distribution and so on so that a "distribution" of the "problem" or solution
as we might call it could be found.  I can believe that when tires of
certain products started to give the vendor to many problems, the most
trivial thing about those products were of interest.
No discussion of how the inversion took place was made. Consequently, I see
that one way is to
do what you defined. Now, I know a little more. Thanks.

Warren Simmoms

fact,

are

> Because the IDs are unique we may decide to build an index on them and

> it a "primary key".  It (the index) looks like this:

INDEXED

was
> NOT Sperry <G>), used to refer to it as INDEXED RANDOM which is quite a
good
> name for it...)
>

> without reading physical records (we will ensure that any indexes we
create

> FILE INVERSION. Here's how it works...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now we can do a check that all records have been accounted for by ORing
the
> 3 indexes together, like this...
> 10000010

> can now search for any value and get back to a record, instead of the
normal
> approach of reading records to find a value.

instructions

> typically MANY times faster than equivalent operations on bytes, if you
care
> about that sort of thing...<G>).
>
> Databases which implement inverted files are typically blazingly fast. The
> best one I ever saw was called "system 2000". Sadly, it has fallen victim
to
> the unstoppable onset of the Relational Model and I don't know of anyone
who
> is using it today. It was a Networked DB (like IDMS), with all sets
> inverted. I saw it search a database of Ford motor parts in the
> early 70s. It searched over 2,000,000 components in under 5 seconds and,
at
> the time, that was just awesome. An IMS database to do the same job would
> have taken several minutes.
>
>  (Willem noted elsewhere in this thread that he believed ADABAS was
inverted
> and NOT Networked. It was common practice for databases at the time to
> establish inverted indexes to the underlying data, but the mechanism for
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> candidate for binary inversion. It would mean having an index with a
single
> bit set, for every key value. However, back in the 60s there were systems
> that did that. Where the file size is, say, not more than 4096 records
> (index requires 512 bytes; easily stored in memory without requirement for
> paging, even on a small system) it was considered feasible to index unique
> values in this way. Today we wouldn't (and don't <G>) do it; we just opt
for
> a non binary unique index, or (preferably, in my opinion) move to RDB.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, that's right, but it's not just the keys. EVERY field is a KEY if it
is
> represented by a binary inverted index. The criteria you use are:
>
> 1. Are there a number of unique fields? (Bad candidate for binary
inversion;
> may be good for Indexed Sequential with Alternate indexes (remember,
> alternate indexes don't HAVE to be non-unique...)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3. How big are the files? (you need 1 bit of storage in the index for
every
> record on the base file, and an index for each unique value of a data
> field...)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is the best "all round" model for a data repository... but programmers
need

- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com> <1f7686d4.0201040740.d518f12@posting.google.com> <3c363914_8@Usenet.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gph8mn0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <f6IZ7.326949$W8.12566349@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3c38f6b3_3@Usenet.com> <fH7_7.31217$fe1.532470@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

warren.simmons <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:fH7_7.31217$fe1.532470@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> Peter, when I first heard about inverted files, T. Wm. Olle who had
created
> a package for one of
> the early Main Frame vendors explained it simple by telling us about an
> application for as an example an Automobile Manufacture who not only
wanted
> to find all v8's built on such and such a date, but needed the color
> distribution and so on so that a "distribution" of the "problem" or
solution
> as we might call it could be found.  I can believe that when tires of
> certain products started to give the vendor to many problems, the most
> trivial thing about those products were of interest.
> No discussion of how the inversion took place was made. Consequently, I
see
> that one way is to
> do what you defined. Now, I know a little more. Thanks.
>
> Warren Simmoms

<snip of original message>

In the night, I remembered one practice the COBOL committee adopted, and it
reminded
me of this discussion.

Many features, though agreeably desired by the whole group of vendors and
users, gave some
vendors problems because the initial definition of the feature reflected
certain hardware features
not universal to all vendors. The feature could be implemented, but not with
the detail specs
submitted which was biased by the submitter's experience.

A concept was quickly developed that was not perfect.  The feature needed to
be defined in a way
that no implemention notions were present, i.e., we will do it but maybe not
that way.

Some of the problems seen today might be the result of that policy. However,
if it had been set
somewhat differently, some things would be more universal now.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Inverted files WAS:Questions about COBOL... sorta...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T12:28:38+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3b86a5_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com> <1f7686d4.0201040740.d518f12@posting.google.com> <3c363914_8@Usenet.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gph8mn0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <f6IZ7.326949$W8.12566349@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3c38f6b3_3@Usenet.com> <fH7_7.31217$fe1.532470@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <szp_7.238529$WW.12870203@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren,

I never cease to be amazed by the far-sighted and altruistic approach
adopted by CODASYL in 1959.

Every time I read the COBOL preface in a manual it reminds me that these
people grasped the nettle, subdued their own personal egos and  business
advantages, and really tried to do something for the good of the Industry.

We are unlikely to ever see the like again.(Just look at the current
scurrilous attempts to remove this preface from the next standard...)

What you describe below is a very sensible approach that goes beyond vested
interest and considers the greater good.

It is no wonder that their creation has been around for over 40 years.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Questions about COBOL... sorta...

- **From:** ookpik1@yahoo.co.uk (Dean Powell)
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T13:19:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f7686d4.0201051319.104c9cf6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com> <1f7686d4.0201040740.d518f12@posting.google.com> <3c363914_8@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3c363914_8@Usenet.com>...
[stuff respectfully snipped out]
> What your post illustrates is the ubiquity of the Relational model.  Even
> things that are not Relational are made to look as if they are, because
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.
[end of snip]

Sorry...a bit off topic, but...

Interesting, isn't it?  I currently work on a Unisys product called
Mapper.  Mapper lays out its data much like Excel does -- very
column-oriented.  Rather than work with the familiar "read a record/do
something/read the next record" Mapper really expects you to do things
in a "whole column at a time" fashion.  Very powerful.  Painful to
learn, but powerful.

Anyway, in our new release, we've been introduced to MQL (Mapper Query
Language).  Essentially, it's a product that allows you to extract and
manipulate Mapper data using standard SQL.  The interesting thing here
is that Mapper actually has nothing to do with a relational database,
and yet here they are allowing you to access information with SQL.

And yes, Pete, your clarification on the different philosophies
underlying DB2 vs. ADABAS is quite correct.  It's been 10 years since
I used ADABAS, and my old (old at 37 -- EEK) brain is starting to
forget such things.

Dean
```

#### ↳ Re: Questions about COBOL... sorta...

- **From:** cesullivan@aol.comsf (Cathy Sullivan)
- **Date:** 2002-01-04T18:12:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020104131227.29679.00000774@mb-cq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com>`

```
In article <cbh93uosvn7fla23falgout7tak5cdo80d@4ax.com>, Ed Stoudenmire
<estoude1@tampabay.rr.com> writes:

>I'm running into requirements today for LINC on a Unisys A-Series.
>Who dat?

I saw a job announcement that wanted someone with LINC on a Unisys A-Series.  I
have over 10 years experience with Unisys A-Series Cobol, COMS, WFL, CANDE, DMS
II and other utilities that HR people put in job specs.

So, I won't apply for the job since I don't have LINC.  And I don't think it is
worth it in the long term for me to learn it.  If there is a way to learn it! 
If I remember, the pay rate was $20 to $35 an hour.  Bet they have a hard time
finding someone.

Cathy
```

---

<a id="t-f29b5c3dd7"></a>
## Thread: fujitsu cobol v3 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### fujitsu cobol v3

- **From:** radami1@uic.edu (ron)
- **Date:** 2002-01-03T15:35:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85105800.0201031535.6ae36196@posting.google.com>`

```
I am using fujitsu cobol version 3 in a batch job on winnt and I
always get a window opening up every time the batch runs. The window's
purpose is to get runtime variable values and it is genreated by the
fujitsu system. The program won't start unless this window is
satisfied. How do I suppress this window so that the job can be
scheduled at a later time for unattended operation?

                Ron
```

#### ↳ Re: fujitsu cobol v3

- **From:** "Dionisis Vrionis" <software@dksoft.gr>
- **Date:** 2002-01-04T09:30:29+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a13lh8$iis$1@ulysses.noc.ntua.gr>`
- **References:** `<85105800.0201031535.6ae36196@posting.google.com>`

```
Use the command on this window typing

@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE

in Cobol85.cbr file.

Take care of upper and lower cases


"ron" <radami1@uic.edu> wrote in message
news:85105800.0201031535.6ae36196@posting.google.com...
> I am using fujitsu cobol version 3 in a batch job on winnt and I
> always get a window opening up every time the batch runs. The window's
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 Ron
```

#### ↳ Re: fujitsu cobol v3

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T17:44:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U7l_7.7332$cD4.11867@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<85105800.0201031535.6ae36196@posting.google.com>`

```
in the COBOL85.CBR file insert this line:

@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE

## Thread: OT - MVS or OS/390 GDG Question — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - MVS or OS/390 GDG Question

- **From:** "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-04T04:00:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gN9Z7.76265$Bd.20858851@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Can someone answer an IBM MVS type question regarding files with Generation
Data Groups (GDGs)?

I create a GDG base, then write a file named A.B.INFILE(+1) containing Rec1:
AAA; Rec2: BBB; Rec3: CCC.  This creates file A.B.INFILE.G0001V00.  I then
write another file A.B.INFILE(+1) containing Rec1: DDD; Rec2: EEE; Rec3:
FFF.  This creates file A.B.INFILE.G0002V00. I then run an IEBGENER to copy
A.B.INFILE without any generation specified (to select all generations) to
file A.B.PROCFILE.  File A.B.PROCFILE now contains 6 records: R1: DDD; R2:
EEE; R3: FFF; R4: AAA; R5: BBB; R6: CCC.  When I now process file
A.B.PROCFILE I have records out of order, processing newer records before
older records.

Does anyone know a way to copy all generations of A.B.INFILE **in order,
oldest first** to one file, without knowing how many generations of
A.B.INFILE actually exist? And/or, does anyone know a better newsgroup on
which to ask this question?
```

```

been asked before.  Use the "search" facility there.
```

<snip>
> Does anyone know a way to copy all generations of A.B.INFILE **in order,

You might also ask on comp.lang.asm370, but it is slightly
off-topic there.

The problem here is the order that the system concatenates a
GDG group. I think you will need to sort your records before
processing them.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT - MVS or OS/390 GDG Question

Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:3C35EBFC.1CE5F2BC@ix.netcom.com...
> William Bub wrote:
> >
> > Can someone answer an IBM MVS type question regarding files with
Generation
> > Data Groups (GDGs)?
> <snip>
> > Does anyone know a way to copy all generations of A.B.INFILE **in order,
> > oldest first** to one file, without knowing how many generations of
> > A.B.INFILE actually exist? And/or, does anyone know a better newsgroup
on
> > which to ask this question?

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'd  suggest the real problem here is that the system was designed to use
GDGs (multiple sets of like or related data) instead of having been designed
with timestamps and/or serial numbers to enable 'in sequence' processing or
re-processing.

<sniff, sniff>.....hmm, does this smell like an attempt to do an upgrade of
an existing system 'on the cheap'  or what?

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - MVS or OS/390 GDG Question

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-04T20:13:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E1oZ7.4253$cD4.7986@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<gN9Z7.76265$Bd.20858851@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Sorting the files may not help if you don't have a field(s) that results in the
same order you'd get inputting in GDG order. Besides, a sort can add mucho time
to the process.

As Terry suggested, go to www.mvshelp.com; their archives mention a CBT tape pgm
that does exactly what you want. CBT tape pgms can be downloaded.

HTH, Jack.

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Interesting question - I haven't got a clue !

There are plenty of work arounds including adding timestamps etc, for
sorting, or using intermediate datasets, to stage into the generations in
"reverse order" so they copy in the correct sequence.

My MVS book is at work - I'll check for an option on Monday. If you can use
the idea of a sort, why not scrap the IEBGENER completely, and just use
Syncsort, to cut out an extra step?

I hope someone here will post a command option, 'cos I love to know!

Run an IEFBR14 step to pre-allocation a sufficient number of generations,
with DISP of MOD.  Then hard code the generations in reverse order in the
concatenation listing. This may not be the cutest solution, but with 2 or 3
comments, should be understood at 3:30am, when it crashes, unlike an obscure
MVS GDG option.
```

input   dd  dsn=a.b.infile,disp=shr
output dd  dsn=a.b.combine,disp=(,catlg,delete),dcb=*.input

<a id="t-9f9701a7ff"></a>
## Thread: WinAPI call to kill an App — 3 msgs

### WinAPI call to kill an App

- **From:** "Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-04T20:39:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c366a1b_3@news1.prserv.net>`

```
If there a way to kill an application that is residing in the Task Bar via
an API call?
```

#### ↳ Re: WinAPI call to kill an App

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T13:30:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eDZ7.31$Fa6.139942@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3c366a1b_3@news1.prserv.net>`

```
Stephen Plotnick <thepla@attglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3c366a1b_3@news1.prserv.net...
> If there a way to kill an application that is residing in the Task Bar via
> an API call?

Yes.  From WITHIN the process (i.e., your program) you use may use
ExitProcess. But I suspect you want to terminate another process. In this
case, you "can" use TerminateProcess, BUT NOTE THE REMARK that says to use
it "only in extreme circumstances!"

FWIW, it's not only the state of global data which may be corrupted. If a
program has installed a hook, the hook chain is not adjusted by
TerminateProcess and you could end up with a GPF in some some other
application, a GPF which points to a "null" or "other" module and will NOT
represent an error in the program which has apparently failed.

This is essentially what is done when you terminate a process from the task
list.

If you are not in an emergency situation, you would do better to send the
process the keystrokes or mouse messages the application uses to exit
"gracefully."

MCM

= COURTESY MSDN ==========================================================
The TerminateProcess function terminates the specified process and all of
its threads.
BOOL TerminateProcess(
    HANDLE  hProcess, // handle to the process
    UINT  uExitCode  // exit code for the process
   );
Parameters
hProcess
Identifies the process to terminate. The handle must have PROCESS_TERMINATE
access.

uExitCode
Specifies the exit code for the process and for all threads terminated as a
result of this call. Use the GetExitCodeProcess function to retrieve the
process's exit value. Use the GetExitCodeThread function to retrieve a
thread's exit value.

Return Value
If the function succeeds, the return value is TRUE.
If the function fails, the return value is FALSE. To get extended error
information, call GetLastError.

Remarks
The TerminateProcess function is used to unconditionally cause a process to
exit. Use it only in extreme circumstances. The state of global data
maintained by dynamic-link libraries (DLLs) may be compromised if
TerminateProcess is used rather than ExitProcess.
=========================================
```

#### ↳ Re: WinAPI call to kill an App

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-06T22:38:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nl4_7.57$bd.34183@monger.newsread.com>`
- **References:** `<3c366a1b_3@news1.prserv.net>`

```
TerminateProcess can be used, but it can cause problems - DLL's aren't
unloaded properly for example.

A safer way that sometimes works is to send a WM_CLOSE message to the top
level window of the application.

You can also see this article on a safer alternative to TerminateProcess:

http://www.wdj.com/articles/1999/9907/9907c/9907c.htm?topic=articles


"Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3c366a1b_3@news1.prserv.net...
> If there a way to kill an application that is residing in the Task Bar via
> an API call?
>
>
```

## Thread: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

- **From:** mshetty@mail.com (mshetty)
- **Date:** 2002-01-04T21:13:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I am a little confused about passing variables / values BY REFERENCE.
Is "the variable" passed by BY REFERENCE or "the value" ??.  When a
pass by REFERENCE is used does the address get passed ??.

Would help if I could get some inputs on the above.

Thanks and Regards,
M Shetty
```

#### ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T05:51:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tXvbCdloevJm@thishost>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com>`

```
On Sat, 5 Jan 2002 05:13:27 UTC, mshetty@mail.com (mshetty) wrote:

> Hi,
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> M Shetty

BY REFERENCE passes the address of the variable

BY VALUE passes the value of the variable (usually restricted to a 
variable that is a native length integer, like a 32 bit integer on 
Intel or 64 bit on a 64 bit CPU)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

- **From:** mshetty@mail.com (mshetty)
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T07:56:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201050756.451c141a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tXvbCdloevJm@thishost>`

```
lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote in message news:<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tXvbCdloevJm@thishost>...
> On Sat, 5 Jan 2002 05:13:27 UTC, mshetty@mail.com (mshetty) wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Intel or 64 bit on a 64 bit CPU)
I come from a C++ / C background. If the address of the variable is
passed then the receiving variable should be pointer(which is not in
case of COBOL)??
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T11:16:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a17ce0$75o$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tXvbCdloevJm@thishost> <bfbb8fd4.0201050756.451c141a@posting.google.com>`

```
What you are "missing" is the fact that (basically) all 01-levels in Linkage
Section are (implicitly) just "pointers".  They have no storage allocated to
them.  Therefore, the COBOL run-time "matches" linkage section 01-levels
(via the ORDER in the Procedure Division HEADER, not the order in the
Linkage Section) with "passed" parameters.

In the case of parameters passed BY VALUE - the "address" is (depending on
the OS architecture) to "the Stack"

In the case of parameters passed BY CONTENT - the address is to a
"temporary" copy of the original item

In the case of parameters passed BY REFERENCE - the address is to the start
of the "original" storage of the passed item.

  ***

Does this help?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>

- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tXvbCdloevJm@thishost> <bfbb8fd4.0201050756.451c141a@posting.google.com> <a17ce0$75o$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Sorry Bill,

I thought I read all the replys and covered ground you did.

Jack

```

"mshetty" <mshetty@mail.com>

> I come from a C++ / C background. If the address of the variable is
> passed then the receiving variable should be pointer(which is not in
> case of COBOL)??

It is, but we in the COBOL community commonly use the word 'Pointer' to
refer to the sign on one of the lavatories at a beer-joint.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T21:39:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-ZqxcG3N6pZPJ@thishost>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tXvbCdloevJm@thishost> <bfbb8fd4.0201050756.451c141a@posting.google.com>`

```
On Sat, 5 Jan 2002 15:56:39 UTC, mshetty@mail.com (mshetty) wrote:

> lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote in message news:<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tXvbCdloevJm@thishost>...
> > On Sat, 5 Jan 2002 05:13:27 UTC, mshetty@mail.com (mshetty) wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> case of COBOL)??

In COBOL, when you describe the parameters passed to a program you 
indicate what their treatment is (reference, value etc)


calling program

call "xyz" by reference doodad by value thing


called program

linkage section

01  doodad-link.
    02  filler  pic x(100).
01  thing-link  pic s9(9) comp-5.

procedure division using by reference doodad-link by value thing-link.

The "by reference" and "by value" must match in the call and the 
called using statements.

In C

xyz(&doodad, thing)

void xyz(char *doodad_link, long thing_link)
```

#### ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T18:05:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LfHZ7.5261$cD4.9058@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

When passing data "BY REFERENCE" the CALLer and the CALLee reference the same
physical piece of main storage for that variable. The implication of this is: if
the CALLee changes the content of the passed data field, the CALLer's subsequent
use of that data, upon return, will reflect that change.

When using "BY REFERENCE", speculation about whether the address or the data is
passed is irrelevant, since reference to the dataname in the CALLee's Linkage
Section provides access to the CALLer's data (the address is ALWAYS used to
access the data). The Linkage Section, in fact, provides the CALLee with
addressibility to the CALLer's data. But that's more than one needs to know to
use the passed data.      

HTH, Jack    

In article <bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com>, mshetty says...
>
>Hi,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>M Shetty
```

```
Normally, I like the way CoBOL uses English where it can in favor of jargon.
 But not in this case.  When using a computer term, I prefer using the
computer jargon that everybody else uses.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T22:46:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uzp_7.238530$WW.12870056@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com> <l6m_7.187776$m05.15892025@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:l6m_7.187776$m05.15892025@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
> Normally, I like the way CoBOL uses English where it can in favor of
jargon.
>  But not in this case.  When using a computer term, I prefer using the
> computer jargon that everybody else uses.

Howard,

Not that much jargon provided debate when COBOL was first defined. Also,
management (picking up the tab for all those meetings) wanted something they
could read without learning the jargon.

Even today, among some IT professionals, there is a division between knowing
English
and jargon.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T15:56:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1f4lc$52d$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com> <l6m_7.187776$m05.15892025@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <uzp_7.238530$WW.12870056@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On  7-Jan-2002, "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> > Normally, I like the way CoBOL uses English where it can in favor of
> jargon.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> they could read without learning the jargon.

Good point.  But as times change the language has changed.  In the case of
linkages, adding an option to use the jargon that programmers understand
would have been nice.

> Even today, among some IT professionals, there is a division between
> knowing English and jargon.

The language to use should be the language that communicates best with our
users.   For business rules, that should be English, or even better,
accounting.  But this isn't a business rule, it is a computerese rule.

The term COMP-3 was changed to PACKED-DECIMAL, to better communicate the
data structure to non CoBOL users.  (business users still shouldn't care).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T20:31:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NGI_7.240045$WW.12963088@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com> <l6m_7.187776$m05.15892025@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <uzp_7.238530$WW.12870056@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <a1f4lc$52d$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
You make some good points, Howard. Yet, the terms programmers use were set
by someone
not interested in communicating with everyone. In some cases, it sometimes
appears, that the
programmer is interested in uniqueness instead of clarity. In the area we
are discussing, these
terms did not exist until someone sat down to solve a problem.  The
assignment did NOT include
using terms everyone might know or understand. For example, I still wonder
what the term "link edit" was to be when first introduced. Likewise, in the
current spec terms are introduced sparingly, but some seem to have meanings
duplicated by existing terms. The choice was made to distinguish the
function being specified from another without introducing another option
word perhaps. Or even worse, because the specifier did not see the
similarity or feared the complexity it would give the compiler instead of
the reader.

Still, my only point is that new words added should reflect some widely
understood idea, not the programmer's jargon.

Warren Simmons
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:a1f4lc$52d$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  7-Jan-2002, "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> data structure to non CoBOL users.  (business users still shouldn't care).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More details on passing parameters BY REFERENCE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T21:23:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3B6455.6B13E2DB@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201042113.2123eb20@posting.google.com> <l6m_7.187776$m05.15892025@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <uzp_7.238530$WW.12870056@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <a1f4lc$52d$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <NGI_7.240045$WW.12963088@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


"warren.simmons" wrote:

>
> Still, my only point is that new words added should reflect some widely
> understood idea, not the programmer's jargon.

Warren,

Agree with your general idea. I can think of a new one for COBOL 2002 - VALIDATE
- could have been either VALIDATE or VET (vetting as in 'security clearance',
not some poor joker sticking his arm up to his elbow in a cow's innards <G>).
Either word expresses the idea.

Unfortunately it does come unglued when you think in terms of OO. ENCAPSULATION
- a real ugly word; I suppose with some thought it could be expressed more
clearly,  perhaps 'a method is a self contained program' ?. POLYMORPHISM -
jeepers ! How do you describe that without some lengthy examples - so the Greek
word has to prevail to get the general idea across. INSTANCE/INSTANTIATION -
well you have a vague idea on that one now, invoke 'new' - but it's still
difficult to grasp. Again, even an easily understood word like INHERITANCE has
to be backed up by examples to show the various ways it can be done.

Now one for your $10 list - TUPLES or SPACE TUPLES - go check IBM and Sun sites
as IBM/Java are big on this, an extension of the OO collection/dictionary
concept..

Jimmy
```

Timo Erbach wrote:

> I'm looking for a free cobol compiler which 
> works under win2000 ?

TinyCOBOL is a free compiler released under the GPL/LGPL.

TinyCOBOL now supports the Win32 platform.

It does require GCC for Win32 (Mingw32).

A binary is available on the TinyCOBOL snapshots web page (1).

 
David Essex

## Thread: Parser to parse a COBOL file — 29 this month / 38 total

#### ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** mshetty@mail.com (mshetty)
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T07:51:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201050751.10cae749@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

This is what I am doing to parse for lines containing PROGRAM-ID/
WORKING-STORAGE/ LINKAGE SECTION etc.
1. I read a line of input
2. Remove delimiters like space, comma, tab and enter.
3. Parse the first word in the "line read" and
4. Compare it with the set of known keywords like PROGRAM_ID,
WORKING-STORAGE etc.

Alternatively, I can read a character from the file and compare it
with the
first letter in the list of known keywords.

Like read the "I" of Identification Division and compare it with the
"P" (PROGRAM-ID/ PROCEDURE), "W"(WORKING-STORAGE) and so on.

If it matches with a particular keyword. I go on to read the next
character from the input file and compare it with the next character
in the keyword. If all the characters match, I have found a line
containing the particular keyword.

Would help if I could get some comments on the advantages and
disadvantages of using Approach I or II.

(In the first approach I can re-use the logic that splits a line into
words and returns the first word.)

Thanks and Regards,
M Shetty
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T16:38:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JZFZ7.368$Ml3.45284@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201050751.10cae749@posting.google.com>`

```
mshetty <mshetty@mail.com> wrote in message
news:bfbb8fd4.0201050751.10cae749@posting.google.com...
> 1. I read a line of input...
> Alternatively, I can read a character from the file...
> ..
> Would help if I could get some comments on the advantages and
> disadvantages of using Approach I or II.

The Obvious #1:
What is important to you? Run-time execution speed? Resource utilization?
Ease of programming/maintenance? (I believe you get your choice of two of
these).

The Obvious #2:
Depending on what is important to you, why not write a little test program
to see which method best serves your desires?

My parsers (of anything) look for whole keywords. Of course, these are
written in BASIC, which supports variable-length strings which are not
supported in COBOL, so that's a consideration.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-05T20:35:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C376463.1984EC06@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201050751.10cae749@posting.google.com>`

```


mshetty wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> M Shetty

Not sure what you are after, but as Michael has already indicated use
'whole words'. I recently went through this exercise checking out Micro
Focus Collection classes, to be able to check something like the
following :-

*>--------------------------
Method-id. "doSomething".
*> This method is PRIVATE

0                  2     3
8                  7     3
procedure division using amessage lnkPtr anelement lnkoccurs
               returning defaultreturn.     OR :-
0                  2         3
8                  7         7
procedure division returning lnkBool.

ETC...

Reading the source in sequentially, I first converted each line with
function  Uppercase. In my case I was checking for (1) Comment lines
(*>), (2) whether the method was 'PRIVATE' as opposed to Public, (3)
'METHOD-ID' (4) 'PROCEDURE DIVISION ' and (5) any series of 'USING'
parameters and (6) any 'RETURNING' parameters.

Having positioned on the keywords, I could then use Reference
Modification to move through each line.

Unfortunately my example uses Collections to store parameters, so it
probably wont be of much use to you. But if you are interested in an
overall general approach, mail me privately.

PS - To avoid confusion - in OO most methods are PUBLIC and can be
invoked(called) from another class. Where designated as 'PRIVATE" as in
the comment line above, this means the method is ONLY invoked from within
the same class. The differentiation of Public/Private is NOT part of
COBOL 2002.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** jraben@cascinc.com (Jeff Raben)

```
mshetty@mail.com (mshetty) wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>with the

snip

For a massive cross referencing process I had to look for source and
object field. i.e. ADD ???? to MASTER-FILE-POST-CTR or MOVE
MASTER_FILE_POST_CTR to ???.
I found that UNSTRINGING DEDLIMITED BY SPACE INTO ... ... ... ...
and a SEARCH into a sorted table of key words worked well.

I had to worry about continuations and quoted text (sometimes
rebuilding the continuations with STRING.

I worked and was fast.

I've done it both in BASIC and COBOL.

Regardless of the total project, I found whole word search much faster
and tighter code (always good for debugging).

Jeff
and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

The following are valid lines of COBOL (adjust columns accordingly):

    05  Data-name-1  Pic X(100)   Value "abc
-   "  continued literal"
         .

   ***

Even

    Procedure Div
-          ision Us
-         Ing  ABC
         ,   XYZ
         ;   yet-more
      .
   Para-Name
      .
      Move ABC
         (sub1) t
-       o   Xyz (1
        :2)

Is valid (but UGLY) COBOL.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T13:18:13+00:00

- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190458.41ce2de5@posting.google.com>`

```

"mshetty" <mshetty@mail.com>

> While parsing a COBOL file, is there a way to identify a group data
> item ?? I want to distinguish between group data items and elementary
> data items.

No.

Why do you want to differentiate between group/elementary data items in the
code generating the data?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T12:35:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2ce9p$88k$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190458.41ce2de5@posting.google.com>`

```
As far as I know, the only "official" way to determine if something is a
group vs elementary item is to look at the LEVEL number (and redefines
phrase) of the next defined item.

A) If the next "phrase" is a new section or division (or in file section, a
new FD/SD), then this item is an elementary item.

B) If the next item's level number is 01 or 77, then this item is an
elementary item.

C) if the next level number is HIGHER (greater) than the current level
number - but is not 88, then this is a group item.  If the next item is an
88 level, then you need to "read" to the next non-88 level (or non-data
item) and apply the other rules in this list to figure out if this is or is
not an group item.

D) If the next data item includes the REDEFINES phrase  then this item is an
elementary item.

   ***

I don't guarantee it, but I think that covers all cases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T14:09:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2cjpo$nek$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190458.41ce2de5@posting.google.com> <a2ce9p$88k$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3C4A8692.D0D93CC3@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I was going to add that when I signed back on.  I remembered that later.  If
the next item is 66, then the current item must be an elementary item.

FYI - depending upon the compiler (and extensions) other "odd" level numbers
(greater than 49) may also exist - such as 78-levels.

Also (although INCREDIBLY uncommon) if the "end of source code" follows the
data item, then it is an elementary item.  (The procedure division *IS*
optional in the '85 Standard - and an EXIT PROGRAM is assumed - but I know
of almost NO source code that is done this way.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T14:59:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com>`

```
Elementary items have PICTUREs; group items don't.  That's the definition.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T12:24:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2cdkl$1fp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Unfortunately, not true.

  01  Group1.
     05  Ptr  Usage Pointer.
     05  Float-for-IBM  Comp-1.
     05 anIndex  Usage Index.

shows at least 3 usages that do not ALLOW picture clauses for elementary
items.

However, simply having a USAGE clause also does not indicate whether an item
is elementary or not.

 01 Group Usage Comp.
    05 ThisIsComp   Pic S9(04).

is valid.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T08:46:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4A83ED.2CB90A27@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Terry Heinze wrote:
> 
> Elementary items have PICTUREs; group items don't.  That's the definition.

No.  There is no need to have a PICTURE clause to define a field, it can
be done with USAGE and SIZE.


> > 01 xxx USAGE POINTER.
> >    02 yyy USAGE POINTER.

USAGE on a group item is valid.  It means that the usage applies to all
appropriate subsiduary items.

> > Would the following be considered as a group ??
> > 01 TEMP PIC 9(9).
> >  88 HOT VALUE 0.
> >  88 COLD VALUE 0.

No.  88 levels are conditionals not fields.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T16:32:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2csfi$hic$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3C4A83ED.2CB90A27@Azonic.co.nz> <yfk28.22976$o_.4886249@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C4A9F6C.B00F8482@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Although, I thought there was a rule specifying which USAGEs could and could
not be specified at the group level, the following is the actual rule from
the '85 Standard (some implementors may have extensions to this).

"(2) If the USAGE clause is written in the data description entry for a
group item, it may also be written in the data description entry for any
subordinate elementary item or group item, but the same usage must be
specified in both entries."

(see page VI-46 - or page 369 in the latest draft of the next COBOL
Standard)

This means that in the '85 Standard

 01  Group-not-index Usage Index.
     05  Ind-data-item  Usage Index.

*is* conforming, but

 01  Group-not-index Usage Index.
     05  Ind-data-item.

is NOT conforming source code. For the next COBOL Standard, this means that
the example (from an earlier part of this thread),

 01 xxx usage pointer.
     02 pointer1.
     02 pointer2.
     02 pointer3.

will *not* be conforming source code, but

 01 xxx usage pointer.
     02 pointer1 pointer.
     02 pointer2 pointer.
     02 pointer3 pointer.

WILL be conforming - while

 01 xxx usage pointer.
     02 pointer1 pointer.
     02 AlphaNum  Pic X.

will NOT be conforming (because the last elementary item has a different
IMPLICIT usage than that specified at the group level.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T15:35:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4AE3D7.B1B9BCAB@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3C4A83ED.2CB90A27@Azonic.co.nz> <yfk28.22976$o_.4886249@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C4A9F6C.B00F8482@Azonic.co.nz> <a2csfi$hic$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> "(2) If the USAGE clause is written in the data description entry for a
> group item, it may also be written in the data description entry for any
                 ^^^
> subordinate elementary item or group item, but the same usage must be
> specified in both entries."

I would interpret 'may' as 'you can do, but don't need to'.

> This means that in the '85 Standard
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> is NOT conforming source code. 

I cannot think of any reason why it isn't conforming.  Earlier I had
thought that a data description entry may be required to attach the
USAGE to, but now I can't find any actual requirement for there to be
anything other than the USAGE on the group in this case. 'May' doesn't
mean 'must'.

>  01 xxx usage pointer.
>      02 pointer1.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> will *not* be conforming source code, but

Really ?  Is there a specific requirement for an individual data
description ?

>  01 xxx usage pointer.
>      02 pointer1 pointer.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> IMPLICIT usage than that specified at the group level.

That's always been true hasn't it, for example with USAGE COMP ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T19:19:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2fqbl$cac$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3C4A83ED.2CB90A27@Azonic.co.nz> <yfk28.22976$o_.4886249@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C4A9F6C.B00F8482@Azonic.co.nz> <a2csfi$hic$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3C4AE3D7.B1B9BCAB@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I am now QUITE confused about what is and is not conforming in the '85
Standard.  I think that you (Richard) are probably right.  However, I have
sent out a couple of notes to J4/WG4 to see if I am missing yet another
rule.

I *clearly* agree that the "may" means that you don't need to use the USAGE
clause for elementary items, I am just not positive (although I am leaning
to agree with you) that the USAGE at the group level APPLIES to the
elementary items.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T13:05:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmU28.103$ST3.89860@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3C4A83ED.2CB90A27@Azonic.co.nz> <yfk28.22976$o_.4886249@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C4A9F6C.B00F8482@Azonic.co.nz> <a2csfi$hic$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3C4AE3D7.B1B9BCAB@Azonic.co.nz> <a2fqbl$cac$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:a2fqbl$cac$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...
> I am now QUITE confused about what is and is not conforming in the '85
> Standard....I *clearly* agree that the "may" means that you don't need to
use the USAGE
> clause for elementary items, I am just not positive (although I am leaning
> to agree with you) that the USAGE at the group level ...


My $0.02 sez that finding all the supported/possible methods of expressing
the USAGE of elementary or group items is just an exercise in exotica.

Seems to me the subject,  "USAGE clause at the group level versus USAGE
clause at the elementary level " is a topic better suited to, and should be
included in,  "shop coding standards."

In this thread, the subject comes in the context of parsing a COBOL source
program; methinks one would need to go one step further, asking, "parsing
for WHAT?".  Looking for unused datanames? Trying to generate a storage map
of group items?  Checking syntax for compliance with shop/ANSI standards? I
can see different *parsing* rules for each application.

My goodness, this almosts appears a nascent "religious" topic like GOTO, the
use of scope-terminators vs. full stops, PERFORM THRU ......

MCM
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T11:16:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<915BD5589E4830B5.F00EB25F6B3B9787.F89597BA5850C503@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3C4A83ED.2CB90A27@Azonic.co.nz> <yfk28.22976$o_.4886249@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C4A9F6C.B00F8482@Azonic.co.nz> <a2csfi$hic$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3C4AE3D7.B1B9BCAB@Azonic.co.nz> <a2fqbl$cac$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Sun, 20 Jan 2002 19:19:37 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>I am now QUITE confused about what is and is not conforming in the '85
>Standard.  I think that you (Richard) are probably right.  However, I have
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>elementary items.

I don't know about standards or not, but in the mainframe world going
back to at least Cobol-D (or whatever the official title is..I knew
them as Cobol-D which was followed by Cobol-F), the USAGE at the group
level was valid.  In fact you didn't even need to say USAGE.  You
could just say something like:

01  MY-COUNTERS   COMP-3.
     05  MY-CNT1                        PIC S999  VALUE +0.
.
.
.
.
    05  MY-CNT9                        PIC S999  VALUE +0.

In this every element would be a two byte packed numeric field.  I've
used this construct many times just to save typing.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

The secret of a happy marriage remains a secret.
                                -- Henny Youngman
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T14:27:30+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4a2198_13@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <wRf28.6103$v44.1088952468@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3C4A83ED.2CB90A27@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3C4A83ED.2CB90A27@Azonic.co.nz...
> Terry Heinze wrote:
> >
> > Elementary items have PICTUREs; group items don't.  That's the
definition.
>
> No.  There is no need to have a PICTURE clause to define a field, it can
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A valid example, but to be completely accurate it is not just SIZE and USAGE
that are required, it is SIZE, CLASS, and USAGE.

I remember coding COBOL  before the PICTURE clause was available. We used
these 3 clauses to describe data. For example:

a-numeric-field  SIZE 4 CLASS numeric         USAGE display. [modern
equivalent = PIC 9(4).]
a-text-field        SIZE 4 CLASS alphanumeric USAGE display. [modern
equivalent = PIC X(4).]

Sometimes the CLASS is implicit in the USAGE (as in the example you quoted).
It is interesting that COBOL still retains the CLASS test (if  NUMERIC...,
if ALPHABETIC.... etc.) even though I believe the CLASS and SIZE clauses may
no longer be in the Standard, having been rendered redundant by "PICTURE".

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T15:06:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-v6V3ELzXdd2l@thishost>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com>`

```
On Sat, 19 Jan 2002 13:33:47 UTC, mshetty@mail.com (mshetty) wrote:

> Hi,
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That should not be valid (I think).
 
> Would the following be considered as a group ??
> 01 TEMP PIC 9(9).
>  88 HOT VALUE 0.
>  88 COLD VALUE 0.
> 

No this is a data element with condition definitions.

Level number 88 is a special use level that defines a condition name 
for the preceding data element.

> Would help if I could get some comments on the above.
> 
> Thanks and Regards,
> M Shetty

A group looks like this

02 data-name.
  03  part-of-group-1 pic x.
  03  part-of group-2 pic xx.

The data element that is the start of a group item does not contain a 
picture clause and is followed by data elements that have a higher 
level number.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T12:24:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2cdm3$fa7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com>`

```
Which compiler allows USAGE POINTER both at the "group" and elementary
level?  I don't recall any that DOCUMENTS this as valid - although there
certainly may be some.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T08:54:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4A85AD.EF4324F2@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201060556.2e38ffb8@posting.google.com> <bfbb8fd4.0201190533.4ac09168@posting.google.com> <a2cdm3$fa7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Which compiler allows USAGE POINTER both at the "group" and elementary
> level?  I don't recall any that DOCUMENTS this as valid - although there
> certainly may be some.

> > 01 xxx USAGE POINTER.
> >    02 yyy USAGE POINTER.

I don't see whay it is _not_ valid.  The usage on the item  yyy may be
redundant but is not invalid.  It may be in fact that it is required
because there needs to be some type definition on yyy to make it a field
rather than a group and no other type clause can be applied.

In the case of:

     01  group-comp        USAGE COMP.
         03  item-number   PIC S9(4).

The PIC is a type definition that carries the COMP from the group but it
would not be invalid to repeat this:

         03  item-another  PIC S9(4) USAGE COMP.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-01-06T12:44:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1010321002@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

<a id="t-e5a9a520d0"></a>
## Thread: The witchcraft of analysis — 51 msgs

### The witchcraft of analysis

- **From:** chims1@aol.com (ChimS1)
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T00:34:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020106193452.11371.00000137@mb-cg.aol.com>`

```
Since the intellectual and industrial revolution which
began in Europe around the turns of the 18th century,
the world has been shifted from the belief in nature to
a firmer conviction in the human mind. Analysis which
is the product of man's thinking on his own 2 feet takes
priority over the practice of other kinds of witchcraft.
Even so, we can still classify analysis as a craft to
implement new systems of values in a world of human
communities.

When Jesus Christ walked on the water, he did not go
into some kind of developmental stage and do the work
to implement the process of walking over the water. It
was a straightforward moral and spiritual affirmation.
The heart, the mind, and the action all synchronize to
make life happening at the instance of practical necessity.
This sounds more like Hollywood where light, camera,
actions are the way to channel human creativity toward
the medium of story telling and public entertainment for
the masses.

Walking over the water, as what Jesus Christ had done
to prove the validity and power of the divine mind, was a
spiritual affirmation at best. This is a one shot deal to
demonstrate the efficacity of the combined spiritual and
moral wills.

The arts and sciences of analysis is based on the
works of observation, experimentation, and intellectual
exercise. Although a lot of analytical work would take
tremendous amount of patient coordination among
groups of people over a long period of time, modern
societies do not recognize spiritual inputs into the
equation of analysis. They tend to look at the process
as a pure cerebral activity. Moreover, any other practices
that believe in the convergence of the stars and folksy
remedies are considered as witchcraft that may play
game on the human mind and they are deemed to be
as not trustworthy or totally effective.

There seems to be a conflict between the rigueur of
scientific methodology and the magical formula of
traditional and folksy practices. Modern industrial
methods insist upon verification. Folksy practices
propagate themselves through faith and tradition.

The witchcraft of analysis depends on intuitive reasoning
and may depend on facts, figures, and tons of solid data.
With the advent of computer technology, it can generate
fairly reliable information upon which one can go about to
make better decisions. It may also depend on physical
evidence and value representation of its arguments in
terms of ideas and thought processes. Walking over the
water, however, is outside the realm of analytical methods
and norms of expanded intellectual perspectives. As such,
the witchcraft of analysis cannot go about to reconstruct
that kind of model and implement the idea. Man of normal
range of behaviors cannot walk over the water.


Chim Sotuon
```

#### ↳ Re: The witchcraft of analysis

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T14:13:20+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c38f6e3_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20020106193452.11371.00000137@mb-cg.aol.com>`

```
Hmmm... I don't know why you're so impressed with walking on water;
technology allows us to ski on it, swim beneath it, and fly over it.

None of this is Witchcraft. It is the logical growth process of the Human
intelligence, allied with the spirit of curiosity, innate in all of us.

The record of Scientific achievement which we see around us is more of an
affirmation of the Human spirit than the real or imagined effects of any
shaman or Holy man.

(Nothing personal against Jesus, but I wasn't there...<G>)

For me at least, analysis remains a cerebral activity and belief systems
(other than the scientific ones validated by past experience) play no part
in it.

Whether it would be improved by introducing an element of "spirituality"
into it is extremely debatable.

The Inquisition developed improved thumbscrews and racks in pursuit of their
spiritual beliefs...

We haven't burned any witches for a while now and there doesn't appear to be
any bad effect from this neglect.

> There seems to be a conflict between the rigueur of
> scientific methodology and the magical formula of
> traditional and folksy practices. Modern industrial
> methods insist upon verification. Folksy practices
> propagate themselves through faith and tradition.

Yes, there is. And if I have to choose, I'll opt for the verification
approach, thanks all the same <G>.

If that means I can't walk on water by the sheer power of my mind (or
spirtual purity), I'll live with that.

(Besides, boats are good fun and have many fringe benefits besides getting
you across water...<G>)

Pete.

ChimS1 <chims1@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020106193452.11371.00000137@mb-cg.aol.com...
> Since the intellectual and industrial revolution which
> began in Europe around the turns of the 18th century,
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The witchcraft of analysis

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-06T21:08:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Gm7_7.6485$os5.599526@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<20020106193452.11371.00000137@mb-cg.aol.com> <3c38f6e3_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Well Peter, I had pretty well decided to ignore that post, but since I just
smoked a joint, and it is Sunday evening, perhaps I will comment.


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3c38f6e3_1@Usenet.com...
> Hmmm... I don't know why you're so impressed with walking on water;
> technology allows us to ski on it, swim beneath it, and fly over it.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> The Inquisition developed improved thumbscrews and racks in pursuit of
their
> spiritual beliefs...
>
> We haven't burned any witches for a while now and there doesn't appear to
be
> any bad effect from this neglect.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Peter, I share most of your distain for traditional religion.  I have been a
proud atheiest for years.  However, I am begining to wonder if a distain for
shamanism is not distorting my evaluation of "spirituality".

As programmers, we have to understand the power of symbolic constructs.  Our
entire "art" consists of inventing entities that only exist as symbolic
constructs, then testing the effect of those constructs on "the real world".
The human Spirit you mention thinks and creates conceptual entities.  Those
entities affect the world.  Whether you call them "good" and "evil"   or
"God" and "Devil" is just a level of thinking.  That the two exist and
affect the world, surely is the more interesting question.

I think they do, not that they did before we thought of them, but that they
do now, because we have thought of them, and millions of people act based on
their concept of good and evil.  That, to my discomfort, makes me wary ...
what is the difference between a real god and devil that exist in my world
now, but did not a million years back, and the traditional?  Aside from the
mythology, that is?

It srikes me that if we are going to invent gods, we should have a pretty
strong desire to understand spiritual values before we start.  Design stage,
so to speak.

Donald
;<)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The witchcraft of analysis

- **From:** chims1@aol.com (ChimS1)
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T16:01:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020107110127.23494.00000257@mb-md.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Gm7_7.6485$os5.599526@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald, confession is good for the soul. From one programmer to another
programmer, get all the facts and requirements, design the program, code, 
test, and implement. Isn't that what we've been doing all along?

Have fun...
Chim
```

```

Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:Gm7_7.6485$os5.599526@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Well Peter, I had pretty well decided to ignore that post, but since I
just
> smoked a joint, and it is Sunday evening, perhaps I will comment.
>

"One toke over the line, Sweet Jesus, one toke over the line....<G>"

I take no issue with your post, Don. There is ONE reservation I have and
that is: disdain.

I certainly don't disdain other people's Religious beliefs; I have the
utmost respect for them. I just don't share them.

And, as I don't go around trying to Evangelize Fundamental Atheism, I think
they could do me the same courtesy...<G>

Some comments below...

Pete.
>
<snipped all of Dashwood's previous post
>
> Peter, I share most of your distain for traditional religion.  I have been
a
> proud atheiest for years.  However, I am begining to wonder if a distain
for
> shamanism is not distorting my evaluation of "spirituality".
>
A disdain for ANY belief is not a good thing. It implies there is only ONE
right way, the disdainer has found it, and everyone else is therefore
wrong...

> As programmers, we have to understand the power of symbolic constructs.
Our
> entire "art" consists of inventing entities that only exist as symbolic
> constructs, then testing the effect of those constructs on "the real
world".
> The human Spirit you mention thinks and creates conceptual entities.
Those
> entities affect the world.  Whether you call them "good" and "evil"   or
> "God" and "Devil" is just a level of thinking.  That the two exist and
> affect the world, surely is the more interesting question.

OK, so are you telling me now there are "good" and "evil" computer programs
which have these intrinsic moral values (not based on the purpose of the
program, but rather on how it is coded?!)...actually, come to think of it,
and reflecting on some "midnight maintenance" I have had to do in my career,
maybe there is something in this...<G>
>
> I think they do, not that they did before we thought of them, but that
they
> do now, because we have thought of them, and millions of people act based
on
> their concept of good and evil.  That, to my discomfort, makes me wary ...
> what is the difference between a real god and devil that exist in my world
> now, but did not a million years back, and the traditional?  Aside from
the
> mythology, that is?
>
The World is now, and always has been, full of dichotomies. Good and Bad,
Hot and Cold, Wet and Dry... You can argue that the non-physical ones have
been invented by Man, but they are just as real whether we invented them or
whether we just slowly came to realise their existence. So I guess I broadly
agree with your point.



> It srikes me that if we are going to invent gods, we should have a pretty
> strong desire to understand spiritual values before we start.  Design
stage,
> so to speak.
>
That is a very good point. However, having spent most of my life coming to
terms with the Gods we have, the last thing I would want to do is invent
some more...<G>. I'll leave this for those much better qualified. And I hope
they take your advice.

Pete.

No, not really.  What I am saying is that "god" effects the world.  Not
because such an entity exists outside the human condition, but because the
concept exists within the human mind.  Lots of things exist only withing the
human mind, yet affect reality.  Infinity is a good example.  Show me an
infinitely large number.  I can argue that such an entity does not really
exist, but the fact still remains that the concept changes reality.

or

broadly
> agree with your point.

The secondary point, though, is that even those of us that do not agree with
the standard definitions need to spend time on a working definition.  If we
do not, then the default that exists now comes into play.  I suppose what I
am really saying is that theology is too important to relegate to the realm
of superstition.  It has too big an effect on our lives (the recent
terrorist acts come to mind) to simply say "nothing to do with me".

>
> > It srikes me that if we are going to invent gods, we should have a
pretty

Donad
