Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> I certainly don't disdain other people's Religious beliefs; I have the
> utmost respect for them. I just don't share them.

I certainly don't respect every other's beliefs.  After all each and
every religion has at its root an implicit or explicit statement that
all the others are wrong.  I agree with every religion only to the
points that they claim or imply all the others are wrong.  ;-)

However, one cannot deny the existence of Gods - Hirohito was as much a
God as anyone else's, and so were his predecessors, as was Chin and his
antecedants.

I have no doubt at all that Moses contracted the covenant, all that is
needed is to identify the actual local warlord or local self-styled
emperor that was the other party, something that doesn't seem to be a
subject that gets any serious study.
```

In article <3c3b64f4$1_1@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or another step towards a completely closed mind...

As the Ancient Hebrews used to say, Mr Dashwood, 'in media felicitas est'; 
moderation (sophrosyne) in all things (including, of course, moderation).  
Good things can quite readily become not-good opposites; mother-love 
becomes smother-love, security becomes Maximum Security (note for those 
unfamiliar with American phrases: Maximum Security is a rating for 
prisons) and a 'completely open mind' can lead to breezes flowing freely 
'twixt one's ears.

DD
```

Well Doc,

I won't contest anyone's right to block anything if they want to. My comment
was simply an observation.
(Personally I don't block anything; I do delete stuff from the server before
it hits my system... But it is also interesting to observe the demented
ravings of the lunatic fringe. I find I can look at stuff without being the
effect of it or having to go into agreement or disagreement with it, but I
accept that  some others may not be able to do this.)

I support your comment regarding moderation but this is an "unmoderated"
News Group...<G>

And if it is a choice between having fresh air in the noggin or rigid
concrete, well the answer to that is a no-brainer <G>


Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The witchcraft of analysis

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** chims1@aol.com (ChimS1)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3c3bfdab_2@Usenet.com>`

```
Pete,

Very good point. One of the facets of the methodology in system analysis is
that we need to carefully weight the pros and cons of a certain piece of data
before we go about to discard it. Effective problem solving can be both
personal and group driven. Sometimes, when we're at it, pounding at the
keyboard, tweaking the data, repositionig the bits and bytes, we can get bogged
down and loose sight of the forest and the whole perspective of the programming
project.

A breadth of fresh air, like taking shower in the morning or going to the
beach,  can also bring more solutions to the arts and sciences of pushing Cobol
codes to timely implementation before the devils of the deadline set in.

Chim

#### ↳ Re: The witchcraft of analysis

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T03:52:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020106225222.11229.00000036@mb-fw.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20020106193452.11371.00000137@mb-cg.aol.com>`

```
>From: chims1@aol.com  (ChimS1)
>Date: 1/6/02 7:34 PM Eastern Standard Time

>Man of normal
>range of behaviors cannot walk over the water.

A priest, rabbi and evangelical minister went fishing. They brought along a few
brews to keep them occupied as they whiled away the hours.  As the afternoon
progressed the priest finished his brew and announced he was going to shore to
get another where upon he got out of the boat, walked to shore, snagged another
beer and returned to the boat.  The minister sat looking slightly agog at this
performance.  A few minutes later the rabbi got up announcing he too was going
for another, got out of the boat, walked to shore, snagged another beer and
returned.  Now the minister was really astounded but not to show that he didn't
know anything announced that he too was going after another brew.  He got up,
got out of the boat and promptly fell into the water.  The priest looked at the
rabbi and asked 'Didn't you tell him about the stepping stones?'
```

LOL!

Good one, Eileen...

Reciprocating...

A man goes to buy a dog. It is  a retriever. The owner claims that it is an
incredible dog. To demonstrate, he takes the prospective buyer to a nearby
lake, shoots a duck which falls in the water about 100 metres offshore. The
dog immediately bounds onto the water, runs along the surface, picks up the
duck, returns and drops it at his Master's feet, without even getting his
paws wet.

"Good Heavens!" says the Buyer. "That is amazing!"

"Amazing?! No, it is miraculous. D'you know that dog can't swim...?"

Pete.

## Thread: long varchar — 9 msgs

### long varchar

- **From:** "Tim Cordsen" <tim.cordsen@web.de>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T12:15:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1bvvr$sjt6@doiftp1.volkswagen.de>`

```
Hallo!
Wer kann mir bei einem Zugriff auf eine Long Varchar Variable helfen?
Ich versuche mit einem fetch into unter anderem auch diese Variable zu
f�llen, bekomme aber immer die Precompiler-Fehlermeldung: UNDEFINED OR
UNUSABLE HOST VARIABLE "..."
Wer kann mir sagen wie ich diese Variable behandeln mu�?

Vielen Dank im Voraus f�r die Hilfe.

Tim Cordsen

P.S. IBM OS390 mit COBOL und DB2
```

#### ↳ Re: long varchar

Translation:

Hi,
Who can help me accessing a Long Varchar variable?
When doing a FETCH INTO I get the follwing error message from the
precompiler:
'UNDEFINED OR UNUSABLE HOST VARIABLE "..."'
Can anyone tell be how I have to treat this variable.
Thanks in advance
--------------
I don't know Cobol, so I can't help there....
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: long varchar

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T03:17:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.databases.ibm-db2
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-WrMFLxe7ydlY@thishost>`
- **References:** `<a1bvvr$sjt6@doiftp1.volkswagen.de> <3C39B509.454CFCC0@ca.ibm.com>`

```
On Mon, 7 Jan 2002 14:47:37 UTC, Serge Rielau <srielau@ca.ibm.com> 
wrote:

> Translation:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

If I do that with IBM Visualage COBOL the LONG VARCHAR variable has to
be defined as

01  longvarchar.
    49  length-varchar  pic s9(4) comp-5.
    49  data-varchar     pic x(32000). (more or less)

The Application Development Guide talks about this....
 
Any definition other than level number 49 fails with the "unusable 
host variable" error
```

#### ↳ Re: long varchar

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T02:30:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020107213051.12283.00000177@mb-mu.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<a1bvvr$sjt6@doiftp1.volkswagen.de>`

```
>From: "Tim Cordsen" tim.cordsen@web.de 
>Date: 1/7/02 6:15 AM Eastern Standard Time

>Precompiler-Fehlermeldung: UNDEFINED OR
>UNUSABLE HOST VARIABLE "..."

I don't understand German (I'm assuming German) but I do recognize this error
and I know Cobol and DB2 so... (If someone wants to translate this for me???)

The first thing to look at is the spelling of the variable in Working-Storage,
the next thing to look at is the spelling in the program - do they match?  Did
you use a hyphen instead of an underscore if a multiple word variable
(part-number instead of part_number)?

Next if using as part of a WHERE clause and the working storage field is not
defined as COMP-3 then it doesn't like it either.

WHERE PART_NUMBER = :WS-PART-NUMBER

In Working-Storage WS-PART-NUMBER should be defined as PIC S9(??) COMP-3.
(where ?? is your length).  It cannot be a group item or just a PIC 9 - it must
be COMP-3.

Check all these out first.  It's these problems that give me that error the
most.

Eileen
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: long varchar

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T03:31:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<a1bvvr$sjt6@doiftp1.volkswagen.de> <20020107213051.12283.00000177@mb-mu.aol.com>`

```

#### ↳ Re: long varchar

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T13:14:10+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3b8dc3_11@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a1bvvr$sjt6@doiftp1.volkswagen.de>`

```
Have you defined the Host Variable correctly in BEGIN DECLARE... END
DECLARE?

A long VarChar may need to have a length field preceding it. (It's a while
since I used DB2, but check the IBM manual)

For example...

myVarChar   VARCHAR(36)  NOT NULL

on the Database, may require...

01  myVarChar.
      12 myVCLen       pic s9(4) comp.
      12 myVCData     pic x occurs 36
                                           depending on myVCLen.

...in the DECLARE section.

Post in English to reach the widest audience and improve your chaces of
getting help...

Pete.

## Thread: NetExpress and export application — 2 msgs

### NetExpress and export application

- **From:** Alain Chappuis <alain.chappuis@medecine.unige.ch>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T14:40:26+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C39A54A.804E989E@medecine.unige.ch>`

```
Hello,
I have a little question about NetExpress and export application.

I have developped one application with NetExpress and this one
work very fine on my system.

Now I would like to export my application under an other system.
When I it on my objet (.exe) the windows close immediatly without
any message?
Did I forget something?
Somebody can it say to me what it is necessary so that my
application run.
Thank you in advance and happy new year.

Ps: My application use some calls for the mouse and indexed files.

Alain
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress and export application

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T18:47:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C39EE2B.6FF97CD5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3C39A54A.804E989E@medecine.unige.ch>`

```


Alain Chappuis wrote:

> Hello,
> I have a little question about NetExpress and export application.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Did I forget something?

>
> Somebody can it say to me what it is necessary so that my
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alain

Alain,

What do you mean by 'export my application' ? To which 'system' are you
trying to export ? Objects (EXEs) are designed to run with the Net
Express  runtime and supporting ADIS files to handle the mouse, (that's
assuming you are working in a DOS/text window), plus support for COBOL
files. (See the on-line help 'Distributing Applications' - BUT REMEMBER
- University Edition is a TUTORIAL - and isn't designed with the intent
of 'allowing' you to export).

You can of course feed your Net Express source into another compiler,
(Fujistsu, AcuCIBOL, RM/COBOL etc.) and re-compile, but the Micro Focus
Mouse routines wont work, and you will need the support files for the
appropriate compiler.

Jimmy
```

Hello,

I want to create a textfile without CR/LF at the end of every line.
I hvae to build this file in many single passes.

e.g.:
 1. Create the file
    Write headerinformation
    Close the file
 2. Open the file with extend
    Write the information
    Close the file
 3. Do 2. as long as information is available
 4. Open the file with extend
    Write the endinformation
    Close the file

  SELECT aFile ASSIGN TO DISK

               ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS aFileStatus.
*----
  FILE SECTION.
  FD  aFile
      RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE
      FROM 5 TO 360 CHARACTERS DEPENDING ON aCount
      VALUE OF FILE-ID IS aFilename.
  01 aPara                      PIC X(360).

it is impossible to write without CR/LF at the end of every line.

Hope somebody can help


Alexander Wittka-Herking

is defined as having a CR LF at the end of every line.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress: Files without CR/LF

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T18:08:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3B3681.93E63B01@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<4e1d4a90.0201070658.10468814@posting.google.com> <z1j_7.12755$os5.962972@news20.bellglobal.com> <4e1d4a90.0201080009.2779438b@posting.google.com>`

```


Alexander Wittka-Herking wrote:

> I took the line aequential out and left the sequential but now there
> is at the beginning of the file a lot of "chaotic" bytes and before
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What are you trying to achieve ? Here's the SHORTEST program you can
write in Net Express :-
*>------------------------
display "Hello World !"                *> No 'intro' - just this one
line
*>------------------------

If you *really* must write it to a Sequential or Line Sequential :-

01 MyRecord pic x(80).

Open MY-file as output
initialize myRecord
move "Hello" to MyRecord(1:5)
move "World" to MyRecord(7:5)
move "!" to MyRecord(13:1)

*> obviously simpler - move 'Hello World !' to MyRecord

*> AND - there's more than one way to skin a cat(fish) :-

*> String  "Hello'       delimited by size
*>             " "            delimited by size
*>.             'World"   delimited by size
*>            " "             delimited by size
*>            "!"             delimited by size
*>            into MyRecord
*> End-string

write MyRecord
close MY-File
Open MY-File as input
Read MY-File next record
    at end continue
    not at end display MyRecord
End-read
Close MY-File
Stop Run.

Those numbers in brackets - check the on-line help or the Language
Reference Manual book, (which is also available on-line) and look at
REFERENCE MODIFICATION. Also check STRING and UNSTRING.

If you want 'Hello World' to show up in a typical Dialog Window - then
you have got to do a lot more reading - either Dialog System or look at
Dialog Editor that is used with OO and GUIs  If you want to work in a
DOS text Window, then take a look at Screen Section..

Yes, API functions are an alternative in Net Express - but at this stage
in the game, as you are a beginner  - don't go there !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress: Files without CR/LF

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T00:57:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0201090057.100f3bb2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4e1d4a90.0201070658.10468814@posting.google.com> <z1j_7.12755$os5.962972@news20.bellglobal.com> <4e1d4a90.0201080009.2779438b@posting.google.com>`

```
Sorry - but could we go back to the start...

Firstly you haven't explained what you are trying to achieve (in an
overall sense) and secondly from your second posting you would appear
to be the reading the file produced as if it were text (or some
such...) and this may be a separate issue.

The "problem" with files has already been explained: in one way or
another each record has to be described (whether fixed or variable) so
that the file is not just a collection of bytes that the program wrote
(although this can be the case with a little effort). BUT however you
write the file (LINE SEQUENTIAL or whatever) you should be able to
read it back in COBOL in such a way as to have the COBOL deal with the
control information.

So: if you are trying to build sets of "words" (shall we call them)
you could write each word to a file as you have been doing as, say,
LINE SEQUENTIAL, and then re-read that file to build the words into
one large record (with appropriate spaces) initially in memory.
Alternatively you could create the same effect directly in memory by,
for example, using STRING, and assemble the set of words in that way.
In both case the result would be a data structure consisting of the
"words", having been assembled in memory. But then what do you want to
do with the, well let's call it a, "sentence"?



graham


aherking@web.de (Alexander Wittka-Herking) wrote in message news:<4e1d4a90.0201080009.2779438b@posting.google.com>...
> I took the line aequential out and left the sequential but now there
> is at the beginning of the file a lot of "chaotic" bytes and before
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alexander
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress: Files without CR/LF

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T09:08:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3C08AB.1903AA40@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<4e1d4a90.0201070658.10468814@posting.google.com> <z1j_7.12755$os5.962972@news20.bellglobal.com> <4e1d4a90.0201080009.2779438b@posting.google.com>`

```
Alexander Wittka-Herking wrote:
> 
> I took the line aequential out and left the sequential but now there
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> ==> One line in the file: HelloWorld!


The system needs to be able to determine when reading a file what
constitutes a 'record'.  When the file is written each WRITE statement
outputs one record.  With LINE SEQUENTIAL the record is terminated by
CR/LF so that reading the file back the records can be separated.

With RECORD SEQUENTIAL the records may be variable length so the file
has to have length headers on each record and it also has a file header.

There is a fixed length format for SEQUENTIAL files but each WRITE will
output that fixed length so that reading the fixed length will recreate
the record as written.

You are trying to WRITE several records (each WRITE is one record) but
have them magically assembled as if they were just one record.

ie you are trying to write a BASIC (or whatever it is you already know)
program in COBOL.

The answer is to assemble the parts of the one record _before_ you write
that record.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress: Files without CR/LF

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T03:27:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nMO_7.240587$WW.12996733@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<4e1d4a90.0201070658.10468814@posting.google.com> <z1j_7.12755$os5.962972@news20.bellglobal.com> <4e1d4a90.0201080009.2779438b@posting.google.com> <3C3C08AB.1903AA40@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
    The only way I know that REALLY does what you want,
in Cobol, would be to define a fixed length record sequential
file, no variable length (what you have), record length 1.

    Then write each byte one at a time.

Notes:
  It will not be fast.
  Some compilers might limit the number of records
you can write to a file.  I remember a very old Micro Focus
compiler that would not allow more than 65,535 records.

```
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
   PROGRAM-ID. MULT-TABLE.
   ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
   DATA DIVISION.
   WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
   01 table-number  PIC 99.
   01 the-multiplier PIC ZZ9.
   01 the-result     PIC ZZ9.
   01 counter        PIC 99.
   01 table-number-out PIC Z9.
   01 counter-out    PIC 99.

   PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           PERFORM get-table-number
           PERFORM VARYING counter FROM 1 BY 1
           UNTIL counter > 12
            MOVE table-number TO table-number-out
            MOVE counter TO counter-out
             COMPUTE the-result = table-number * counter

            DISPLAY table-number-out " "
                    counter " " = " result "
            END-PERFORM
            STOP RUN.

            get-table-number.
            DISPLAY " Please enter the multiplication table "
            DISPLAY " you wish to practice: " WITH NO ADVANCING
            ACCEPT table-number
               END-PERFORM.

please reply.

I am using cobol 85 ansi. I have the book Matering cobol by Roger Hutty &
Mary spence. I found

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress: Files without CR/LF

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T11:48:15+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3ccbab_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<4e1d4a90.0201070658.10468814@posting.google.com>`

```
The problem here is that you are trying to use COBOL in the same way you use
your current programming language (BASIC?). I think Richard pointed this out
and he is 100% right.

You need to change your concept of what a FILE and a RECORD are and maybe
read the COBOL manual on file handling.

It is possible to do what you are trying to do in COBOL, but COBOL is NOT
the best tool for this particular job.

Pete.

Hi,

The COBOL I am using supports POINTERS that are four byte long.
Displaying the value in the POINTER type variable shows

+0074957556

This is the definition of the POINTER variable
01 WS-INTF2_PTR          USAGE POINTER.

DISPLAY "PTR RETURNED" WS-INTF2_PTR.

As such addresses do not have a sign. The actual address seems to be
1074957556, that has been truncated to +0074957556. Is there is a
problem in the display statement??

Would help if I could get some comments.

Thanks and Regards,
M Shetty

I would guess that DISPLAY of a POINTER is not too meaningful and a four byte
binary field maps to S9(9) COMP.

> Hi,
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> M Shetty

Pointers are binary numbers and the display statement is probably 
doing some under the covers conversionto show the value in text

Move the value of the pointer to a display field like 
01  disp-pointer pic 9(10) 
then use that field for the display statement.

```


Steven De Groote wrote:

> hello,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>  http://www.f1technical.net/

Go to cobolreport.com and check Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ (Frequently Asked
Questions), plus the latest copy of cobolreport.com has an article with a
list of sample references. Would be useful to get a copy of Thane Hubbell's
'Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 hours". There are many options to using SORT. To
get you started. here's an example based on Thane's text :-

*>-------------------- collect2.cbl----------------------------

     *> Output sorted into the sequence :-
     *> Public, MethodName then Class

Input-Output Section.
File-Control.
    Select In-File
        Assign          In-Filename
        Organization    Line Sequential
        Access          Sequential
        File Status     In-FileStatus.

    Select Sort-File
        Assign          Sort-Work.

    Select OUT-File
        Assign          OUT-Filename
        Organization    Line Sequential
        Access          Sequential.


Data Division.
File Section.

FD  IN-File.
01  IN-Record.
    05 PublicPrivate  pic x(08).      *> This contains "Public" or "Private"
    05 MethodName            pic x(32).
    05 ClassName             pic x(09).
    05 Params.
       10 XUsing             pic x(80).
       10 XReturning         pic x(80).

SD  SORT-File.
01  SORT-Record.
    05 PublicPrivate         pic x(08).
    05 MethodName            pic x(32).
    05 ClassName             pic x(09).
    05 Params.
       10 XUsing             pic x(80).
       10 XReturning         pic x(80).

FD OUT-File.
01 OUT-Record pic x(209).

Working-Storage Section.

01 IN-Filename pic x(100) value
   "c:\cobol\examples\Collection-Classes\collectA.txt".
01 Out-FileName  pic x(100) value
   "c:\cobol\examples\Collection-Classes\collectM.txt".

01  Done-Flag   Pic X Value Spaces.
    88  All-Done      Value "Y".
01  In-FileStatus Pic XX Value "00".
Procedure Division.
Declaratives.
In-File-Error Section.
    Use After Standard Error Procedure On In-File.
Dealer-Error.
    Display "Unhandled error on In File " In-FileStatus
    Set All-Done To True
    .
End Declaratives.
Mainline.
    Sort Sort-File Descending Key PublicPrivate of Sort-Record
                   Ascending  Key MethodName    Of Sort-Record
                                  ClassName     Of Sort-Record
         Input Procedure Sort-In
         Giving Out-File
    Display "Sort Complete"
    Stop Run
    .
Sort-In.
    Open Input In-File
    Perform Until All-Done
       Read In-File
            At End Set All-Done To True
            Not At End
               Move Corresponding In-record To Sort-Record
               display IN-record
               Release Sort-Record
       End-Read
    End-Perform
    Close In-File
    .
*>-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: study example

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T00:05:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3A38AA.ABB7F7E0@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3c39d1e1$0$75148$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <3C39E96E.5F40FED3@shaw.ca> <ZTo_7.7719$cD4.14325@www.newsranger.com>`

```


John H Sleight Jr wrote:

> Hi James,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Firstly, if you want to see some really good 'stylish' programming - get Pete Dashwood
to send you a sample of his - now Kiwi don't get a swollen head <G>.

As to my own style - not quite sure where this starts. Back with RM/COBOL('74)  it was
of course all Uppercase. Differentiated between  W/S and Files with prefixes like :-

    WS-ITEM-NUMBER
    CUS-PRIME-KEY

Then into MS/COBOL and MF/COBOL and switched to Lowercase - much 'softer' on the eyes
and easier to read. ( I shudder when I see mainframe Uppercase examples here - just
find it hard to read).

Then into OO and found that folks like Will Price were talking 'CamelBack' - so above
became :-

ws-ItemNumber
CUS-PrimeKey

Note I really like to emphasize 'file formats' with caps in the prefix. Another
feature Will Price uses is :-

        Evaluate
        End-Evaluate

In fact I think most of his 'reserved' words are UpperCase. I tend to go with the
'caps' as shown above, but sometimes forget and finish up with :-

    perform
    End-perform - ( There's inconsistency on the first (P)erform  !!!!<G>)

Then move on to OO :-

 invoke anObject "doThis"    - ('ORRIBLE !, 'ORRIBLE !)

I H-A-T-E that 'anObject' and prefer :-

invoke os-CustomerCollection "doThis"

You *know* exactly which object I'm referring to.

Coupled with OO I have my 'own standard' :-

    - Working-Storage (i.e. 'Global'), mostly prefixed with ws-
    - Object-Storage Section (again 'Global') and it IS W/S in COBOL 2002 -
      here I prefix with os-
    - Method - Local-Storage section - prefixed with ls-
    - Method - Linkage section prefixed with lnk- (both using and returning)

Interesting thing about the latter, both Will and I believe J4 examples use the prefix
'ls' for Linkage - just seems more concise as "lnk-".

Now as to the example you were looking at - that's a bit of a mish-mash. Why re-invent
the wheel; took the code from Thane's CD and much of it shows his original code like
the Declaratives and Flags; then I adapted bits to suit my preference.

One thing I'm 'thrilled' about is the Micro Focus feature which lets me leave out the
'red tape' when not required. If you look at my original source I don't have
Program-id - that appears as my first comment line. (I haven't checked but Im pretty
certain I could have left out Thane's lines 'Input-Output Section.' and 'Data
Division.'). The payoff here is your eye is focused on the immediate logic you are
perusing rather than laboriously following the full COBOL syntax.

I'm also a great believer in copyfiles - so that I can prefix with CUS-, ls- or lnk-
as appropriate, with a copy replace ==(tag)== by ==xxxx==.

Really just a question of what appears pleasing to the author, and retains clarity.

As Michael would say "It's the house decorator not the paint roller" <G>. (Want to see
some elegant code - get a copy of some of his BASIC).

Jimmy

>
> In article <3C39E96E.5F40FED3@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan says...
…[112 more quoted lines elided]…
> >*>-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: study example

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T13:44:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3AF7AF.41D409B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3c39d1e1$0$75148$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <3C39E96E.5F40FED3@shaw.ca> <ZTo_7.7719$cD4.14325@www.newsranger.com>`

```
John H Sleight Jr wrote:
> 
> I noticed you use upper/lower case notation in your code. Would you mind taking
> the time to explain your reasons for using that approach and what you see as its
> advantages and/or disadvantages?

As a contrast to the example I use 'Modula2' style with all reserved
words in upper case and user words in mixed case.  I find it easier to
visually separate the user words and the reserved, also my editor has
syntax colouring so misspelt reserved words stand out because an upper
case word is coloured wrongly.

However, another reason for this is that my editor generates reserved
words from keystroke macros in upper case - eg F9-P gives PERFORM, F9-V
gives VARYING, etc.
```

Richard Plinston wrote:

> John H Sleight Jr wrote:
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> gives VARYING, etc.

Interesting point about the macros Richard. The old Wang word processors provided 'les
girls' with macros to 'Load file', 'Save file' etc. and I have written a purchase
order application stringing those macros together to form 'word processing' programs.
Even did programs for statistics which they ran overnight - only took about 8 hours
searching ALL text, character by character  <G>. (The tech guys from Wang HQ nearly
blew a fuse when they found out. "We've never tested to see if it is capable of
sustaining that long..... !").

The macros were a nifty feature with DOS applications; pity they didn't get carried
over to Windows type IDEs. The nearest to your current macros within Net Express would
be to use a facility where it checks spelling and you can make it go 'technicolor',
plus you can use it to check/replace misspelt words automatically - in theory I could
enter "pfm" and it would give me 'PERFORM' or 'Perform', depending upon what I had set
up in my own checker. However, in the main, I still use the DOS text editor that came
with MS/COBOL V5 (Programmers' Workbench).

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: study example

- **From:** "Alain.FELER" <Alain.FELER@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T22:51:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1ig75$eh9$1@wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<3c39d1e1$0$75148$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
There are two kinds of Cobol sorts:
1. file sort
2. procedure sort
You may combine it: one file in input and one procedure in output,
or reverse.
Here, a sample of each kind in nearly standard 85 Cobol:
1. file sort
       identification division.
      * file sort
       program-id. trific.
       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select entree assign to "input.txt"
               organization is line sequential
               file status fs-entree.
           select sort-file assign to "sorttemp"
               sort status is sort-status.
           select sortie assign to "output.txt"
               organization is line sequential
               file status fs-sortie.
       data division.
       file section.
       fd entree.
       01 entree-enr           pic x(4).
       fd sortie.
       01 sortie-enr           pic x(4).
       sd  sort-file.
       01  sort-enr .
           02 terme            pic x(4).
       working-storage section.
       01 sort-status pic xx.
       01 fs-entree                      pic xx.
       88 eof-entree value "10".
       01 fs-sortie                      pic xx.
       88 eof-sortie value "10".
       01 fs-sort                        pic xx.
       88 eof-sort value "10".
       procedure division.
       tri section.
       x.
           display "Sorting..."
           sort sort-file ascending terme
               using entree
               giving sortie
           display "Sort is finished"
           stop run.

2. procedure sort
       identification division.
      * sort with procedures
       program-id. tri.
       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
       select entree assign to "input.seq"
               file status fs-entree.
       select sort-file assign to "sorttemp"
              sort status is sort-status.
       data division.
       file section.
       fd entree label record standard.
       01 entree-enr pic x(13).
       sd  sort-file.
       01  sort-enr .
           02 insee pic x(13).
       working-storage section.
       01 sort-status pic xx.
       01 fs-entree                      pic xx.
       88 eof-entree value "10".
       01 fs-sort                      pic xx.
       88 eof-sort value "10".
       procedure division.
       sort sort-file descending insee
           input procedure proc-entree
           output procedure proc-sortie.
       stop run.
       proc-entree section.
           open input entree.
           perform until eof-entree
               read entree end-read
               move entree-enr to sort-enr
               release sort-enr
           end-perform.
       proc-sortie section.
           perform until eof-sort
               return sort-file at end move "10" to fs-sort end-return
               display insee
           end-perform.



Steven De Groote <sgroote@yucom.be> a �crit dans le message :
3c39d1e1$0$75148$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be...
> hello,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Comparing a boolean value with a constant — 6 msgs

### Comparing a boolean value with a constant

- **From:** mshetty@mail.com (mshetty)
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T09:56:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201070956.235585e@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I have a boolean variable 
01 HAS_VAL PIC 9 VALUE 0.

when I compare this with 0(IF HAS_VAL IS EQUAL TO 0) it fails. If I
add a COMP to the definition the comparision works. But, I can't add
the COMP. Is there a way to compare the two ??

Would help if I could get some comments on the same.

Thanks and Regards,
M Shetty
```

#### ↳ Re: Comparing a boolean value with a constant

- **From:** Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T13:36:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C39F8D8.48429C0C@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201070956.235585e@posting.google.com>`

```
You are comparing an alphanumeric value to a numeric literal.

Either make the literal an alphanumeric literal by enclosing it in
single quotes or change the definition of your boolean variable, e.g.:

    01  BOOLEAN-VARIABLES.
          05  HAS-VAL  PIC 9  COMP VALUE 0.

A level 01 definition is by definition a group variable and therefore
character.

mshetty wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> M Shetty
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Comparing a boolean value with a constant

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T20:37:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nGn_7.188679$m05.15954833@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201070956.235585e@posting.google.com> <3C39F8D8.48429C0C@sprynet.com>`

```

"Greg Ferro" <gferro@sprynet.com> wrote in message
news:3C39F8D8.48429C0C@sprynet.com...
> You are comparing an alphanumeric value to a numeric literal.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> character.

A GROUP ITEM is an item having subordinate entries; An ELEMENTARY item is on
with no subordinate entries. The level number of either is irrelevant. A
GROUP item (remember, that's one that has subordinate entries and - by
implication - no associated PICTURE clause) is treated as alphanumeric. But
that's not the case here.
>
> mshetty wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Comparing a boolean value with a constant

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T15:45:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<duE_7.51751$wa.3507999@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201070956.235585e@posting.google.com> <3C39F8D8.48429C0C@sprynet.com>`

```

On  7-Jan-2002, Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com> wrote:

>     01  BOOLEAN-VARIABLES.
>           05  HAS-VAL  PIC 9  COMP VALUE 0.
>
> A level 01 definition is by definition a group variable and therefore
> character.

A level 01 definition can be a group variable, but doesn't have to be one. 
In this case it is one.
A group variable can often be treated as character, but there are some
differences.  In this case, treat it as character.
```

#### ↳ Re: Comparing a boolean value with a constant

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T20:41:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0201070956.235585e@posting.google.com>`

```

news:bfbb8fd4.0201070956.235585e@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the COMP. Is there a way to compare the two ??

Works for me. Is there something you're leaving out?

I assume by 'it fails,' you mean during execution, not at compile time. And
how does it fail? As an aside, if you 'can't add the COMP' how do you know
if the comparison works when you do 'add the COMP?'
```

## Thread: function-oriented cobol compiler — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### function-oriented cobol compiler

- **From:** vronii@web.de (Verena Grassmann)
- **Date:** 2002-01-07T11:41:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5a01bd40.0201071141.57f1a5eb@posting.google.com>`

Who may help me?

## Thread: Modify Word Docvariables with Cobol — 2 msgs

### Modify Word Docvariables with Cobol

- **From:** "Thomas H���rkens" <thoerkens@rsw-orga.de>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T09:00:45+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ev8e1a.iho.ln@gw1.rsw-orga.de>`

```
Hello,

i try to modify a Word-Document out of my Cobol-Program. The Application
Word is running and i have open a document. Now I want change some
Docvariables within the Document. Have someone an idea to solve the problem
or have someone an example source code?

I work with the AcuBench 5.2 from ACUCORP

Thanks for your help

Thomas
```

#### ↳ Modify Word Docvariables with Cobol

- **From:** Gisle Forseth <GForseth@acucorp.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T23:57:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9332A1B4B5CED2119FCE00105AD16A4D41725C@hubcap.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<ev8e1a.iho.ln@gw1.rsw-orga.de>`

```
Here is a sample of print preview.

       print-preview.
      * Create "Word.Application" object
             create @Application of @Word
                 handle in word-app.

      * Make Word invisible
             modify word-app @Visible = 0.

      * Open file
             modify word-app @Documents::Open(filename,0,1)
                 giving word-doc.

      * Print Preview
             modify word-doc @PrintPreview().
             modify word-app @Visible = 1.

      * Destroy the objects
           destroy word-doc.
           destroy word-app.

Gisle

> From: Thomas H�rkens [mailto:thoerkens@rsw-orga.de]

…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: Q: Performing INSPECT on Tab-delimited File in MFCOBOL V3.4.18 — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Q: Performing INSPECT on Tab-delimited File in MFCOBOL V3.4.18

- **From:** "S M Trushell" <strush3@home.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T13:28:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_tC_7.326661$ez.46822820@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
Greetings,

In program READTAB.CBL, I have created and have successfully read a
tab-delimited file(ORG is LINE SEQUENTIAL), straight forward.
I am now trying to parse records from the tab-delimited file.
For each record read, I want to determine number of tabs in that record
using INSPECT:

           INSPECT fd-tab-infile-record
        TALLYING tab-count FOR ALL (**TAB CHAR**)
           DISPLAY 'tab count is  : ' tab-count.

I am unable to find literal value for tab-character (**TAB CHAR**) to use in
this case.
I am using MicroFocus Cobol V3.4.18.

Please advise.

TIA

S M Trushell
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: Performing INSPECT on Tab-delimited File in MFCOBOL V3.4.18

- **From:** leclaire <leclaire@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T13:48:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3AF7DA.F5CE0649@rr.com>`
- **References:** `<_tC_7.326661$ez.46822820@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
The TAB character has a value of 9, which I believe you may specify as
"0x09" in Microfocus (someone please correct me if I'm wrong).  Exact
syntax for expressing hexadecimal value varies among vendors, so I
typically prefer using decimal values, as follows:

01	TAB-CHAR.
	03 filler	pic 99 comp value 9.

This generally works fine, except that some compliers want to store
everything as words rather than bytes and may require a special
directive in order to allow for single-character values.

A greater problem you will encounter is that Microfocus typically
expands tabs into spaces when reading line sequential files.  This was
the topic of an earlier thread about a month or two ago, and
unfortunately there is no *easy* solution.  In the past, I have relied
upon an assembly language routine to read such files.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Q: Performing INSPECT on Tab-delimited File in MFCOBOL V3.4.18

- **From:** leclaire <leclaire@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T02:21:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3BA852.D13EF5DA@rr.com>`
- **References:** `<_tC_7.326661$ez.46822820@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3C3AF7DA.F5CE0649@rr.com>`

```

> The TAB character has a value of 9, which I believe you may specify as
> "0x09" in Microfocus (someone please correct me if I'm wrong).  Exact
> syntax for expressing hexadecimal value varies among vendors ...

On second thought, I think in Microfocus you would specify the
hex value as:
			pic x value x"09".

Your syntax for the inspect statement itself is correct.
```

## Thread: benefits of cobol in the present? — 15 msgs

### benefits of cobol in the present?

- **From:** Chris Humphries <chumphries@devis.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T15:01:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3AC316.8080705@devis.com>`

```
Hello All,
     I am starting to learn COBOL, and am just curious what would be the
  benefit of learning it besides to update older programs? I am learning
  it for fun (most i tell that too tell me "I'm sorry" or "Are you out of
  your mind?!").
     Is cobol "worth" learning in todays environment, with things like
  python and c++?


     Thanks in advance.

-chris humphries
  systems analyst
```

#### ↳ Re: benefits of cobol in the present?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T15:42:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<krE_7.51743$wa.3506395@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3C3AC316.8080705@devis.com>`

```

On  8-Jan-2002, Chris Humphries <chumphries@devis.com> wrote:

>      I am starting to learn COBOL, and am just curious what would be the
>   benefit of learning it besides to update older programs? I am learning
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>   python and c++?

It depends on what's "fun".

CoBOL's benefits are pretty much the same as they always were - being the
dominant language for business applications.   (not counting whatever
"language" spreadsheets are).  While it's not quite as dominant, as business
needs have broadened into such things as XML and such, most business rules
are still being implemented in CoBOL.

I'm not sure though that you will find business rules, accounting, and
typical business needs to be fun.   So we need a better definition of your
goals before we can give you a meaningful answer.
```

- **From:** Chris Humphries <chumphries@devis.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3AD438.5090106@devis.com>`
- **References:** `<3C3AC316.8080705@devis.com> <krE_7.51743$wa.3506395@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
fun as in learning a new language. Not really after any specific
goals (like building a game or something). I just enjoy learning
new languages, and that is satisfaction in and of itself.

just curious on the road ahead, and how cobol may be better
at some things and how it may compare to other languages in the
scope of neat and powerful things you can do with it.

thanks :)
-chris

> On  8-Jan-2002, Chris Humphries <chumphries@devis.com> wrote:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: benefits of cobol in the present?

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T08:11:29-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3B2841.44C0270B@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<3C3AC316.8080705@devis.com> <krE_7.51743$wa.3506395@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C3AD438.5090106@devis.com>`

```
On the one hand I can think of very little that is better for learning good
programming techniques than COBOL. Of course I only work in 2 languages-COBOL and
VB. (I used to work in C but haven't used it in years.)

Working with VB programmers I find that they place so much emphasis on the
interface that they (in general) lack the ability to follow the data. Further,
they are so blinded by VB's 'event-driven' model that they don't seem to realize
that nearly *all* programs are actually sequential. VB simply hides/automates the
main control loop. (The only reason I said nearly all is because there are
supposedly some multi-threaded VB programs out there. I've never seen any but if
they exist then those programs, as a whole, would not be truly sequential. Each
thread, however, would be.)

These lacks IMO make VB programmers unsuited for 'the big time'.

Now COBOL doesn't force you to write well-designed programs, but it does force you
to pay attention to what happens to the data as you execute the program. Moreover,
it also forces you to think about the order in which code is actually executed-not
only within each 'module' but also the order in which the different modules are
executed. In my experience all of that is important to writing a well-designed
program. It's really not any harder to write a well-designed program in VB than it
is in COBOL, but it seems as if VB-only programmers simply lack the
experience/training to do so. At least that's been my experience with them.

Chris Humphries wrote:

> fun as in learning a new language. Not really after any specific
> goals (like building a game or something). I just enjoy learning
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> scope of neat and powerful things you can do with it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: benefits of cobol in the present?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T19:09:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HtH_7.129$zq1.452462@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3C3AC316.8080705@devis.com> <krE_7.51743$wa.3506395@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C3AD438.5090106@devis.com> <3C3B2841.44C0270B@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us> wrote in message
news:3C3B2841.44C0270B@dced.state.ak.us...
> Working with VB programmers I find that they place so much emphasis on the
> interface that they (in general) lack the ability to follow the data.
Further,
> they are so blinded by VB's 'event-driven' model that they don't seem to
realize
> that nearly *all* programs are actually sequential. VB simply
hides/automates the
> main control loop. (The only reason I said nearly all is because there are
> supposedly some multi-threaded VB programs out there. I've never seen any
but if
> they exist then those programs, as a whole, would not be truly sequential.
Each
> thread, however, would be.)

I do not work in VB (I use PowerBASIC), but in Windows in general
"sequential" is relative: it depends on how you define "re-entrant."

It would not be uncommon at all for a procedure to call itself when
processing messages. The closest I could write this in COBOL might be
something like....

PROGRAM-ID. MAIN.

...
   CALL 'function' using parm-1 parm-2

END PROGRAM MAIN.
PROGRAM-ID. FUNCTION.

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING parm-1, parm-2
....
     CALL 'function' using a, b


Under Windows, in the "main control loop" (the message loop), it is really,
really easy to execute some system call which triggers a series of other
calls to the "main control loop", totally transparently. (It is all
documented quite nicely, but looking up each action to see what additional
actions it may rmay not trigger can be quite tedious). Not to mention, user
or other actions can "CALL FUNCTION" while "function" is still processing an
earlier request.

VB's design philosophy is to "hide" all this from the programmer - at the
cost of some functionality (i.e., true multi-threading). So, yes, VB
programmers "should" be able to handle "pure" sequential processing, as
using the "VB default processing" is pretty much that. However, writing
re-entrant code is advanced in any language.

Let's not forget that Microsoft VB was designed to allow non-rocket
scientists to write simple programs, and has succeeeded (spectacularly!) in
attracting those non-rocketeers to its ranks. As a result, IMNSHO,  the
average COBOL programmer is a far better programmer than the average VB
programmer; and what is "simple" to the average COBOL programmer may in fact
be totally alien to the average VB programmer.

MCM
```

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: benefits of cobol in the present?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T11:50:08+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3b787e_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C3AC316.8080705@devis.com> <krE_7.51743$wa.3506395@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C3AD438.5090106@devis.com>`

```
This is a good response Chris; I admire (and share) your sense of seeking
new things for the "fun" of it.

Normally, I wouldn't respond to a thread such as this because these
arguments have been flogged to death here many times and are, frankly,
boring. (Well they are to me, at least...<G>)

However, here's what you asked for:

1. COBOL is the best language bar none for manipulating data, both in memory
and from external media. Type casting is unnecessary (it is done for you)
and it is transportable across platforms, ensuring that the form most
efficient on the platform it is compiled to is utilised automatically. For
example, you can define arithmetic fields as USAGE COMP and know they will
be efficient on ANY platform. In recent years (well, the 80s are "recent" to
old timers like me...<G>) with the advent of "Little Endian" processors,
this usage has been extended to COMP-5, so we could use this across all such
platforms. A small and simple enhancement to the code for machines with this
architecture, implemented easily with a text editor, and providing optimum
efficency.

2. COBOL (when written properly) is the easiest source code to maintain.
This is arguable of course (one man sees "readability" as another's
"verbosity"), but its English like nature does make it quick to pick up when
you have to change it after a year or so.

3. COBOL provides a level of control which allows you to do very "clever"
things with data. For example, I never use "bound" controls on Databases.
While they work OK, I prefer to use COBOL with ESQL to manipulate my
Relational DB stuff. This way I have total control over what I want to do
and am not constrained to what the bound control can do. (This one will be a
personal choice and I don't think using bound controls is "wrong", I just
prefer to use COBOL).

4. In today's Web Oriented World, COBOL has a useful place in CGI
programming where its data manipulation capabilities make it more useful
than Python or PERL and a serious contender to Java Servlets. (Watch for the
 release of  DOT NET next month and Fujitsu's NetCOBOL which is designed to
integrate with it. Fujitsu and MicroSoft are partners.)

5. Object Oriented COBOL is ideal for building ActiveX (COM and DCOM)
components and my personal belief is that this is where the future lies. The
days of Source Language being relevant are nearly over. Systems like .NET
will run code that is written in a plethora of languages and we are at last
approaching a stage where it will be "the best language for the job" that
decides what a given component will be written in.

I already have applications where components written in C++, VB, and COBOL
all peacefully co-exist on the same Windows form and I see this being
extended in the future. Note that the person using the application neither
knows nor cares what source was used, as long as the components function as
required (and they do).

If 300,000,000 people are all accessing the Internet, it is fair to asssume
they have a computer <G>. If they have a computer they will want to use it.
They are not about to become computer programmers and we are already seeing
"Wizards" which enable non-technical people to achieve a level of competence
for certain jobs where specific code is necessary. This idea will expand
rapidly in the present and next decade and we will see people interacting
with their Wizards to get the functionality they require. The Wizards will
get much "smarter" and will have access to thousands of components, which
will be written in the "best language for the job".(And the users won't
care...the arguments about code elegance and choice of source language will
be left to academics (and that's properly where they belong, in my
opinion...))

COBOL IS the "best language" for certain jobs. However the public perception
that it is a Legacy Language for mainframes is hampering its advancement,
and the attitudes of COBOL programmers and the COBOL "establishment" is
doing nothing to help it either. "Fortress COBOL" dug in on the mainframes,
is NOT where the Language has to go if it is to survive. Client/Server
systems written in COBOL, utilizing the COBOL file system, so they are
"closed" and can only be accessed by COBOL programs, are another nail in
COBOL's coffin. Today's businesses require interconnectivity and "open"
exchange of data. Code that can't participate in this will become "pariah"
code to be shunned and discouraged by all (and quite correctly; there is no
need for this, and people who do it believing they are ensuring COBOL's
continued use are just plain wrong...)

Please go to www.aboutlegacycoding.com. Click the "Archive" button and read
an article that was published in April 2001, called "Meet the Future". This
article covers most of my arguments as to why you should learn COBOL.

I wish you every success in the future and please don't let anyone grind the
edge off your sense of what is "fun" <G>.

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: benefits of cobol in the present?

- **From:** Richard Riehle <richard@adaworks.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T09:39:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3B2EE6.1474D0A7@adaworks.com>`
- **References:** `<3C3AC316.8080705@devis.com>`

```
Chris Humphries wrote:

> Hello All,
>      I am starting to learn COBOL, and am just curious what would be the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>   python and c++?

One of the most dreadful things that has happened in the world of software
is the widespread adoption of C++.   Even worse has been the replacement
of COBOL in many shops with C++.

COBOL, in its earlier forms, with high dependance on global data was
certainly difficult to maintain, error-prone,  and needed updating.  It
has, in fact, proven quite resilient to such updating, and the current
versions provide good techniques for support of both low-coupling
and high-cohesion, qualities missing in earlier versions.

COBOL continues to be easy to learn and use for business data processing
applications.    It is much maligned by those who have not kept current
in their knowledge of its progress.

C+ began as a fairly simple set of extensions to C.   It too has evolved.
Unfortunately, the evolution of C++ has produced a large, complex, and
error-prone language that is inaccessible to the typical business data
processing programmer.

C++ is its own virus.

All that being said, my own preference, currently is for Ada 95.  Once again,

those who malign Ada are unaware of the evolution of that language into
a first-rate language for business data processing (including COBOL-like
picture clauses and COBOL-compatible decimal types), as well as for
many other kinds of programming.

It is all too easy for those who are ignorant of the current state of COBOL
or
Ada to criticize, especially when the popularity of another language is
(often
undeservedly) high in the current literature.

Make you decision on the basis of sound criteria, not on the basis of what
seems
popular.   COBOL, in its current version, is still a viable language for
writing
programs targeted to business data processing problems.

Richard Riehle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: benefits of cobol in the present?

- **From:** Richard Riehle <richard@adaworks.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-08T10:43:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3B3DCD.F6FF7F3F@adaworks.com>`
- **References:** `<3C3AC316.8080705@devis.com> <3C3B2EE6.1474D0A7@adaworks.com> <3C3AEED6.6000907@devis.com>`

```
Chris Humphries wrote:

> yeah i'm not backing c++ at all. i personally am not a big fan of
> it. i prefer python and C, though know many more.  I will learn it
> for my own personal interest, not for career gain or such.was just
> curious if there was is a market for it. I assume that it would be
> in a slump after the rave of Java and C++.

Just one caution.  There are some truly awful COBOL books out there.
One of the worst is the  COBOL in 21 Days Book.    A better one, oddly
enough, is the COBOL in 21 Hours book.   Also,  focus, if you can, on
the current ideas related to the object-oriented standard.    There are
some good books by Wison Price and by Ed Arranga as well as others
that deal with contemporary COBOL.   I cannot for the life of me undestand
why people continue to publish out-of-date COBOL books.

I was asked by a publisher several years ago to write a COBOL book for
them.   It turns out they wanted the same tired old COBOL, mainly to meet
the demand of the Y2K hysteria.    I wanted to do a book that would clearly
illustrate the value of modern COBOL.    Publishers and witers don't
always see eye-to-eye.

Richard Riehle
```

Rational One wrote:

> gee I was not trying to convert or offend anyone.......
> thank god for my flying saucer

Personally no offence taken. We are all free to choose what we believe or
believe 'in' - what's important is that somebody doesn't come on like they've
discovered the 'best thing since sliced bread', and like my own 'brethren' in
the past - treat you to an early version of the Golden Arches, (medium to rare),
to teach you the error of your ways..

Interestingly Russell's comment about Atheism being a 'religion'. I do remember
as a kid (about 14) walking along the sea front, Southsea, Portsmouth. On a soap
box stood a speaker, an acknowledged atheist, spewing 'hate' about Christianity.
That's many years ago, so perhaps we have all learned lessons from things like
the KKK,
Fascists, Communists, neo-Nazis, and the rigid fundamentalists in many religions
etc., that tolerance is the best solution.

Now let's get back to COBOL <G>

Jimmy
```

revisionist
> <G>.
>
> What the hell is an SR-33 ? Bill G's Dictionary was no help.
>

An Sr-33 was a teletype (paper not CRT) machine that ran at 110 baud,
uppercase only.  It also had a papertape/punch, and was my first "home
terminal" connected to my first home computer which was a 4k PDP-8.  I did
actually do some Cobol on that thing, but it was hooked up to a PDP-10 via a
modem.  Still have the modem, actually ... max 300 baud. About a foot square
and four inches in height.


> > As  for this oop stuff ...
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Bugger! I can't see Michael's post anywhere in the thread...

It looks like another case of the server not relaying EVERYTHING outside
North America.

And I ALWAYS enjoy Michael's posts.

Anyway, comment on your comment below, Jimmy.

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C3C8178.4F345E3E@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does that imply McCarthy or Ollie North were great American patriots ?
And of
> course, there's always Watergate.
>
It would imply that, if Goldwater was right. I seem to remember the American
people overwhelmingly rejected his hawkish position when he ran for
President. Seems nobody was really keen to start a nuclear war (except
Goldwater and some of the military, who were itching to play with their new
toys...).

Growing up in the Cold War with the threat of Nuclear annihilation hanging
over us like a Damoclean sword, it always worried me that all of us outside
the USA never even got to vote on the man who could decide our fate. I was
very worried when Goldwater started his campaign. Fortunately, despite the
castigation and denigration the American people have always been subject to,
in the majority of cases the majority get it right and Goldwater was
sidelined. We all breathed sighs of relief. And, yes, I do remember where I
was when Kennedy was shot (I was walking to the canteen on the campus of
Ardmore College in South Auckland, where I was studying to become a teacher)
and I freely admit I wept over it. It was like the light of hope had been
snuffed out. If Goldwater had got in, it is much more likely that a lot more
than hope would have been eliminated...

Bottom line: a soundbite from Barry Goldwater carries no credibility in my
book.

Pete.

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

>
> Growing up in the Cold War with the threat of Nuclear annihilation hanging
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> than hope would have been eliminated...

Putting aside the traumatic affect of World Wars I and II - you've zeroed in on
a defining moment in 20th century history. JFK was the King Arthur of his
favourite musical 'Camelot'. Well heeled from Dad's fortunes made during
prohibition, handsome in a rugged way, an ex-military man (USN), above all
YOUTHFUL to the people of the sixties, and married to a dishy girl. Youth was
predominant, with the four cheeky lads from Liverpool starting to make their
mark on the music scene.

My memories of that day are just as vivid as yours. Living in Yeovil, Somerset
at the time. Had just picked my wife up about 5pm on my Lambretta (scooter) -
remember those ?  Got home and switched on the radio. We normally listened to
the BBC Light Programme (music and comedies) but switched to BBC Home Service
for the 6pm news. Switching stations I accidentally hit on AFN (American Forces
Network in Germany). "This just coming in from Dallas. President John F. Kennedy
has been shot.......".

We were INCREDULOUS. We switched to BBC to get more full coverage. (And that
poignant image of his little son saluting the casque and the riderless horse).

As a footnote - didn't have TV until '65. Our grand kids here asked us, "What
did you sit in front of with your popcorn if you didn't have TV ?". Our reply ,
"We didn't have popcorn". <G>

Jimmy
```

I remember, when the '64 campaign began, feeling the same way you did, Peter.
Then I read a collection if BG's speeches and essays: Quite a different man than
the one I had been led to beleive was a maniac with an itchy trigger finger.
Don't beleive everthing you read in the newspaper, especially the NYT.

In the '80 campaign the Liberal Elite were saying the same things about RR. A
very dangerous man; will lead us into WWIII, an itchy trigger finger, no too
bright, etc. Look where he led us. Now, now, no cracks about the trillion+$ debt
(interesting that the # roughly equalled the cost of social programs introduced
by the Democratic congress during the period). <G>

Jack

Americans are generally treated well in many Arab & Muslim countries. That's
part of the reason that over 10,000 Americans make home in Egypt, 6,800 in
Indonesia, 8,000 in Jordan, 7,700 in Kuwait, 10,000 in Lebanon, 10,000 in
Nigeria, 4,000 in Pakistan, 36,000 in Saudi, 3,800 in Syria, 16,500 in UAE,
and in total about 3,700,000 Americans make home abroad (excluding military
& government employees).

But my point was that if this really is "The land of the free", then the
laws of this country should be applied impartialy to US citizen & visitor
alike, regardless of creed or color. I've lived in a foreign country (the
USA) for the last 8 years, and that may have given me a broader perspective.
Perhaps you should try it, oh Rational One. There are 1.2 billion Muslims,
most are more concerned with where the next meal is going to come from, and
how to live past the age of 50.

business ?
otherwise, why in heck would anyone want to live in a desert under extreme
islamic laws, edicts and fatwahs, except for MONEY

muslims do for their people with all of the oil money they get ?  why the
rulers get multiple wives, Mercedes and Rollers, plus huge palaces,
educations in the USA and on and on
like I've said before why don't they take care of their own ?
and how (to paraphrase Al Gorlione) 'fair' is it for our citizens to have to
dress as muslims in their countries (women in Chadors, etc...) but they get
to dress whatever way they want when over here ?
I mean, why do you think everyone wants to come to America ?   Because we
can do just about whatever we want as long as it is legal (or in slick
Willie's case as long as you can get away with it)

what we should focus on is bringing the muslim countries into the real
'free' world. Let them have their Allah but let's let citizens be free to
choose Allah or not and engage in the civil liberties that we have and stop
spending al of their money on weapons
We have a United Nations that should be the peacekeeper and all nations
should contribute and be protected fully by mutual cooperation of the group.

thanks for the conversation Rufio.....

> USA) for the last 8 years, and that may have given me a broader
perspective.
> Perhaps you should try it, oh Rational One. There are 1.2 billion Muslims,
> most are more concerned with where the next meal is going to come from,
and
> how to live past the age of 50.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > How about the extremist attitude of various national security
personnel
> > > which has gone against the spirit (if not the letter) of the laws of
> this
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

"Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com> wrote in message
news:KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net...

> I can't dispute your figures on Americans living in muslim countries but
> have you ever considered the fact that they live there becuase of the oil
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> speaking of money again....what do the rulers (enslavers) of the 1.2
billion
> muslims do for their people with all of the oil money they get ?  why the
> rulers get multiple wives, Mercedes and Rollers, plus huge palaces,
> educations in the USA and on and on
> like I've said before why don't they take care of their own ?
> and how (to paraphrase Al Gorlione) 'fair' is it for our citizens to have
to
> dress as muslims in their countries (women in Chadors, etc...) but they
get
> to dress whatever way they want when over here ?
> I mean, why do you think everyone wants to come to America ?   Because we
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> choose Allah or not and engage in the civil liberties that we have and
stop
> spending al of their money on weapons
> We have a United Nations that should be the peacekeeper and all nations
> should contribute and be protected fully by mutual cooperation of the
group.
>

Frankly, I am more worried about Bush and his coherts taking over the world
than I am about the muslim hordes.  I like my American neighbours, but I
have no desire to "come to America", nor do the majority of my
country-mates.  I raher suspect that there are a few other people in the
free world that think the same.  We are not all desparate to renounce our
own countries and become American, regardless of what you and your ilk
think.

I rather suspect that a good number of Americans are rather interested in
MONEY as well, even those that live at home.  Yesterday, 12,000 children
died of starvation.  Today, another 12,000.  Tomorrow, another 12,000.  I
see no war on hunger.

Fanatacism works on all levels, as does ideology, even politics.  It seems
it only works in politics when the ability to make money is hampered.  When
the cost/benefit analysis goes into the red, then fighting the good fight
goes rapidly downhill.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T16:34:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair,

but then why did WWII happen to France, England, Poland, Norway, Egypt,
China, Korea, and the rest of the world ?   what did British citizens do to
Hitler to provoke him ?
and who bailed them all out ?
I am not trying to brag for the USA but simply stating the facts. And let's
not forget WWI also. The USA has been thrust into the leading role as world
policeman and like regular blue shirted policemen, don't always do the right
thing or treat 'suspects' gently. The police forces of the world are made up
of men and women, with all their faults and strengths. As I recall from my
history books the US tried to stay out of the wars but the help was accepted
by just about all concerned.
As for being afraid of Bush, et al. trying to take over the world.......that
is a good one.....on what basis do you make the assumption ?   As I recall
the wars we have fought have been to save other countries from oppressors in
one form or another. WWI and WWII....Vietnam was attacked by North Vietnam
and China. Speaking of China, we also fought and killed over 1 million in
the Korean conflict where another country attacked an ally of ours.

Now we have engaged in some dirty politics by propping up people like the
Shah of Iran, the Saudi royals and so on but consider that just maybe we
were trying to replace one evil with a slightly lesser one and at least we
took (what our leaders thought) was a step in the right direction, although
not a perfect one.

One last thought on the lesser of two evils....here in the US we had to
choose from God-monger GW Bush and communist leaning, crooked Al Gorlione
and the people chose Bush. I support Bush because he (and this all my
opinion) is the less distasteful to me.



"Alistair Maclean" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >choose Allah or not and engage in the civil liberties that we have and
stop
> >spending al of their money on weapons
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alistair Maclean
```

```


Rational One wrote:

> Alistair,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> by just about all concerned.

WHOA ! I wasn't going to get into this one - but you've hit a 'nerve'. YES - the
US did stay out of WWI 1914 - 1918 and you only 'rescued' the Allies in 1917 as
a result of the Lusitania being sunk.

WWII -  Sep 1939 - Aug '45. Again following the Monroe doctrine you stayed out
of it and didn't join in until December 1941, when you were attacked at Pearl
Harbor by the Japanese, not the Nazis.

Accept the fact that the US is the GREATEST military and economic power - but
don't expect your friends to not get tired of you constantly bleating about it.
That power should be used with a touch of humility - the audience will be more
receptive.

To many non Americans your foreign policy is seen as appalling. Don't repeat the
mistake in Afghanistan.

But we still like you <G>

>
> As for being afraid of Bush, et al. trying to take over the world.......that
> is a good one.....on what basis do you make the assumption ?   As I recall
> the wars we have fought have been to save other countries from oppressors in
> one form or another. WWI and WWII....

I'm no historian on Vietnam - but if my memory serves me correctly, there was
initially a reaction by Bhuddists to the Catholic controlled government,
(S.Vietnam), and supported, understandably, by JFK (an RC). This royal screwup
gave the N. Vietnamese the right opportunity.

> Vietnam was attacked by North Vietnam
> and China. Speaking of China, we also fought and killed over 1 million in
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You betcha. Just finished reading 'Spy Catcher' by Peter Wright a former Deputy
Director of MI5. He was approached by some idealistic right-wingers in MI5 that
they should get into the disinformation game to get rid of the Harold Wilson
Labour government, replacing it with a coalition headed by Mountbatten - God
forbid ! And yes their American friends in the CIA, (particularly one with the
last name Angleton), thought this was a great idea ! Needless to say, Ronnie's
friend Maggie Thatcher tried to get the book banned.

>
> One last thought on the lesser of two evils....here in the US we had to
> choose from God-monger GW Bush and communist leaning, crooked Al Gorlione
> and the people chose Bush. I support Bush because he (and this all my
> opinion) is the less distasteful to me.

When are you right-wingers going to accept that those that don't agree with the
likes of Barry Goldwater, Jesse Helms etc. are not ' goddam communists' - there
is a middle ground between right and left-wing politics. No wonder Don is all
for staying Canadian, although militarily, because of geography and 'culture',
we have to throw in our lot with the US - but that still leaves us as Nervous
Nellies about your foreign policy.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T19:35:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w2m%7.604173$8q.48724597@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54> <3C3DE58D.312E1F8D@shaw.ca>`

```

On 10-Jan-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Accept the fact that the US is the GREATEST military and economic power -
> but
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> more receptive.

Nobody wants their kind to be represented in the minds of others by the
biggest braggarts and blowhards.  But you know it happens.  Wherever you're
from, there are people "representing" you whom you would just rather shut up
and sit down.

Still - you're doing us a service by reminding ourselves to see ourselves as
others see us.  There is a small percentage of people who will stop and do
so who wouldn't have done so otherwise.  Every bit helps.


> To many non Americans your foreign policy is seen as appalling. Don't
> repeat the mistake in Afghanistan.

Our foreign policy is big and powerful.  Nobody agrees with all of it -
fortunately.  (we WANT some dissension in the ranks here).  But I don't
think I could come up with a policy that I could agree with unless it was
one based upon powerlessness (where it wouldn't matter).  Is it too
isolationist or too interventionist or paradoxically both?   I want to save
lives where I can - but does that mean I make peace with tyrants?   I want
to promote freedom - but does that mean I don't allow cultures to set their
own standards?  Nothing is simple here.
```

you're
> from, there are people "representing" you whom you would just rather shut
up

> Still - you're doing us a service by reminding ourselves to see ourselves
as
> others see us.  There is a small percentage of people who will stop and do

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> isolationist or too interventionist or paradoxically both?   I want to
save
> lives where I can - but does that mean I make peace with tyrants?   I want
> to promote freedom - but does that mean I don't allow cultures to set
their
> own standards?  Nothing is simple here.

And that is the problem, Howard.  While I rant against the Americans on
occasion, I still see them as caring and concerned neighbours.  I've bitten
my tongue over events in the US for several months now. When a good
neighbour is in trouble, then you help and keep your mouth shut, IMO.
Eventually, though, it becomes time to speak plainly.

Funny that this thread should be labeled "religion and Cobol".  At least
part of the problem, as I see it, is that Amercan's have always approached
free enterprise and individual freedoms as a religion, rather than as a
mechanism for social betterment.  Thus we get the CIA funding terrorists
like Bin Laden, regardless of morality, simply because he is not that
horrible thing called a communist. It is the exact same mindset that makes
it alright to kill innocents, because they are not Muslim.  It is just as
wrong, and just as much a terrorist act for the CIA to support the Shaw of
Iran as it is for the Taiban to support Bin Laden. The fact that it is done
in the name of "freedom" instead of the name of "allah" does not excuse it.

I'll take a courteous opponent that believes in good will towards their
fellow man over a fanatical partner that believes in "playing hardball" any
day of the week.

Donald
```

Not entirely, Jimmy.  I might be in favour of a United States of the World,
provided there were at least 75 new states outside the current ones.
However, there is no way on earth that the United States of America would
ever dilute their power to that extent.

As for the United States being benevoent towards other countries, this guy
has to be deaf dumb and blind.  Compare Castro to Babtista.  Babtista was
one of the most corrupt, totalitarian leaders of the free world, and ran
Cuba with the assistance of a partnership of the US mafia and politicians.
Castro, on the other hand, is loved by the people of Cuba, and considered a
saviour.

The United States is a bastion of freedom for Americans.  For the rest of
the world, their record is actually quite horrible, and resembles schoolyard
bullies more than anything else.  While I honestly believe that to be more
the result of big bussiness than individual Americans, their government has
a long history of opressing people of other countries to gain advantage for
American corporations.  If American corporations behaved at home like they
behave in third world countries, they would be closed down.

Donald
```
