```

On 10-Jan-2002, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Not entirely, Jimmy.  I might be in favour of a United States of the
> World,
> provided there were at least 75 new states outside the current ones.
> However, there is no way on earth that the United States of America would
> ever dilute their power to that extent.


Not one country would be happy to allow "the world" to decide what is good
or bad for that country.  Certainly there are instances where the net
advantage might be sufficient for them to allow it, but usually not.  
Should the world culture be a compromise between the world's cultures,
homogenized and compromised?

Also - when a state makes mistakes - it is awfully nice if its neighbors try
something different.  Let's see which policy works best and learn.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:7Km%7.215321$m05.18795927@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> On 10-Jan-2002, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > However, there is no way on earth that the United States of America
would
> > ever dilute their power to that extent.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am sure the same thing was said by the 13 colonies when the united states
was formed.  I think it time for a world government.  If we cannot do that
without homogenizing everything into one mass, then we are not nearly as
smart as we think.  But that is the point.  We are a global village now,
like it or not.   It is time we started acting like one, instead of like a
dozen warring states from a previous millenium.

> Also - when a state makes mistakes - it is awfully nice if its neighbors
try
> something different.  Let's see which policy works best and learn.

Absolutely.  There are only a few basics though.  Make sure everyone is fed
and has a place to sleep, and do not let individuals act violently.  To get
things back to Cobol, perhaps the greatest dis-service our trade has done
was to turn the milleneum into a technical event.  Instead of the biggest
party in history and a vow to end world hunger, we all hunkered down and
concentrated on "Y2K " problems.

We seemed to be close to the end of war.  Perhaps I am getting old, I I
still belive in all those hippie values I adopted forty years back.  We are
at a point in history where we can turn the world into a garden.  Time to
start, in my opinion.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T21:47:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3E0CFE.9258B4DD@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54> <3C3DE58D.312E1F8D@shaw.ca> <a7m%7.17277$B36.2944896@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> Not entirely, Jimmy.  I might be in favour of a United States of the World,
> provided there were at least 75 new states outside the current ones.
> However, there is no way on earth that the United States of America would
> ever dilute their power to that extent.

It probably hasn't appeared in the American press, or anywhere else for that
matter, but I was thinking of our current effort to get our Princess Pat's into
Afghanistan. The 'offer' was rejected by the Brits, so we've just gone cap in
hand to Uncle Sam asking "Can we join with your lot ?". The British attitude was
condemned in today's paper by the Conservative foreign affairs spokesman.. I
understand Tony Blair's conundrum, keeping his feet stretched across the
Atlantic, and at the same time, trying to act as an influence on the rest of
Europe. (Also makes sense that the Turks as Muslims, should be in the
forefront). Meanwhile, back to US and Canada, we are integrated, like NORAD
although they *really* only accept us politely as a junior partner. (I hope to
God nobody asks us to put our navy on a real war footing - I'm not sure they
have enough oars in stock for the rowing boats !).

>
> As for the United States being benevoent towards other countries, this guy
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> saviour.

BATISTA not Baptista - see did it UPPERCASE just like your SR-33 would <G>

I wonder what the average Joe Blow in the US thinks about Cuba as opposed to
government policy. Interesting how 'terrorists' or 'nationalists' eventually
become 'leaders' or 'revered' by their own :-

    - Michael Collins and de Valera - Ireland
    - Irgun Zei Leumi or Hagganh (got spellings wrong) - but at least one became

    Israeli PM.
    - Gammel Abdel Nasser - Egypt
    - Jomo Keynatta - Keyna
    - Fidel - in Cuba

and probably many more that I can't immediately think of.

Yes the communist takeover of Cuba was a genuine threat, leading up to 'Missiles
of October'. Nevertheless, given the collapse of the USSR the States could have
taken a fresh look at Cuba. Theoretical - but had they 'sweetened it' by
offering Fidel economic incentives, leading to a jaundiced type of capitalism as
in the current Russia - Fidel's regime might no longer be around.

The guy that started all this did query about foreign policy. This one is a real
downer - the USSR collapsed and naturally the US (as many others of us), were
pleased to see it happen. But having happened, the US let the Russians stew in
their own juice, leading to their questionable government and mafia-type
commercialism.  Just like George Marshall had the foresight to see that there
needed to be a reconstruction of Europe after WWII, it is tragic that a current
US politician didn't have the same vision to help the Russians to get on a sound
financial footing. (Before somebody from the States asks - it has to be YOU -
nobody else has the money).

>
> The United States is a bastion of freedom for Americans.  For the rest of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> American corporations.

They call the press the 'Fifth Estate' (?). Well I would suggest US commercial
interests are the 'Sixth Estate' and an additional arm of government. Consider
1918, Britain carved up the eastern coast of the Mediterranean, down to Saudi
Arabia. They 'let' the French have the bits they didn't want and retained the
rest - O-I-L !
Move on to the end of WWII and exhausted European colonial nations. As a former
colony the US just couldn't abide the colonial idea and with great zest
encouraged the dismemberment of former colonies.

Two results followed :-

- Africa - a complete balls-up as the totalitarian regimes took over - Congo,
Idi Amin and the latest clown in Zimbabwe. I'm not defending colonialism, but I
think perhaps another 50 years of benevolent colonial rule, (you could
substitute 'arrogant' for benevolent),  might have benefited the average African
peasant, as opposed to what he/she has now. Given that they learned to spell the
word d-e-m-o-c-r-a-c-y, perhaps they could have achieved independence later
without the terrible bloodshed.

- Middle East - eventually Britain got kicked out of Persia by Mossadeq. Hung on
to interests in Iraq, Saudi/Aden peninsula for a while and then quit. Surprise,
surprise - who controls those oil interests in the M.E now - Uncle Sam ! And it
is the getting of the oil which controls their ME foreign policy, to the extent
of supporting dubious 'monarchies'.

We only know too well up here that when US business flexes its muscles with a
beef that commerce is not working as they think it should, lobbyists ensure the
US government puts the squeeze on Canada. NAFTA - it's a joke !

> If American corporations behaved at home like they
> behave in third world countries, they would be closed down.

But they do ! It's called Microsoft - and now with a Republican government, as
predicted in the US press some two years ago,  MS is still hopping along like
the Energizer bunny <G>

And your other message to Howard :-

>We seemed to be close to the end of war.  Perhaps I am getting old, I I
>still belive in all those hippie values I adopted forty years back.  We are
>at a point in history where we can turn the world into a garden.  Time to
>start, in my opinion.

A very idealistic thought but a BEATUTIFUL one. (Perhaps it is experiences and
age which lets us see the possibilities of what could be).

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> .
>
> The guy that started all this did query about foreign policy. This one is
a real
> downer - the USSR collapsed and naturally the US (as many others of us),
were
> pleased to see it happen. But having happened, the US let the Russians
stew in
> their own juice, leading to their questionable government and mafia-type
> commercialism.  Just like George Marshall had the foresight to see that
there
> needed to be a reconstruction of Europe after WWII, it is tragic that a
current
> US politician didn't have the same vision to help the Russians to get on a
sound
> financial footing. (Before somebody from the States asks - it has to be
YOU -
> nobody else has the money).

We sent them billions. Backed loans by the World Bank, etc. Most of the
money disappeared. They've since turned things around on their own. For
example, Russia junked their entire body of tax law and replaced it with a
flat tax. Compliance moved from near zero to a high percentage indeed.

>
> They call the press the 'Fifth Estate' (?).

Not exactly. The press is the 'Fourth Estate' or 'Fifth Column.' Same thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T19:58:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<unm%7.5808$JF.44924@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54> <3C3DE58D.312E1F8D@shaw.ca>`

```
Jim


I think we might have stayed away from WWI due to the somewhat recent US
civil war where millions died....?

As for the humility, I think Americans do want to behave humbly. I for
myself only fall back on the 'what has the US done' tactic when people seem
to complain that we are so bad.
So both sides can be blamed and excused for their points of view, I guess.

I do take issue with the right winger label (politely). I voted Republican
but only because the Democrats are so awful in my opinion. The vast right
wing god-mongers scare me too, just less than the left.

I am curious for my own enlightenment about our foreign policy and what we
could do wrong in Afghanistan.

I believe we have a foreign policy that is more prominent than say Canada is
because of the wealth and power, real or perceived. Since we get into so
many other affairs I think we get more flak than credit just because people
seem to like only bad news. Plus our very own news media likes to bash us.
They will show two Afghan babies hit by a box of food dropped on them and
talk about how evil we ourselves are but NOT show the babies who died in the
WTC or the orphans left when mom/dad dies in WTC. Our own press I believe is
to blame for a lot of how the world sees us.

Another example or two - the Nixon Watergate mess and the Clinton scandals
and resulting wag the dog missile launching he did caused us to look really,
really bad.
How many baby milk factories in Sudan did Clinton bomb ?
On the other hand, I think Saddam should be blamed for their starvation
because he gets billions a year in oil for food and does not spend it. Our
own press really whacks us on this because it is bad news and bad news
sells.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T14:52:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tzVlSIAx2EQ8EwOq@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54> <3C3DE58D.312E1F8D@shaw.ca>`

```
Nice reply JJG. Thanks.

With regard to Vietnam, I read a book regarding the closure of the WW2
and the finger of blame was pointed at the British for, initially,
enlisting Ho Chi Minh's support against the Japanese with vague promises
of independence from France for a united Vietnam. We then reneged on
that promise and, having supplied arms to Ho Chi Minh, left the
Vietnamese no option but for armed insurrection. One final point on
Vietnam, we Brits fought a counter-insurgency operation in Malaysia and
won. How come the mightiest economic and military force in the world
failed in Vietnam?

Finally, Oh Rational One, my mother assures me that WW1 and WW2 also
involved Scotland, Wales and N. Ireland against the Bosch, and not just
the English. A small but VERY important point to the Scots (like me).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T22:16:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0201112216.179ea289@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54>`

```
"Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com> wrote 

<snip>
>....Vietnam was attacked by North Vietnam
> and China. 
When the United States first got involved, there were a small number
of North Vietnamese "advisors" working with the National Liberation
Front, which was involved in a civil war with the French-backed
national government. When the US-backed forces started winning, North
Vietnam sent increasing amounts of support to the Viet Cong, which WAS
communist, and which was a member of the NLF.  In time, because of
this support, the VC became the dominant element in the NLF. 
Eventually, North Vietnamese regular army troops joined the conflict. 
China was never involved, with the exception of occasional observers,
the same as the USSR.  But they weren't even "advisors".  The point
is, it is completely incorrect to say that the United States got
involved because of an attack or invasion.
 
<snip>
> 
> One last thought on the lesser of two evils....here in the US we had to
> choose from God-monger GW Bush and communist leaning, crooked Al Gorlione
> and the people chose Bush. I support Bush because he (and this all my
> opinion) is the less distasteful to me.

Whoa! The PEOPLE did NOT choose Bush! The PEOPLE chose Gore, by a
half-million votes. But, because this is a Federal Republic, the
STATES chose Bush.   And because the Constitution is respected, Bush
assumed office with the (eventual) consent of Gore and the acceptance
of 99% of the people.

Stephen J Spiro
```

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>

```
I have to respond the your 'hereditary royalty' comment.....

Al Gorlione's father was a career politician in the days when Congressmen
and Senators were paid poorly. His dad never served in a congress where the
pay was above 35 thousand dollars per year.

Then how if he was so 'honest' did the Gorlione family come to be so dang
rich.

They are multimillionaires in tobacco and oil, yes OIL !!!!!

GW may have inherited money, but his daddy made his money wildcatting OIL.
How did the Gore family get so damn much money ?

Oh yeah and remember whose daddy voted against the LBJ civil rights acts in
the 1960's ?
Why it was Al Gore senior !!!  The same man so fond of using the dreaded 'N'
word.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-14T16:36:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HOD08.17692$Tq.162623@rwcrnsc54>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54> <928495c6.0201112216.179ea289@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen,


The published popular vote shows Al Gorlione winning by 500,000
votes.....Ok, I agree with published statements but have some problems.

First, slick Willie forbade the U.S. military from voting on base, depriving
1.5 million voters (who likely would've voted for Bush) from an easy chance
to vote. When I say easy I mean you have military bases often out in the
boonies......why make a serviceperson drive 50 miles from some base in South
Dakota to the nearest town ?

Second, there were several proven incidents of vote buying in different
parts of the country where homeless people and college students were paid to
vote AND there were convictions. I don't even remember even ALLEGATIONS that
the republicans did this.

Third, the Florida panhandle and other media bias trying to influence the
vote to lean left.
For those of you outside the US.....our left leaning media called the
election for Al Gorlione before the voting booths had closed. Now tell me
honestly - would you go vote after the election was over ?
Let's also not forget about voting irregularities in California where some
cities had to disclose massive fraud or mismanagement. Take the example of
the peoples republic of San Francisco where they overturned an entire
election because of irregularities.
Also - California had 3 million absentee votes. Got that figure ?   They
were never added to the nationwide total because they were counted a month
after the election. Why not added to the total, you ask ?   Because in
California absentee ballots tend toward the right and maybe, just maybe
Bushie would've overtaken Gorlione in the popular vote.

And how about St. Louis Missouri where a judge allowed blacks (read -
democrats) to vote an extra four hours. No, please don't use the racist
card - it is a fact that inner cities are comprised of black people in far
greater numbers that non-blacks (aka honkeys, peckerwoods, enslavers,
oppressors, racists).


Et cetera.......your buddy slick Willie did not even get close to 49.9
percent of the vote. Thank Ross Perot from siphoning off 18 percent in 1992
and a lesser amount in 1996.
If Al Gorlione had lost the popular vote you bet he would have taken the
presidency if he got the Electoral College.

Et cetera ad nausem.....the US supreme court vote SEVEN to ZERO to overturn
the Florida supremes and their biased and illegal decision to allow a manual
recount of only certain votes in certain places. The US supremes voted 5-4
(ok I will agree along the apparent ideological lines) to send it back to
Florida for rework.

Finally, and I mean do disrespect to you and other left sided voters, face
the facts.....the vast left wing conspiracy is out of the White House and
adults are back in charge.


"Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@mail.com> wrote in message
news:928495c6.0201112216.179ea289@posting.google.com...
> "Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com> wrote
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

Gorlione
> > and the people chose Bush. I support Bush because he (and this all my
> > opinion) is the less distasteful to me.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Stephen J Spiro
```

```
Stephen,


Wait a minute, please......no disrespect intended.....

I am no ultra right wing Republican.....heck I don't believe in god any more
than I believe in UFO's and I am also pro-choice....if you want an abortion

election.

inclined.


R.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D9jeCASuEQ8EwPt@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54>`

```
My response was a criticism of the following text which had been posted.   

>> >what we should focus on is bringing the muslim countries into the real
>> >'free' world. Let them have their Allah but let's let citizens be free to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

I personally find the 'bring the muslim countries into the real world'
offensive and insensitive. I am sure that Muslims will find it more so.
I understand from political analysis in the post-September 11th era that
the Muslim perception of America's role on the world stage coupled with
their own senses of poverty, injustice and powerlessness were major
contributors to the rise of Muslim terrorist organizations.

I have no personal dislike of the USA (I grew up years ago) but I feel
that the negative comments made by an earlier contributor do nothing but
pour oil on troubled waters.

As for WW1 and WW2, you guys were late for those two; are you trying to
be in at the start of the next big one?   ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-13T08:17:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C41429B.57B8D03C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <jnZ_7.409971$C8.29722597@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <XLZ_7.869$z27.131751@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <fN3%7.16477$762.131379@rwcrnsc54> <cA5%7.115282$Wd.35933098@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KH8%7.17468$DG5.166027@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <q$pkRLA8xYP8EwXk@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Koj%7.21142$762.170633@rwcrnsc54> <3D9jeCASuEQ8EwPt@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> >> >what we should focus on is bringing the muslim countries into the real
> >> >'free' world. Let them have their Allah but let's let citizens be free to
> >> >choose Allah or not and engage in the civil liberties 

Muslims do not have any ability to 'choose', they must follow their
religious laws or be burned in hell, or in life.

> I personally find the 'bring the muslim countries into the real world'
> offensive and insensitive. I am sure that Muslims will find it more so.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> contributors to the rise of Muslim terrorist organizations.

I don't think that 'poverty, injustice and powerlessness' are what they
feel at all.  It is goodness (them) against 'the forces of evil godless
devil-worshopping atheistic heathans'.  Poverty is a virtue, as it used
to be Christians.  Injustice only comes from the devil, whatever happens
is 'justice' if it is muslim or from Allah.  All power of good is Allah,
if things are not going right it is because there is too much evil (ie
devil led Americans) and Allah needs help to fight it.

The evolution of the Catholic religion took it through periods of
inquisitions, witch-hunts, persecutions, crusades and holy wars in which
Christians either converted heathans or sent them to 'God' for him to
deal with.  The muslims are going through one of those now, they are
just several hundred years behind (or 50 years the McCarthy witch-hunt
against the forces of evil godless ...).

They see the 'bring the muslim countries into the real world' as yet
another religious crusade led by the devil (who is very real to them)
designed to eliminate the muslim religion - which in would actually have
to do to make it what americans call 'real'.  To them (or specifically
to the leaders) it is not 'theocracy vs democracy', it is 'the people of
Allah' against Bealzebub and the forces of evil.

Actually it is just like the crusades were Americans want to convert
them to Democracy (a Christian based democracy) or send them to God (the
Christian God) to be dealt with.

> As for WW1 and WW2, you guys were late for those two; are you trying to
> be in at the start of the next big one?   ;-)

They're trying to be early to make up.    ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T08:55:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1k6fr$1ka$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<As3%7.114910$Wd.35867346@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <20020109225318.28515.00000572@mb-mp.aol.com>`

```
In article <20020109225318.28515.00000572@mb-mp.aol.com>,
YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote:
>>From: "Rufio" davecawdell@home.com 
>>Date: 1/9/02 5:26 PM Eastern Standard Time
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>deserve neither liberty nor freedom'   -  Ben Franklin

Ummmmm... that is almost as exact as 'Go, thou, and try not to sin as 
much'; my memory - admittedly, a porous thing - has the quotation as 'they 
that can give up essential liberty to obtain a little temporary safety 
deserve neither liberty nor safety.'

Note the qualifiers of 'essential' and 'temporary'; this is not the first 
time that I have seen these shifted into 'a little' and I find that kind 
of corruption... amusing, among other things.

DD
```

```
According to some quotes I had stashed away, the exact statement that
Ben wrote was, ""He who would trade an ounce of freedom for a pound of
security loses both and deserves neither."  

Ben also said, "Democracy is two wolves and a lamb voting on what to
have for lunch.  Liberty is a well-armed lamb contesting the outcome
of the vote."

Ben was a bit of a radical for his time, but I think his message is
still true.

Regards,
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a1n4ed$cqv$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article
<8B9610FCCD5A4CD1.6CBB40BCECACC309.80C97A5CD54D6E1E@lp.airnews.net>,
> SkippyPB  <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
> >On 10 Jan 2002 13:24:36 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:
> >
> >>In article
<B8CF4EA5C08919C3.01082D124C8EC92C.0C3F5ACB5A7A03BB@lp.airnews.net>,
> >>SkippyPB  <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
> >>>On 10 Jan 2002 08:55:07 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>>>>>How would people feel if the extremism took the form of a a law
requiring
> >>>>>>everyone to carry ID, with fingerprints, retinal prints & DNA
information?
> >>>>>>I've heard this proposed recently. I don't totally oppose it, as I
think we
> >>>>>>have to give up some liberties, in order to protect other liberties.
> >>>>>
> >>>>>"sound byte" (in quotes because they didn't have sound recording back
then) and
> >>>>>I may not have it exact....
> >>>>>
> >>>>>'Those who are willing to give up a little liberty for a little
security
> >>>>>deserve neither liberty nor freedom'   -  Ben Franklin
> >>>>
> >>>>Ummmmm... that is almost as exact as 'Go, thou, and try not to sin as
> >>>>much'; my memory - admittedly, a porous thing - has the quotation as
'they
> >>>>that can give up essential liberty to obtain a little temporary safety
> >>>>deserve neither liberty nor safety.'
> >>>>
> >>>>Note the qualifiers of 'essential' and 'temporary'; this is not the
first
> >>>>time that I have seen these shifted into 'a little' and I find that
kind
> >>>>of corruption... amusing, among other things.
> >>>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>bit of provenance for these citings other than 'some quotes I had
stashed
> >>away'?  Searches done at http://www.google.com/advanced_search for exact
> >>phrases "ounce of freedon for a pound of security" and "Liberty is a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

After going through all this discussion about BF - he didn't have the issue
of someone with little more than a pair of nail clippers being able to kill
a 747 full of people, along with several thousand people on the ground. He
also wasn't living in today's society, where you already HAVE to give up
some INDIVIDUAL liberty, to go out in SOCIETY. On your own land, you can go
where you like, but try driving down the left of the freeway, or on the
shoulder. On your own land, you can stop and do whatever - try having a
picnic in the middle of an intersection. On your own land, you can live
without being watched - but do you want to take police off the streets. On
your own land there doesn't have to be any no-go areas, but try walking
round South Phoenix at night, loaded down with jewelry, furs, air-Jordans
etc.

America may have lost sight of the inevitable conflict/compromise between
freedom for the INDIVIDUAL and freedom for the SOCIETY. In my own opinion,
the individual in the USA has a great deal of  freedom, at the expense of
society, when compared to most other countries. But perhaps BF was talking
about giving up freedom of society for security of society?

P.S. anyone who opposed a loss of freedom should probably be lobbying for
the removal of all airport checks. They should pester their elected
officials to have the freedom to carry M16's on commercial flights - for
personal protection. They should also seek the abolition of the police, FBI,
& all other groups who restrict individual freedom.
```

```
In article <lhI%7.121693$Wd.37184951@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>,
Rufio <davecawdell@home.com> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a1n4ed$cqv$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
>a 747 full of people, along with several thousand people on the ground.

This was never contested.

>He
>also wasn't living in today's society, where you already HAVE to give up
>some INDIVIDUAL liberty, to go out in SOCIETY.

This, too - along with the rest of what you've written - was never 
contested.  What was questioned was the actual quotation... I do not know 
what your usual manner-of-approach to such matters is but mine, 
personally, is that of a moderately stodgy classicist; as such I find that 
it is best to get the quotation *right*, first, before discussing how it 
might be thought of and applied.

[snippage]

>But perhaps BF was talking
>about giving up freedom of society for security of society?

Perhaps Mr Franklin was saying 'I fear I've lost my powdered wig and 
there'll be the Devil toupee'; until the quotation is rendered accurately 
one possibility is nigh as good as another.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT The ghost of Patrick Henry, was: religion and COBOL

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T11:55:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3e1d9e_9@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <z$1iREAMrYP8EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```

Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:z$1iREAMrYP8EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> In article <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospa
> m.enternet.co.nz> writes
> >Why would an open-minded Rationalist object to anyone putting whatever
tag
> >lines they like on their mail?
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ah, I think you missed the point Alistair. I will defend to the death the
right of people in a democratic society to disagree. That is NOT the same as
defending what they believe in. The point is that even if I personally don't
agree with their belief, I DO believe in their right to express it.

While this may sound dramatic, it is absolutely true. I fervently believe in
the democratic system (despite its imperfections) and my personal integrity
requires me to recognise that there are things more important than my
personal survival.

So would I die for a principle?

Yes.

But only as a last resort...<G>.  We are all going to die sometime and it
might as well be in a good cause, as lying in bed at age 90...

"Then up spake brave Horatius
  The Captain of the Gate.
  'To every man upon this Earth,
  Death cometh soon or late.
  And how can man die better
  Than facing fearful odds
  For the ashes of his Fathers
  And the temples of his Gods.' "
-- Lord MacCaulay, The Lays of Ancient Rome (and no, it wasn't Cleopatra
<G>) --

Before the younger, rasher, elements among you, inspired by the above, race
out to enlist, let me make a few observations....

1. This is a very PERSONAL viewpoint. It is right for ME. It may not be
right for YOU...<G>.
2. I have lived a VERY full life and operated each day on the principle
"Well, if I get hit by a truck today at least I had a great time..."
3. I have no responsibilites to anyone other than myself. So my life is my
own.
4. I am over 50 and have no real right to have lived this long (especially
considering some of the places and situations I have found myself
in...Beirut when the trouble started, Bangladesh during the revolution,
behind the Iron Curtain during the Cold War, and downtown Auckland on a
Friday night...)
5. I believe there is no life after death and I have no problem with that.
It has been one of the things that has prompted me to enjoy things while I
am able to.

Right now, the sun is shining, I am meeting some friends for lunch. Good
food, good booze, good company. Life is good. I have no particular desire to
end it. But if I felt the Freedom which allows to me live this life was
threatened I would gladly put it on the line. Part of that Freedom is
maintaining the right of Free Speech, without which democracy cannot
function.

And that's where we came in...<G>

Pete.

"Give me Liberty or give me Death."  - Patrick Henry -






 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT The ghost of Patrick Henry, was: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T00:12:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3E2EDA.15A1B51C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <z$1iREAMrYP8EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3c3e1d9e_9@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> 5. I believe there is no life after death and I have no problem with that.
> It has been one of the things that has prompted me to enjoy things while I
> am able to.
>

I don't have a problem with that either. You believe what you believe and don't
shove it down other's throats. See if I've got my logic right :-

Table 1 :
----------------------------------------
There is a God                            y    -
There's no such thing as God        -    y
----------------------------------------
Go to Table 2                              x    -
Go to Table 3                               -   x
-----------------------------------------

Table 2 -  There is a God
-----------------------------------------
Pete is there a God ?                n    -
Jimmy is there a God ?             -    y
-----------------------------------------
The other Pete responds           x    -
"Why should I let you in you
SOB. You don't believe in the
boss !".

St. Pete says "Let's check            -    x
your record and see if we
should let you in".
--------------------------------------------

Table 3 - There is no such thing as God
----------------------------------------
Pete is there a God ?                n    -
Jimmy is there a God ?             -    y
-----------------------------------------
Who cares ?                            x    x
-----------------------------------------

If Table 2 works out and I snuff it before you do, I'll try and pay you a visit.
You'll know I've been around. I'll leave a copy of the latest J4 Agenda :-

    Item 1. - Revision # 1,876 to the Draft ( formerly COBOL 2002)

On advisement from Stephen, I'll make bloody sure I don't show up in the guise
of a sheep - that would be fraught with danger <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT The ghost of Patrick Henry, was: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T15:24:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1mvsd$knp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <z$1iREAMrYP8EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3c3e1d9e_9@Usenet.com> <3C3E2EDA.15A1B51C@shaw.ca>`

```

On 10-Jan-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> If Table 2 works out and I snuff it before you do, I'll try and pay you a
> visit.
> You'll know I've been around. I'll leave a copy of the latest J4 Agenda :-

Not necessarily.  Just because there is a God doesn't mean that "you" will
be around.  And believers in an afterlife than not believe that you won't be
happy with what you find.  (there is no majority belief on who will be
saved).   Meanwhile, if you happen to believe in Hell, you are stuck
worshiping a being that allows a vast majority of His children to be
tortured forever and ever without hope of parole.   Which would make any
caring person miserable beyond belief.

Admittedly, there are some people who believe in heaven and death (Jehovah's
witnesses for instance).  They can look forward to reward without hating
everybody else.
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> I don't have a problem with that either. You believe what you believe and don't
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> There's no such thing as God        -    y

That is a very narrow view, almost to the point of saying 'my god is so
much better than everyone else's god(s) that I'll ignore their existence
and/or non-existence'.

Of course religionists like to simplify these things to there being only
two options: '_My_ religion' or 'evil devil-worhipping atheistic
anarchist witches who will burn in hell for all eternity'.  It makes it
so much easier to classify people if there are only two boxes.

Anyway there is no question about the existence of 'gods'.  Hirohito was
as much a God to his people as anyone else's God(s) are to others.  The
only actual question is the nature of these God(s) and whther these have
any significance beyond myths and legends.
```

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>I seem to have vaque recollections of that book.  The basic theory revolved
>about the earth flipping into new poles due to gyroscopic effect, 

I'm not sure that Velikovsky actually gave a mechanism for change in
the axis of rotation of the Earth, except to say that there may be
"electromagnetic forces" at play, since he doubted that gravitation
forces alone could account for the changes.

Velikovsky published a paper on electromagnetic forces and the cosmos
in a paper: "Cosmos Without Gravitation", online at
http://www.varchive.org/ce/cosmos.htm



Ian Tresman
Society for Interdisciplinary Studies
www.knowledge.co.uk/sis/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT The ghost of Patrick Henry, was: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T20:00:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4095C8.D0489BB3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <z$1iREAMrYP8EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3c3e1d9e_9@Usenet.com> <3C3E2EDA.15A1B51C@shaw.ca> <3C3F0972.C8B91C45@Azonic.co.nz> <1Et%7.244983$WW.13199508@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3c3fbc31_7@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> If memory serves, it was by Anton(?) Zelikovsky and postulated the collisons
> between the Earth and Venus and the various "flippings" of the Earth upon
> its axis due to other impacts (and confirmed by the magnetic polarity in
> various rock strata.)

The changes to the magnetic polarity do _not_ confirm that the Earth
'flipped' on its axis due to impacts or any other cause.  The reverses
take place over hundreds or thousands of years and are understood to
show a gradual decay and change, nothing like what an impact or other
'flip' would do.  There is also no evidence of any impact at all or any
flip.

The changes of polarity may indicate that the layers of the core that
are molten iron may have convection currents, just as the magma does,
and these change the generation of magnetic field.

Velikovsky's theories were not accepted at the time and 50 years of
additional knowledge of the inner workings of the Earth have shown them
to be even less credible.  For example continental drift was not
understood or accepted when Worlds in Collision was written yet it was
known that Antartica had once been tropical, thus 'flipping' may have
been a reason.
```

```
I

t's tough   to connect on the same issue so to elaborate as best I can from
memory
the part that interested me most
about the book
was the corralation between events, and conclusions
by groups in different parts of the globe.  What I got out of the book is
that given
some event, different groups will explain it in different ways.  I believe
this ties in with
the way religion was defined in different ways. Then much of what was
concluded
was close to the same thing and accounts for some of the similarity of
"gosples". Or today
how we are made to feel sad or glad by the conditions of things "over
there":. I should
add that time in the CBI gave me some broading besides in my beam.  It
certainly was not Indiana.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT The ghost of Patrick Henry, was: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-13T19:02:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C41D9CF.CD59AE74@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <z$1iREAMrYP8EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3c3e1d9e_9@Usenet.com> <3C3E2EDA.15A1B51C@shaw.ca> <3C3F0972.C8B91C45@Azonic.co.nz> <1Et%7.244983$WW.13199508@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3c3fbc31_7@Usenet.com> <3C4095C8.D0489BB3@Azonic.co.nz> <dh208.259745$WW.13385481@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
warren.simmons wrote:

> that given
> some event, different groups will explain it in different ways.  

While it is true that a single event may lead to multiple different
explanations, reversing this, as Velikofsky does, that different
explanations indicate a single event is not valid deduction, interesting
though it may be.

> I believe this ties in with
> the way religion was defined in different ways. 

Presumably you are starting with the assumption that there is one basis
of religion (yours perhaps) and this is an explanation of why the others
got it wrong.

Variations in 'flood stories', for example, are much more likely to be
the result of many different local floods, as in fact can be seen from
time to time even today, other possible explanations are also being
discovered.  It may be that the filling of the Black Sea may be the
basis of the story of Noah rather than some unsupported 'collision' for
which there is no mechanism by which it could have happened.  

I think that WIC does show some interesting evidence:- about how people
will show irrational belief in stories that have been made up to suit
mythology and are against the evidence.  People believe what they want
to believe in spite of the evidence.

The book also showed how even scientists can go on witch hunts and how
the media will misrepresent anything and everything to create controvasy
and support their circulation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT The ghost of Patrick Henry, was: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Ian Tresman <ian@knowledge.couk>
- **Date:** 2002-01-13T23:15:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d9444uk5750a9rncob09n7s3lnk8tjhq62@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <z$1iREAMrYP8EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3c3e1d9e_9@Usenet.com> <3C3E2EDA.15A1B51C@shaw.ca> <3C3F0972.C8B91C45@Azonic.co.nz> <1Et%7.244983$WW.13199508@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3c3fbc31_7@Usenet.com> <3C4095C8.D0489BB3@Azonic.co.nz> <dh208.259745$WW.13385481@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3C41D9CF.CD59AE74@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>While it is true that a single event may lead to multiple different
>explanations, reversing this, as Velikofsky does, that different
>explanations indicate a single event is not valid deduction, interesting
>though it may be.

Although it is not a valid deduction (ie incorrect reasoning), that
does not necessarily invalidate the supposition. And indeed, there
would be every reason to dismiss several account of a spectacular
event, by putting it down to coincidence, or migration, or whatever.

However, there comes a stage when the number of different stories,
from cultures all around the world, become overwhelming, that you
begin to wonder whether coincidence and migration can account for them
all.

And then when there are subtle difference in such stores which
migration does not account for, you wonder again. For example,
Velikovsky recounts the story of Joshua and the Sun standing still in
the sky. (Incidentally, Velikovsky does not take this literally, and
accepts that the original account may be exagerated). If this story
did migrate, we would expect something similar around the world.
Interesting, cultures on the opposite side of the world speak of a
time of an extended night.

>Variations in 'flood stories', for example, are much more likely to be
>the result of many different local floods, 

It is more likely that people are able to accept this explanation, but
I would suggest that in practice, local floods do not match the
magnitude and awesomeness of what cultures describe as "The Flood". 

>as in fact can be seen from
>time to time even today, other possible explanations are also being
>discovered.  It may be that the filling of the Black Sea may be the
>basis of the story of Noah rather than some unsupported 'collision' for
>which there is no mechanism by which it could have happened. 

That is a good suggestion, but unfortunately a Black Sea flood would
only affect Europe and Asia. Flood myths are more wide-ranging.

You are correct in saying that a collision is unsupported, but that
would assume that only a collision could cause a flood of such
magnitude. I would suggest that a near miss by a large watery body
could do the trick.

>I think that WIC does show some interesting evidence:- about how people
>will show irrational belief in stories that have been made up to suit
>mythology and are against the evidence. People believe what they want
>to believe in spite of the evidence.

You are absolutely correct; people will believe what they want to
believe. Not only the uneducated, but also academics who are trying to
defend their own research. 

>The book also showed how even scientists can go on witch hunts and how
>the media will misrepresent anything and everything to create controvasy
>and support their circulation.

Here here.


Ian Tresman
Society for Interdisciplinary Studies
www.knowledge.co.uk/sis/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT The ghost of Patrick Henry, was: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ian Tresman <ian@knowledge.couk>
- **Date:** 2002-01-13T23:15:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<58544u495ln2cf3nuagtu99uvnuoqe1r49@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5gI_7.29201$Sf2.268554@rwcrnsc52> <3c3b6c11_9@Usenet.com> <z$1iREAMrYP8EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3c3e1d9e_9@Usenet.com> <3C3E2EDA.15A1B51C@shaw.ca> <3C3F0972.C8B91C45@Azonic.co.nz> <1Et%7.244983$WW.13199508@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3c3fbc31_7@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I read it 30 years ago, Warren.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>various rock strata.)

The author was Immanuel Velikovsky, more about is book can be found at
www.knowledge.co.uk/velikovsky/

Many people have suggested that Velikovsky described the Earth
"flipping" due to impacts. In fact he only suggested that the Earth's
axis and rotation was affected by a near pass of a planetary sized
body passing close to the Earth.

Changes in magnetic polarity of rock on the sea bed neither confirm
Velivosky, nor confirm continental drift; but it is consistent with
both theories.


Ian Tresman
Society for Interdisciplinary Studies
www.knowledge.co.uk/sis/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT The ghost of Patrick Henry, was: religion and COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

```
thanks for the responses.....

Jim - I was not referring to Judson but a person in the above post
(alternatives to newsranger)

Peter - I am not against religion, just wondering why people have to
broadcast their beliefs on others at any chance. Say - if someone believed
in say UFO's and broadcast that belief they would be labeled a nut most
likely. I believe in God about as much as UFO's.....neither has been proven
to my satisfaction. People have to have 'faith'...me I like tangible proof.

Also - why do boxers (for example) always thank god for letting them beat
the you know what out of their opponent. Related thought, why does the loser
not blame god ?

Rufio......you sound a little defensive. But honestly, what have muslims
done for anyone lately ?     Muslim countries control much of the world oil
supply. They rake in billions and billions of dollars a year in revenue and
spend what on their own poor people in their own countries and send what aid
to those in time of crisis ?  From the top down, almost if not every muslim
country is run by people who either support or allow the preaching of a
brand of 'religion' where intolerance of others (non-muslims and Americans
in particular - even our 'friends' the Saudi's) is spewed from all levels of
government and media.
Muslims make enough money so no one should be poor - where does the money go
? There are so few Saudis for example, compared to the income - why don't
they are drive Mercedes ?   Ditto for Iran, Iraq (yes Iraq, they still sell
billions in oil for food) and the rest. Even in our own state of Alaska
where a very small amount of oil is drilled, residents get free money from
the state every year. As for your 4% that do not believe in god - I can't
dispute or agree but how many of the 96% believe in UFO's too ?   Same
theory 'faith'....

Steve, I can't disagree that wars are the result of lust and greed. They
just get disguised as some kind of religious jihad (muslims) or anti-jew
tirade (Hitler/KKK).

have a good day all of you - I am going back to planet Sarcastica on my UFO
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Religion 2

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T16:01:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VPZ_7.590284$8q.47597276@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<yVL_7.2838$fG.8626@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <u3oj364sqq12aa@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net>
>
> Look around some day.......
>
> Or, read the eyewitness accounts of Jesus rising from the dead in the
> Bible.

Uh, there are no eyewitness accounts. No one saw Jesus rising from the dead.
And the reports of witnesses who saw Him post-dead (so to speak) are
recorded anonymously (the Gospels are not self-identifying as to who wrote
them). Of course if we agree there was evidence that Jesus WAS dead then
subsequently alive, implicit is the notion that he miraculously came back to
life, but, still, there were no eyewitness accounts (Jesus could be dead,
for example, and his twin made a live appearance).

Further, the Old Testament has reports of at least one person who did not
die, so that trick is no big deal. Of the thirty-nine miracles attributed to
Jesus, all had been performed before. Point is, proof of miracles or rising
from the dead, etc., are not proof of divinity. God, I'm told, does not
expect us to be able to distinguish between miracles and magic.
```

```
What it really boils down to is faith in something unseen, plain and simple.
```

```
I guess each person has to decide what they consider a reliable
source.  I don't really want to argue how the New Testament was
written, but there is growing evidence that it was written when it
says it was.  There are documents (1st century documents) from very
early church leaders that quote various parts of gospels and letters,
which would seem to indicate that the books were already written.

Speaking of believing witnesses, there are people today who don't
believe the holocaust occurred, and that is despite having living
eyewitnesses.  So people choose to believe what they want.
```

```
What I meant was they written within the lifetime of the authors -
Matthew, Mark, & John.  (Luke wrote from second hand accounts and my
earlier statement mentioned eyewitnesses).  John wrote his account
later than Matthew and Mark, but all were written 55-90AD.

Look, I shouldn't have jumped in this thread, so I am going to jump
out.  People who study this for their entire life can't agree with
each other, so I doubt anything will be resolved by a bunch of COBOL
programmers.  I've read books that lay out great evidence for the
authenticity of the Bible, and then I've read tons of stuff by people
who rebut the evidence, and then there's the answers to the rebuttals,
etc.  I've chosen the side I believe but I continue to read much of
the atheist, naturalist, and humanist side of things.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<iL3%7.208666$m05.17889157@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<yVL_7.2838$fG.8626@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <u3oj364sqq12aa@corp.supernews.com> <Ha__7.14307$DG5.129218@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <u3ov0vfn3a3jda@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net> wrote in message
news:u3ov0vfn3a3jda@corp.supernews.com...
> I guess each person has to decide what they consider a reliable
> source.  I don't really want to argue how the New Testament was
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> which would seem to indicate that the books were already written.

Uh, there is ONE fragment (about 2" square) from the book of John that
predates the fourth century. There are no other fragments of the New
Testament prior to that. Dating of the writing of the New Testament is based
on linguistic and holographic styles, not physical manuscripts. Mark is the
earliest (but of course Mark was the travelling companion of Peter and never
met Jesus himself). The books of Matthew and Luke were written later than
Mark and the authors of these two books had the Mark document in front of
them when they wrote. (The also had another document - called "Q" (for
'quella' - German for 'lost' as an additional reference.) John stands alone
and may have been written as late as A.D. 135.

There is nothing wrong with an oral tradition that was later redacted.
```

```
SkippyPB wrote:

> Close, but no cigar.  "For those who believe, no proof is necessary.
> For those who disbelieve, no amount of proof is sufficient." - St.
> Ignatius of Loyola.  Can't believe a rabbi would steal a Christian
> saint's quote!

I think is is quite possible Ignatius stole the quote from some Rabbi.

And the Rabbi stole it from a Druid.   And the Druid stole it from
a ...

> >Let's get back to discussing COBOL.   Did Grace Murray Hopper really
> >exist when we think she did?   Was CODASYL really the source of our
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> However, ALTER was definitely penned by Lucifer.

I once took on a contract at a shop where the programming manager
had died suddenly and left a huge library of COBOL code that needed
to be updated.   It was riddled with ALTER statements.   Only he knew
what was supposed to happen under any peculiar set of circumstances.
No documentation.    No explanation within the code. I was becoming
more and more convinced that I was being punished for my sinful ways
while still alive.   I began to fantasize that the deceased programmer
had sold his soul to the proverbial Devil after leaving behind this
Satanic tangle of code, more reminiscent of a Gordian Knot than of
a structure program.

Richard Riehle


>
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve
```

### question

- **From:** "M turner" <miturner70@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T02:52:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bgO_7.35626$LQ1.11132605@news2.nash1.tn.home.com>`

```
I have an output field with a pic x(8) value.  I need to find the best way
to check the 7th or 8th character by itself to see if it is a certain value.
How do i set up to do this? if you have a good idea please email to me at
miturner70@hotmail.com
```

#### ↳ Re: question

- **From:** "Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T04:26:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yDP_7.9931$DG5.96158@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bgO_7.35626$LQ1.11132605@news2.nash1.tn.home.com>`

```

you must be new to programming....... so here goes....

working-storage section.
01  ws-fieldname.
      03  filler     pic x(6).
      03  ws-field1    pic x.
      03  ws-field2    pic x.
procedure division.
move your-field to ws-fieldname.
if ws-field1 = "V"  next sentence
if ws-field2 = "v" next sentence.....yada yada yada
or perhaps
if ws-field1 = x"30" next sentence   if you need to look for hex 30  (zero)
for example


of course you could also make a pic x(1) field occurring 8 times and inspect
in a table
you could also just use the inspect tallying trick too
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T14:18:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1hjfm$2et$1@ins22.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<bgO_7.35626$LQ1.11132605@news2.nash1.tn.home.com> <yDP_7.9931$DG5.96158@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <xJP_7.3764$JH4.498062370@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <hVQ_7.32575$Sf2.292887@rwcrnsc52>`

```
I have always told my students that there are three elements to learning 
to write a program:

1.  Learn to use the compiler, linker, etc.

This is, of course, the easiest part.  It will take single lecture - with 
later followups on compiling multiple compilation units and using the 
debugger, etc.

2.  Learn the syntax

This is a bit more difficult - but with most languages haveing between 50 
and 100 keywords, it is not too difficult.  If this was all there was to 
programming, I could almost hand out a list of keywords on the first day 
of class and have a memory test on the last day.

3.  Learn to solve problems

This is the most important part.   It is why it takes two to three 
semester to adequately cover a students first language.  No employer would 
allow an employee 1-1/2 years to learn a new language.  But, if the tools 
and skills needed for problem solving are learned well (and properly) they 
provide the background needed to learn other languages relatively quickly.

In practice, at least at my institution, this means that students will 
have three semesters to learn Java and programming skills [:(].  But they 
have the File Structures (i.e. COBOL) course after having two semesters of 
Java.  During their junior or senior year they have a programming 
languages course.  One of the requirements for this course is to prepare a 
manual (20+ pages) and three hours of instruction on a language they have 
not previous seen.  Students in the class then must use these language as 
part of their final exam.  This fall, languages included APL, Prolog, and 
ADA.  Final exam questions included

	Develop a HI-LOW game in APL and ADA
	Create a sin table using the Taylor series in ADA
	Report the average of all elements in a PROLOG list

There were two or three others - I don't remember them sitting here.  But 
the point is that students have moved from having 1-1/2 years of 
instruction to having 3 hours of formal instruction in a language - and be 
able to produce working (useful ?) code.  

COBOL is easy - programming is not.  

Floyd Johnson
```

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:

> I have always told my students that there are three elements to learning
> to write a program:
>
> 1.  Learn to use the compiler, linker, etc. <snip>

> 2.  Learn the syntax <snip>

> 3.  Learn to solve problems <snip>

As you've indicated, the real problem is the lack of time you are allowed to cover
these elements or a language. Programming is an end-tool. What really concerns me
now that the title Systems Analyst is defunct - exactly where do upcoming students
learn to design comprehensive, and truly, 'user-friendly' business applications,
before even starting to tap out code on a keyboard ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T19:17:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1i50i$lbm$1@ins22.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<bgO_7.35626$LQ1.11132605@news2.nash1.tn.home.com> <yDP_7.9931$DG5.96158@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <xJP_7.3764$JH4.498062370@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <hVQ_7.32575$Sf2.292887@rwcrnsc52> <a1hjfm$2et$1@ins22.netins.net> <3C3C8723.6A535AD3@shaw.ca>`

```
Good questions.  This is the last year that we are including a systems 
analysis course in the curriculum.  Beginning next year, this course is 
being replaced by "Human-Computer Interaction".  The focus will will be 
designing I/O that will interact with the user.  Though there will be a 
unit on general design, the focus will be on I/O issues.  There is some 
instruction on program design in entry level programming courses (now 
Java, but always subject to change).  

"Computer Science" education seems to be in the midst of change.  When I 
first started teaching the general advice is to use different languages in 
different classes as appropriate (e.g. File Structures in COBOL, Graphics 
in C++, Numerical Analysis in FORTRAN, etc.).  There are some, though I am 
not sure how many, that now advocate using the same language throughout 
the curriculum.  The single exception being Programming Language.  I don't 
know how this will shake out in the future.

Floyd Johnson 

===========================================================================

James J. Gavan (jjgavan@shaw.ca) wrote:


: Floyd Johnson wrote:

: > I have always told my students that there are three elements to learning
: > to write a program:
: >
: > 1.  Learn to use the compiler, linker, etc. <snip>

: > 2.  Learn the syntax <snip>

: > 3.  Learn to solve problems <snip>

: As you've indicated, the real problem is the lack of time you are allowed to cover
: these elements or a language. Programming is an end-tool. What really concerns me
: now that the title Systems Analyst is defunct - exactly where do upcoming students
: learn to design comprehensive, and truly, 'user-friendly' business applications,
: before even starting to tap out code on a keyboard ?

: Jimmy
```

```
think they would be moving closer together.

covered without remote abstract mathematical examples. Why not real world
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3CF959.33FDFD92@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bgO_7.35626$LQ1.11132605@news2.nash1.tn.home.com> <yDP_7.9931$DG5.96158@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <xJP_7.3764$JH4.498062370@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <hVQ_7.32575$Sf2.292887@rwcrnsc52> <a1hjfm$2et$1@ins22.netins.net> <3C3C8723.6A535AD3@shaw.ca> <3c3cc51f_9@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3C3C8723.6A535AD3@shaw.ca...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Pete,

You make a further good point. Within one institution the CS and Commerce
faculties should be aligned, ideally using a business model which can be used
by both faculties. Although I may not know specifics, I'm aware that B.Coms
certainly take computer courses as part of their curriculum.

So the following is food for thought for Floyd (and friends in academia).  I
most certainly am not going to detract from either Thane's (TYCI24H) or Will
Price's (EofOOC). Both are excellent and naturally to get the ideas across,
deal in modules.
What's missing is a merging of the various themes into one real-world
application.

A good example (and this is OO of course) would be the appendix in Ed
Arranga's book dealing with an in-house library application. (For Gawd's sake,
don't latch on to the ATM model that J4 and others use !). As an example this
particular application would allow introduction to design, Accept/Display, and
file handling - although data files are W/S Tables in this particular
reference.

The major stage would be a non-OO approach, introducing 3-tier systems. In
Floyd's academy, regardless of language being taught, it  could be developed
by instructors, and paralleled in each language. Of course, if somebody is
taking Java or C++ they steal a jump on COBOL because they in effect can't
work without GUIs.  For a realistic file system both these require ESQL -
meanwhile on the COBOL course, initially COBOL file handling is taught;
subsequently introduce ESQL. Certainly the same model used in several
languages is going to cause some positive reaction from students as to the
merits or demerits of the respective languages they are learning.

Logically the COBOL course should introduce OO - taking the same business
model and switching it from non-OO to OO, and a GUI tool of choice.

Meanwhile our friends in the Commerce faculty want a model that they can play
with and query. You can get them into SQL/ESQL using the same model DB to
raise queries and produce statistics.

Give it a thought Floyd - and your first hurdle will be the instructors for
the computer group.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck7%7.28282$os5.2641032@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<bgO_7.35626$LQ1.11132605@news2.nash1.tn.home.com> <yDP_7.9931$DG5.96158@rwcrnsc53.ops.asp.att.net> <xJP_7.3764$JH4.498062370@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <hVQ_7.32575$Sf2.292887@rwcrnsc52> <a1hjfm$2et$1@ins22.netins.net> <3C3C8723.6A535AD3@shaw.ca> <3c3cc51f_9@Usenet.com> <3C3CF959.33FDFD92@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C3CF959.33FDFD92@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >

<big snipage>

I am reminded of a good friend, a writer with a dozen books under his belt.
We were discussing writing, and a young grad, dealing with ejection slips,
asked him how he learnt the ropes.

He said "Any idiot can learn to write.  Most have mastered it by the time
they get to high school.  What most people never master is learning WHAT to
write. The only way to do that is to write for a few years. After about
twenty, you start to get a feel for it."

I do not think you can "teach" someone to construct large systems.  You can
teach them technique, and you can teach them methodology.  However, learning
to write a large system from scratch takes practice, and I do not see how
you can possibly get that in an student capacity.  There is simply not the
time for a student to build a 25 or 30 thousand line application, and it is
all just academic until you actually roll up your sleeves and do it.  In
fact, I would venture you have not really gotten there until you have
maintained it for the first 35 or 40 users. It takes that long for reality
to rear it's ugly head.

Donald
```

```
> I do not think you can "teach" someone to construct large systems.  You can
> teach them technique, and you can teach them methodology.  However, learning
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don,

Oh I agree - you can't teach systems design, as opposed to programming - only
the principles and the concept that program 'units' are part of the 'whole'. But
it is interesting to note that Floyd's school is considering User Interaction as
a topic, with the intent of making screens intuitive and full and comprehensive
validity checks - I'm guessing, but I bet that's where he is going.

Quite correctly you would need a MONSTER application to cover everything.
But at the back of my mind is Ed Arranga's Library application - it is not big
but it does lend itself to COBOL file handling and/or DBs - not too many files
or tables, incorporates things like date checks and money - related to late
fines. Further you could spin-off reports for 'Library members' or inventory
checks on books.

It represents an application which could be designed in non-OO and obviously
adapts to OO.

So now HELP WANTED - who has any ideas ?

1. I have a copy of Ed's book - but be damned if I'm going to tear the relevant
pages out of mine. I'll see if he has a spare copy.
2. Nor am I going to laboriously type it all in line by line.
3. Given that I can get a copy of the pages  - Can they be scanned so that the
images can finish up in text files, i.e. COBOL source files ?

Jimmy
```

```
"M turner" <miturner70@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bgO_7.35626$LQ1.11132605@news2.nash1.tn.home.com...
> I have an output field with a pic x(8) value.  I need to find the best way
> to check the 7th or 8th character by itself to see if it is a certain
value.
> How do i set up to do this? if you have a good idea please email to me at
> miturner70@hotmail.com
>
>

The easiest way is with reference modification.  The 7th character of

     02 this-field    picture x(8).
is
     this-field (7:1).  Characters 5 and 6 are this-field (5:2), and so on.
```

```
You really should invest in a couple of reference books (over a certain
percentage of income they are even tax deductible)

I like:
COBOL Unleashed (for the more advanced stuff)

tutorials
```

<a id="t-7e8b309c4f"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Cobol SQL Access — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Fujitsu Cobol SQL Access

- **From:** Binew.@web.de (Binew)
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T11:12:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a184a886.0201091112.2b16051e@posting.google.com>`

```
Ich versuche mit Cobol auf eine Accessdatenbank zuzugreifen.
Es soll ein bestimmter Wert aus der Datenbank gesucht werden.
Codiere ich die Where-Bedingung fest ein, bekomm e ich das richtige
Ergebnis, verwende ich jedoch eine Variable, kommt kein Ergebnis. Wer
kann mir helfe?
Hier der Code-Teil:
001260     Move 'DEM' To Waehr
001270
001280     EXEC SQL
001300       SELECT Kurs INTO :Kurse
001310
001320       FROM KURSTAB
001360*       WHERE Waehrung = 'DEM'
001370       WHERE Waehrung = Waehr
001380
001390       END-EXEC.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol SQL Access

- **From:** pvieira@emporsoft.pt (Paulo Vieira)
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T01:09:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5b8d7c7.0201100109.e7a280f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a184a886.0201091112.2b16051e@posting.google.com>`

```
Binew.@web.de (Binew) wrote in message news:<a184a886.0201091112.2b16051e@posting.google.com>...
> Ich versuche mit Cobol auf eine Accessdatenbank zuzugreifen.
> Es soll ein bestimmter Wert aus der Datenbank gesucht werden.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 001390       END-EXEC.

Hi,
Apart from the COBOL snip, I couldn't understand a single word.
However, I assume you are geting errors with the above, which works
with the commented line but not with the uncommented one. Remember
that all host variables referenced inside a "EXEC SQL" - "END-EXEC"
must be "qualified" with ":". That is true even for "Waehr" which, at
line 1370 should be :Waehr.

regards,
Paulo Vieira
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol SQL Access

- **From:** "pwmeister" <peter.meister@tietoenator-nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T16:20:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1mvsq$o20$1@news1.enator.se>`
- **References:** `<a184a886.0201091112.2b16051e@posting.google.com> <b5b8d7c7.0201100109.e7a280f@posting.google.com>`

```
leider spreche/schreibt ich nicht zu gut.  H�ffentlich gehts gut mit
english?

For some pre-compilers (such as COBOL on AS400) sql vars must be
declared in  BEGIN DECLARE - END DECLARE block:

    EXEC SQL
           BEGIN DECLARE SECTION
    END-EXEC.
01  Waehr           PIC X(3)
    EXEC SQL
           END DECLARE SECTION
    END-EXEC.

Peter Meister
TietoEnator Schweden
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol SQL Access

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T17:12:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3fb883_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a184a886.0201091112.2b16051e@posting.google.com>`

```
Das "Host Variable" "Waehr", muss richtig definiert werden, zwischen BEGIN
DECLARE... und END DECLARE satze.

Ausserdem, wenn dieses variable ist im SQL benutzt (das heiss, zwischen EXEC
SQL... END-EXEC) dann ist ein kolon notig...sowieso, ":Waehr"

In COBOL, als normales "dataname", das kolon is nicht notig und nicht
benutzen werden mussen.

Dafur, die losung:

> 001370       WHERE Waehrung = Waehr

bekommt

> 001370       WHERE Waehrung = :Waehr

...und dann kommt alles in ordung.

To reach a wider audience, and therefore have a greater chance of getting a
solution, please post in English to this forum.

Your English is unlikely to be any worse than my German <G>...

Pete.





Wenn mann
Binew <Binew.@web.de> wrote in message
news:a184a886.0201091112.2b16051e@posting.google.com...
> Ich versuche mit Cobol auf eine Accessdatenbank zuzugreifen.
> Es soll ein bestimmter Wert aus der Datenbank gesucht werden.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
```

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Administrivia (sort-of)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T13:49:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1i6sd$mvc$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As your friendly NON-moderator of this group, may I
  Remind?
   Suggest?
     Request?

That those of you starting *OR* responding to "off-topic" threads place

  OT

at the beginning of the subject line of your posts.

NOTE: there are technical reasons that using
  OT:

is *not* as good as simply starting the subject with "OT " (space following
OT).

As this is a non-moderated group, anyone MAY post whatever they want.
However *some* readers prefer having non-COBOL related posts "identified" by
the subject line.  No one will/does "monitor" borderline issues (such as
"how to do JCL for ..." or "what does Windows expect for ..."?) but issues
of religion, belief structures, humorous (non-COBOL) stories, etc USUALLY
are best served by an "OT " at the beginning of the subject line.
```

```
Here is the situation:

My company will soon be installing SHA-1 encryptionon an IBM OS/390
mainframe.  Since this is totally uncharted territory for us, I was
wondering if there was anyone out there who has experience calling this
encryption technique via EasyTrieve?  We will be using the CSNBOWH1 one-way
hash generate.

I have gone through the IBM OS/390 V2R10.0 ICSF Application Programmer's
Guide and have yet to find enough information.  If there is someone out
there that can help, please let me know at:

rpchristina@home.com
    or
robchris@yahoo.com

Thanks in advance!

Rob!
```

```
cleanly if you don't like it.  A license is $35 US.  A great tool for
legacy code.  I believe it has an FTP hook in it but it may be
designed for Unix - something you can inquire about and expect an
answer from them.   A well written, award winning programmers editor -
and inexpensive!

www.ultraedit.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I find a Windows Editor that hooks into the AS/400 to edit Cobol Code?

- **From:** "Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T07:16:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u3r1f5ge3mfn65@corp.supernews.com>`
