```
"Axel Brass" <AxelBrass@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:_%9%7.162111$pa1.48298508@news3.rdc1.on.home.com...
> Hi,
>
> I've been looking everywhere for a Windows Editor that hooks into
the AS/400
> to edit Cobol Code?
>
> Preferrably hooking in via ftp.
>
> Or even just a nice Cobol editor that runs on the Windows
environment.

For more years than I care to remember, I have used MultiEdit
(www.multiedit.com).  I've used it for PC development and I have used
it for AS/400 development by sending the source back and forth with
FTP macros.  (You do lose the change dates off to the right of the
source when you upload).

A few years ago, I finally moved to NetExpress, so I use it's built in
editor on the PC, and I also decided to switch to Code/400 which works
great on 400 code (COBOL and RPG).  It took a little work to get it
setup but it works great when your not connected and then when you are
connected it is still great.  (I work on a notebook that I take with
me to various clients).  Getting Code/400 is tricky.  It used to be
available for download in a 'test' version, but now it comes with a
whole AS/400 set of development tools.

(Code/400 has a nice cross reference feature that brings up a list of
either field or paragraph names.  Then you can click on the list and
you go right to that point in your source code.  For big programs it
is a huge help).

Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I find a Windows Editor that hooks into the AS/400 to edit Cobol Code?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T14:01:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1k6m0$32p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<_%9%7.162111$pa1.48298508@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I used Multi-Edit since before Windows and have upgraded through version 8.
But I haven't been as satisfied with it under Windows as I was under DOS. 
(I even bought an add-on that included a dictionary for Paradox)

Lately I have been using the much cheaper UltraEdit 32.  I like it much
better.  I use it for HTML, XML and even VXML.  I use it for binary editing.
 I haven't done any PC editing of CoBOL since I got it.

It is much easier to edit on the Unix machine via its internal FTP than
Multi-Edit is.  I don't need to set up projects and such.  Maybe I stopped
using Multi-Edit because it is project based and I never learned how to use
it that way.
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I find a Windows Editor that hooks into the AS/400 to edit Cobol Code?

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T19:06:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3f3662.1529599@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<_%9%7.162111$pa1.48298508@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
"Axel Brass" <AxelBrass@hotmail.com> wrote:

>I've been looking everywhere for a Windows Editor that hooks into the AS/400
>to edit Cobol Code?
>Preferrably hooking in via ftp.
>Or even just a nice Cobol editor that runs on the Windows environment.

Try Vim (vi improved) - it's available as charityware on almost every
system in texmode and GUI versions. It has syntax highlighting for
COBOL (and hundreds of other file types), folding, indenting, a mighty
diff feature,...

Vim can access files via ftp and serveral other network protocols.

Kind regards

Sven
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I find a Windows Editor that hooks into the AS/400 to edit Cobol Code?

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)

Sven

<a id="t-669d9739eb"></a>
## Thread: Net Express ESQL Busy Bug — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Net Express ESQL Busy Bug

- **From:** none@none.com (wildman)
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T05:59:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3d452c.2508835@news.earthlink.net>`

```

Using Net Express 3.1 COBOL ESQL to NT Sequel Server.  Windows 2000
client.

Getting frequent though inconsistent sqlcode -10000 connection busy.

Have a cursor (for read only) open in one program, then writing to a
different table in another program which bombs.

Using SET CONCURRENCY 'allow dirty read'  before opening the cursor
and set the directive SQL(THREAD=ISOLATE) for the project.  No luck.


Sometimes the writes work, sometimes they fail.  Any idea what is
happening? 
 
Thks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express ESQL Busy Bug

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T06:58:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3D3C71.E8239CB@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3c3d452c.2508835@news.earthlink.net>`

```


wildman wrote:

> Using Net Express 3.1 COBOL ESQL to NT Sequel Server.  Windows 2000
> client.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thks.

I'm using N/E with the ESQL Assistant, but I'm too much of a novice to
make a definitive statement. I don't know the answer to this - but can
you have a Cursor open when attempting to (Exit current Program ?) and
go into another ( to  build your fresh table ?). It seems to me you
would lose reference to the cursor when you EXIT a program. (an
afterthought - can you also lose CONNECTION switching from one program
to another ?).

On that assumption, I've done it the following way using OO - I want to
build either a Listbox or DropLists contents, sorted :-

EditClients   ----> invokes ClientsDBI -------> invokes ClientsTable

1. From the Edit invoke the ClientsDBI(Data Base Interface) to
createATable
2. The DBI passes itself as a 'handle' (object reference) to the Clients
Table (the DBI or lnk-Caller becomes os-DBI in the ClientsTable)
3. The DBI now invokes ClientsTable to SELECT DISTINCT with a cursor
4. The method in the ClientsTable :-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "makeClientCollection".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
copy "clilistrec.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ls==.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-PrimeKey.
   05 lnk-ClientNumber               pic x(03).
   05 lnk-FacilityNumber             pic x(02).
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using     lnk-PrimeKey
                   returning lnk-SqlResult.

set ResultOK to true
initialize ls-CliListrecord
invoke os-SqlConnection "DBconnect"
       using ws-dbID returning lnk-SqlResult

if TableError
   EXIT METHOD
End-if

EXEC SQL
 DECLARE CSR29 CURSOR FOR SELECT
        `A`.`CliId`
       ,`A`.`FacId`
       ,`A`.`Shortname`
   FROM `Clients` A
  WHERE ( `A`.`FacId` = '00' )
END-EXEC
EXEC SQL OPEN CSR29 END-EXEC

PERFORM UNTIL SQLSTATE <> '00000'
  EXEC SQL
  FETCH CSR29 INTO
      :Clients-CliId:Clients-CliId-NULL
     ,:Clients-FacId:Clients-FacId-NULL
     ,:Clients-Shortname:Clients-Shortname-NULL
  END-EXEC

   Evaluate true

      when SQLSTATE = "00000"
        move Clients-CliID     to Cli-ID
        move Clients-FacID     to Fac-ID
        move Clients-shortname to Cli-ShortName
        invoke os-DBI "newListRecord" using ls-CliListRecord

        *> Note : above data is returned to os-DBI to add
        *> the appropriate collection/dictionary entry

     when SQLSTATE = "02000"               *> no more rows
          EXIT PERFORM
     when other
          move Clients-CliID to ws-ErrorID(1:3)
          move Clients-FacID to ws-ErrorID(4:2)
          invoke self "setErrorMessage"
          set TableError to true
          EXIT METHOD
   End-evaluate
END-PERFORM
EXEC SQL CLOSE CSR29 END-EXEC

End Method "makeClientCollection".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

The majority of the code above is ESQL Assistant, generated. (I've
substituted SQLSTATE for SQLCODE).

See where I say "invoke os-DBI "newListRecord" using ls-CliListRecord ".
I'm passing it back to the DBI to a specific method (entry point if you
like) called
"newListRecord". This way I'm retaining the cursor position within the
method."makeClientCollection" until I specifically tell it to exit the
method as a result of  'No more Rows' or a Table Error.

Tell you what - even if my assumption above is wrong - it works out just
fine - because I intentionally have my collection/dictionary methods
held in the DBI, and confine the class(program) ClientTable logic to
accessing the DB  <G>

Jimmy
```

> client.

Hi,
I use Fujitsu, not Net Express, but the problem you are geting seems
to me that isn't related to COBOL, instead is a SQL Server (or it's
ODBC driver) issue.
While scrolling thru a cursor, the only operations you can do are
related to that cursor. Every time I need to do something like you
describe, I either open a second connection to the DataBase, or if the
dataset is small enough, fetch all the data to a WS table, close the
cursor and then carry on with the following operations.

regards,
Paulo Vieira
```

wrote:

>none@none.com (wildman) wrote in message news:<3c3d452c.2508835@news.earthlink.net>...
>> Using Net Express 3.1 COBOL ESQL to NT Sequel Server.  Windows 2000
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>Paulo Vieira


Thanks.  Actually, I found that the THREAD directive will create
individual logical connections for a single physical connection, but
the directive does not take effect when 'default result sets' are
returned by SQL.    I had set it to read-only which of course was the
default.  So I set a higher optimistic level then set the scrolloption
to fake read-only and it worked.   I open one connection, several
cursors and write to a dozen or so tables, including records I have in
a open cursor, (from another program) with no contention.   Even the
transaction rollback works.
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express ESQL Busy Bug

- **From:** "Randy Zimmerman" <rzimmerman@wi.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-14T20:32:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VfH08.215361$8w3.49706626@typhoon.kc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3c3d452c.2508835@news.earthlink.net>`

```
A SQLCODE of -10000 is a SQL Server ODBC driver error - if you lookup
sqlcode in the on-line NX docs, you'll see the following info:

-10000
(The first error message returned by the ODBC call SQLError after the
OpenESQL run-time module detected an error)

See your ODBC driver documentation, or other ODBC reference documents for
details of ODBC error messages. SQLSTATE will contain a unique code for each
ODBC error condition.

You'll then to go into the MS SQL Server on-line docs and look for "SQLSTATE
Messages" index entry and use the sqlstate that is returned with the error
to figure out what kind of SQL Server error you are getting.

Randy Zimmerman
Micro Focus

"wildman" <none@none.com> wrote in message
news:3c3d452c.2508835@news.earthlink.net...

```

Thanks Randy, I have since figured out the generic nature of a -10000
and used your knowledge base for help.   

What is giving me difficulty is THREAD=ISOLATE and CONCURRENCY used
with different cursor declarations...

EXEC SQL DECLARE cursor FOR SELECT DISTINCT (with a WHERE)

requires  THREAD=ISOLATE and a non default result set or CONCURRENCY
setting to avoid contention amoung subprograms.   However, (and this
is what confuses me) the opposite is required for a serial read on the
same table, for example

EXEC SQL DECLARE cursor FOR SELECT ...(with no where clause)

requires removing the CONCURRENCY setting, otherwise it complains
(with a -10000) that the feature is not implemented.  In both
contexts, updating via subprograms works.  

So, does a CURSOR SELECT and a CURSOR SELECT DISTINCT have different
default result set meaning in MS Sequel Server?   Do the SQL
environment rules change?   

I just loath wasting time trying to find something relevant in
Microsoft's doumentation - or worse, from their support.  I have the
application working, but the solution will make little sense to those
who will have to support it.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express ESQL Busy Bug

- **From:** "Randy Zimmerman" <rzimmerman@wi.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T22:06:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w%028.254828$8w3.59258287@typhoon.kc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3c3d452c.2508835@news.earthlink.net> <VfH08.215361$8w3.49706626@typhoon.kc.rr.com> <3c44df81.545579@news.earthlink.net>`

```
I ran your question by one of my contacts who is more knowledgeable on SQL
Server issues - his comments:

I think there's some confusion here between transaction isolation and
concurrency. There is no concurrency setting for 'allow dirty read', that's
a transaction isolation setting.
Are they running with MTS? When setting transaction isolation normally you
MUST do a commit or rollback IMMEDIATELY bore the 'set transaction
isolation'. However, with MTS managed transactions you can't use
commit/rollback in OpenESQL, so it hase to be done IMMEDIATELY after
connecting. It's always worth checking SQLCODE after a 'set transaction
isolation' to be sure you got what you wanted.

Now for the busy stuff...

The 'connection busy' happens when a firehose cursor (forward+readonly,
'client cursor' or 'default result set' in MS docs) is open and you attempt
to do something else. If you need to do other things while reading a cursor
with SQL Server, a scrollable cursor (static, keyset, dynamic) will do the
job, but is quite slow. The best choice when you don't need to update via
the cursor is fast forward + readonly. This gets you a 'server cursor'. It's
not as fast as a forward+readonly (great choice of terminology on MS part!),
but it does let you do other things.

A select distinct may well force a sort, so it may result in a non-default
result set, or it may cause the 'feature not implemented', though this is a
bit unlikely.

I'd suggest using putting the concurrency and scroll settings on the
declares to make it more obvious what's happening and use

        exec sql declare x fast forward read only cursor for select ...

if you don't need updates and

        exec sql declare x forward lock cursor for select ...

if you do need updates

You may have problems with lock concurrency on a select distinct, since if
it has done a sort it may be reading result from a temp table rather than
direct from the database.

Hope this helps.

Randy Zimmerman / Micro Focus

…[38 more quoted lines elided]…

each

"SQLSTATE
> >Messages" index entry and use the sqlstate that is returned with the
error
> >to figure out what kind of SQL Server error you are getting.
> >
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

I used  STATIC to fix the problem in the main program.  Still, a
second main program produces 'feature not implemented' and works as
expected without using STATIC - a contradiction of sorts.   Both main
programs use the same connect strategy and suite of subporgrams which
are database access modules ported from legacy.   The only difference
is a DISTINCT cursor verses a serial cursor SELECT.  I think I can
document this properly now.

>A select distinct may well force a sort, so it may result in a non-default
>result set, or it may cause the 'feature not implemented', though this is a
>bit unlikely.

This makes sense.  Reviewing the code, the SELECT DISTINCT all use
ORDER BY.  This seems to answer my question about the change in
behavior.  The serial SELECTs have no WHERE and no ORDER BY.

>
>I'd suggest using putting the concurrency and scroll settings on the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>        exec sql declare x forward lock cursor for select ...

I'll investigate this, time permitting.  The CONCURRENCY setting in
the main program seesm to have solved this (for distinct cursors).

>
>if you do need updates
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The application does no positioned updates with cursors, rather it
calls for an open cursor (distinct) in a READ module to drive
processing and calls other service programs to INSERT or UPDATE
explicitly using WHERE.  This works whether the writes occur to the
open cursor rows or to a different table.  Again, the same is done
with serial processing of tables.

>Hope this helps.

Sure does.  Thanks again.  I was talking to the Toronto office
yesterday and Ill use support if I run into this again during
deployment.  The application is server side only.

>

## Thread: Future of COBOL — 76 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Future of COBOL

- **From:** matt@greenroom.com.au (matt)
- **Date:** 2002-01-09T23:57:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com>`

```
COBOL has been in existence since 1959, its first 
standard in 1968 (i think). Sooo many corporations 
have been tied up in knots of <shudder> COBOL and 
have resisted the temptation (& cost) of porting to
a more modern system. It is inevitable that at some
point our world will become far to complex for such
a medieval system to stay in existence. Try writing
Web-Services with even the latest implementations, 
you'll find yourself having fantasies about PHP, Python,
ASP, Perl... etc.

My question is: how much longer will it last?
```

#### ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca

> COBOL has been in existence since 1959, its first 
> standard in 1968 (i think). Sooo many corporations 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> My question is: how much longer will it last?

Probably long after the Web-Services fantasies have expired from being
over hyped.

Try writing a complex business application as a Web Service app and 
you will find yourself having fantasies about COBOL.

Business applications have requirements not addressed by PHP Python 
ASP Perl (you forgot Java in your list... :-). IIRC when C++ was the 
"latest and greatest" the trolls were raising the same call only they 
referred to some other language.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

matt@greenroom.com.au (matt) wrote:

> COBOL has been in existence since 1959, its first
> standard in 1968 (i think). Sooo many corporations
> have been tied up in knots of <shudder> COBOL and

What makes you think they were 'tied up in knots' ?  Now it may be that
you would not cope with the scale and scope of corporate applications,
but the corporates do.

> have resisted the temptation (& cost) of porting to
> a more modern system. It is inevitable that at some
> point our world will become far to complex for such
> a medieval system to stay in existence. Try writing
> Web-Services with even the latest implementations,

I do write Web Services in Cobol.  It is easy to have them work and
interact consistently with the business applications.

> you'll find yourself having fantasies about PHP, Python,
> ASP, Perl... etc.

Nightmares more like.  The people who fantasies about these languages
are the newbie kiddies who fantasise that they can be used for anything
that would count as being a 'complex' system, or would be around in a
few years.

40 years ago there was Cobol and the 'computer science' people were
doing Algol.  30 years ago there was Cobol and CS was doing Pascal, 20
YA there was Cobol and CS was into C.  10 YA there was Cobol and CS was
doing C++.  Now there is Cobol and CS is doing Java (with side salads of
the toys: PHP, Python, ASP, Perl).  In 10 years time there will be Cobol
and CS will have something completely new to play with.

>
> My question is: how much longer will it last?

In 5 years time ASP will have been completely discarded, Perl will have
evolved to be unrecognisable and current programs rewritten or
discarded, PHP will be ignored and replaced by YAKHPKS (Yet Another
Kewell Home Page Kiddie Script).  Python may develop to be worthwhile
(it already is).

Cobol will still be Cobol and will still be running the businesses.
```

My information is that Trolls live under bridges, not rocks <G>.

But then I'm basing it on the Three Billy Goats Gruff and that's just a
fairy story...like C Structures being more elegant than COBOL data
descriptions.

You really SHOULD get into the new COBOL but I refuse to be drawn into
another discussion of when it will be "available"...<G>. Some vendors have
already implemented it and I have personally been using OO COBOL for over 2
years now so it really isn't too important when the Standard is officially
released. It has all the facilities of your beloved C, plus it is
maintainable.

However, my position is that NO language should be used exclusively.

We should always use the best tools for the job. Sometimes this is C or
Java, sometimes it is COBOL.

It is NEVER VB <only joking...>!!

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T07:33:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C467E66.CF258543@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-3oxvmFddyrmq@thishost> <3C414CC7.8EAC459@Azonic.co.nz> <c7fe88fd.0201142248.33f239c6@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0201150552.6faef5d7@posting.google.com> <c7fe88fd.0201160723.2c20ab04@posting.google.com>`

```
matt wrote:
> (is my ignorance still as clear as ever?)

Yep.

> Again, the tree is only one branch deep. The only solution is to add
> clumsy, slow 2 type prefixes. C's struct eliminates the problem almost
> completely. 

With C you do:

   struct datatype { char name[32]; char street[32]; ... } delivery;

            delivery.name
            delivery.street

With Cobol you do:

   01  Delivery.
       03  Name        PIC X(32).
       03  Street      PIC X(32).

            Name   OF Delivery
            Street OF Delivery

Please do come back when you have learned Cobol enough to make informed
comment.

> The other possible solution is a packaging system like
> that of ada and java. Ada has its records and java its classes as
> well. 

OO in Cobol has been available for many years, the standard just hasn't
caught up yet.  'Packaging' has been in Cobol since the '60s, it doesn't
_need_ an OO syntax.

> COBOL '85 will dry up your var names unless you split 'em up wit

That must be why Cobol failed to catch on and nobody ever wrote any
large programs or system in it.

Please do come back when you have 2 clues to rub together.

> Okay, i'll confess, i don't know a lot about the 'NEW' cobol so, i'll
> just keep quiet on that. 

If you kept quiet about all the things you didn't know a lot about ...

> Though, so far i haven't seen too many
> companies lashing out to prepare to comply with the new standard, or
> am i living under a rock?

One of the significant _advantages_ of Cobol is that there is no need to
'lash out' to comply with a new standard or a new language.  Businesses
are there to run their business not to employ programmers.  Computing is
an _overhead_ not a purpose.

While it may seem important to you to use the 'latest new language'
because it is interesting, it is not important to most businesses where
this would be simply throwing money away.  If their 30 year old system
still sends out the invoices and gets the accounts done then what
advantage would acrue from spending millions to have it rewritten into
something that doesn't work ?

Cobol can get passed down from one generation of programmers to the next
while continuing to work on newer compilers.  This gives continuity to
the business.  It is modular enough that parts can be replaced without
throwing out the rest.

Cobol is about business needs, not about programmer's trendy wants, or
computer companies annual revenues.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T21:51:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0201162151.3630c3fa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-3oxvmFddyrmq@thishost> <3C414CC7.8EAC459@Azonic.co.nz> <c7fe88fd.0201142248.33f239c6@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0201150552.6faef5d7@posting.google.com> <c7fe88fd.0201160723.2c20ab04@posting.google.com> <3C467E66.CF258543@Azonic.co.nz> <TEn18.693331$8q.55592665@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Ummm, something everyone shoul know about the COBOL standard:

We work VERY hard to keep the language "upwardly compatible".  As a
general rule, most programs written in the 1968 standard will compile
unchanged with the 1985 standard compilers --  and the 2002 standard
compilers.  A few things are deleted in each standard (but usually
there are TWO standards of notices: First a language feature is made
Archaic, in the next standard it is made Obsolete, and THEN, in the
next standard, dropped.)
Therefore, as a general rule, no changes must be made to make existing
programs conform to a new standard.
However, compiler makers sometimes add features based on early drafts
of a standard, which are not the same as the final syntax, or their
extensions are ultimately adopted, but not quite the same way that
they had implemented them.  These features are gradually dropped by
the implementors, and these features are 99% of what is usually
involved in "updating" COBOL programs to conform to a new standard.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

matt wrote:

> > Okay, i'll confess, i don't know a lot about the 'NEW' cobol so, i'll
> > just keep quiet on that.
>

But you DON'T keep quiet. You come back comparing C++ to COBOL 85 when Richard has already told you
there is COBOL 2002  with OO. (I think OO COBOL has been around since about '96).

>
> You seem to miss my point.
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes the above C++ manipulation is possible and is CURRENTLY available in OO COBOL -
Collections/Dictionaries (Bags, ValueSets, Ordered and Sorted Collections, plus Dictionaries (key
plus data)). I can use indexing to get Previous and Next etc., plus the ability to clone CollectionB
from CollectionA, or clone one collection type to another,  etc.

Within non-OO COBOL (a) Files - Read Next and Read Previous, or (b) Table manipulation.

Please re-read Richard's message below, slowly  :-

>
> > Please do come back when you have learned Cobol enough to make informed
> > comment.

Jimmy
```

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   03 DATA          PIC ??? VALUE ????

The syntax that you seem to not know is:

    03  Previous      USAGE POINTER.
    ...

        SET Previous TO ADDRESS OF data-item.

However, it is just as easy to create linked lists, trees, heaps in an
array where the previous and next 'pointers' are subscripts or indexes
and dereferencing is done with subscripts.

        MOVE Tree-Next(Tree-Current)  TO Tree-Current
        IF ( Tree-Data(Tree-Current) ....

Using arrays (or disk) means that there is much less chance of memory
leaks or wild pointers or other frequent bugs caused by use or misuse of
pointers.  Business apps have to be reliable, very little code using
pointers is (see MS).

There is no need to write C++ programs in the Cobol language, Cobol
programs do the job just fine.

In fact there is little need for these complex data structures in
memory.  With Cobol there are indexed and relative files,  These provide
all the searching and storing needs and can be _shared_ with other users
and may be updated by other programs while the program is running
ensuring that the data is up to date.  It is also permanent (unless
deleted).

Business applications have to keep data accessible to all users. 
Storing it away in memory structures is only useful for simple,
single-user programs.  All that tree and heap stuff is CS student fodder
and has little relevance to well designed business applications.

> > If you kept quiet about all the things you didn't know a lot about ...
> I'll take your advice..

You failed to yet again.

> Don't feel obligated to respond, Illustrative purposes only.....

Well it certainly illustrates something.
```

- **From:** Bill <wfsent@optonline.net>

```
But who'd write a web service in COBOL ?

Try writing a payroll application. Hourly paid, weekly paid, monthly
paid, bonus, overtime rates, tax, fica, holidays, tax code tables, then
the person decides to take unpaid leave so you have to reverse all the
calculations to put the money back, end of year tax returns, federal
reporting, 401k plans etc etc etc) (and the payroll system is one of the
simpler corporate applications)

More likely the web service will call a COBOL stored procedure to do the
real work and the web service just deals with the presentation


Bill


(10 years ago the hot language was SmallTalk. Now it's referred to as
the legacy GUI in 'my' shop. We no longer code SmallTalk but were still
coding COBOL  (as well as ASP, JAVA, EJB's, maybe VbNet). The other
languages have a shelf life of about 3 or 4 years, COBOL's still going
strong after 45 years.

Web is UNavailable to process your payroll?  The Web has a history of being

secure, I'd prefer my paycheck be handled by a mainframe and have nothing to
do with the Internet.

Terry,

I remember a payroll that had to get out, and the Univac I was down.
The VP of Accounting had to make arragements for his company plane
to take the tapes, the operators, and key programmers to a site Univac
made available. The weather was very bad, but the DC3 made it into NY
O.K., and with around the clock effort, the payroll went out. A supervisor
had to be drugged to keep him out of the way.  Several years later this
same payroll had to be re-run for several years to correct an error in the
logic caught by
the employees who had incentive pay.  If anything is close to disaster, not
making payroll
has to be the worst there is for everyone involved.  Every one on a payroll
is an auditor.


Warren Simmons

large winery in the early 80s.  Most of the 5,000 hourly employees belonged
to a union.  Unions get very upset if their members' paychecks are not out
on time.  Critical, I know!

"matt" <matt@greenroom.com.au> wrote in message
news:c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com...
> COBOL has been in existence since 1959, its first
> standard in 1968 (i think). Sooo many corporations
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> My question is: how much longer will it last?

I can't imagine a sensible corporation 'tempted' to port
an application that has worked for forty years to the
language-de-jour. They've not been tempted for four
decades, what's different now?

As for the world being too complex, that's precisely
the where COBOL excels. Consider writing the back-
office function of the New York Stock Exchange in Java.

How long with COBOL last? Who knows? It's certainly
outlasted many other languages that, in their time, were
more popular than Python or Perl (TurboPascal, for one).

Check back in forty years for an update.
```

#### ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T15:07:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1muu9$k4u$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com>`

```

On 10-Jan-2002, matt@greenroom.com.au (matt) wrote:

> COBOL has been in existence since 1959, its first
> standard in 1968 (i think). Sooo many corporations
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> My question is: how much longer will it last?

While CoBOL is changing, many of the functions for which it was designed are
not changing.   It isn't a good idea to have one tool solve all problems. 
If you have a problem which CoBOL isn't ideally suited to solve - use a
better tool.   But keep your hammer even though you're currently cutting a
board in half.

In the evolution of languages think of CoBOL as a shark.  This design was
perfected long before dinosaurs came on the scene.  And you know what?, it
is still working well today.

So pick a half dozen languages and try to predict whether they will be
popular in 30 years.   Will C be popular?  C++,  Java, CoBOL, assembler,
javascript, Perl?

If a language is based upon a rapidly changing environment - then the odds
of that language remaining dominant are low.   If that language is suitable
to be used in a stable environment, then the odds of that language remaining
suitable are high.
```

#### ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

- **From:** "Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T21:43:52+00:00

Cobol will go on as long as there is data to process  (and you can quote me
on that)

it is an ideal if not THE ideal data processing language

if you need a quick modification to a report, for example, anyone - even
someone not the original programmer should be able to easily make a change

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T22:23:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54>`

"Rational One" <noreligion@nogod.com> wrote in message
news:s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54...
> Cobol will go on as long as there is data to process  (and you can quote
me
> on that)
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree about Cobol's prospects, but have to offer experiences of
creating/updating Easytrieve reporting programs and Cobol programs. I have
written Easytrieve programs in far less time, tested far quicker and still
got a correctly functioning report program, because it does the little
things for you, totaling & formatting the reports. I think Cobol's place as
a language of choice to process large quantities of data on a single
platform is pretty safe - I've been writing PL/I for the last couple of
months - it was easy to pick up, but not as good as Cobol (just my opinion).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T05:32:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
Rufio, 

You've lived the Sheltered COBOL life I once lived.  Now that I've "discovered"
report writer (still an Alien thing on OS/390 without an add on) I can tell you
this - I've used em all - RPG, DYL260, DYL280, EasyTrieve - and I'll tell you -
NOTHING and I mean NOTHING beats the simplicty and power of COBOL report writer
for createing reports like you have described.  It's fast, it's easy and you
simply describe your record layout, what you want the report to look like
(another simple record layout) and then all you need is one line:

GENERATE ....

Way - and I mean WAY - COOL.


In article <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>, Rufio says...
>
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

What I have seen over the last 2-3 years is that with the growth of
internet/intranet, Client/Server etc. this is becoming a moot point. I have
seen users moving away from weekly 100 page reports towards much more
specific data requirements. Where there is a large population, I've had more
requests for control totals & summaries than large reports.

Q. Anyone want to comment on Crystal Reports? I've kept seeing contracts for
it, but not had time to learn it (or SAS). Hopefully, I'll find a Java perm
post in 2002, and leave some of this behind,.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-14T15:31:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1utdf$57a$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <4D47005820A1B7E4.31AA3ED9C3DFD9AF.EA793D60C892D3C3@lp.airnews.net> <sc208.125788$Wd.37872477@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```

On 12-Jan-2002, "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com> wrote:

> Q. Anyone want to comment on Crystal Reports? I've kept seeing contracts
> for
> it, but not had time to learn it (or SAS). Hopefully, I'll find a Java
> perm
> post in 2002, and leave some of this behind,.

Without having any significant experience, I believe that should be the way
corporations should go.  The mainframe CoBOL or SQL query language should
extract the data - and the user should format the way he wants it with
Crystal Reports.  Making it pretty should be the user's job.


Elsewhere in the thread - I've never been a fan of CoBOL report writer. 
Maybe this is because I already don't need to think to write a report in
CoBOL, that I don't see any significant advantage in RW - and I do know that
not all CoBOL programmers are conversant in RW.

EasyTrieve is pretty easy though - for simple reports why not use it?  We
have cases where some of the code in an EasyTrieve program (from multiple DD
statement includes) is under user control.  They actually add an IF
statement before the run.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T07:48:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C43DED9.A013A1E1@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <4D47005820A1B7E4.31AA3ED9C3DFD9AF.EA793D60C892D3C3@lp.airnews.net> <sc208.125788$Wd.37872477@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <a1utdf$57a$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Elsewhere in the thread - I've never been a fan of CoBOL report writer.
> Maybe this is because I already don't need to think to write a report in
> CoBOL, that I don't see any significant advantage in RW - and I do know that
> not all CoBOL programmers are conversant in RW.

The main problem that I see with Report Writer is that it is a
programmers' tool and not a users'. The user must specify the
requirement and then wait for the programmer to write it.  A better
tools is where the user can get the report from his own efforts. 
Chrystal Reports for example, or, in theory, SQL.

I have written a couple of Reporting programs where the program produces
reports from a simple script which is executed at run time.  There are
also programs to write the scripts based on dialog box and list box
selections, though I haven't covered all the options yet with these.

This does allow the user to extract a report with selection in sorted
sequence with page breaks and sub-totals showing any of the fields in a
range of files with some multi-file capability.  

Some users can do this quite well, others just don't get it, but then I
can write a script in a few minutes for them.  It doesn't do everything
but saves me writing 98% of all reporting programs.  It is, however,
tied into my 'Base' system quite significantly and is not in any way a
'stand-alone'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T12:36:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c436f50_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Thane,

I am amazed you are promoting this. It is archaic technology tied to an
archaic File System.

Users should NOT have to rely on COBOL programmers to produce reports. End
of Story.

It doesn't matter how "way cool" the tools are if it is Programmers who get
to use them.(I am NOT suggesting that programmers should NOT have "way cool"
tools...just that where programmer involvement in a User Interface can be
avoided, it should be...the ultimate arbiter is the User.)

I don't doubt the latest incarnation of RW is excellent (actually, I thought
the previous ones were pretty good) but that is NOT the issue. The issue is
the perception of COBOL as a CLOSED system.

On the (extremely) rare occasions these days when I need to access the COBOL
file system for reporting, I simply send the report data stream to MSWord so
the User can manipulate it however he likes (Fonts, formatting etc are
handled by styles in a standard template and if the User doesn't like it he
can easily amend it.) More and more users are becoming proficient in using
tools like Word Processors and are very happy to manipulate what they
perceive as THEIR data... This gives them a sense of ownership and
involvement which is just plain good for all concerned (including IT).

It is NOT the programmer's job to decide how data should be presented. It is
the system designer's job, in conjunction with the end User. The User should
decide ultimately how he wants screens and reports to be presented. Tools
which enable him to do this (Like Crystal Reports and QBE) are infinitely
preferable to tools which require a Programmer in the loop (like RW...).

Yes, I know there are many sites where they still use green lineflo and
produce reports from COBOL programs as they have always done. The difference
is that they no longer HAVE to; they have a choice. Our encouraging them not
to make that choice is simply not good for the long term future of COBOL.

Pete.

writer
> for createing reports like you have described.  It's fast, it's easy and
you
> simply describe your record layout, what you want the report to look like
> (another simple record layout) and then all you need is one line:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

says...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **Date:** 2002-01-15T06:02:22-08:00

- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0201150602.291ab64b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com>`

```
Sorry Pete you miss the point.

RW output doesn't have to be a REPORT.  It's excellent when the data
source is a database or whatever you want and when the output is to be
a "Word" document if that's what you want.  It's power is in data
selection of the input, proper and complex control breaks and then any
output you desire.  While the output "IS" some kind of flat file -
this can be fed to word and fontized as you desire.  The cool part of
it is in the selection, control breaks, instant subtotallying and
variable output formats.  It's cool and I DO recommend it.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T12:07:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c459071_12@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0201150602.291ab64b@posting.google.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0201150602.291ab64b@posting.google.com...
> Sorry Pete you miss the point.
>
I think we are concerned about different points...<G>

> RW output doesn't have to be a REPORT.  It's excellent when the data
> source is a database or whatever you want and when the output is to be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK, that is all fine. Now what about MY point regarding its use by
non-Programmers?

I'm not arguing whether it is a good tool or not, Thane. My problem is in
the fact that an end User (AFAIK) cannot sit down and use it. It is part of
what makes COBOL a closed system and that is what is killing COBOL in
corporates.

I have discussed this at length in this forum and elsewhere so I won't go
over it all again (sighs of relief all round...<G>) but the bottom line is
that systems that don't facilitate the free and open exchange of data, or
require access through a Programmer, are becoming increasingly unpopular and
will soon be relegated to the "Don't use this for new development" folder.
COBOL does not deserve to be in this folder as it is very capable of
accessing modern databases. Only when we tie it to the file system do we
clip its wings.

Now, above, you are saying that the data can come from a database and you
make the point about the power of its selection facilities. Maybe it could
be "packaged" so that an end user could interact with it and get reports
from whatever datasources he wanted? But then I believe there are already
some excellent tools that do just that (Monarch, Crystal Reports, etc.)

As a tool for programmers I can see why you would recommend it. The problem
I have with that is that programmers shouldn't be producing reports in this
day and age. And they don't have to if open databases and the concomitant
tools to interrogate them are available.

Furthermore, we should be encouraging users not just to move away from
reports but to do much more on-line interrogation and run their own Queries.
(Hard copy at the touch of a button; no programming required).

I also support Howard's argument (having lived it all my COBOL life) that
any COBOL Programmer worth his salt knows how to write reports, understands
control breaks and heading production, page overflow and page and line
counting and really has no need of RW. I have written countless report
programs in the days when we had no other option and I have never used RW.

Pete.

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:<3c436f50_3@Usenet.com>...
> > Thane,

get

cool"

is

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T21:59:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0201162159.37746fea@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0201150602.291ab64b@posting.google.com> <3c459071_12@Usenet.com>`

```
Snipped a bunch of stuff.. .Want to comment on this point.

If your software system doesn't offer Ad Hoc reporting it is hard to
sell.

If it does offer it - no average user will use it.  You'll be called
upon to create the ad hoc reports for the users.  It's a strange
thing.

I've actually created, over the years, about a dozen Ad hoc reporting
tools for various systems and used many others.  Guess what?  About 1%
of the users actually use them.  The rest are canned reports using the
generator that the programmer create.

Even Crystal reports doesn't help much because if the user doesn't
understand the underlying data they can't create a decent report.

It's a red herring - but one we MUST live with and a feature that MUST
be there - just for marketing if nothing else.


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3c459071_12@Usenet.com>...
> OK, that is all fine. Now what about MY point regarding its use by
> non-Programmers?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T14:04:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VFW08.147$8z1.123856@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3c436f50_3@Usenet.com...
> Thane,
>
>..just that where programmer involvement in a User Interface can be
> avoided, it should be...the ultimate arbiter is the User.)

Say What?!!!

This is how you end up with Microsoft "Bob." (or native unix commands)....

I hope you did not mean this literally, because if you did it is a violation
of every tenet of programming in which I have ever believed. OF COURSE the
programmer needs to be involved, or you end up with software which does not
serve the user.

OF COURSE the user is the ultimate artibiter - after all, the software is
created FOR users, not for the self-gratification of the programmer.

Yes, it would be nice if users would/could  "paint their own screens and
reports"  but they don't. Why not? Because users are not "screen designers,"
they are USERS.

How easily we forget that we (programmers, designers) exist not for
ourselves, but to serve the real users.

MCM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T03:51:18+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4c2bc3_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com> <VFW08.147$8z1.123856@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Comments below, Michael...

Pete.

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:VFW08.147$8z1.123856@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3c436f50_3@Usenet.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I hope you did not mean this literally, because if you did it is a
violation
> of every tenet of programming in which I have ever believed. OF COURSE the
> programmer needs to be involved, or you end up with software which does
not
> serve the user.
>
Weeeellll....yep, I did mean it literally and I am genuinely sorry if you
feel violated <G>.

Maybe I can qualify it a little for your comfort... (or not <G>)

See, when you say "OF COURSE the Programmer needs to be involved..." that is
not strictly true any more. (I completely agree that it WAS true for over 30
years.) Things have moved on and whether Programmers (COBOL or any other
ilk) can see it or not, Users are getting very pissed off with HAVING to use
programmers in order to get what they want, computer wise.

When you say that you end up with software that "doesn't serve the User" if
programmers are not involved, I'm afraid that is demonstrably untrue. More
and more Users are serving themselves...

We are approaching 300,000,000 Users on the Internet. This is unprecedented.
They are not all about to become computer programmers...instead they will
(and are) demand(ing) more responsive, User Friendly systems so they can do
more themselves.

The first steps towards providing this are the "Wizards" which allow
untrained Users to interact with the system and get it to do what they want
(You may recall I predicted this would happen around 5 years ago in the old
MF forum... The keys are INTERACTION and ITERATION and that is exactly what
Users who are using a Wizard, do...) The Wizards are often component based
and I predicted that would happen too...I expect it to go further than it
has. (XP uses more components than previous versions of Windows, and is MUCH
more Object Oriented).

So it is no longer true to say that Programmers need to be involved in the
User Interface as a matter "of Course"...Tools are available which allow
Users to define their own reports and screens, PROVIDED the data is open and
accessible.
(It is the realization and understanding of this critical factor which has
caused me to become such an ardent proponent of Databases and Open systems.
The COBOL file system is killing the language. And it is the File System
that requires a Programmer in the User interface...)

Relatively untrained Users can run quite complex Queries against RDBs using
SQL, Query By Example, Monarch, Crystal Reports, and other similar tools.
These tools are becoming more and more User friendly (with attached Wizards
and Tutorials) and the users are becoming more and more and more computer
literate (thanks mainly to the Internet, and the rising generation being
exposed to computer technology all their lives). Extrapolate these two
trends and their intersection is inevitable. Users will move to producing
their own reports and screens if they are encouraged to do so and it is made
easy and fun.

Personally, I reckon this is a Good Thing. I have always believed in placing
computer power directly in the hands of Users wherever possible; there are
enough REAL programming challenges without having to create bloody reports
and screens too... Especially when they change every 5 minutes <G>.


> OF COURSE the user is the ultimate artibiter - after all, the software is
> created FOR users, not for the self-gratification of the programmer.
>
Good. We agree on that.<G>


> Yes, it would be nice if users would/could  "paint their own screens and
> reports"  but they don't. Why not? Because users are not "screen
designers,"
> they are USERS.
>
Well, they certainly are in the Mainframe environment (currently). But it
won't always be that way (see above).

(It is also true that many Programmers are not screen designers
either...<G>)

And, as "Users" they have a pretty fair idea of WHAT they need to see on a
screen or report. The problem is that they are often intimidated by the
technology and so have no idea HOW to produce it. Give them some
encouragement however, and let them play with a tool like Crystal for a
while (where they can drag and drop the fields they want to see, and the
system actually helps them to see what data is available) and, as their
confidence grows, there are fewer and fewer requirements for IT to produce
screens and reports.

BOTTOM LINE:

1. We should be generally encouraging Users to move away from Paper reports.
(They sit around and collect dust or end up in a bin, and trees are
destroyed in order to produce them...no wonder we can't breathe...<G>)

2. Given that people become more used to seeing immediate real-time data
displayed on their screens, we should then empower them to produce this for
themselves.

While I realise this will be an unpopular view in a forum such as this,
where certain programmers will feel threatened by it, such considerations
have not stopped me from expressing controversial views in the past and they
are not going to do so now...<G> I stand by my statement (which really
wasn't that strong...I said "where it CAN be avoided"). Programmers should
keep out of the User Interface and we should empower Users to do their own
reports and screens.

"There, I've said it three times.

What I tell you three times is true."
(Lewis Carroll - The Hunting of the Snark")

> How easily we forget that we (programmers, designers) exist not for
> ourselves, but to serve the real users.
>
I certainly agree with that. It has been my position all my working life and
I have ALWAYS seen IT /CS as a SERVICE department.

And the best way we can serve the Computer Users in the Organisation, is to
provide tools which they can use to do it themselves...

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T15:35:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020121103509.18357.00003914@mb-fl.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3c4c2bc3_2@Usenet.com>`

```
Comments below, Pete...

[snip]

>
>We are approaching 300,000,000 Users on the Internet. This is unprecedented.
>They are not all about to become computer programmers...instead they will
>(and are) demand(ing) more responsive, User Friendly systems so they can do
>more themselves.

The only problem I see with this, Pete, is that most users are rather naive.
They can drag and drop and answer fill-in-the-blank questions from wizards, but
they are not aware of subtleties that skew the results they get back. For
example, the impact of fields that can have 'null' values has been hotly
debated in the relational database groups for years. 

Or, another example, the subtleties of Boolean logic cause users to ask the
wrong questions (or the right questions wrongly, maybe), such as "give me all
customers who are under 30 and over 70". Then, when they get no entries back in
their list, they assume they have the right answer to the right question, when
they only have the right answer to the wrong question.

This is what progammers bring to the table. Hopefully. The ability to think
logically and carefully about the task at hand.

However, I must say in advance, I find many programmers cannot think logically
and carefully about the task at hand, either. Sigh. Then you must train them or
fire them, I guess.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T11:56:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0201211156.5e636110@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com> <VFW08.147$8z1.123856@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3c4c2bc3_2@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote 
> The COBOL file system is killing the language. And it is the File System
> that requires a Programmer in the User interface...)
> 


There is no such thing as a "COBOL file system".  See the recent
thread on "COBOL files and COBOL data types".

Yes, self-help tools usually work best with a particular file
structure, usually an RDMS, because that is for what the tyools were
written.  It is also true that certain types of files are better
suited for particular tasks.  In the course of processing, most large
mainframe shops (for example, because that is where most of my
experience lies) will have, in the course of processing, several file
versions of the same data, including, possibly, sequential, indexed
and relational, indexed or sorted in a varuity of ways, not to mention
the format used by FDR (Fast Dump/Restore) which can be understood by
no other tools.  If there is a need/use for data in a particular
format, it is not a big deal to provide it.

Also relevant to the "empowerment of users" discussion is this
observation:
There are serious reasons why clerks are not bookkeepers, and why
bookkeepers are not accountants, and why not all accountants are CPAs
or Auditors.

When automobiles were first sold, almost every owner had (or was,
personally) a driver who was also a mechanic.  In time that was not
necessary.  The same process is happening with computers.  Users can
do, and will be able to do, more and more without the assistance of
professionals.  But there are still chauffeurs, and there are still
mechanics.  To continue the metaphor a little bit, you still need
special licenses (demonstrating special knowledge and expertise) to
drive trucks or racing cars professionally.

More and more, users will be doing more and more that programmers did
once.  But programmers will not go away, they will do more specialized
work.  And blaming a lack (or slowness) of progress on "COBOL file
systems" is silly.   It is so-o-o-o easy to convert any kind of file
into whatever you need.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Thanks, Stephen..you said it far better than I could have.
After the DBTG effort, the ability of COBOL to interface with
other file kinds expanded, and  in what we call the public domain.
To bad vendors still feel they need a unique way to do I/O for
their software. But look how long it took Big Blue to adopt
standards, and MS to adopt security. <G>

Warren Simmons

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T14:29:07+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4cc16a_9@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com> <VFW08.147$8z1.123856@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3c4c2bc3_2@Usenet.com> <928495c6.0201211156.5e636110@posting.google.com>`

```
Comments below, Stephen.

Pete.

Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@mail.com> wrote in message
news:928495c6.0201211156.5e636110@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote
> > The COBOL file system is killing the language. And it is the File System
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I am pretty familiar with "COBOL files and COBOL data types" and I
understand your objection to this terminology. I agree that it is quite
incorrect to describe Packed Decimal (for instance) as a COBOL data type, or
an ISAM file as a COBOL file type. All of these can be (and are) accessed by
other languages like PL/1 as well as COBOL. (There is a decline in the use
of PL/1 for the same reasons as there is a decline in the use of COBOL...)

I use the term loosely. So do the people who write COBOL Manuals, THEY refer
to it as "the COBOL file System"

Let's refer to it as a "system of I/O which, when it is accessed from a
COBOL Environment, requires a COBOL program in order to access it." (Sounds
like the language used in the Standard, so you should have no trouble with
that...<G>)


So, if you substitute this somewhat more unwieldy phrase in the places where
I refer to "the COBOL File System" we should be able to have a much better
argument <G>.

> Yes, self-help tools usually work best with a particular file
> structure, usually an RDMS, because that is for what the tyools were
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And in so providing it, proliferate the the plethora of duplicated data you
describe above... I agree that many mainframe sites DO find themselves in
this situation, and that is one reason why they are moving away from COBOL
and PL/1 to more open data storage. Notice the increase in Data Warehousing?
Ever wondered "Why?".

> Also relevant to the "empowerment of users" discussion is this
> observation:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 This is an excellent point and I'm really glad you raised it.

In Victorian times there was a very rigid hierarchy so that military
discipline could be maintained in the workplace. This hierarchy ensured that
people did not get "ideas above their station" and also meant a very rigid
division of work.

Everyone knew their place, understood the career path and how long it would
take them to move from "Bookkeeper level 3" to "Clerk Level 1" , how many
further years of training would be required to attain "Accountant" and who
would have to die before "Financial Director" could be attained (if
ever...If you were not of the correct Social Class you had no show...)

The vestiges of this system are still visible in many Corporations, although
it has been watered down by the attainment of 37 hour weeks, Trade Unions,
and provision of Staff canteens and Toilets...

The majority of the Dickensian Corporations are now long gone and the only
place I have seen such a setup in Modern Times was in Leeds (in the Midlands
of England) where I did some work on a site that had not changed its model
in 150 years. (They have since done so, and brought the company back from
the verge of ruin to a prosperous Enterprise.)

In today's Corporate climate, the sky should be the limit. The hierarchic
Management Structure which has proven to be so inefficient and error prone
that even the Military are discarding it, is being replaced in the more
forward looking Companies by Networked Management structures where people
work together as teams to attain a given goal and share joint responsibility
for it. This means there are no longer the rigid lines you describe. (At
least there aren't in the more progressive and successful Enterprises; I
accept that there are still many places with a ways to go in this
area...maybe your comment is based on experience with these?).

This means that knowledge and skill are not viewed as part of a job
description, but simply as a resource that needs to be acquired by the
"Team" in order to do the job in hand.

For example, I have no Accounting qualifications whatsoever, yet I have
acquired enough expertise to file my own Company accounts and to implement
Ledger systems (Accounts Payable, Accounts Receivable, General Ledger, and
Fixed Assets) in major Corporations, working with people who are NOT
"Bookeepers" or "Accountants" or "Clerks" but simply "Account Administration
Facilitators" with a wide range of general skills and specialist knowledge.
Yes, it is still desirable for the Financial Director to be a qualified
Accountant, but this goal is now attainable by anyone who is prepared to hit
the books (the best Corporations will even encourage their people to further
their education, and will assist the process...) and study this particular
discipline; not decided by their social standing (at least, it SHOULDN'T
be...<G>)

I therefore contend that your "serious reasons why clerks are not
bookkeepers, and why
bookkeepers are not accountants, and why not all accountants are CPAs or
Auditors" are more to do with a certain mindset than they are to do with the
actual requirements in the workplace.


> When automobiles were first sold, almost every owner had (or was,
> personally) a driver who was also a mechanic.  In time that was not
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, absolutely. The empowerment of computer users is as inevitable as the
advent of automatic transmission and cruise control... And I am not arguing
(and have never argued) that Programmers will not be required for system
support (at least in the medium future...say 15 years. I HAVE postulated
that specialist system software will be developed to take over this role in
the more distant future).

Using your own analogy...

When I was a young man we used to work on our cars at the weekend and did
things like top overhauls, replaced worn valves, decoked cylinders, adjusted
timing to the nth degree and took delight in maximising the efficiency of
our engines.

Today I simply spray something into the inlet to do a decoke, I would not
dream of touching the timing  because the engine is managed by a computer
chip which dynamically adjusts the timing in accordance with the load on the
engine and the grade of fuel being used, and the engine is ALWAYS running at
maximum efficiency as its performance is being sampled 15 times a second and
sent back to a unit which analyses the requirements and ensures the right
quantity of fuel explodes in the right place at the right moment...(a long
way from the mechanical vacuum line that was used to retard the spark in the
old days...)

Now, despite all this apparent complexity, has the skill of the mechanic at
the local garage increased? No. Quite the contrary. He simply plugs a
computer into a port provided by the engine manufacturer and receives
complete diagnostics on the performance of the engine, along with any
recommended part replacements or maintenance or repair procedures.
The only "skills" he needs are the basic ones of using a wrench, a hammer,
and a screwdriver.

So what was it that drove this progress over the last 30 years? Was it the
onward advance of Science and the general march of progress?

Only partly. The main driving factor was that as BILLIONS of people moved to
motor cars they REQUIRED faster, more efficient solution of mechanical
problems and better efficiency and performance from their automobiles.
(They also required, particularly in the USA, a simpler interface to control
the vehicle. Hence, automatic transmission...)

It was simply too big and competitive a market to be ignored.

Why shouldn't the same thing happen with software?

I believe it will.

> More and more, users will be doing more and more that programmers did
> once.  But programmers will not go away, they will do more specialized
> work.  And blaming a lack (or slowness) of progress on "COBOL file
> systems" is silly.

Well, you may think it's silly, but I have seen it at first hand. It is
definitely one of the major factors contributing to the decline of COBOL.

(BTW, I NEVER argued that progress towards more User friendly systems is
being slowed by the COBOL File system; my argument is that the decline of
COBOL is being sped by its perception as having a closed file system.)

Here's how it works:

"We need some of the existing mainframe Legacy data to be integrated with
the new Web Enabled Cashwank system...Can we write some Java and JDBC to get
what we need?"

"Oh God, that stuff is all on Indexed Sequential Files and the code is in
programs that are thousands of lines long and there is one guy in the
company who really understands it and he's having time off on sick leave
after playing with his Grandchildren..."

"But can't we just access the data we are actually interested in, with Java,
and forget about the existing code?"

"Well, we COULD if it was on a standard Database, but this stuff is written
in COBOL and that means you can't get to it. I have heard rumours that there
are tools available to extract COBOL data but I don't understand them, and
neither do any of our people...This is an archaic language that locks stuff
up. By the time we locate, purchase, and get up to speed with the tools
(assuming they do actually exist) we will have missed the deadline the
Chairman set when he promised Tosser Corporation we would have Cashwank
on-line by April...Not to mention the additional non-budgeted expense of
buying the tools and training...."

"Jesus, this bloody COBOL Legacy stuff is a pain in the arse. Time we got
shot of the lot. Unfortunately we can't, because 70% of the Business is
being run by it. Make sure that NO new development is done in COBOL. Get a
contractor to extract the data we need from Legacy for Cashwank, and tell
Margaret to get some quotes for converting the Legacy stuff to Relational
Database. Maybe we can just convert the whole bloody lot so it is
accessible..."

And we end up with very badly converted systems that are STILL problematic.
(See my comments elsewhere in this Newsgroup)

>  It is so-o-o-o easy to convert any kind of file
> into whatever you need.
>

Easy, huh?

But if you use an open storage system in the first place there is no need to
convert anything. It doesn't get any easier than that.

And, having had a bit to do with various conversions (both within and across
platforms), I would say that YOU are silly if you really believe it is
"so-o-o-o easy" <G>. It is never an exercise to be undertaken lightly and
considerable thought and planning is required to ensure success. Converting
ONE FILE may be easy; converting hundreds of them is not so. It is a
tireseome, repetitive, error-prone process. (That's why I wrote tools to do
it...)

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T20:48:00+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<220120022048001825%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com> <VFW08.147$8z1.123856@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3c4c2bc3_2@Usenet.com> <928495c6.0201211156.5e636110@posting.google.com> <3c4cc16a_9@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3c4cc16a_9@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> And in so providing it, proliferate the the plethora of duplicated data you
> describe above... I agree that many mainframe sites DO find themselves in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Actually, Data Warehouses/Marts are the most efficient method of
duplicating data on the planet. 
Firstly, Almost no frontline opertional data are stored in a data
warehouse for operations. These operations usually require access times
vastly faster than can be had from normalised data. So these data are
stored in specialized "COBOL file system" files or specially designed
relational tables designed for access and update speed. Typically, this
means that there is only a subset of the data present - only the
"active" data. This data (plus other sources) is trolled and normalised
into a data warehouse (first copy). Then the data is summarised into
various tables and views (a copy for each summary table). Even then,
the users have no idea the problems that they can cause when they
average averages. Users typlically have NO (read nada) ability to
create views and tables in a data warehouse. They usually request the
warehouse administrator  who then gets a programmer and a DBA to
cooperate to create the neccessary views/tables.

Empowerment of users only works when the users know something about the
data they are manipulating. In a big organisation this is almost never
the case.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T14:01:15+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4e0e56_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com> <VFW08.147$8z1.123856@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3c4c2bc3_2@Usenet.com> <928495c6.0201211156.5e636110@posting.google.com> <3c4cc16a_9@Usenet.com> <220120022048001825%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```

Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:220120022048001825%josephk@iinet.net.au...
> In article <3c4cc16a_9@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
><snipped interesting description of  DW 101>

> Empowerment of users only works when the users know something about the
> data they are manipulating. In a big organisation this is almost never
> the case.

Bollocks!

Just because a user doesn't understand the technical details of what the
Warehouse gnomes have done with his data does not mean he knows nothing
about the data he is manipulating.

It is precisely this attitude of "We can't empower Users because they
couldn't possibly understand the beauty and elegance of our data storage
system" that is screwing so many organisations and leaving them at the mercy
of the technocrats.

The fact is (and I'll warrant I have worked inside at least as many "big
organisations" as you have, Joe) that where users are able to access and
manipulate their own data the Business runs better, and staff morale is
higher.

Empowering people is the ONLY way to consistently improve the bottom line,
but so many Businesses are run by control freaks (usually these guys are
throwbacks to the Victorian Management I wrote about elsewhere) that they
just can't do it.(They can if you encourage them, and when they see the
results they are very happy to take credit for having done it...<G>)

The technical details of how and where data is stored are irrelevant, as
long as Users have free access to it.

To continue locking the data resource away in the care of  DBAs and
programmers is folly. These people are very necessary to handle the
technical details, but they provide a service to the Business and the tail
does not wag the dog...

I am not advocating that every department should hold its own data locked on
a local hard drive (although even this is OK, as long as copies of it are
filed daily in the central repository, using the facilities provided by the
IT people, so that a corporate picture can be obtained by senior Management
when required. And it can be fed to other departments by use of exported
spreadsheets or databases).

What I AM saying is that storing data in a way that is Open and allowing
Users to access it with tools for that purpose, is a better solution than
locking it up on a mainframe and providing access to it through IT
department provided Reports and Queries.

The Client/Server model leans inherently in this direction, allowing groups
of Users to exchange and share data easily and conveniently, and that's why
this model has been (and is continuing to be) so successful.

Pete.

```

```
In article <3c4e0ed5_2@Usenet.com>, Peter E. C. Dashwood
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> The first sentence is axiomatic; the second one is Bollocks!
I don't think I'm going to respond to that!
> 
> Just because a user doesn't understand the technical details of what the
> Warehouse gnomes have done with his data does not mean he knows nothing
> about the data he is manipulating.
> 
Look I did say a BIG organization. How can you expect the sales manager
to understand the details of the warehousing function (that maybe only
one consultant understands and no one in the company does) when asked
to produce a report on the prdicted future impact of sales on the stock
control of the company. This is just an example mind.

> It is precisely this attitude of "We can't empower Users because they
> couldn't possibly understand the beauty and elegance of our data storage
> system" that is screwing so many organisations and leaving them at the mercy
> of the technocrats.
It's got nothing to do with beauty or elegance and everything to do
with accuracy and timeliness.
Look, the reason dataMarts etc were created is so that this level of
nonsense could be removed from the users. The users would have a vast
array of reports in the Marts. These reports would not be in report
format, but in tabular format familiar to spreadsheet users. It is
anticipated that the user would point their spreadsheet at the
appropriate database table in the Mart and would then format the report
and do other computations as is required. DataMarts were created to
EMPOWER users. It's just that the raw data from the operations is often
optimised (for performance reasons) and not immediately usable by a
user without manipulation. The solution was to extract this data,
normalise it, apply all the grungy mathematics to convert it into
information and then put it into data tables that the user can use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of COBOL

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>

Actually there is no rest - I ran out of steam!

Data Marts/Warehouses have been around for some time - at least 6 to 7
years.

They are not easy to set up, they are very expensive, but they do
achieve their aim, which is to empower the users.

You might want to search on the web via google etc. I don't have any
pointers to web sites describing data marts. However, I came close to
implementing one once using the SAS statistical system.
