- **References:** `<c7fe88fd.0201092357.3f50c4d8@posting.google.com> <s0J%7.439$Tq.1878@rwcrnsc54> <8BJ%7.121811$Wd.37212817@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <CTP%7.1861$TS6.4411@www.newsranger.com> <3c436f50_3@Usenet.com> <VFW08.147$8z1.123856@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3c4c2bc3_2@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> > Yes, it would be nice if users would/could  "paint their own screens and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> won't always be that way (see above).

What led to the IBM PC being made was that users were bringing in their
own Apple IIs to the mainframe sites so that they could use Visicalc and
write a bit of Basic, this was so much more accessible than the
mainframe.  The IBM PC was built for just those people to try to keep
the sites 'IBM only'.

Now, ironically, it seems that Windows (especially 2000/XP) is the new
mainframe in terms of complexity and control (but not reliability or
power).  Windows is very much "do it _my_ way, signed Bill". With BSA
software audits, IT imposition of what the machine must be set up with,
instability caused by anything non-standard (ie non-Microcrosoft), and
controls and restrictions within the software itself (eg non-'approved'
software, content control) it is increasingly becoming unapproachable.

In this environment we see Linux boxes creeping in, often below the IT
management line of sight, just as the Apple and PCs crept in 20 odd
years ago.

Microsoft has now issued memos to sales staff instructing them to patrol
their sites to ensure that it is "Microsoft only" in just the same way
that IBM did in those days.
```

```
language of business - COBOL and the vbers and C sharpers can inherit
from it for their own use!
```

## Thread: continue-Statemen in perform — 28 msgs

### continue-Statemen in perform

- **From:** Daniel Niedzielski <daniel.niedzielski@web.de>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T13:20:25+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de>`

```
Hello,
in C, perl or C++ can I do:


while (    )  <-+
{               |
  statement;    |
  statement;    |
  if (      )   |
  {             |
   continue   --+
  };
  statement;
  statement;
};


with "continue" I can jump to the head of the Loop. How Can I make this in 
Cobol
perform with test after until (   )


    exit perform  <-- jump out / not to the head
end-perform
```

#### ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

- **From:** leclaire <leclaire@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T14:35:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3DA5ED.F7E6D373@rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de>`

```
The traditional COBOL clause for a NO-OP is NEXT SENTENCE, if that was
your question.  Some compilers also allow CONTINUE.  Neither actually
has any effect on the PERFORM statement.  As far as I know, the only way
to exit a PERFORM loop is to incorporate a controlable condition in your
UNTIL clause.


Daniel Niedzielski wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> News suchen, lesen, schreiben mit http://newsgroups.web.de
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T14:04:45-05:00

```
"leclaire" <leclaire@rr.com> wrote in message
news:3C3DA5ED.F7E6D373@rr.com...
> The traditional COBOL clause for a NO-OP is NEXT SENTENCE, if that was
> your question.  Some compilers also allow CONTINUE.  Neither actually
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not quite true.  NEXT SENTENCE is a got to the first statement after the
next period, which is not quite the same thing as a no-op.  CONTINUE is a
no-op.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T21:22:46+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brtr3u8q8rof7s6i5pshsf7683brgk4ddn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de> <3C3DA5ED.F7E6D373@rr.com>`

```
On Thu, 10 Jan 2002 14:35:25 GMT, leclaire <leclaire@rr.com> wrote:

>The traditional COBOL clause for a NO-OP is NEXT SENTENCE, if that was
>your question.  Some compilers also allow CONTINUE.  Neither actually
>has any effect on the PERFORM statement.  As far as I know, the only way
>to exit a PERFORM loop is to incorporate a controlable condition in your
>UNTIL clause.

Hm.

that shows how little I know.  I always though NEXT SENTENCE to
definitely DO something (namely to branch to the statement after the
next dot.  Let us just play around a little:

You might want to try the following program

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. TESTIT.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  A PIC X VALUE 'A'.
       01  B PIC X VALUE 'B'.
       01  C PIC X VALUE 'C'.
       01  D PIC X VALUE 'D'.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           DISPLAY 'Case 1'
           IF B > A THEN
              NEXT SENTENCE
 S1:          DISPLAY 'B is greater than A'
           .
           DISPLAY 'Case 2' 
           IF B > A THEN
              CONTINUE
              DISPLAY 'B is greater than A'
           .
           DISPLAY 'CASE 3'
           IF B > A THEN
              IF D > C THEN
                 NEXT SENTENCE
 S2:          END-IF
              DISPLAY 'WE ARE STILL IN THE OUTER IF'
           .
           DISPLAY 'CASE 4'
           IF B > A THEN
              IF D > C THEN
                 CONTINUE
              END-IF
              DISPLAY 'WE ARE STILL IN THE OUTER IF'
              .
           GOBACK
           .

Mind you, there will be at least one syntax error, in the part with
label S1:

A "NEXT SENTENCE" phrase was followed by imperative or conditional
statements.  These statements will not be executed.

This clearly shows that NEXT SENTENCE is NOT a no-op

Another problem might be flagged at S2:, because NEXT SENTENCE
together with END-IF definitely is a no-no in the standard.  There are
compilers though (notably IBM) that allow this (They call it a
feature, I call it a bug, see execution log further down) 

Lets see what happens when we run the program  

F:\IBMCOBW\samples>test
Case 2
B is greater than A
Case 3
Case 4
WE ARE STILL IN THE OUTER IF


If NEXT SENTENCE were a NO-OP, we should see a message in case 3 (but
we don't)


And finally:  How to exit a PERFORM (Not that I would ever use it)

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. TESTIT.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  I   PIC S9(5) COMP-3 VALUE 0.
       01  The-Sum PIC S9(5) COMP-3 VALUE 0.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           COMPUTE I = 0
           PERFORM UNTIL I > 16
              COMPUTE I = I + 1
              COMPUTE The-Sum = The-Sum + I
              DISPLAY 'Sum of the first ' I ' integers is ' The-Sum
              IF The-Sum < 100 THEN
                 DISPLAY 'We do this a few times at the beginning'
              END-IF
           END-PERFORM
           GOBACK
           .
 
And the result:

Sum of the first +00001 integers is +00001
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00002 integers is +00003
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00003 integers is +00006
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00004 integers is +00010
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00005 integers is +00015
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00006 integers is +00021
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00007 integers is +00028
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00008 integers is +00036
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00009 integers is +00045
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00010 integers is +00055
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00011 integers is +00066
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00012 integers is +00078
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00013 integers is +00091
We do this a few times at the beginning
Sum of the first +00014 integers is +00105
Sum of the first +00015 integers is +00120
Sum of the first +00016 integers is +00136
Sum of the first +00017 integers is +00153



                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)         
______________________________________________________________________________
Posted Via Binaries.net = SPEED+RETENTION+COMPLETION = http://www.binaries.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T07:34:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3E9590.6EE50ACA@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de> <3C3DA5ED.F7E6D373@rr.com>`

```
leclaire wrote:
> 
> The traditional COBOL clause for a NO-OP is NEXT SENTENCE, 

That is not true.  NEXT SENTENCE is _NOT_ a no-op.  It is a 'GO TO
statement after next full stop'.  However, it is only usable as a clause
in an IF statement that does not include END-IF and thus cannot be used
in any other structure.

> Some compilers also allow CONTINUE.  

All ANS85 compiler should include CONTINUE which is a no-op.

> Neither actually has any effect on the PERFORM statement.  

NEXT SENTENCE cannot be used inside an in-line PERFORM (unless the
compiler is broken) as it would require the IF to have an explicit scope
terminator which makes the clause unusable.

> > in C, perl or C++ can I do:
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > };


    PERFORM UNTIL ( condition )
        statement
        statement
        IF NOT ( condition2 )
           statement
           statement
        END-IF
     END-PERFORM
```

```
I wonder if there is any consideration of adding a command to loop
back to the start of a perform in any of the new COBOL standards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T19:07:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ADl%7.2534$n93.260929@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de> <u3rjrf8usrkg87@corp.supernews.com> <a1kl84$atv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <u3rmpa4e56hq8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Dave <begleyd@chartermi.net> wrote in message
news:u3rmpa4e56hq8b@corp.supernews.com...
>
> I wonder if there is any consideration of adding a command to loop
> back to the start of a perform in any of the new COBOL standards.
> I've been using COBOL for a long time, but I haven't been paying
> attention to the proposed changes.

Hmmm, these all do the same thing; you'll probably find one you'll like....


MOVE 1 to N
PERFORM
   DISPLAY N
   IF N LESS  9
        ADD 1 TO N
       EXIT PERFORM CYCLE    <<< go to implied label immediately preceding
END-PERFORM
   ELSE
      EXIT PERFORM                 <<< go to implied label immediately
following END-PERFORM
   END-IF
END-PERFORM


MOVE 1 to N
PERFORM 9 TIMES
  DISPLAY N
 ADD 1 TO N
END-PERFORM

MOVE 1 TO N
PERFORM UNTIL N GREATER 9
   DISPLAY N
   ADD 1 TO N
END-PERFORM


MCM
```

```
I think the posters original question was how to get back to the start
of the PERFORM. It sounds like "Exit Perform Cycle" does that, and for
my own curiosity, I am going to see if it's available on any platform
I am currently using.

work.  The other examples are just normal loops, where I don't see any
break to the top?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T16:05:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3e02b3.0@newsman.viper.net>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de> <u3rjrf8usrkg87@corp.supernews.com> <a1kl84$atv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <u3rmpa4e56hq8b@corp.supernews.com> <ADl%7.2534$n93.260929@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <u3rtmmbvuthjca@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> I think the posters original question was how to get back to the start
> of the PERFORM. It sounds like "Exit Perform Cycle" does that, and for
> my own curiosity, I am going to see if it's available on any platform
> I am currently using.

in microfocus cobol, exit perform cycle does indeed get you back to the top
of inline perform...comes in handy sometimes...not sure if other vendors
have this implemented or not...

perform until exit        <--
   if a = a                 |
     exit perform cycle   ---
   end-if
   do this
   do that
   if b = b
     exit perform         ---
   end-if                   |
end-perform.                |
                          <--

kenmullins
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:a1ldf3$1vru$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
> EXIT PERFORM and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE are in the proposed standard for
COBOL.

We use EXIT PERFORM to replace the following silliness (as one example):

PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL INDX > 1000
    ...
   IF something
      MOVE 1000 TO INDX
      END-IF
END-PEFORM.
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T19:32:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f5H%7.707$uo.375112@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de> <u3rjrf8usrkg87@corp.supernews.com> <a1kl84$atv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <u3rmpa4e56hq8b@corp.supernews.com> <ADl%7.2534$n93.260929@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <u3rtmmbvuthjca@corp.supernews.com> <3c3e02b3.0@newsman.viper.net> <u3s26a7ilq30b1@corp.supernews.com> <a1ldf3$1vru$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ybC%7.434088$C8.31622844@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse <jerrymouse@invalid.com> wrote in message
news:ybC%7.434088$C8.31622844@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...

> We use EXIT PERFORM to replace the following silliness (as one example):
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Although I rarely see others use it (why not I have no clue), for processing
each item of a table like this, I really love the "TIMES" option....

MOVE 1 to SUB1
PERFORM
         NUMBER-OF-ITEMS
               TIMES
     dosomething with ITEM(SUB1)
    ADD 1 TO SUB1
END-PERFORM


Bonus #1:  If you set up WS items representing your table sizes, you can use
this to write code in which, if you need to change a table size, you only
have to change the WS items and the OCCURS clause, and never have to change
any of the in-line processing code. (In MF cobol, I think you can define a
table, " OCCURS '78-level item' TIMES" , but I know that does not work in
IBM COBOL/390).

Bonus #2: If NUMBER-OF-ITEMS is zero, the PERFORM loop executes --- ZERO
times, which is usually just what you want!

MCM




MCM
```

```
On 11-Jan-2002, "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

> Although I rarely see others use it (why not I have no clue), for
> processing
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> IBM COBOL/390).

You can do the same thing with a perform varying.  It accepts variables -
with any compiler.

> Bonus #2: If NUMBER-OF-ITEMS is zero, the PERFORM loop executes --- ZERO
> times, which is usually just what you want!

Same thing with a perform varying.

If there is a variable that iterates, I prefer a PERFORM VARYING, with the
iteration right in front.  If someday someone adds an exit to the loop
logic, the iteration still occurs.   The only time I code ADD 1 TO SUB1 is
if I don't always want to do this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T23:00:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0201112300.3b9d846a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de> <u3rjrf8usrkg87@corp.supernews.com> <a1kl84$atv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <u3rmpa4e56hq8b@corp.supernews.com> <ADl%7.2534$n93.260929@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <u3rtmmbvuthjca@corp.supernews.com> <3c3e02b3.0@newsman.viper.net> <u3s26a7ilq30b1@corp.supernews.com> <a1ldf3$1vru$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ybC%7.434088$C8.31622844@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com> wrote in message news:<ybC%7.434088$C8.31622844@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
> news:a1ldf3$1vru$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-PEFORM.

Instead of 
 PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL INDX > 1000
     ...
    IF something
       EXIT PERFORM
       END-IF
 END-PEFORM.

Try it this way:
 PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1
                 UNTIL INDX > 1000
                 OR    something 
     ...

 END-PEFORM.

Note that the END-IF **MUST** be coded, even tho it is NEVER
"executed"!  In a more complex statement, that EXIT PERFORM could kill
ya!  There are very good reasons for the "rule" of one entrance, one
exit in Modular Strustured Programming.  I realize that the
"something" might be very complex, but one other advantage is that you
know, going into the PERFORM, what are its limitations/conditions.

EXIT PERFORM
EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
EXIT PARAGRAPH
EXIT SECTION
are ALL non-structured syntax, and should be avoided.  I did my best
to keep them out of the new standard, but....
The EXIT PERFORM CYCLE can usually be avoided by careful construction
of the conditions inside the PERFORM.
For example:

PERFORM etc.
     statement 1
     IF CONDITION-1
          EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
     END-IF
     statement 2
     statement 3
END-PERFORM

can be replaced with 

PERFORM etc.
     statement 1
     IF CONDITION-1
          CONTINUE
     ELSE
          statement 2
          statement 3
     END-IF
END-PERFORM

or 
PERFORM etc.
     statement 1
     IF NOT CONDITION-1
         statement 2
         statement 3
     END-IF
END-PERFORM

Personally, I think it is clearer, easier to maintain, and less likely
to cause problems if the PERFORM gets complex enough to split up.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T07:54:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3E9A2D.EF29AEB2@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de> <u3rjrf8usrkg87@corp.supernews.com> <a1kl84$atv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <u3rmpa4e56hq8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Dave wrote:
> 
> I wonder if there is any consideration of adding a command to loop
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> method to get back to the start of a perform you miss it.

All languages are, to a greater or lesser extent, idiomatic.  Each idiom
is somewhat different from others.  The Cobol idiom for 'return to start
of loop' from a couple of decades ago was:

      PERFORM Do-it-again
          UNTIL
      ....


   Do-It-Again SECTION.
   Do-It-Start.

      ....
      IF ( rest-not-needed )
          GO TO Do-it-Again
      .
      ....

   Do-It-Exit.
      EXIT.

As you can see the 'continue' in other languages functions as a GO TO.

In modern Cobol the idiom may be:

          PERFORM UNTIL ...
              ....
              IF ( rest-needed )
                  ....
              END-IF
          END-PERFORM
```

#### ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-10T13:49:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1kr7t$tr6$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de>`

```
Tell us what COBOL compiler you are using.

If you are using Micro Focus (for example), the
   EXIT PERFORM
     and
   EXIT PERFORM CYCLE

allow for (respectively) the exit of an inline perform and the return to the
start of the perform "cycle"

(See one of the notes in this thread for more details).

All COBOL compilers that support the *next*  COBOL Standard will also
support these options (along with EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT SECTION)
```

#### ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>

```
I read most of the replies.... but didn't see many solutions:

Funny too - most of the QUESTION is in the ANSWER - that's how English like
COBOL is!

Your quesiton for reference:

>with "continue" I can jump to the head of the Loop. How Can I make this in 
>Cobol
>perform with test after until (   )


Ansi-85 Solution

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL (     )
statement
statement
IF (   )
CONTINUE
ELSE
statement
statement
END-IF
END-PERFORM

New COBOL Solution

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL (   )
statement
statement
If NOT (   )
EXIT PERFORM
END-IF
statement
statement
END-PERFORM

>Hello,
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: continue-Statemen in perform

- **From:** Rosa Fischer <Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-14T11:11:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C42AEDF.60C86B75@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<3c3d8709$1@netnews.web.de>`

```
Hi everybody,

in the next standard there will be a
"EXIT PERFORM" to leave the inline PERFORM loop and
"EXIT PERFORM CYCLE" to leave the PERFORM loop for the next test. In some of
the newer compiler like Fujitsu-Siemens mainframe compiler this feature was
supported from the beginning.

Best wishes for the new year to everybody
Rosa
```

## Thread: Reading Cobol files (.DAT) — 11 msgs

### Reading Cobol files (.DAT)

- **From:** "Alessandro Iavarone" <alex.iava@tiscalinet.it>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T14:39:49+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1mq26$s2j$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>`

```
Hi,
I would like to read some Cobol files (.DAT - .IDX - from MicroFocus) from
ASP pages (or inside a VB component).
Any ideas?

Thanks
Alex
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading Cobol files (.DAT)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T13:50:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R4C%7.433982$C8.31617484@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<a1mq26$s2j$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>`

```

"Alessandro Iavarone" <alex.iava@tiscalinet.it> wrote in message
news:a1mq26$s2j$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it...
> Hi,
> I would like to read some Cobol files (.DAT - .IDX - from MicroFocus) from
> ASP pages (or inside a VB component).
> Any ideas?

If the file is an ordinary text file, read it like any other.

If the file has a companion .IDX file, the file is a MicroFocus COBOL
Indexed file. All indexed file formats are proprietary to the compiler
vendor and cannot be accessed directly. You must either write a COBOL
program to reformat the file in question to a text file or obtain a
third-party conversion tool (sorry, I don't keep up with these tools but I'm
sure someone else will help out here).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading Cobol files (.DAT)

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T16:36:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3f5b8d.0@newsman.viper.net>`
- **References:** `<a1mq26$s2j$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it> <R4C%7.433982$C8.31617484@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C3F2DFD.5992@paralynx.com>`

```
I believe that the MicroFocus's external file handler can be called from
another language to access MF datasets...EXTFH...If you have an MF compiler,
there should be documentation for using EXTFH...Basicly you would call EXTFH
passing an op-code and an FCD (File Control Description) area...

I've pasted some of the available op-codes for demo purposes...

Opcode  Operation                     File Types
------  ----------------------------  ----------
00      OPEN INPUT                    L  S  R  I
01      OPEN OUTPUT                   L  S  R  I
02      OPEN I-O                      L  S  R  I
03      OPEN EXTEND                   L  S  R  I
04      OPEN INPUT WITH NO REWIND     L  S
05      OPEN OUTPUT WITH NO REWIND    L  S
08      OPEN INPUT REVERSED           L  S

You can obtain samples of cobol programs calling EXTFH to access files from
a cobol program instead of the normal open, get, write, close, etc...Should
provide enough info for developing a module in another language to do the
same types of things...See the mf support web site for samples...

kenmullins
newnan, ga usa
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading Cobol files (.DAT)

- **From:** leclaire <leclaire@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T13:59:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C3EEF16.7F645B51@rr.com>`
- **References:** `<a1mq26$s2j$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>`

```
If you wish to actually use the index then you're probably out of luck
(anyone else here know of an add-in that reads isam?).  But converting
the data should be relatively simple.  

The .DAT file is probably fixed-length records with a 2-byte prefix on
each record.  The .DAT file itself has a 128-byte header.  I frequently
use Opt-tech sort to do this.

If the file is variable length records, there is a 4-byte header which
contains each individual record length.
```

```
MicroFocus) from
> ASP pages (or inside a VB component).
> Any ideas?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have used CObject control from Ratrix (http://www.ratrix.com/) to
read MicroFocus data files in a VB program.  It maps the fields and
creates some code for reading and writing records.  You have to have
the Cobol DLL's which you probably do if you have the files.

Another option would be an ODBC product for the files.  I tried
something from MicroFocus called "Correlate" a long time ago and if it
is still around it will work, but it was expensive (at least for me).
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading Cobol files (.DAT)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T10:02:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4009B5.E97F6B47@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a1mq26$s2j$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>`

```
Alessandro Iavarone wrote:
> 
> I would like to read some Cobol files (.DAT - .IDX - from MicroFocus) from
> ASP pages (or inside a VB component).

I use Cobol (MF as it happens) to do Web serving, but then I find ASP to
be backwards to the way I want to do things.  Also I want to update
files that are used by the main business application (eg allocating
stock as an order is placed over the Web) so just reading isn't an
option and correct locking is vital to ensure there is no corruption of
the index files.

Where ASP is HTML with references to executable code to 'fill in the
data' I use templates which are HTML with tags where the data is to be
merged (including repitition and selection).  This (IMHO) gives much
better control over the sites while giving even better isolation of
content and appearance.

It also will run just about anywhere - DOS, Windows, Unix, Linux, OS/2,
or whatever comes next.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading Cobol files (.DAT)

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>

- **References:** `<a1mq26$s2j$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>`

```
Hi Alessandro,

Since you need to develop an ASP application, using ODBC could be a very
good choice for you. There are a number of ODBC drivers for Cobol Data Files
nowdays that provide you with an SQL view (which is perfect for ASP's
VBScripts) of a traditional indexed file.

Check www.transoft.com for U/SQL, a Cobol ODBC driver that I have been using
for years without any problems (from ASP pages too!).

Of course, this software does not come for free and you will have to check
for pricing with the vendors.

Panos
```

## Thread: MicroFocus Fileshare - HP-UX - Can't get it started — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus Fileshare - HP-UX - Can't get it started

- **From:** bstokesb@dunn-edwards.net (Bob Stokesbary)
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T10:46:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb005c56.0201111046.12de40fa@posting.google.com>`

```
I really have 2 questions.

1) Is anybody using MicroFocus Fileshare in a real production
environment?  We have 73 remote sites and 3 servers that need to be
networked together.  Am I wasting my time to even consider this
product for this environment?  I can find nothing in any of the
threads on this subject that gives me a clue as to how people are
using this product or even if they are using it in a serious
implementation.  (All the threads I could find looked like experiments
and not serious implementation issues.)

2) How did others get CCITCP up and running? We have read all the
threads and done all "the right things" and still we are getting the
classic 'cannot find mfcobol port in the services file' message.  We
have done everything we can think of.  We have put it at the top of
the list, we have put it in numeric order (with 86 right behind 85). 
We have separated the mfcobol and the 86/udp with spaces and with a
tab and with two tabs.  (And yes, we have no leading or trailing
spaces in the services file.)  Nothing works.  The current environment
for HP-UX is B.10.20 (which cannot change due to an Oracle
installation -- "you just have to love it") and MicroFocus cobol
v4.0.05L-e.

I guess there is a third question: Am I just wasting my time with this
or is there just one small step to go and everything will be
beautiful?  We have a lot of development that is depending on this and
cannot take the risk of failure.  So if this is a problematic feature
of MF COBOL, please let me know now.

Thanks in advance for any input.
Bob Stokesbary
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Fileshare - HP-UX - Can't get it started

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2002-01-11T16:47:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c3f5e21.0@newsman.viper.net>`
- **References:** `<fb005c56.0201111046.12de40fa@posting.google.com>`

```
Well, I am not a unix person...This is how we do fileshare via an NT or 2000
server machine...

We run fileshare without using CCITCP2 at all...We use a CCI.INI file to
define the fixed ports and protocols to be used...Sample below...

[ccitcp-servers]
ESSRV01=MFPORT:8000
ESSRV02=MFPORT:8100
SCSRV01=MFPORT:8200
SCSRV02=MFPORT:8210
NPSRV01=MFPORT:8300

[ccitcp-targets]
ESSRV01=MFNODE:DELL4200,MFPORT:8000
ESSRV02=MFNODE:DELL4200,MFPORT:8100
SCSRV01=MFNODE:DELL4200,MFPORT:8200
SCSRV02=MFNODE:SCSRV02,MFPORT:8210
NPSRV01=MFNODE:TOSHIBA,MFPORT:8300

If the cci.ini file is set up this way, we do not need to run CCITCP2 on any
of the server machines...

Again, not sure about fileshare on a unix machine though...Here is an
excerpt from the NetExpress CCI Book:

5.2.2.1 Direct Connection and Starting Servers on Fixed Ports
A CCITCP2 registration daemon is not necessary if the CCI client application
and the CCI server application establish a connection directly between each
other. This can only be done when the server application starts up on a
specific TCP port, and the client knows which address and port the server is
using.

In a relatively static application enterprise environment where the server
application location is well-defined and persistent, then using direct
connection can have advantages over using a CCITCP2 registration process to
help establish a connection.

Allowing a server application to listen on a known, fixed port means that
clients can contact the server through a security firewall if a gap has been
configured for that port and address.

A CCI server can support both direct connection and normal connection via a
CCITCP2 daemon simultaneously. If a server starts on a fixed port, and a
CCITCP2 daemon is present, then it will register a Server Name with it as
normal, but in addition clients will be able to connect to it directly
because the port it is using is known. If the CCITCP2 daemon is not present
or reachable then a fixed port server will still start successfully, but
only clients who attempt to connect directly will be able to contact it.


hope this helps some

kenmullins
newnan, ga usa

> Bob Stokesbary
```

```
Ken,

Thanks very much.  I did read that part and was just about to try it. 
But, I was so discouraged with the CCITCP problem I didn't get there. 
I can see that you also seem to be on at least more than a one-to-one
situation.  Would you rate your environment as "iffy" or "solid as a
rock" (or somewhere in between).

And too, according to all the documentation I have (including
MicroSoft's on-line documentation) your method will work on unix as
well as NT.  And fortunately, we have a stable network and a lot of
the addresses are "firm".

One thing that was not clear, but is now with your configuration (and
makes sense once you see it) is the different ports for each of the
clients to talk with the server.  I take it that MicroFocus handles
file/record locking below that point so that you would not have data
corruption if two processes were trying to get the same record at the
same time.  (Not a serious concern to us at the moment, but I want to
be careful.)

Thanks ever so much for your feedback.  Yours is the first post I have
seen that really dealt with a real production environment.

Sincerely,
Bob Stokesbary
```

```
We have been using Fileshare in Unix for several years with several SCO Unix
and IBM AIX servers on a LAN as well as both local clients and SCO clients
on a WAN.  The application is a distributed point of sale system.  We run
the single ccitcp2 session on a SCO server and all servers and local and WAN
clients see it properly and it works fine.  The only problem we had with
Fileshare was when we tried to run the ccitcp2 program on an AIX system,
only the local clients could see it but the WAN clients could not.  MF
support informed us that since it worked okay for local clients the problem
had to be in the network configuration of AIX and when we went back to
running ccitcp2 on a SCO box everything works fine.

I did not realize that the Netexpress direct access for fileshare would work
with our Object Cobol Development Systems (4.1.30).  Where do you put the
"ini" file? I will have to check with the answerlab.

Joshua Seltzer
Larich Distributors, Inc.
```

```
available for download from http://www.adtools.com/dotnet/index.html.

to start migrating their COBOL code into the .NET Framework. Also the syntax
extensions supporting .NET coding have been firmed up so you can work with
the syntax that will be in the released product.

download service, and answer a few survey questions.

98, Me, and NT 4.0 as well.
```

```
Rick,

I've never heard of XCOBOL on VMS - but for DEC COBOL on VMS I use
this:

COBOL/LIST/COPYLIST whatever.COBOL

Now, this DOES do a compile but who cares?  It doesn't link it, and
does give you a full listing with copybooks expanded.
```

## Thread: Memory file I/O operation. — 22 msgs

### Memory file I/O operation.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T04:29:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
           I would like to know whether there is command in COBOL for
memory file I/O operation similar to C language.
For example what is command in COBOL similar to 
                  FILE *fp1;
         fp1=fopen("a.b(mbr1)","w,type=memory"); 
in c.

Thanks and Regards.
shyam nivas
```

#### ↳ Re: Memory file I/O operation.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T14:06:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ypX%7.102$J_3.109824@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com>`

```
shyam nivas <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>            I would like to know whether there is command in COBOL for
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> in c.

AFAIK, the COBOL language does not (currently? Wasn't this one of the things
under consideration for future COBOL standards?) have any native "language"
support for dynamic memory allocation. However, I know  COBOL/390 (IBM
mainframe) and any COBOL/Windows which can call the WInAPI can access system
services to do this.

However, when doing this there is no ability to use "regular" file verbs
like READ, WRITE, etc on the handle obtained from "fopen", unless the
operating system treats a handle is a handle is a handle and the COBOL
system can be configured/set to use the system handle rather than its own
internal handle. (Under windows, the API provides read/write/position calls
which allow files to be treated transparently regardless of "physical"
storage).

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Memory file I/O operation.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T23:38:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0201152338.95ca3f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com> <ypX%7.102$J_3.109824@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Hi Michael,
                   I tried memory file I/O in  cobol and it is running
fine.It has no relation with dynamic memory allocation because before
execution of job step which is using memory file one have to create
the file in previous job step,the only difference is instead of giving
UNIT=SYSDA for DASD file you have to give UNIT=VIO for memory
file.Following is the jcl and program i used.
***************************************************************************
(a).COBOL PROGRAM:
  IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.CBPROG.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
            SELECT WRKFILE ASSIGN TO WRKDD.
      DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
      
       FD  WRKFILE
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           RECORD CONTAINS 200 CHARACTERS
           LABEL RECORD STANDARD
           RECORDING MODE IS F.
       01  RCL-WRK-FILE                     PIC X(200).
      WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      
       01 WS-VAR                             PIC X(200).
       01 WS-VAR1                            PIC X(200).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
            MOVE 'THIS IS WRITTEN IN MEMORYFILE MEMORY FILE I-O
HAPPEN'
                       TO WS-VAR.
                       OPEN OUTPUT WRKFILE.
                       WRITE RCL-WRK-FILE FROM WS-VAR.
                       CLOSE WRKFILE.
                       OPEN INPUT WRKFILE.
                       READ WRKFILE INTO WS-VAR1.
                       CLOSE WRKFILE.
                       DISPLAY WS-VAR1.
 STOP RUN.
*****************************************************************************

(B).JCL:
/SWI6078R JOB MSGCLASS=X,MSGLEVEL=(1,1),CLASS=C,                      
JOB04223
//         REGION=5M,NOTIFY=&SYSUID
//STEP1    EXEC PGM=IDCAMS
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
//INFILE   DD *
         THIS IS LINE 1
         THIS IS LINE 2
//OUTFILE DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=SWI6078.BUFFER.TEST(MEM1)
        REPRO INFILE(INFILE) OUTFILE(OUTFILE)
//SYSLIN  DD  DSN=&&TEMPPDS(WRKFILE),UNIT=VIO,
//             DISP=(MOD,PASS),SPACE=(TRK,(50,20,1)),
//             DCB=(RECFM=FB,LRECL=200,BLKSIZE=400)
//*****************************************************************
//STEP2   EXEC PGM=CBPROG,REGION=4096K
//STEPLIB  DD  DSN=SWI6078.TEST.LOAD3,DISP=SHR
//WRKDD    DD DSN=*.STEP1.SYSLIN,DISP=SHR
//SYSOUT   DD  SYSOUT=*
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*

*************************************************************************
                   Regarding dynamic memory allocation in COBOL if
program is compiled with 'DYN' option then working storage variable is
allocated at run time but this is not like more advance language C/C++
where we can also control amount of memory allocated at run time.
                  Another option for dynamic memory allocation is
calling subroutine of LE/370 if you have LE.
****************************************************************************
Thanks and Regards.
shyam nivas
Cognizant Technology Solution.
*************************************************************************
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:<ypX%7.102$J_3.109824@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>...
> shyam nivas <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
> news:c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Memory file I/O operation.

- **From:** none@none.com (wildman)
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T14:38:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c405ffa.749303@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com>`

```
You need to describe the compiler and platform.  Memory I/O is
platform specific.  It is not part of the COBOL language standard.  In
C, memory allocation appears standard but it really depends on the
platform - Unix, Windows, VAX, HP, etc.  You CALL a memory routine
just like you do in C language.   Neither language has a  given
'command'.

With Micro Focus COBOL on a Windows/DOS platform, the compiler is
shipped with a set of routines that include memory allocation.  For
example, you can allocate a chunk of memory and access it with the
MOVE statement using reference modifiers.   

If I'm not mistaken, it is virtually the same routine that C language
uses with an interface suitable for COBOL, just like the Stream IO
library for COBOL  - sound familiar?

On 12 Jan 2002 04:29:31 -0800, nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam
nivas) wrote:

>Hi,
>           I would like to know whether there is command in COBOL for
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>shyam nivas
```

#### ↳ Re: Memory file I/O operation.

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T12:00:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0201121200.76d6d490@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com>`

```
nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas) wrote in message news:<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com>...
> Hi,
>            I would like to know whether there is command in COBOL for
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> shyam nivas

It has been possible to use files in memory on IBM mainframes for any
programming language since at least the mid 70's.  This is done by
specifying UNIT=VIO in the JCL for the execution of the program, The
TSO ALLOC command can be used similarly.  Some installations might
disallow this or give it different names depending on their standards.
 In these cases the files can have seqential, indexed or relative
organisation and can be QSAM or VSAM.  The normal file commands OPEN,
READ, WRITE, REWRITE, START, DELETE and CLOSE can all be used with
such files, their use is even transparent to the configuration
section.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Memory file I/O operation.

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-01-13T13:42:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0201131342.2c096402@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0201121200.76d6d490@posting.google.com> <c02fd744.0201130427.6d1b3aad@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

Thinking about your original question some more, I think that the file
a.b(mbr) probably already exists and is permanent, i.e. resides on
disk as it appears to be a PDS.  (I don't know whether PDSes can use
VIO, but can't see much point as they are normally permanent files.) 
In this case UNIT=VIO can not be used unless the member is copied to a
temporary file for which UNIT=VIO can be used. Obviously a file in
memory isn't permanent, so if you want to keep it and use memory, then
it must be loaded up into memory to do the processing, then unloaded
to disk to keep it.  However, since PDS members are usually not very
large, I doubt whether there is much point, the overheads of the
temporary location in memory may outweigh the benefits.  Another way
of getting a file into memory is specify the buffering in the JCL such
that the entire file is in the file buffers, this is more likely to be
the solution to your problem.  Another solution would be to read the
entire file into a table and use normal table processing for the
accesses, then, if necessary, rewrite the amended table back to the
file at the end of the job.

I am not very familiar with C so don't know what the FILE command you
used as an example does.

Hope this helps, Robert

nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas) wrote in message news:<c02fd744.0201130427.6d1b3aad@posting.google.com>...
> Hi Robert,
>                   As you suggested UNIT=VIO will serve our purpose of
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Memory file I/O operation.

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-14T18:48:03+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3q564ukqna6pe33op4vjho6u2mr9q18bb6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0201121200.76d6d490@posting.google.com> <c02fd744.0201130427.6d1b3aad@posting.google.com>`

```
On 13 Jan 2002 04:27:25 -0800, nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam
nivas) wrote:

>Hi Robert,
>                  As you suggested UNIT=VIO will serve our purpose of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>*****************************************************************************
VIO files (Virtual IO) can only be used for temporary data sets, i.e.
datasets that are created and deleted during one and the same job.
For these files all the normal commands, like OPEN, CLOSE, READ, WRITE
etc are indeed possible

Now I know just enough about C to cause serious harm.  What does the
memory file I/O operation really do?  Without more info I can only
guess what could be of help to you.  One thing that comes to mind is
DIV (Data In Virtual) which allows you to associate a huge in memory
array (in virtual storage) to a VSAM file.  You then do not use any
file access commands, but just access the table/memory location you
are interested in.  Only the data that you access will we read into
memory (automatically), and the updated records will be written back
at task/program/process termination

You should also look up Data Windowing Services, which allow a high
level language nearly the same functionality as DIV
                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

```

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)

```
Hi,
         Regarding your first reply about VIO file I tried open and
close verb in cobol but Error message is sysout indicates that
open,close like verbs are not allowed on VIO file .The error message I
got is
  " There was an unsuccessful OPEN or CLOSE of file WRKDD in program
CBPROG,The status code was 90."
              If you have done it previously then please send me the
program and jcl.Program and JCL I am using is as follows.
*****************************************************************************
(a).My cobol source code is :
******************************************
*This is a test program for VIO file I/O.*
******************************************
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.               
 PROGRAM-ID.CBPROG.                     
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                  
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.                 
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.                  
 FILE-CONTROL.                          
     SELECT WRKFILE ASSIGN TO WRKDD.   
DATA DIVISION.   
 FILE SECTION. 
  FD  WRKFILE                                         
      BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS                        
      RECORD CONTAINS 200 CHARACTERS                  
      LABEL RECORD STANDARD                           
      RECORDING MODE IS F.                            
  01  RCL-WRK-FILE                     PIC X(200).   
  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                        
  01 WS-VAR                             PIC X(200).               
  01 WS-VAR1                            PIC X(200).    
********************************************************
*Ws-var is written in VIO file WRKFILE after that same * 
*written record is read to ws-var1 and displayed.      *
********************************************************            
  PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                             
       MOVE 'THIS IS WRITTEN IN MEMORYFILE MEMORY FILE I-O HAPPEN'
                  TO WS-VAR.                                      
                  OPEN OUTPUT WRKFILE.                            
                  WRITE RCL-WRK-FILE FROM WS-VAR.                 
                  CLOSE WRKFILE.                                  
                  OPEN INPUT WRKFILE.                             
                  READ WRKFILE INTO WS-VAR1.                      
                  CLOSE WRKFILE.                                  
                  DISPLAY WS-VAR1. 
    STOP RUN.   
*****************************************************************************
(b).Run jcl for cobol:
//SWI6078R JOB MSGCLASS=X,MSGLEVEL=(1,1),CLASS=C,              
//         REGION=5M,NOTIFY=&SYSUID                            
//*******************************************************************
//*Dummy step because temp PDS should be allocated before execution *
//*                of cobol program.                                * 
//*******************************************************************
//STEP1    EXEC PGM=IDCAMS                                     
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                                         
//INFILE   DD *                                                
         THIS IS LINE 1                                        
         THIS IS LINE 2                                        
//OUTFILE DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=SWI6078.BUFFER.TEST(MEM1)           
        REPRO INFILE(INFILE) OUTFILE(OUTFILE)  
//*************************************************************
//*       Here a temp. PDS is allocated in VIO.               *
//*************************************************************
//SYSLIN  DD  DSN=&&TEMPPDS(WRKFILE),UNIT=VIO,                 
//             DISP=(MOD,PASS),SPACE=(512,(50,20)),            
//             DCB=(RECFM=FB,LRECL=200,BLKSIZE=0)              
//*************************************************************
//*          cobol execution step                             *
//*************************************************************
//STEP2   EXEC PGM=CBPROG,REGION=4096K                         
//STEPLIB  DD  DSN=SWI6078.TEST.LOAD3,DISP=SHR                 
//WRKDD    DD DSN=*.STEP1.SYSLIN,DISP=SHR                      
//SYSOUT   DD  SYSOUT=*                                       
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*     
****************************************************************

Thanka and Warm Regards.
shyam
********************************************************************
```

```
VIO was not invented when COBOL was first defined so all of this has been a
learning
curve thing for me.

However, IF my memory serves, your program does this.

create a VIO file
write a record to the file
read the VIO file
print the content of the record.

If VIO is like any other file, then it seems to me that this should be
modified like this:

create a VIO file
Open the VIO file for Output.or perhaps I/O
Write the record
Read the record
print


At least that is what I got out of the exchange occuring here on this
subject.

Warren Simmons
```

```
Hi Dissen,
               you are right Dissen, my IDCAMS step is a dummy step
because it has no relation with my job but before execution of STEP2
temporary file should exist so i put STEP1 and this can be any step,
it does not have any relation with SYSLIN.    If you put
***************************************************************************
//WRKDD   DD  DSN=&&TEMPPD  (WRKFILE),UNIT=VIO,                 
//             DISP=(MOD,PASS),SPACE=(512,(50,20)),            
//             DCB=(RECFM=FB,LRECL=200,BLKSIZE=0)  
***************************************************************************
in execution step you will get JCL error with message in  JESYSMSG "
WRKDD - DATA SET NOT FOUND".
            Regarding JCL, above is not the correct one now I am using
:
****************************************************************************
//SYSLIN  DD  DSN=&&TEMPPDS(WRKFILE),UNIT=VIO,        
//             DISP=(MOD,PASS),SPACE=(TRK,(50,20,1)), 
//             DCB=(RECFM=FB,LRECL=200,BLKSIZE=400)  
****************************************************************************
   and my problem is solved.
Thanks and Regards.
shyam nivas
****************************************************************************
```

```
Hi Shyam,

I'm sorry to say that I am a bit pressed for time tonight, but can
hopefully be some use.

I don't currently have access to a mainframe, so can't produce tested
examples, however, I think that your allocation of the temporary PDS
is suspect for the following reasons

a)   The SPACE parameter looks very strange, I am not familiar with
the use of a  number as the first parameter, but it might be the size
of each block, in which case it should be the same as BLKSIZE in the
DCB - check the JCL manual

b)   For a PDS, I think the SPACE parameter should have a fourth
number to indicate the number of directory blocks? required e.g.
50,20,10 for ten directory blocks - check in the JCL manual for the
exact terminology

c)   BLKSIZE=0 in the DCB parameter looks really strange, I would have
thought that it should be a number that is a multiple of 200, but it
is possible that it is an IBM addition to specify something like full
track blocking - check the JCL manual

The program looks OK, though I am not quite sure about the SELECT
statement, I would have used "SELECT WRKFILE ASSIGN UT-S-WRKDD", but
this may not be necessary now, it's sometime since I used IBM COBOL
and things may have changed.

I have only used VIO for ordinary QSAM files and not PDSes, but I
expect they could be used this way, though I can't see much purpose as
files on VIO can only be temporary.  While I believe that VSAM files
can also be used in VIO, I have no practical experience of doing so
and would prefer to check the manual before trying it.

Regards Robert
```

- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0201121200.76d6d490@posting.google.com> <c02fd744.0201130427.6d1b3aad@posting.google.com> <3q564ukqna6pe33op4vjho6u2mr9q18bb6@4ax.com> <c02fd744.0201150001.60bc80df@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0201151451.6b61baf0@posting.google.com> <c02fd744.0201152305.5bdfbc86@posting.google.com>`

```
nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas) wrote in message news:<c02fd744.0201152305.5bdfbc86@posting.google.com>...
> Hi Robert, 
>                Many Many thanks Robert.Its really a mistake in JCL.I
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> shyam nivas

Hello Shyam

just use "write output-record from input-record"
where output-record is the record description in the output file FD
and input-record is the record description in the input file FD.

However, for this task as you have explained it so far, I would tend
to use file buffering to hold the records in memory from the file you
are reading and not bother copying the file to a temporary file in
VIO, you can specify the BUFNO=n parameter in the DCB to do this,
where n is the number of buffers required.

e.g. //inputdd    dd dsn=infile,disp=shr,
     //              dcb=(bufno=n)

To work out the number of buffers you want to use, divide the number
of records to be read by the number of records in a block.  There is
probably a limit to the number of buffers that can be used, so a large
blocking factor would be desirable.

for effective use of VIO, I would be inclined to use a block size of
4000 and allocate the space in units of 4000, since the file is being
held in memory and 4000 is the highest multiple of 200 that will fit
in 4096 bytes which is the size of a page in memory.  Larger multiples
of 200 could also be used, such as 8000 which would fit in two pages,
I think the lowest multiple using no wasted space in pages is 102,400
for 25 pages at a time.  Whether or not the operating system would
accept such a block size, you would have to try out yourself. 
However, there wouldn't be any point using such a large block size if
the file doesn't contain enough records to use it all.  I don't know
what the operating system think the size of a track is in VIO, it may
be that it just uses an optimal multiple, on the other hand it might
not.


Regards Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Memory file I/O operation.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

```
There have been a TON of replies to this original note.  Having been off CLC
for a week or so, I haven't seen ANY explanation of WHY one would want to do
this (other than to "write C in COBOL").  It seems to me that the programmer
is "thinking" in C and writing in COBOL.

Especially as (from later notes) we "know" that

A) This is being done in an OS/390 environment
B) The programmer wants to "pass" the file from one step to the next

I just canNOT think of a good reason to do this.  (I am a great believer
that there is always at least ONE program somewhere that has a "real world"
need to do ANYTHING - so I am not saying that there isn't a good reason for
this ... I just can't PERSONALLY imagine what it is)

   ***

Especially given the SPACE= definition given, this seems to that there are
probability MUCH better ways to do this in COBOL that the specific method
mentioned.  (Most likely either a "storage table" or simply saving one or
two records as you read the next one or two).

Shyam,
  Can you let me (and the rest of CLC) know WHY you want to this?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Memory file I/O operation. ===> BatchPipes ???

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T00:54:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.16b5770cca94958598968b@news.charter.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201120429.27d2ea3c@posting.google.com> <a29m0l$s35$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
just a thought, do you think that he may be attempting to pass data 
between processes in memory ???  

I have used BatchPipes in OS/390 very effectively under the right 
conditions. We we able to reduce a batch stream from 12 hours to less 
than 5 using BatchPipes. Since batchpipes only communicates between 
jobs, we had to break jobs up into single step jobs to make maximum use 
of batch pipes...

                    Chuck Haatvedt

In article <a29m0l$s35$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,
```

## Thread: Parsing a copyfile to an XML and create DTD .. — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parsing a copyfile to an XML and create DTD ..

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2002-01-13T06:41:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0201130641.4607ddff@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Ei%%7.559$8e6.20281@news4.ulv.nextra.no> <c8035ce7.0201121449.d14a379@posting.google.com> <_s508.742$8e6.24157@news4.ulv.nextra.no>`

```
We are currently using this only in the lab and internally for some CA
products. It is not ready for prime time, even as an alpha product.

The current version is highly dependant on our COBOL compiler so we
could only consider shipping test copies to exisiting CA-Realia
clients.

What are you expecting as output? The current version generates an XML
definition of the copy member in a generalized format. I'll post the
schema (XSD) Monday. Variations could include generating a schema of
the copy member directly but of course that could be done with post
generation XSLT.

"Tommy Nilsen" <nilse-to@online.no> wrote in message news:<_s508.742$8e6.24157@news4.ulv.nextra.no>...
> Is it possible to download/test this prototype ?
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tommy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parsing a copyfile to an XML and create DTD ..

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2002-01-13T18:19:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0201131819.2f01002a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Ei%%7.559$8e6.20281@news4.ulv.nextra.no> <c8035ce7.0201121449.d14a379@posting.google.com> <_s508.742$8e6.24157@news4.ulv.nextra.no> <c8035ce7.0201130641.4607ddff@posting.google.com>`

```
Here's the schema I promised. I tried and failed to send it as an attachement.

<?xml version = "1.0" encoding = "UTF-8"?>
<!--Schema of COBOL Data Record Structure
    Copyright (c) 2001 Computer Associates International, Inc. -->
<xsd:schema xmlns:xsd = "http://www.w3.org/2000/10/XMLSchema">
    <xsd:element name = "group">
        <xsd:complexType>
            <xsd:sequence>
                <xsd:element ref = "group"/>
                <xsd:element ref = "character"/>
                <xsd:element ref = "internal"/>
                <xsd:element ref = "numeric"/>
            </xsd:sequence>
            <xsd:attribute name = "level" use = "required" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "stride" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "offset" use = "required" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "size" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "occurs" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "name" use = "required" type = "xsd:string"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "ID" type = "xsd:ID"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "src_line" type = "xsd:int"/>
        </xsd:complexType>
    </xsd:element>
    <xsd:element name = "numeric">
        <xsd:complexType>
            <xsd:attribute name = "level" use = "required" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "stride" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "offset" use = "required" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "scale" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "precision" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "size" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "picture" type = "xsd:string"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "occurs" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "usage">
                <xsd:simpleType>
                    <xsd:restriction base = "xsd:string">
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "comp"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "comp-1"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "comp-2"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "comp-3"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "comp-4"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "comp-5"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "binary"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "packed"/>
                    </xsd:restriction>
                </xsd:simpleType>
            </xsd:attribute>
            <xsd:attribute name = "sign">
                <xsd:simpleType>
                    <xsd:restriction base = "xsd:string">
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "unsigned"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "signed"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "signedleading"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "signedtrailing"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "seperateleading"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "seperatetrailing"/>
                    </xsd:restriction>
                </xsd:simpleType>
            </xsd:attribute>
            <xsd:attribute name = "name" use = "required" type = "xsd:string"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "ID" type = "xsd:ID"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "src_line" type = "xsd:int"/>
        </xsd:complexType>
    </xsd:element>
    <xsd:element name = "character">
        <xsd:complexType>
            <xsd:attribute name = "level" use = "required" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "stride" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "offset" use = "required" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "size" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "occurs" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "name" use = "required" type = "xsd:string"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "ID" type = "xsd:ID"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "src_line" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "picture" type = "xsd:string"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "justified" type = "xsd:string"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "usage">
                <xsd:simpleType>
                    <xsd:restriction base = "xsd:string">
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "display"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "national"/>
                    </xsd:restriction>
                </xsd:simpleType>
            </xsd:attribute>
        </xsd:complexType>
    </xsd:element>
    <xsd:element name = "internal">
        <xsd:complexType>
            <xsd:attribute name = "level" use = "required" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "stride" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "offset" use = "required" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "size" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "occurs" type = "xsd:int"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "usage">
                <xsd:simpleType>
                    <xsd:restriction base = "xsd:string">
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "index"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "pointer"/>
                        <xsd:enumeration value = "procedure-pointer"/>
                    </xsd:restriction>
                </xsd:simpleType>
            </xsd:attribute>
            <xsd:attribute name = "name" use = "required" type = "xsd:string"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "ID" type = "xsd:ID"/>
            <xsd:attribute name = "src_line" type = "xsd:int"/>
        </xsd:complexType>
    </xsd:element>
    <xsd:element name = "COBOLSymbolInformation">
        <xsd:complexType>
            <xsd:sequence>
                <xsd:element ref = "group"/>
                <xsd:element ref = "character"/>
                <xsd:element ref = "internal"/>
                <xsd:element ref = "numeric"/>
            </xsd:sequence>
        </xsd:complexType>
    </xsd:element>
</xsd:schema>
```

```
The DMS Software Reengineering Toolkit could easily do this.
It already has a full COBOL front end parser,
and name/type resolver.   Using an attribute evaluator,
it would be easy to walk the parse tree and spit
out and XML DTD.
```

## Thread: OT: To Doc - Ben Franklin Quotes — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: To Doc - Ben Franklin Quotes

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T13:50:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<04A3C2DE3AEEE3E7.73872ED26B73A338.82F0C4B4E5E45B74@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Doc,

I'm not sure what you are asking for in regards to your comments about
the Ben Franklin quotes I posted.  If you are wondering where Ben
first wrote those, I can't help you as the book I gleaned them from
with just a book of quotes of many people and did not often list he
original source.  My guess is that the majority of them cam from his
writings in "Poor Richard's Almanac".  However, here are a couple of
web sites that do give the source of some really good Ben quotes:

http://www.cp-tel.net/miller/BilLee/quotes/Franklin.html

and

http://www.ushistory.org/franklin/quotable/

is really good.

According to one of Ben's quotes, lawyers in his day were as respected
as they are in our day, hence the following:

"A countryman between two lawyers is like a fish between two cats."

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"The FBI is urging all Americans to beware of any 
letters or packages that have badly misspelled words. 
Man, this is going to be terrible news for the rap 
industry." -Jay Leno
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: To Doc - Ben Franklin Quotes

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-12T23:44:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1r3au$c6k$1@panix2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<04A3C2DE3AEEE3E7.73872ED26B73A338.82F0C4B4E5E45B74@lp.airnews.net>`

```
In article <04A3C2DE3AEEE3E7.73872ED26B73A338.82F0C4B4E5E45B74@lp.airnews.net>,
SkippyPB  <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>Doc,
>
>I'm not sure what you are asking for in regards to your comments about
>the Ben Franklin quotes I posted.

I'm looking for a kind of provenance outside of 'a book I read somewhere' 
or a geocities web-site... something from a library or a reputable source.  
A while back I ran across a quotation which was attributed to Franklin 
referring to Jews as 'vampires'; it contained the phrase 'subsist on 
Christians and other people not of their' and I did both Altavista and 
Google searches on it...

... and came up with nothing that I would respect for academic rigor.

>If you are wondering where Ben
>first wrote those, I can't help you as the book I gleaned them from
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.cp-tel.net/miller/BilLee/quotes/Franklin.html

Ahhhh... how *very* interesting!  While this, to me, counts as 'just 
another website take a look at this from that page:

--begin quoted text:

"They that can give up essential liberty to obtain a little temporary 
safety deserve neither liberty nor safety."
- Benjamin Franklin, Historical Review of Pennsylvania, 1759.

Now a search on "Historical Review of Pennsylvanie" leads one to, among 
other places:

http://www.bartleby.com/100/245.1.html

... and this shows a Bartleby's cite from Bartlett's.

To my training this is a bit more... substantial than 'a book I 
remembered'.

(speaking of Franklin and Jews... the quote of 'Think of these things, 
whence you came, where you are going, and to whom you must account' occurs 
in 'The Ethics of the Fathers')

>
>and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>is really good.

Greatly appreciated.

DD
```

```
Picked up the reference to this in today's paper. Considering the topic
came up recently, US citizens might be interested in taking a looksee.
Certainly gives you some idea where your foreign policy may be going.
Look at the document on 'Global Demographic Trends' :-

    www.cia.gov/

I'm incredulous that Wild Bill Donovan's OSS, which became the CIA, and
is essentially about spooks, decided to get into the PR business ! Must
be a goldmine for America's non-friends.

Can anybody help with the correct e-mail address for the KGB Archives ?
Having just read 'Spy Catcher', I'd love to find out for sure whether
Sir Roger Hollis, a former DG of MI5, was or was not a Soviet agent <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ISPF editor for PC

- **From:** cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass)
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T10:21:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<163715e7.0201151021.19e5416c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<CWt08.375240$5A3.142114030@news1.rdc2.pa.home.com> <0QF08.17944$Tq.166875@rwcrnsc54>`

```
"JerryS" <consults@netbox.com> wrote in message news:<0QF08.17944$Tq.166875@rwcrnsc54>...
> I use a product  "TEXTPAD" and it works great.  You can download a demo
> version at http://www.textpad.com
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Although TextPad is indeed a very nice editor for a very nice
registration price (about $25 US), it doesn't really "emulate" ISPF. 
OTOH, it does have many of the features that ISPF has, including block
selecting and hex mode editing. Also, you can set TextPad up to run
external programs from within the editor, so you can conceivably
compile from within TextPad.


Curtis
```
