<a id="t-19fe48538c"></a>
## Thread: C-Isam in Cobol/C — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### C-Isam in Cobol/C

- **From:** Daniel Niedzielski <daniel.niedzielski@web.de>
- **Date:** 2002-01-14T17:10:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4302dc$1@netnews.web.de>`

```
Hello,
Hallo,
I have problems with C-Isam. I would produce and read ISAM-Files in
C and in Cobol parallel, but I have problems with integer respectivily
9(0?). 


For Example in C: 


 ...
 TEST_KEY.k_flags=ISNODUPS;
 TEST_KEY.k_nparts=1;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_start=0;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_leng=2;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_type=INTTYPE; <-- wrong size?
 ...


in Cobol:
        01  TEST0001-SATZ.
            05 TEST-KEY.
               10 TEST-NR                  PIC  9(02). <--
            05 TEST-TEXT                   PIC  X(60).
            05 TEST-TEXT-2                 PIC  X(60).



this is o.k.:


 ...
 TEST_KEY.k_flags=ISNODUPS;
 TEST_KEY.k_nparts=1;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_start=0;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_leng=2;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_type=CHARTYPE; 
 ...


in Cobol:
        01  TEST0001-SATZ.
            05 TEST-KEY.
               10 TEST-NR                  PIC  X(02). <--
            05 TEST-TEXT                   PIC  X(60).
            05 TEST-TEXT-2                 PIC  X(60).



Who is able to help me.


the full source:
----------------



#include <stdio.h>
#include <isam.h>
#define SUCCESS 0


struct keydesc TEST_KEY;
int cc, fdTEST0001;
char TEST0001_SATZ[122];
int NUM;
char line[122];
main()
{
//TEST0001-anlegen 
 TEST_KEY.k_flags=ISNODUPS;
 TEST_KEY.k_nparts=1;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_start=0;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_leng=2;
 TEST_KEY.k_part[0].kp_type=INTTYPE;
 fdTEST0001=cc=isbuild("TEST0001", 122, &TEST_KEY, ISINOUT + ISEXCLLOCK);
 if (cc<SUCCESS)
 {
  printf("isbuild error %d for TEST0001 file\n", iserrno);
  exit(1);
 }
 isclose(fdTEST0001);


//TEST0001-lesen
 fdTEST0001=cc=isopen("TEST0001", ISAUTOLOCK+ISOUTPUT);
 if (cc<SUCCESS)
 {
  printf("isopen error %d for TEST0001 file\n", iserrno);
 }
 getTEST0001();
 addTEST0001();
 isclose(fdTEST0001);
}
getTEST0001()
{
 printf("Nummer eingeben: ");
 fgets(line, 80, stdin);
 sscanf(line, "%i", &NUM);
 stint(NUM, TEST0001_SATZ);


 printf("Text: ");
 fgets(line, 80, stdin);
 ststring(line, TEST0001_SATZ+2, 60);


 printf("Text 2: ");
 fgets(line, 80, stdin);
 ststring(line, TEST0001_SATZ+62, 20);


}
ststring(char *src, char *dest, int num)
 {
  int i;
  for (i=1;i<=num && *src != '\n' && *src != '\0' && src != 0; i++) 
*dest++=*src++; 


  while (i++ <=num) *dest++=' ';
}


addTEST0001()
{
 int cc;
 cc=iswrite(fdTEST0001, TEST0001_SATZ);
 if (cc != SUCCESS)
 {
  printf("iswrite error %d for TEST0001\n", iserrno);
  isclose(fdTEST0001);
  exit(1);
 }
}







       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. READING.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.


           SELECT TEST0001
           ASSIGN TO "TEST0001"
           ORGANIZATION INDEXED
           ACCESS DYNAMIC
           RECORD KEY TEST-KEY
           FILE STATUS TEST0001-STATUS.


       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
        FD  TEST0001
            LABEL RECORD IS STANDARD
            DATA  RECORD IS TEST0001-SATZ.
        01  TEST0001-SATZ.
            05 TEST-KEY.
               10 TEST-NR                  PIC  9(02).
            05 TEST-TEXT                   PIC  X(60).
            05 TEST-TEXT-2                 PIC  X(60).


       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  WAHL                            PIC  X(01).
       77  TEST0001-STATUS             PIC XX.
       77  DATEI-STATUS                PIC XX.
       77  FEHLER-DATEI                PIC X(08)       VALUE SPACES.
       77 INV-79                       PIC  X(79)      VALUE SPACES.
       77 FILLER                       PIC X(01)       VALUE "J".
          88 LOOP                                      VALUE "J".
          88 END-LOOP                                  VALUE "N".
 
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.


       DECLARATIVES.


       TEST0001-FEH SECTION.
           USE AFTER ERROR PROCEDURE ON TEST0001.
       TEST0001-FEH-001.
           MOVE TEST0001-STATUS TO DATEI-STATUS.
           MOVE "TEST0001" TO FEHLER-DATEI.
       TEST0001-FEH-999.
           EXIT.


       END DECLARATIVES.


       ANFANG SECTION .


           OPEN INPUT                  TEST0001.
           IF TEST0001-STATUS > "00"   GO TO FEHLER-ROUT          END-IF


           INITIALIZE                  TEST-KEY
           START TEST0001 KEY NOT LESS THAN TEST-KEY
             INVALID KEY PERFORM TEST0001-FEH-001
                            THRU TEST0001-FEH-999
           END-START
           IF TEST0001-STATUS = "23" PERFORM FEHLER-ROUT ELSE 
           IF TEST0001-STATUS > "02" GO TO FEHLER-ROUT     END-IF END-IF


           PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE UNTIL 
           (END-LOOP OR TEST0001-STATUS = "23")
            READ TEST0001 NEXT        AT END EXIT PERFORM       END-READ
            IF TEST0001-STATUS = "92" PERFORM FEHLER-ROUT
                                      EXIT PERFORM CYCLE          END-IF
            IF TEST0001-STATUS > "02" GO TO FEHLER-ROUT           END-IF


            DISPLAY TEST-NR           LINE 10 POSITION 10
            DISPLAY TEST-TEXT         LINE 11 POSITION 10
            DISPLAY TEST-TEXT-2       LINE 12 POSITION 10
            ACCEPT WAHL END-ACCEPT
           END-PERFORM
           CLOSE                      TEST0001
           STOP RUN.
           .
         FEHLER-ROUT.
             DISPLAY INV-79 LINE 24 POSITION 01 REVERSE.
             DISPLAY "(FEHLER)" LINE 24 POSITION 01 REVERSE
             DISPLAY DATEI-STATUS LINE 24 POSITION 12 REVERSE.
             DISPLAY "00" LINE 24 POSITION 14 REVERSE.
             DISPLAY FEHLER-DATEI LINE 24 POSITION 18 REVERSE
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "04"
                DISPLAY "*** FALSCHE SATZLï¿½NGE ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "05"
                DISPLAY "*** PERMANETER FEHLER ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "22"
                DISPLAY "*** SATZ BEREITS VORHANDEN ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "23"
                DISPLAY "*** SATZ NICHT VORHANDEN ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "30"
                DISPLAY "*** ERFOLGLOSE E/A-OPERATION ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "90"
                DISPLAY "****** DOPPELTER OPEN *******"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "91"
                DISPLAY "*** PERMANETER FEHLER ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "92"
                DISPLAY "*** SATZ IST IN BENUTZUNG ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS = "93"
                DISPLAY "*** DATEI GESPERRT ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             IF DATEI-STATUS > 30 AND DATEI-STATUS < 90
                DISPLAY "*** PERMANENTER FEHLER ***"
                LINE 24 POSITION 30 REVERSE.
             ACCEPT WAHL LINE 24 POSITION 00 TAB
             DISPLAY INV-79 LINE 24 POSITION 01.
             .
         FEHLER-ROUT-90000.
             STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: C-Isam in Cobol/C

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp081100@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-14T22:35:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G2J08.3056$5R2.281494749@news1.van.metronet.ca>`
- **References:** `<3c4302dc$1@netnews.web.de>`

```
A PIC 9(2) in COBOL is just a PIC X(2) that is right-justified and
zero-filled on the left, i.e. the value 3 would be stored as "03". If you
have a signed field, it does something weird with the units character, but
it's still essentially an alphanumeric string. To get numbers stored as
integers, you have to start playing with USAGE COMP-X stuff.


"Daniel Niedzielski" <daniel.niedzielski@web.de> wrote in message
news:3c4302dc$1@netnews.web.de...
> Hello,
> Hallo,
…[272 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C-Isam in Cobol/C

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp081100@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T17:31:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qHZ08.3061$5R2.282064611@news1.van.metronet.ca>`
- **References:** `<3c4302dc$1@netnews.web.de> <G2J08.3056$5R2.281494749@news1.van.metronet.ca> <3c44349b$1@netnews.web.de>`

```
The comp-x usages of cobol tend to be implementation-specific. Read your
manual to find out what comp-3 is on your machine. It it's stored internally
as a native INT, then you can easily read the field in C.

"Daniel Niedzielski" <daniel.niedzielski@web.de> wrote in message
news:3c44349b$1@netnews.web.de...
> "Dennis Edward" <temp081100@ipipeline.net> wrote:

> >zero-filled on the left, i.e. the value 3 would be stored as "03". If you
> >have a signed field, it does something weird with the units character,
but
> >it's still essentially an alphanumeric string. To get numbers stored as
> >integers, you have to start playing with USAGE COMP-X stuff.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C-Isam in Cobol/C

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Daniel Niedzielski <daniel.niedzielski@web.de>

```
"Dennis Edward" <temp081100@ipipeline.net> wrote:
>The comp-x usages of cobol tend to be implementation-specific. Read your
>manual to find out what comp-3 is on your machine. It it's stored internally
>as a native INT, then you can easily read the field in C.
>


Hallo,
the manual say:
For COMP-3 (packed-decimal storage), two digits are stored in each byte. An
additional half byte is allocated for the sign, even if the value is unsigned.
The sign is placed in the rightmost position. The sign value is 0x0D for
negative; all other values are treated as positive (but see rule 18 below).  Th
e size
of an item is divided by two (after including one for the implied sign) to
arrive at its actual size (rounding fractions up).


What do you think, can I read such value in C, C++ ... ?
```

```
Of course you can -- however, since it isn't a "native" format, the C
program will have to know how to handle it.

to IEEE datatypes (it was designed to allow non-COBOL programmers to access
COBOL-created data). So the answer is, "yes, you can."

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C-Isam in Cobol/C

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T12:13:40-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2cd0t$74a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3c4302dc$1@netnews.web.de> <G2J08.3056$5R2.281494749@news1.van.metronet.ca> <3c44349b$1@netnews.web.de> <qHZ08.3061$5R2.282064611@news1.van.metronet.ca> <3c4846d9$1@netnews.web.de> <kb9i4ukegsnu50ib18avggb5v6k47kri00@4ax.com>`

```
I hate to disagree with you Volker, BUT ...

Given the reference to "C-ISAM" in the subject line, my GUESS is that this
is NOT an IBM mainframe environment.  THEREFORE, the chances are (medium
good) that X'0B' is NOT a valid negative sign-nibble.

Even on IBM mainframes (with COBOL for this-and-that) the NUMCLS compiler
(installation) option determines whether COBOL does or DOES NOT accept X"0B"
as a valid negative sign (in an IF NUMERIC test, for example), see:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3c101/2.41?

Notice that the IBM supplied default *only* accepts C, D, and F as "valid"
sign-nibbles.  Of course regardless of the COBOL option, "native" S/370,
OS/390, and z/OS assembler instruction DO accept A-F as valid signs.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5vhj4uov7drvrre0u4vq1b6316007rspqb@4ax.com>`

```

<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>I hate to disagree with you Volker, BUT ...
>

Guilty as charged.  To my defense, though, please note that so many
people talk about ISAM and just mean any type of keyed dataset, that
the C-ISAM thingie just didn't ring a bell here

>THEREFORE, the chances are (medium
>good) that X'0B' is NOT a valid negative sign-nibble.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>as a valid negative sign (in an IF NUMERIC test, for example), see:

Maybe, but this can be overridden by the NUMPROC(PFD|NOPFD) compile
time option

>
>http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3c101/2.41?
>
>Notice that the IBM supplied default *only* accepts C, D, and F as "valid"
>sign-nibbles.

NUMPROC(PFD) doesn't even accept X'0F'


Thanks for your message

## Thread: NetExpress PC_PRINTER...BMP routines — 2 msgs

### NetExpress PC_PRINTER...BMP routines

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T00:58:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0201150058.2c252f2b@posting.google.com>`

```
Does anyone have experience of using these routines??

We've tried using them and while the status returned is zero, nothing,
bitmpa-wise, appears on the printer. Actually that's not quite true
because sometimes a part of the BMP does appear, but the right hand
10% is not too useful!

Do they really work? In particular what are the units for the start
position and width and height of the bitmap: character positions or
what ? And what is the procedure - presumably load the BMP at the
beginning and free at the end but should the ...WRITE_BMP be issued
prior to every page written, or is it meant to issued just once before
the first page and the subsequent pages, until the free, are "loaded"
with the BMP automatically ? The Help describes the individual
routines reasonably well, although not completely clearly in some
cases, but not the overall usage.

For a variety of reasons it seemed a good idea at the time...

The alternatives looked to require effort especially if it meant
finding the correct sequences for a variety of printers for loading
graphic data to a page and then sending that sequence from the
program. Generating PDF also came to mind and this is looking more
attractive now!

graham 
Olympic Computers
```

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T10:12:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1011089505@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

## Thread: Fujitsu 6.1 CGI facilities — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Fujitsu 6.1 CGI facilities

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T00:42:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c441a17_3@Usenet.com>`

```
I am quite impressed by the "new" support for CGI in Fujitsu V6.1. and have
started using it for a "real" project.

However, I am really sweating the conversion of an HTML skeleton for output
directly from the CGI. Every time I issue a COBW3_PUT_HTML it returns code
6101 which means it can't read the skeleton file. I have tried using
relative and absolute paths for this file, placed it in the "scripts"
directory on the server, and even used a URL for it, all to no avail.

I would appreciate it if anyone else is using this product and can contact
me with any ideas, or indeed if anyone has any actual code that works and
does a conversion of output HTML.

It is being tested on MS Personal Web Server which supports CGI and SSI and
ISAPI (although I am not currently using ISAPI for this exercise.)

With Version 5, I used a third party product to output HTML but it could
only do one line at a time. V6 correctly outputs single lines of HTML using
the COBW3_PUT_TEXT function.

I have spent many hours trying to get the "bulk output" function to work and
am now looking at writing my own version of this function so I can get the
same effect using COBW3_PUT_TEXT. I really don't want to re-invent the wheel
(but when the wheel won't turn it is sometimes necessary...) Before I embark
on this 2 day exercise, I would really appreciate hearing from anybody who
is doing the same thing or has the delivered software working correctly.

Thanks,

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu 6.1 CGI facilities

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T05:27:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0201150527.3d7e509b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3c441a17_3@Usenet.com>`

```
Without seeing your code, I don't know if you have the data area setup
property for the call or if you are setting all the necessary flags. 
This is from a Fujitsu example - there are several of them you can
find by putting COBW3_PUT_HTML in a Google search.


* Setup the prototype HTML file name
     MOVE "c:\ORANT\OWS\admin\OWS\WAS\HTTPD_SPICE\WWW\doc\messageResponse.htm"
       TO COBW3-HTML-FILENAME
* Set the CONTENT-TYPE to HTML
       SET COBW3-CONTENT-TYPE-HTML TO TRUE
* Send the completed HTML to the user
       CALL "COBW3_PUT_HTML" USING COBW3.

Where COBW3 is a Fujitsu copybook.

There's also a lot of other pre-initialization you need to do for
COBW3 -
see the example here:

http://www.adtools.com/news/prelease/oraclecart.htm



"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3c441a17_3@Usenet.com>...
> I am quite impressed by the "new" support for CGI in Fujitsu V6.1. and have
> started using it for a "real" project.
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T11:40:25+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c44b5bd_14@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3c441a17_3@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0201150527.3d7e509b@posting.google.com>`

```
Thanks Thane.

There is nothing new here and I am doing everything you describe (and more
besides...<G>)

I have also examined the sample code (but didn't run a GOOGLE search; that's
a good idea...<G>) but the sample code (cgismp01) as delivered cannot run.
(The library paths will only work on the machine of the guy who wrote the
sample, as they are incorrect). I corrected the sample code and ran it, but
get the same result. I also find that the Debugger will not invoke through
@CBR_ATTACH_TOOL so I can't really examine what is going on. The message
written to the message file says "Service not run" and the Manual says I
need to start the "COBOL ATTACH TOOLS" service. I haven't the faintest idea
what this means or how to do it and can't find anything in the documentation
which explains it or even refers to it.

I did manage to get messages from CGI appearing on the Browser by using
COBW3-DMODE-DBG (as described in the manual) and that is how I know it is
returning error 6101.

After 25 hours I am running out of options. If a tool takes longer to get
working than doing the job manually, then it is probably wiser to do the job
manually in the first place.

The Web support in V6.1 overall, is impressive and the generation capability
through the Editor is excellent. However, it is not much help if the
generated code won't run...

I'll explore the other options you have posted and I'll try the GOOGLE
Search.

It is very possible that it just won't work with Win 98SE using PWS, or
maybe it is necessary to use the multi-threading RTS, there are many
possibilities...

Thanks for your post and suggestions.

In the meantime, I would really like to hear from anyone who has actually
DONE it, so we can compare environments, etc.

Pete.

<a id="t-b3e79aa5cd"></a>
## Thread: COBOL language on OS/390 and LBI — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL language on OS/390 and LBI

- **From:** billlalonde@rocketmail.com (Bill)
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T08:37:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28b8d30e.0201150837.6e621019@posting.google.com>`

```
I've encountered a bit of a interface challenge in COBOL on OS/390.
I found that COBOL has support for LBI (tape block sizes > 32760).
As far as I know so far, this support has not turned up in other high
level languages. I thought that RECORDING MODE IS U would provide a
useful tool for looking at tape datasets using LBI. However, because
of language coding peculiarities, when you work with this type of file
in COBOL, you need to specify the block size as the record length:
   RECORD VARYING FROM 1 TO 32760 DEPENDING ON BLOCK-LEN
would return the size of the output block. If you want to look at
bigger blocks than 32760, you can (for the moment at least) code a
larger maximum value. Note that COBOL won't be able to return the
whole block because a record can't be longer than 32760, but you can
at least find out the actual block size.

The problem is that COBOL compares the maximum value in the RECORD
VARYING clause to the LRECL of the file even though for RECORDING MODE
IS U these values actually relate to the blocksize. So, unless the
input LRECL=0, since you can't specify an LRECL > 32760 in JCL, you
can't open a file using RECORDING MODE IS U if the block size is >
32760. Well, actually you can, but then COBOL will tell you that all
the blocks are less than or equal to the maximum value in the RECORD
VARYING clause, which some folks might consider a data integrity
issue.

All this to say, does anyone out there have any creative suggestions
for making this work in this somewhat wacky environment?
Alternatively, is there any support out there for asking for a
rethinking of the rules for RECORDING MODE IS U?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL language on OS/390 and LBI

- **From:** Don Devlin <dondev1@attbi.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T05:13:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C5780F0.7030701@attbi.com>`
- **References:** `<28b8d30e.0201150837.6e621019@posting.google.com>`

```
You might try having an assembler program do your I/O against the 
   file in locate mode and then pass the address of the buffer 
containing the complete block back to the COBOL program fro 
whatever processing you need to accomplish.  The maximum block 
size will be 64K.

Bill wrote:

> I've encountered a bit of a interface challenge in COBOL on OS/390.

## Thread: DB2 Stored Procedure in COBOL — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### DB2 Stored Procedure in COBOL

- **From:** wjdennis@rmi.net (Bill Dennis)
- **Date:** 2002-01-15T16:53:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c023a27b.0201151653.518640a1@posting.google.com>`

```
We've written a Stored Procedure in COBOL which runs under DB2 7.1 in
a VSE environment.  The procedure is called by Java via JDBC (IBM's
DB2 driver over DB2Connect).  Though we've followed to the letter all
the examples we've seen, we can't get the Stored Procedure to return
values to the Java program.  Here's the setup.  Can someone tell me
what we're doing wrong?  Thanks.

DDL:
CREATE PROCEDURE PROCSAM1 (IN ITEM CHAR(10),                        
  OUT RESULT CHAR(80))                                               
  EXTERNAL NAME PROCSAM1, SERVER GROUP, PARAMETER STYLE GENERAL,     
  COMMIT ON RETURN NO,                                               
  LANGUAGE COBOL, RESULT SETS 1;

COBOL:
LINKAGE SECTION.                                   
       01 MITEM-ID        PIC X(10).                     
       01 RESULT-STRING   PIC X(80).                                  

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MITEM-ID, RESULT-STRING.            
      * Do Some Program Logic (not shown)
        MOVE 'EVERYTHING WORKED JUST FINE.' TO RESULT-STRING.
        GOBACK.

JAVA:
  CallableStatement cstmt = con.prepareCall("CALL PROCSAM1(?, ?)");
  cstmt.setString(1, "1234567890");
  cstmt.registerOutParameter (2, Types.CHAR);
  cstmt.execute();
  System.out.println(cstmt.getString(2));
  
Unfortunately, it always prints "null".  

Bill Dennis
```

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T04:34:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0201160434.44688db0@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
             I have a pointer refrencing to a variable in cobol.I
would like to write the same variable in a file by using pointer not
using variable.
            Genarally we use WRITE OUT-REC.
but i would like to use:
            WRITE OUT-REC-POINTER 
 but it is  NOT WORKING.
where out-rec-pointer is pointer type variable pointing to out-rec.
thanks and Regards.
shyam nivas
```

#### ↳ Re: WRITE USING POINTER IN COBOL.

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T14:23:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020116092317.00742.00001438@mb-ck.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201160434.44688db0@posting.google.com>`

```
shyam nivas writes ...

>Hi,

Hi, yourself, Shyam. Did you get the DLL problem working? Never heard if you
were successful.


>             I have a pointer refrencing to a variable in cobol.I
>would like to write the same variable in a file by using pointer not
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>            WRITE OUT-REC-POINTER 

Well, are you trying to write out the pointer or the data pointed at by the
pointer? I presume the latter, since writing out a pointer is meaningless.

Remember, COBOL is not C (and it's a good thing, too). It sounds like you can
accomplish the task you want by using the FROM option:

    WRITE OUT-REC FROM REC-AREA

Now, if REC-AREA is in working-storage, no problem. But I gather your data may
be somewhere else. Then, you have to define REC-AREA in linkage section and
say:

     SET ADDRESS OF REC-AREA TO OUT-REC-POINTER
     WRITE OUT-REC FROM REC-AREA

> but it is  NOT WORKING.
>where out-rec-pointer is pointer type variable pointing to out-rec.
>thanks and Regards.

Let me know how it goes. [Are you sure you don't want us to come teach a
class?]

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WRITE USING POINTER IN COBOL.

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T13:23:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020117082356.22244.00002212@mb-mm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201170130.11483add@posting.google.com>`

```
Shyam,

I am looking at this:

>> 
>>      SET ADDRESS OF REC-AREA TO OUT-REC-POINTER
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Keep things simple. The Write statement must name an 01-level data item that
follows the FD (that is, not in working-storage or linkage sections); so you
can't use a pointer for that.

Rec-pointer must point to an area in linkage-section, then the two lines I
coded above will work.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WRITE USING POINTER IN COBOL.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T11:58:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a29no7$h8f$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201160434.44688db0@posting.google.com> <20020116092317.00742.00001438@mb-ck.aol.com> <c02fd744.0201170130.11483add@posting.google.com>`

```
"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0201170130.11483add@posting.google.com...
 <snip>
>             Now my Problem of using WRITE verb in COBOL by pointer.
> My basic problem is I would like to do some manipulation on 3MB of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> shyam
 <snip>

GREAT, now you have told us WHY you want to do what you are asking about.

Now we can tell you that you will GAIN *nothing* be putting your 3MB in
"some other virtual storage" location rather than in your own working
storage.

If this is a "batch" program (and in at least one of your notes indicating
passing data from step-to-step - so I would guess that it is), then having
3MB WS items is NOT a big deal in an OS/390 (or z/OS) environment.  Any
"tricks" you might do to try and keep this "separate" from your program
would be MAINTENANCE nightmares and would NOT be a "good" think to do.

This can't be a CICS program (where a 3MB WS item *might* be a problem) -
because you wouldn't be talking about the WRITE statement.

It COULD be an IMS BMP program - and if so, having a 3MB WS item *might* be
a minor concern.  But again, doing "tricks" would probably NOT make it
worthwhile to separate your data from your program.  If you have been
"given" by your systems designer a REQUIREMENT for a "small" program, find
out WHY and let us know that.  There may well be a better way to approach
your entire application, depending on the real need.
```

#### ↳ Re: WRITE USING POINTER IN COBOL.

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T21:30:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a25cgp$gad$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201160434.44688db0@posting.google.com>`

```
 Shyam,

            My biggest question would be WHY ? What are your benefits ?

            If you write the record from the POINTER and the File is
variable-length (minimum length of 4), you'll wind up with a 4-byte record,
whose contents will be meaningless.

            If the File is fixed-length and not equal to an LRECL of 4,
you'll get an error on the WRITE.

            If you define a LINKAGE SECTION "01" level that's equal in
length to OUT-REC, then set this "01" level to the POINTER and WRITE from
it.

            Forgive me, but I just can't fathom why you would want to do
this.

Puzzled,

Bill

"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0201160434.44688db0@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>              I have a pointer refrencing to a variable in cobol.I
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> shyam nivas
```

<a id="t-72f60ae9b1"></a>
## Thread: Report Writer Debate — 1 msgs

### Re: Report Writer Debate

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-16T19:35:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hCk18.105145$fe1.1556918@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
A general comment.  Previously there was a suggestion to preceed subjects
with OT (for off topic). Other prefixes where not adopted.

During the discussion of RW, another prefix occured to me.

It is CH (change hats).

Why?  Some discussions about COBOL programming vasilate between
the programming mind set, and the manager or CEO mindset.

In the case of the RW feature in COBOL a great deal of progress has been
made to assist the "programmer." Altimately, this helps both groups of hats.
The discussion of an altimate tool such as a word processor would move the
programming to the user. Those hats may or may not be willing to do it
themselves. An ideal CEO might be one who has very few adminstrative
personel. This CEO knows how to type, or press buttons, or talk to some
interface to let him do all he wants him or her self.  In the ultimate
solution from
a systems view point, one CEO may not be interested in the same things as
the previous CEO. So hire or promote someone to push the keys.

I believe we have this situation now.  We just have to put on the right hat.

Warren Simmons
```

<a id="t-fb6a5e7f16"></a>
## Thread: COBOL 2002 for the AS/400 — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### COBOL 2002 for the AS/400

- **From:** david.silber@hpdsoftware.com (David Silber)
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T05:44:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad85f189.0201170544.2765ec91@posting.google.com>`

```
At the end of November 2001, IBM applications development people in
Toronto were unable to confirm that they would support the forthcoming
COBOL 2002 standard (acc. to an email sent to me by the UK AS/400
(i-Series) Programme Manager. Has anyone heard anything different?
While other vendors like Micro Focus have been very active, it would
appear that IBM have been sitting on their hands. I would suggest an
intensive lobbying effort by those concerned.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 2002 for the AS/400

- **From:** Fred Snyder <fred_snyder@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T09:28:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C46DF7A.5332C629@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<ad85f189.0201170544.2765ec91@posting.google.com>`

```
I'll start my response with IMHO!! I doubt IBM or any other major (those
that are left) hardware vendor is inclined to put the money into a new
COBOL standard at this point. Had the new standard come out when it was
supposed to in 1997 it would have had a better chance of being accepted by
the IBM's of the world. With new development platforms such as Java and
C++ changing almost weekly its hard for a major vendor to put people and
money into something that only changes every 11 years or so. Companies
like Micro Focus have the talent and committment to be able to add
extensions to the language as they have done over the past 25 years.
Should you stay in a client/server environment where you use compilers
such as Micro Focus then you will be able to take advantage of the new
features but I don't think you'll ever see them running on the mainframe.
This is in no means a dig at the standards committee, they do great work
but are not organized in a way to be able to respond to a changing
development environment.

David Silber wrote:

> At the end of November 2001, IBM applications development people in
> Toronto were unable to confirm that they would support the forthcoming
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> intensive lobbying effort by those concerned.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 2002 for the AS/400

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T11:08:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a29l46$lo3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ad85f189.0201170544.2765ec91@posting.google.com>`

```
IBM has made *no* comment concern a "statement of direction" for *any*
platform - including OS/390 (z/OS) where COBOL is still one of their "major"
application products.

HOWEVER, be careful in reading this.  I did say they have made NO statement
and that means they have not made any statement PRO or CON for supporting
the next Standard.

They have (several years ago) indicated that they would NOT implement the
various "iterations" of the draft Standard as:

 A) it was constantly changing - so anything they provided one year, would
be incompatible with what would come out the next year (and therefore cause
their customers migration problems)

 B) there were/are parts of the draft that were changing from "required" to
"processor dependent" and they did NOT intend on implementing some features
that ended up as "processor dependent" where their customers would NOT want
to use the features (e.g. character-based screen handling and
file-sharing/record locking)

C) They were (and ARE concentrating) on those features/functions (in and OUT
of the draft Standard) that THEIR customers have expressed a serious
"demand" for (e.g. improved performance for binary items, XML support, and
Java interoperability, and on OS/400 "locale" support)

  ***

As far as I know, *ONLY* Micro Focus has made a "public statement" that
their NEXT release will conform to the draft (final?) Standard.  I believe
that Fujitsu has indicated that they are staying RELATIVELY current with the
developments of the draft - but usually new features/functionality comes out
in the Japanese products about 6-12 months before it reaches the "US"
product.  Just this week they came out with the MS "NET" product (in beta)
so it seems that has been where the MAJORITY of their development as been.
(Actually, as far as I can tell from "externals" the Siemens portion of
Fujitsu is staying "closer" to the draft Standard than the PC portion.)

   ***

You are correct that *IF* Standard conformance is IMPORTANT to you (your
company), that you should communicate this to IBM.   I believe the "normal"
path for OS/400 people is thru "COMMON" (while the normal path for z/OS
customers would be SHARE).

Do a REQUIREMENT and see how many shops actually agree with you that this
should be done.  IBM will listen - but (IMHO) they won't necessarily do a
lot based on "theory" rather than paying customers' needs/desires.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2002 for the AS/400

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T07:13:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C491CA3.CF16D692@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ad85f189.0201170544.2765ec91@posting.google.com> <a29l46$lo3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> IBM has made *no* comment concern a "statement of direction" for *any*
> platform - including OS/390 (z/OS) where COBOL is still one of their "major"
> application products.

> that ended up as "processor dependent" where their customers would NOT want
> to use the features (e.g. character-based screen handling and
> file-sharing/record locking)

While this may be true for OS/390 it is not the only IBM system.  Linux
will run on most IBM hardware and would require both character screen
based (for console/telnet) and file share/locking.  Given that IBM is
pushing Linux then a new Visual-Age Cobol for Linux would be useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2002 for the AS/400

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T13:31:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a29vet$qdd$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ad85f189.0201170544.2765ec91@posting.google.com> <a29l46$lo3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3C491CA3.CF16D692@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
If you think those would be useful in those environments *and* you are a
current IBM (paying) customer for those products, then I *strongly*
recommend that you use "normal" IBM enhancement request (requirement)
procedures for communicating this to IBM.

IBM *does* listen to PAYING customers; they do NOT (normally) implement
feature "just because someone thinks they MIGHT be useful - someday)

NOTE: I am *not* disagreeing (necessarily) with your perception - I am only
saying that without user input to request these features (in those
environments) that you should NOT expect IBM to provide them (even IF they
do provide a "fully conforming" 2002 compiler).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2002 for the AS/400

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T15:48:17+00:00

- **References:** `<ad85f189.0201170544.2765ec91@posting.google.com> <a29l46$lo3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3C491CA3.CF16D692@Azonic.co.nz> <a29vet$qdd$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> If you think those would be useful in those environments *and* you are a
> current IBM (paying) customer for those products, 

No, I am not a 'current paying customer for those products' because
there is no product 'IBM Cobol for Linux' (that was the point of the
message).

If they were to release such a product as VisualAge Cobol for Linux then
I may well become a paying customer for it.  However, I would only do so
if it had console ACCEPT/DISPLAY (preferably X/Open) and file
sharing/locking, even if mainframers don't see the point.
```

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2002 for the AS/400

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T19:22:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2fqga$dpf$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ad85f189.0201170544.2765ec91@posting.google.com> <a29l46$lo3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3C491CA3.CF16D692@Azonic.co.nz> <a29vet$qdd$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3C4AE6C1.33C50D2F@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Please note that *both* the Accept/Display *and* the
File-sharing/record-locking support in the 2002 Standard are NOT identical
to that in the last (before it became "defunct") X/Open COBOL specification.
Therefore, I think that for *new* products, it is HIGHLY likely that when
you do see these facilities, they will probably match the ANSI/ISO Standard
and not the X/Open definition.  My guess is that vendors that currently
provide the X/Open support will continue to provide it (at least for a
while) as an OPTION, but not necessarily their "preferred" support.
```

## Thread: Where can I learn CICS and DB2 ? — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Where can I learn CICS and DB2 ?

- **From:** Nomad <abc@Avenue1.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T17:56:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g04e4uoimdbg5q6slunf22a5phofm55eif@4ax.com>`

```
Hello,
I am a Cobol-programmer but so far had only experience with 
Micro Focus (Merant) Cobol under DOS.

I would like to get Mainframe-experience and learn about CICS and
DB2.

Is there anybody here who would be willing to teach me in something
like an online-course ?   Or do you know any good tutorials on the
net that would teach me these skills ?

I live in Raleigh, NC, anybody from this region ?

Thanks for your answers.

Reinhard
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I learn CICS and DB2 ?

- **From:** Mark Allen Framness <farmer@netnet.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T21:58:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<02012322015001.03455@framnett>`
- **References:** `<g04e4uoimdbg5q6slunf22a5phofm55eif@4ax.com>`

```
On Thu, 17 Jan 2002, Nomad wrote:
>Hello,
>I am a Cobol-programmer but so far had only experience with 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>DB2.

Ever work with a relational database such as sql-server (ugh), access (ugggghh)
or Oracle?  If so then most likely you already have a good start on DB2 at
least with the SQL part of it.  There are a few things that are different but
it should not be too tough to catch on to.

As far as CICS goes that is trickier.  Ever work with web page programming? 
The basic idea is the same, you write up a map (form) that will get assembled
into the screen the user sees and then you write a program (cgi script or
program) that takes the data in, processes that data and then generates some
sort of ouput or performs a task.

Of course you may never have worked with a relational database or web
programming but at least I tried!

Anyway
```

## Thread: Length of data set name — 10 msgs

### Length of data set name

- **From:** david.i.smith@farmersinsurance.com (David Smith)
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T10:14:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98bf16f9.0201171014.4898e6b9@posting.google.com>`

```
Could someone please tell me why the maximum length of any particular
qualifier in a data set name in IBM mainframe jcl is eight characters?

As PC file systems evolved, the 8.3 naming convention was no longer
required..  Why didn't a similar evolution happen on mainframes?

Yours,
David Smith
```

```

wrote:

:>Could someone please tell me why the maximum length of any particular
:>qualifier in a data set name in IBM mainframe jcl is eight characters?

It isn't.

:>As PC file systems evolved, the 8.3 naming convention was no longer
:>required..  Why didn't a similar evolution happen on mainframes?

Total length is 44.
```

:>Talking MVS here, not USS

Put the dsname in quotes in the JCL.

Of course, SMS might not like it but MVS does not have a problem with it. Try
is to a tape or a non-SMS volume.

I don't think there is a way to make the TSO syntax accept it, but DYNALLOC
should.

On Sat, 19 Jan 2002 20:41:50 -0600, Binyamin Dissen
<postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

>
>Put the dsname in quotes in the JCL.

>I don't think there is a way to make the TSO syntax accept it, but DYNALLOC
>should.

It should, but it doesn't (bad text unit x'002', which is DALDSNAM).
Another nasty thing to remember - those "nonstandard" dsnames cannot
be catalogued (bad thing)

Good question. I've wondered too why they haven't changed it so you
can have more than 8 characters between the dots. Or why the first
character after the dot HAS to be an alpha. It probably has something
to do with the system catalog structure, the vtoc structure or both.
You might want to pose this question on the mainframe group.



"David Smith" <david.i.smith@farmersinsurance.com> wrote in message
news:98bf16f9.0201171014.4898e6b9@posting.google.com...
> Could someone please tell me why the maximum length of any particular
> qualifier in a data set name in IBM mainframe jcl is eight characters?

#### ↳ Re: Length of data set name

- **From:** "Edwin Earl Ross" <EdwinEarl@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T19:57:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jF28.4133$Hi3.1443805277@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<98bf16f9.0201171014.4898e6b9@posting.google.com>`

```
Early operating systems limited file name sizes because of memory
limitations. At one time, mainframes with 64KB were big honkers--especially
when memory was magnetic cores and cost $1 a bit.

"David Smith" <david.i.smith@farmersinsurance.com> wrote in message
news:98bf16f9.0201171014.4898e6b9@posting.google.com...
> Could someone please tell me why the maximum length of any particular
> qualifier in a data set name in IBM mainframe jcl is eight characters?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> David Smith
```

<a id="t-4c55c22f52"></a>
## Thread: Record Length for PC files — 15 msgs

### Record Length for PC files

- **From:** NimbusRising <JPRQ@8888.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T19:27:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com>`

```
I am mainframe cobol programmer wishing to write
cobol program for PC. I wish to be able to read
ANY file from PC and process it. How do I find
out the record-length of each record I read?
I need to know record length because I want to
compress the whole record.

In mainframe cobol, I usually know record length
so I code..

FD INFILE RECORDING MODE F DATA RECORD INREC.
01 INREC PIC X(2000).

for example in 2000 byte record.

If I want to be able to read ANY PC file, 
including types DLL, DOC, XLS, etc. how
can I find record-length?

I am using OTCOBOL (a free compiler written
by a French person). 

Thank you in advance.
```

```

> I am mainframe cobol programmer wishing to write
> cobol program for PC. I wish to be able to read
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: Record Length for PC files

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T21:16:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PaH18.768$9B2.658521@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com>`

```
NimbusRising <JPRQ@8888.com> wrote in message
news:na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com...
>
> If I want to be able to read ANY PC file,
> including types DLL, DOC, XLS, etc. how
> can I find record-length?

On PC's there is no such thing as record length, because there are no such
things as discrete physical records: all files are simply streams of bytes.
It is up to the application to decide if there are logical records of a
fixed or variable length within the byte stream.

As for your specific example, if "DOC" refers to a Microsoft Word (r)
document, you've hit the triple jackpot, because DLL, DOC and XLS files are
all built from a series of variable-length records in which there are either
"assumed" or explicit (embedded) logical record lengths.

MCM
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T16:53:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8EH18.19868$jt2.2871930@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com> <PaH18.768$9B2.658521@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <33ge4u8c799eo5knqgtf40g4e5ce8sfqmr@4ax.com>`

```
"NimbusRising" <JPRQ@8888.com> wrote in message
news:33ge4u8c799eo5knqgtf40g4e5ce8sfqmr@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 17 Jan 2002 21:16:32 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
> <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >On PC's there is no such thing as record length, because there are no
such
> >things as discrete physical records: all files are simply streams of
bytes.
> >It is up to the application to decide if there are logical records of a
> >fixed or variable length within the byte stream.
> >
> >As for your specific example, if "DOC" refers to a Microsoft Word (r)
> >document, you've hit the triple jackpot, because DLL, DOC and XLS files
are
> >all built from a series of variable-length records in which there are
either
> >"assumed" or explicit (embedded) logical record lengths.
> >
> >MCM
> >
>

> Thanks for that, but there must be *some* way of telling the
> length, otherwise utilities like hex-editors wouldn't
> be able to work properly. For example I have a hex-editor
> that can read *any* file.
>

A hex editor of the type you describe reads the file as one large record, so
if that is all you want, just look in the directory and find the file size
in bytes.  Then load the entire thing into an array dimensioned at one byte
by as big a file as you want to edit.  A hex  editor is rather trivial in
Cobol, provided you have enough memory.

If you cannot figure out how to read a directory, set the Cobol file up as a
single byte record, and count them until you hit the end of the file, the
same as you do in C.  That is fairly simple.

Records, as defined in Cobol, have little to do with the rather primitive
byte I/O done by low level languages such as C.

> It definitely knows the file-size because it stops display
> at that point!
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance
```

so

size

byte
> >by as big a file as you want to edit.  A hex  editor is rather trivial in
> >Cobol, provided you have enough memory.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Someone in here can give you the API call, I am sure.  MF and Fujutsu both
have standard subroutines to do it.  A really mickey mouse way that will
work is to execute a directory command and redirect the output into a file,
then read the file as line sequential.


> > set the Cobol file up as a
> >single byte record, and count them until you hit the end of the file, the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes. It will work just fine for a conversion style of program.  I'd suspect
that the overhead is not a lot worse that doing the exact same thing in C or
VB.  Byte stream IO is always done out of disk cluster sized buffers anyway.
How many times per second are you planning to read the file, and how big is
it?

Donald

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Length for PC files

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T23:50:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brJ18.721514$8q.56704749@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com> <PaH18.768$9B2.658521@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <33ge4u8c799eo5knqgtf40g4e5ce8sfqmr@4ax.com>`

```

"NimbusRising" <JPRQ@8888.com>
> >
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> at that point!

A hex editor (for example) stops when told by the OS it
has reached the end of the file. You originally asked for a
way to tell the length of a RECORD. There isn't one.

>
> So, in the case of Cobol, what to I put in place of ???
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> that is not possible in COBOL?

There is NO general purpose "End-of-Record" marker for ANY
language, let alone COBOL. IF you know, in advance, the file
is of a certain type (say an ordinary text file), then you may make
use of the conventions of that file type to detect when you encounter
the end of a record (in the case of a text file, the convention is
Carriage-Return or Carriage-Return/Line-Feed).

The operating system informs your program when the end of the
file is reached. The OS gets the length of the file from the file's
directory (you can too, but not directly with COBOL - you must
invoke either and OS API or a vendor-supplied subprogram).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Length for PC files

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T15:32:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C484008.EF9848C6@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com> <PaH18.768$9B2.658521@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <33ge4u8c799eo5knqgtf40g4e5ce8sfqmr@4ax.com>`

```
NimbusRising wrote:
> 
> So, in the case of Cobol, what to I put in place of ???
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 01 ANYREC PIC X(?????).


	1	(one)


You need to have the file defined as a fixed sequential format so that
each byte is treated as one record.  You then need to read sequentially
assembling each structure within the file in turn in W-S areas.  

This may depend on actual compiler used and on specic options.


> If I want to write (say) a compression routine, I
> have to know where the last byte is - are you saying
> that is not possible in COBOL?

If you keep reading then eventually you with get a '10' file status or
take the AT END clause.

Everything is possible in Cobol, some things may be less convenient than
in other languages, others may be more convenient.
```

#### ↳ Re: Record Length for PC files

- **From:** jraben@cascinc.com (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T17:00:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c48521a.3601311@news.bullseyetelecom.net>`
- **References:** `<na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com>`

```
>
>In mainframe cobol, I usually know record length
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
snip
Production code on the micro is the same ast mainframe.  You code
record size.  
If the code is text unusally lf/cr or just lf are terminators.  If
binary data is in the record lf cannot be depended upon.  Various
systems have various vary of delimiting/defining variable length.
... If I give you a hex dump of a file I bet it could be hard if not
impossible to fig a rec length.  even if you found all the factors of
the filesize for guesstimates.  If I had variable length (but byte
read) based on a flagbyte good luck.

It sounds like you are trying to write a compression routine at the
record level that users would get altomatically when reading after you
have pre-compressed.   Try reading up on DOUBLE-SPACE etc for
compression on the fly in/out.  The atom is not "record" but "sector".
You got a better chance compressing without knowledge of length.
(Some index files mix sizes/recordtype/structure under one "file" or
one alocation; think VSAM/ISAM type)

Good luck.
and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

#### ↳ Re: Record Length for PC files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T11:47:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a29n2u$c2f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com>`

```
There have been LOTS of replies to this note - and I won't go over their
information in my reply.  However, I did want to mention one thing that is a
BIG shock to those moving from (IBM - probably most) Mainframe environments
to PC environments.

On (IBM) mainframes, there is an entire "product" (or 2) that defines
"FILES" and these are available to all programming languages and software
packages (e.g. QSAM, VSAM - older ones like ISAM and BDAM - and even ones
NOT supported "well" in COBOL such as BPAM and the lowest level of EXCP).

Every product (whether from IBM or a 3rd party) uses THESE file formats -
and they all understand each others files.  (Well actually, some vendors -
including IBM use "funny" records occasionally to create "proprietary" file
formats - but even those are based on the "basic" ones that come with MVS,
OS/390, z/OS, VM, etc)

  ***

Now in the PC (and UNIX) worlds, there is NOTHING like this.  Usually
(possibly always) the Operating System "knows" about

   byte-stream I/O (read a file, executable, or  even "junk" one byte at a
time)
    AND
   line sequential files (a file where CR/LF - carriage-return/line-feed -
or just LF) indicates a "break" between records

*ALL* other "file types" need to be provided by the specific software
vendor.  This is why a Micro Focus "variable length sequential file" canNOT
be read by a Fujitsu program (normally) - and an indexed file created by
(almost) any COBOL compiler is TOTALLY useless to the "native" I/O of any
other programming language.  There are some 3rd party product (such as
C-ISAM and Btrieve) that are specifically INTENDED to provide
"multi-language" support for files, but they cannot be assumed as being
present - much less used in creating a COBOL file.

When you start talking about .DOC, .XLS, etc files you are talking about
INCREDIBLY "proprietary" file formats.   You *can* parse them in COBOL - but
there is no way to do so without
  A) using that vendor's documentation on the file formats
  B) knowing what type of file it is BEFORE you try and read it.

 ***

The best example, I can think of for an IBM mainframer is to ask you to
imagine the task (in a COBOL program) of figuring out the actual UNDERLYING
VSAM, OSAM, whatever record size for a SQL or IMS record.   It could be
done - but certainly not very easily.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Length for PC files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T14:28:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2a0j4$v39$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<na9e4uk2pf4e03o3d4puhtsuoe0bm505h1@4ax.com> <a29n2u$c2f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3C487CCD.D75EE944@shaw.ca>`

```
Really????

Again, assuming an IBM mainframe environment, try porting a DB2 database to
an ORACLE site - without having to do an "on-load/re-load" - or using
"external" software to read each others data.  The COBOL *language"
(actually SQL) would be identical in the two environments - but the
underlying "files" (databases) are as unusable across software environments
(ORACLE/DB2) as is a Micro Focus indexed file in a Realia environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Length for PC files

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T12:24:10+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:a2a0j4$v39$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> Really????
>
> Again, assuming an IBM mainframe environment, try porting a DB2 database
to
> an ORACLE site - without having to do an "on-load/re-load" - or using
> "external" software to read each others data.  The COBOL *language"
> (actually SQL) would be identical in the two environments - but the
> underlying "files" (databases) are as unusable across software
environments
> (ORACLE/DB2) as is a Micro Focus indexed file in a Realia environment.
>

I guess my issue is with: "...but the  underlying "files" (databases) are as
unusable across software environments..."

They are not UNUSABLE they are just not directly accessible. But they don't
need to be.

I don't understand the objection to "on-load/re-load"

It is a one-time process and you would have to load the data even if you
just made another copy of the database in the same environment (DB2 or
ORACLE).

If by "external" software you mean ODBC drivers, well, where's the problem?

At least I CAN access an ORACLE and a DB2 database even if they are on
different platforms.

As you point out the SQL would be the same. (As would the COBOL indexed file
access code). The difference is I can run the SQL WITHOUT COBOL (or anything
else, other than the RDB software).

I can't do that with an Indexed file.

Pete.

## Thread: Uniquely defined name error — 3 msgs

### Uniquely defined name error

- **From:** vbiver@yahoo.com (VicB)
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T13:29:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eb0d659.0201171329.77af07d7@posting.google.com>`

```
From this code:

IF X(SUB1,1) IN X-IN-RECORD= 
   X(SUB2,1) IN X-OUT-RECORD THEN.....
 
I received these messages:

1420  IGYPS0037-S   "X" was not a uniquely defined name.  The
                    definition to be used could not be determined from the
                    context.  The reference to the name was discarded.
1420  IGYPS0001-W   A blank was missing before character "1" in column 35.  A
                    blank was assumed.
                    Same message on line:   1421
1420  IGYPS2120-S   Expected a reference-modification specification but found
                    "1".  The "IF" statement was discarded.
                    Same message on line:   1422

I have used the name X in other places, so it is defined ok, but is the problem 
the double indexing?

Thanks
```

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-17T19:04:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a27sb5$8oe$1@suaar1aa.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<eb0d659.0201171329.77af07d7@posting.google.com>`

```
IF X IN X-IN-RECORD (SUB1, 1) =
     X IN X-OUT-RECORD (SUB2, 1)  THEN....      IT WORKS!


"VicB" <vbiver@yahoo.com> wrote in message news:eb0d659.0201171329.77af07d7@posting.google.com...
> From this code:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Uniquely defined name error

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T15:43:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C48429C.576EC9D2@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<eb0d659.0201171329.77af07d7@posting.google.com>`

```
VicB wrote:
> 
> From this code:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>                     Same message on line:   1421

A comma must be followed by a space.  This is what the message is
telling you.

> 1420  IGYPS2120-S   Expected a reference-modification specification but found
>                     "1".  The "IF" statement was discarded.
>                     Same message on line:   1422
 
> I have used the name X in other places, so it is defined ok, but is the problem
> the double indexing?

Are the 'X' in the records actually defined with two levels of OCCURS ?

I suspect that there is no such OCCURS, or they are not nested correctly
and that the compiler cannot determine which 'X' you are trying to use. 
The message that suggested it was trying to use the (SUB2,1) as
reference modification tends to suggest that two subscripts are invalid
on 'X OF X-IN-RECORD.

Show us the definitions of the records.
```

<a id="t-340735585f"></a>
## Thread: Converting ISAM files to RDB — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Converting ISAM files to RDB

- **From:** gnuoytr@rcn.com (robert)
- **Date:** 2002-01-18T12:26:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<da3c2186.0201181225.60adf8d4@posting.google.com>`

```
folks:

(for unknown reasons, google won't let me reply to
the thread, but will let me start anew.  sorry for
the inconvenience).

i've just come across this thread, and have a question
with regard to the process.  my employer (who shall 
remain nameless to protect the guilty), converted its
system from VSAM to DB2 prior to my arrival.  sometime
after that, i had a hissy fit when i found out that, in
the VSAM version they had made use of "pointers" as
data in fields, and left it this way after "converting"
to DB2.  said pointers are strings containing
file (now table) names, e.g. 'TableOne'.  so, records
form a linked list within/across files, making use of
these "file pointers" in various fields.  thus making 
joins of these data impossible; although oracle is said 
to allow the embedding on syntax in data.  

the software is essentially a bill of materials problem,
though these folks clearly don't realize it.  i've looked
for, but haven't found, an answer to the question of how
best to approach converting such a VSAM structure to RDB.

does NEGLCED propose a way to do this?  does the group have
thoughts on a "best practice" with regard the problem?

toodles,
robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting ISAM files to RDB

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T12:00:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c48ac4e_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0201181225.60adf8d4@posting.google.com>`

```
Robert,

I have been doing this kind of thing for many years now and have acquired a
large amount of experience, expertise and tools for the exercise.

I wrote DECLGEN for the PC so that PC COBOL programmers could generate Host
Variables and record layouts from RDB tables.  This tool is useful once you
actually have a Relational Database.

NEGLCED was a name I proposed for a function that does the "other side" of
DECLGEN. That is to say it actually builds a 3NF Relational database from
your existing ISAM files. I have now written this tool and it has proven
itself in a major software conversion a few months back.

However I didn't call it NEGLCED. It is called ISAM2RDB.

Both DECLGEN and ISAM2RDB run as Windows applications in a Win32
Environment. But, as I'm sure you are aware, there is much more to
converting files than just running a couple of tools.

It looks as though the people you are working for have not given any thought
(or understood) the concept of the Relational Model and have simply
converted the existing files as a decree of fashion... Many Corporates are
being driven to do this because their data is locked into the COBOL file
system  and they figure if they can get it to an RDB at least it is open and
available to standard tools and software. They are right, but it can be a
horrendous mess if they just convert the existing files willy nilly.

Now to your problem...

If the data has already been converted and contains columns that  reference
other tables, you will need a normalization process to remove the references
and join existing key dependent data directly to the referenced tables.

It is a major effort. The fact is that the data is there, but it is
unnormalized. You need to determine which of these converted tables share
the same key dependencies and restructure the data to a single table, based
on that key dependency. Sorry, it is hard to explain this in simple terms in
a few minutes...

If you have a table (Table A) which has a column (A3) that references Table
B, then the key of Table A is probably the same as Table B. Normalization
requires you to remove key dependencies between tables by combining tables
which depend on the same key structure.

The good news is that it isn't too hard to write something that can do this.
The bad news is that the existing code then also has to be amended.

The process of converting Flat Files to Databases involves three steps:

1. Create the Relational structures to hold the existing data. (ISAM2RDB
automates this and provides a 3NF Relational database).

2. Load the RDB with data currently held on the flat files. (I have a
semi-automatic process that does this and cuts out much of the "donkey
work". It will be fully automatic by the end of this year.)

3. Convert the existing application code to access the new RDB. This has to
be manual, but I have tools that can help, and there are a large number of
"rules" derived from experience at doing it that can save much heartache.
The best architecture is a multi tier one, and some of the code at some of
the levels can be generated. Other code can have minimal maintenance by
using calls to generated routines for updating.

I am very much in favour of automating as much of the above work as
possible, not just because it saves work and time, but because it eliminates
human error. Humans tend to make errors when they have to do repetitive
tasks. Some of these errors can be very hard to find and will trip you up
later.

In your case (and it is sadly often the case) they are so obsessed with
getting the data into RDB that they just don't really consider the other
things that must be done also. They figure they will minimise the impact on
existing code by converting directly on a 1 for 1 basis to RDB. It is  a
false hope and it provides none of the benefits of the Relational Model (you
noted the inability to join such data as just one shortcoming...there are
many others.).

I really hope that anyone considering this exercise will write me. I have a
large amount of useful material and tools and there is not always a cost
involved.

Pete.

robert <gnuoytr@rcn.com> wrote in message
news:da3c2186.0201181225.60adf8d4@posting.google.com...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting ISAM files to RDB

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T13:22:11+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4e0747_11@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0201181225.60adf8d4@posting.google.com> <3c48ac4e_8@Usenet.com> <rWm38.6276$eL.1832@news1.bloor.is>`

```

Timothy <tim-antie.s_pam-hillock@rogers.com> wrote in message
news:rWm38.6276$eL.1832@news1.bloor.is...
>
>
> I used to work in a shop that used IDMS for it's data base systems.  The
DBA
> did the normalization.  We found normalization fine and dandy, however, we
> found out that too much increased the amount of time that both online and
> batch jobs required.  We had to ask the DBA to have some fields
duplicated.
> By doing this we had the jobs run more quickly and a faster response for
the
> online programs.  I just can't remember the exact relationship between the
> "parent" and "child", but it had to do how the information was stored in
> each set.
>
It is good practice to normalize the system and then build redundancy back
in to improve performance, IF REQUIRED.

I don't personally adhere to the dictum that only a "pure" database  (3NF
guarantees non-redundant non-key data) is acceptable. In the real world
there are databases that are less than perfect being accessed in ways that
are less than perfect. Sometimes a small amount of redundancy can improve
access pathways and ease bottlenecks, but it needs to be carefully
considered and tested.

This is what your DBA did and I commend him for it.

This kind of "tuning" is much more frequent on Databases that are not based
on the Relational Model.

IDMS is a Network Model based database.

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

<a id="t-59d0c940b7"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Files and COBOL Data types. — 4 msgs

### COBOL Files and COBOL Data types.

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T10:36:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0201191036.1749feb2@posting.google.com>`

```
There are no such things as COBOL files and COBOL data types.

COBOL is, far and away, the most versatile high level language in the
computer industry.  From the beginning, COBOL was designed to be
flexible in handling all manner of file structures and data
structures.

Data types and file organization is determined by vendors to operate
on particular hardware and operating system architectures.   COBOL is
designed to process these formats.

HIDAM databases (such as IMS) came with their own Application
Programming Interfaces (APIs), so "native" COBOL syntax was never
developed.  Relational databases came with SQL from the beginning, so
"native" COBOL syntax was never developed.  The CODASYL Journal of
Development had some syntax for new datatypes, but it has not been
adopted because a commercial need has never developed.

COBOL syntax handles sequential, relative, and indexed files natively.
 "Line Sequential" should have been added to the standard, but all
COBOL compilers which run on systems which use it, have it as an
extension.

There was a great deal of effort to expand the new COBOL standard to
handle many more datatypes than previously.  Incomprehensibly, 3 data
types were omitted, and they must be processed in COBOL using kludges:
 (SQL types VARCHAR and VARCHAR-LONG, as well as field-mark deleimited
data types).  Nevertheless, COBOL remains the most flexible high-level
language in the industry.

A note: the format of PACKED-DECIMAL is not specified in the COBOL
standard. Its internal representation varies from computer-maker to
computer-maker.  Not only is the internal representation different,
the Syntax is not standard, either.  Some call it COMP-3, others
COMP-2 or anything else. ALL COMP-x data types are
IMPLEMENTOR-DEFINED. They are ALL extensions to the standard.

Programmers using state-of-the-art whiz-bang computer-science
leading-edge languages frequently need to access files and/or data
types originally created by COBOL programs, and find that their
state-of-the-art whiz-bang computer-science leading-edge languages are
totally inadequate to handle anything except some subset of their
systems capabilities.  ERRONEOUSLY, they blame this on COBOL, rather
than the inadequate state-of-the-art whiz-bang computer-science
leading-edge tools which they are using.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

## Thread: OT but kinda fun — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT but kinda fun

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T13:44:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%2j28.34162$4i5.5098013@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Here is a link that I found a week or so back, and had some fun with.  I
know there are other insane Cobol folk out there that like to have fun.  I
particularly liked the warning the page starts with, which I have quoted
below.  Peter, creating the ball lightning in the pyrex cup is particularly
flashy.

It is not Cobol, but I will vote it my favourite site of the week.

http://www.amasci.com/weird/microexp.html  <-- neat micro wave stuff

Are you a kid? Does your microwave oven belong to your parents? If so, then
don't even THINK about trying any of these experiments. I'm serious. If I
wreck my microwave oven, I can buy another. Also, I'm a professional
electrical engineer. I know enough physics and RF effects to take correct
safety precautions when I'm experimenting. But you don't know the
precautions, so you should be smart: read and enjoy my writing, but don't
duplicate my tests unless you grow up to become an electronics tech,
engineer, etc., and buy your OWN microwave oven.

Donald
```

## Thread: optimum value of cpu time/clock time. — 11 msgs

### optimum value of cpu time/clock time.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-01-19T22:57:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0201192257.207ab52d@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
              Is there any optimum value of CPU time/clock time for
efficient application?As we all know higher CPU time/clock time mean
bad subroutine call or coding practice whereas lower CPU time/clock
time mean bad I/O practice or bad database/file access method.

Thnaks and Warm Regards.
shyam
********************************************************************************
```

#### ↳ Re: optimum value of cpu time/clock time.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T16:00:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MQB28.210$ok7.216398@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201192257.207ab52d@posting.google.com>`

```
shyam nivas <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0201192257.207ab52d@posting.google.com...
> ......As we all know higher CPU time/clock time mean
> bad subroutine call or coding practice whereas lower CPU time/clock
> time mean bad I/O practice or bad database/file access method.

As we all know? you need to correct that, to,  " as we all (except Mattias)
know..."

That is a rather broad statement. Seems to me "clock time" is going to be
affected by the number of jobs running, assigned queue priorities and a host
of other issues totally unrelated to coding style.

That is, "clock time" would to me seem totally irrelevant as a factor in
analyzing the "performace" of applications.
```

#### ↳ Re: optimum value of cpu time/clock time.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T12:06:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fJC28.93$JM6.195376@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201192257.207ab52d@posting.google.com>`

```
Really?  Without any regard to what is being done?  You mean if I run ten
trasactions through a program, and it takes 10 seconds, then run 100
transactions and it takes 50 seconds that is poorer performance?   CPU time
is a measuement of how many CPU cycles a process took.  It does not "mean"
anything.  It is a number, one bigger than the number before it, and one
less than the number after it.  Any meaning beyond taht is inflicted on the
poor thing from outside.

Donald
