#### ↳ Re: optimum value of cpu time/clock time.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T18:58:28+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4baf71_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201192257.207ab52d@posting.google.com>`

```
Shyam,

This is simply nonsense.

A higher CPU time/clock time does NOT (necessarily) indicate a "bad
subroutine call" (whatever that means...I don't see how a call to subroutine
can be good or bad, any more than it can be happy or sad or interested or
bored. It either succeeds or it doesn't, emotions and morals are not a
consideration... ). Neither does it (necessarily) mean "bad coding
practice".  It CAN be an INDICATOR of these thngs, but only by investigation
and examination can it be confirmed that there is, indeed, a problem.

The second part of your statement is even worse...

"...whereas a lower CPU time/clock time means bad I/O practice or bad
datbase/file acess method"

It indicates NO such thing. It simply indicates that there is not a lot of
work for the CPU to do when accessing this particular data. Normally this is
viewed as a desirable state of affairs and certainly not a cause for
castigating the DBA... (there are enough REAL causes for this without
inventing imaginary ones...<G>).

I don't know where you picked up these ideas or who has been filling your
head with such rubbish. Start thinking for yourself and you will realise the
absurdity of it.

To try and establish the validity of these claims with the old "Everyone
knows..." chestnut is perhaps the worst folly of all... You will find that
"Everyone knows" no such thing... Most people here, for instance, will
distance themselves from what you wrote.

So, to answer your question:

The OPTIMUM value of cpu time/clock time is 42.

Or 31.

Or 57...

Or possibly, the same value as the percentage of  milk to oatmeal in my
breakfast this morning... or the number of cracks in the pavement you
stepped on, on your way to work/school....

It is a number which, in and of itself, has no meaning or relevance.

Pete.
```

```
On 19 Jan 2002 22:57:22 -0800 nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas) wrote:

:>              Is there any optimum value of CPU time/clock time for
:>efficient application?As we all know higher CPU time/clock time mean
:>bad subroutine call or coding practice whereas lower CPU time/clock
:>time mean bad I/O practice or bad database/file access method.

Not at all.

A report program against a huge file will use very little CPU but a lot of
elapsed. 

Conversely, a code-cracking program will use a lot of CPU but little I/O.
```

```
In my past and experience i found a good time between cpu and 
duration:

one cpu-second aprox one minute clock time

if this is quite good, down to 40 Seconds and up to 1 minute 20, i dont 
take a look to tune the job. If ist more (1 CPU-second > 2 Minutes), i 
must do something in i/o Prozessing, if it's less (10 
CPU-Seconds/Minute) i must look upon routines

The use of tools like OMEGAMON, Strobe or anythink else is 
recommended!

Have a nice day
```

#### ↳ Re: optimum value of cpu time/clock time.

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T02:31:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4CCFBB.40348069@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201192257.207ab52d@posting.google.com>`

```

shyam nivas wrote:
...
>               Is there any optimum value of CPU time/clock time for
> efficient application?As we all know higher CPU time/clock time mean
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> shyam

The optimum CPU time is no more than is needed for the process at hand;
the optimum number of I/O's is the minimum required to access/update the
data required by the process.  That said, the ratio of
CPU-time/clock-time really has no general correlation with efficiency. 
A process that must read millions of records with minimal processing may
be the best possible with a very low CPU/clock ratio because it is
heavily I/O bound;  a perfectly designed algorithm with I/O minimized
and completely overlapped with CPU may be ideal with a CPU/clock ratio
of 1.0, or higher if it uses multi-tasking (and whether it can achieve
that ratio may be completely dependent on competition for the CPU with
other concurrently running programs, and not on any inherent merit of
that particular application).  If you upgrade to a faster CPU, or if you
upgrade to faster DASD, you change the balance between CPU and I/O and
alter the CPU/Clock ratio for existing applications; yet, there has been
no real change in the "efficiency" of the application.

The questions you instead should be asking are "can I choose a different
algorithm for the process that involves less computation (or perhaps
even redefine the process itself, if you have that flexibility)?", "can
I reduce the number of records my program needs to read by using a
different algorithm, or a different file/record organization?", or "can
I in some way tune the execution of the program to improve the
efficiency of reading/updating records?"  In the later case that tuning
could involve using different buffering techniques or more buffers,
redefining the file or data base with additional indexes, organizing the
file or data base differently, etc.

Invariably, the largest decreases in CPU time come from choosing a
better algorithm, although any tuning that significantly reduces the
number of physical I/O operations required can also significantly reduce
CPU time.  I have seen cases under OS/390 where aggressive buffering on
KSDS VSAM files cut  physical I/O from several million down to a few
thousand, and cut the CPU by a factor of 10 or better.  There may be
some pathological cases where the specific implementation of an
algorithm becomes significant if it uses some language feature that is
particularly inefficient, but these are usually secondary issues.  When
DataBase queries are involved, CPU can also be heavily influenced by
using more efficient query formulations or better indexing, as this can
greatly reduce the number of physical records that must be processed to
satisfy the query.

Invariably, the largest decreases in elapsed time come from
significantly reducing the number of physical I/O operations involved. 
For a given file structure, the optimum number of I/O's is to read each
block containing needed information only once, to write each block
containing information that must be updated once, to block reads and
writes of consecutive blocks together in a single I/O operation where
possible, and to touch no unneeded blocks.  Whether this is achievable
depends on what buffering and access techniques are available to you,
which may also depend on whether the file is concurrently shared.  If
you have an indexed file structure with only 500 blocks and you are
performing millions of I/O's to the file, this is obviously worth
investigating!

In a real production environment there are many applications that are
sub optimal.  You don't generally have the luxury of dealing with
performance/efficiency considerations unless the application is a
problem area:  you are forced to deal with these issues when (1) the
application needs CPU or I/O resources at a time they are in short
supply, (2) the application is using more resources than the user is
willing to pay for, (3) the application is unable to complete within
required deadlines, of (4) the application is using resources in a way
that adversely affects the system throughput and/or other users of the
system.  Applications that are not perceived to be a problem are left
alone in order to have time to deal with those that are.
```

<a id="t-fbadcbd18e"></a>
## Thread: USAGE POINTER at the group level — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### USAGE POINTER at the group level

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-20T20:17:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2fu0j$fsj$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This has NOTHING to do with

 A) USAGE INDEX at the group level (allowed - I think - in both '85 Standard
and draft Standards)
 B) What various vendors will/do allow (as extensions)

BUT the draft of the next COBOL Standard (aka PROBABLY 2002) has an explicit
rule that says,

"13) A USAGE clause with the OBJECT REFERENCE, POINTER, or PROGRAM-POINTER
phrase may be specified only for an elementary data item with level 1 or
subordinate to a type declaration that includes the STRONG phrase."

(See page 370 of the December draft).

Therefore, *both*

 01 aGroup Pointer.
    05  Ptr1 Usage Pointer.

*and*

01 aGroup Pointer.
   05 Ptr1.


will be "non-conforming" in the next Standard.
```

---

<a id="t-f9710a02bc"></a>
## Thread: I need your experience - classification and comparison of languages — 9 msgs

### I need your experience - classification and comparison of languages

- **From:** yvan.radenac@equant.com (Yvan Radenac)
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T00:10:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.ada,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9660815.0201210010.53b3266@posting.google.com>`

```
I am writing a small report about "Object oriented languages and their
public implementations" for a course in Software Engineering.
I am trying to classify and compare different oo languages.
As i am not programmer, analyst or responsible of projects and the
criterias are subjective, i am interesting in your experiences of
object oriented language(s).

I think that there's no universal language, so it's not to compare, in
a global way, each language.
The goal is to create some tables to find the best choice for a kind
of software development.

Thanks you to complete the 2 tables below for each language you use.

Classification:
--------------
Language|Paradigm(s)|Generality of use|Abstraction level|Area(s) of
application
--------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------
        |           |                 |                 |

Comparison:
----------
It's based on this scale table (to simplify):
Very bad|bad|Correct|Good|Very good|
--------|---|-------|----|---------|
  - -   | - |   O   |  + |   + +   |

Language|Readibility|Writability|Reliability|Cost
--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----
        |           |           |           |

Regards,
Yvan

P.S.: a resume of the criterias, based on a course of The University
of Ottawa by Szpakowicz:

Classification:
--------------
Paradigms: imperative, logic-based, functionnal, object-oriented, ...
Generality of use: general purpose, specialized like database
language, ...
Abstraction level: low-level (assemblers), high-level (a majority of
languages), very high level (Prolog)
Area of application: data processing (business applications),
scientific computing, artificial intelligence, in-house computing
applications (compiler construction, systems programming, ...)

Comparison:
----------
Readability:
  - abstraction, support for generality: procedural abstraction, data
absraction.
  - absence of ambiguity (and of too much choice).
  - Orthogonality: no restrictions on combinations of concepts. For
example, can a procedure parametrer have ANY type? Can EVERYTHING be
evaluated?
  - Expressivity of control and data structures. (Exemples of low
expressive power: machine languages).
  - Appearance: style of comments, ...
Writability:
  - Abstraction and simplicy like readibility.
  - Expressivity, like readibility.
  - Modularity and tools for modularization, support for integrated
programmer's environments.
Reliability:
  - Safety for the programmer (type checking, error and exception
handling, unambiguous naming).
Cost:
  - Development time (ease of programming, availability of code).
  - Efficiency of implementation: how easy it is to build a language
processor.
  - Translation time and quality of object code.
  - Portability and standardization.
```

#### ↳ Re: I need your experience - classification and comparison of languages

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T09:44:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.ada,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2h9h3$ggo$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b9660815.0201210010.53b3266@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <b9660815.0201210010.53b3266@posting.google.com>,
Yvan Radenac <yvan.radenac@equant.com> wrote:
>I am writing a small report about "Object oriented languages and their
>public implementations" for a course in Software Engineering.

Please do your own homework.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I need your experience - classification and comparison of languages

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T12:11:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.ada,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<b9660815.0201210010.53b3266@posting.google.com> <a2h9h3$ggo$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<a2h9h3$ggo$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> In article <b9660815.0201210010.53b3266@posting.google.com>,
> Yvan Radenac <yvan.radenac@equant.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD


Not fair, Doc.  He is doing generalized research on an extremely wide
field, in which he cannot possibly have personal expertise.  Given the
topic, and in spite of the refernce provided by another post-er, it is
sufficiently current and topical that there is probably very little in
the literature which is relavant.  "Field research", such as inquiring
of experts, is completely appropriate in such a case.

When I was in college, I had a "major paper" topic for which I could
not find a resolution.  I had done what I considered a very thorough
analysis of the literature.  Finally, I realized that one of the
frequent contributors on the topic was on the faculty of my school. I
looked him up, and aked him if there was (yet) a firm determination of
the Genetic Cause of Audiogenic Seizure Susceptibility in Mice. 
Rather than tell me to do my own homework, he invited me to sit down,
and told me what he knew.

I think, given the topic, Mr Radenac is entitled to the same courtesy.

{Just for the record, Doc, I usually back you up when you give that
advice.  For someone who just hasn't paid attention in class, or
hasn't cracked the book, it's FINE advice.  Mr Radenac's assignment,
on the other hand, requires a legitimate search for expert opinion.}

Stephen J Spiro
```

#### ↳ Re: I need your experience - classification and comparison of languages

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T02:24:19+00:00

```


Yvan Radenac wrote:

> I am writing a small report about "Object oriented languages and their
> public implementations" for a course in Software Engineering.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yvan,

You've already been referred to one book. If your university has the following,
then it is a good reference covering all OO languages with the EXCEPTION of COBOL,
and of course, excluding any language du jour, which has popped up since the book
was printed :-

'Object Oriented Program Languages" - Handbook of Programming Languages Volume I.-
Peter H. Salus, Series Editor in Chief. (ISBN : 1-57870-009-4 ) -
Macmillan Technical Publishing, 1998.

To get a background on OO COBOL, (which has been around since '96), see the draft
for the next standard due December 2002  :-

        http://www.incits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

The draft (particularly Annex C) gives simple OO coding examples. Two things which
I am currently using which as yet are not part of the above COBOL standard,  (and
are still waiting to be determined)  :-

    - finalizing (destruction of objects - 'garbage' collection)
    - collections/dictionaries ( Those available from Micro Focus closely follow
the
      structures in Smalltalk.  If you are lucky, and your CS Department has a CD
      copy of  Micro Focus Net Express, University Edition - then you could access
      the on-line help to see the structure of collections/dictionaries.) Both
Fujitsu and
      Hitachi have collections - but I'm not aware of the details.

At the current time there are four vendors with OO COBOL compilers - IBM, Fujitsu,
Hitachi and Micro Focus, (the latter was temporarily under the name 'Merant'). IBM
is a very 'cut-down' version as they are having a re-think. No information is
available from Hitachi as it is only marketed in Japan. You can access the Fujitsu
and Micro Focus sites for further information. (Fujitsu is currently enhanced to
work with Microsoft .Net).

> Thanks you to complete the 2 tables below for each language you use.
>

Sorry that's a very subjective thing. I'd probably give COBOL high marks which
other language users might dispute <G>. (Although I have access to COBOL-designed
Java classes - currently I work TOTALLY in COBOL including classes for generating
GUIs).

Jimmy, Calgary AB

>
> Classification:
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
>   - Portability and standardization.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I need your experience - classification and comparison of languages

- **From:** yvan.radenac@equant.com (Yvan Radenac)
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T06:55:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.ada,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9660815.0201280655.2bfdea9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b9660815.0201210010.53b3266@posting.google.com> <3C4CCE5E.DC2187C2@shaw.ca>`

```
Thank you for your posts, it's the first time i use the news and my
english is far from perfect.
Thank you for all url you gave me.
It's a good beginning for my report.

I'm interresting in your experience as programmer, responsible of
projects, ....
As i will make a average of all results to obtain something the most
objective i can.

I found some old documents and comparisons (they are written between
1992 and 1997) or very subjective (like Java versus others by Sun,
...).

It seems that the languages the most used will be:
Ada (95), Beta, OCaml, CLOS, C++, Objective-C, Eiffel, Java, Modula-3,
O Oberon, Object Pascal (like Turbo-Pascal, Delphi, ...), Perl,
Python, Ruby, Sather?, Simula, Smalltalk.

I don't add to this list all the languages that have only a commercial
implementation, like VBA, ...
as i write a report about "oo languages and their public
implementation".

If you think i forget one or more oo common languages, let me know.

The result will be posted under the Free Documentation Licence from
GNU.

Of course, if you want to answer this table, thank you.

Regards
Yvan

Comparison:
----------
It's based on this scale table (to simplify):
Very bad|bad|Correct|Good|Very good|
--------|---|-------|----|---------|
  - -   | - |   O   |  + |   + +   |

Language|Readibility|Writability|Reliability|Cost
--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----
        |           |           |           |


P.S.: a resume of the criterias, based on a course of The University
of Ottawa by Szpakowicz:
Comparison:
----------
Readability:
  - abstraction, support for generality: procedural abstraction, data
absraction.
  - absence of ambiguity (and of too much choice).
  - Orthogonality: no restrictions on combinations of concepts. For
example, can a procedure parametrer have ANY type? Can EVERYTHING be
evaluated?
  - Expressivity of control and data structures. (Exemples of low
expressive power: machine languages).
  - Appearance: style of comments, ...
Writability:
  - Abstraction and simplicy like readibility.
  - Expressivity, like readibility.
  - Modularity and tools for modularization, support for integrated
programmer's environments.
Reliability:
  - Safety for the programmer (type checking, error and exception
handling, unambiguous naming).
Cost:
  - Development time (ease of programming, availability of code).
  - Efficiency of implementation: how easy it is to build a language
processor.
  - Translation time and quality of object code.
  - Portability and standardization.
```

## Thread: DOS switch for Fujitsu V3 compiler — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### DOS switch for Fujitsu V3 compiler

- **From:** "Jessop" <Jessop_Torrid@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T21:23:14+10:30
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<newscache$vbbaqg$eqo$1@maggie.netlink.com.au>`

```
Does anyone know the dos switch(es) to use so that I can compile source code
straight from the DOS prompt using Fujitsu V30L10?

e.g. wincob -i {file}
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS switch for Fujitsu V3 compiler

- **From:** "mkozelka" <mkozelka@remove.ameritech.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T19:57:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<to_28.2955$58.2431421@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<newscache$vbbaqg$eqo$1@maggie.netlink.com.au>`

```
Hi,

    I use the following batch file with replaceable parameters to compile &
link COBOL source code at the DOS prompt in Windows 2000.  It may have to be
modified for Fuji Cobol V3. I call the batch file "CL.BAT" for CompileLink.
The syntax is "CL Program".

echo off
rem Compile - Link COBOL source code using Fujitsu COBOL 5

set CobPath=D:\PROGRAM FILES\Fujitsu Cobol\cobol
SET @WINCLOSEMSG=OFF
rem Compile program as MAIN, using DOS Console

"%CobPath%\COBOL32.EXE" -WC,MAIN(MAIN) %1.cob

rem Pause to allow Control/Break if errors in compile
pause

rem Link, using standard Libraries
"%CobPath%\LINK" %1.obj F3BICIMP.LIB LIBC.LIB KERNEL32.LIB
/subsystem:CONSOLE /out:"%1.exe"


End of Batch file.  Note that the lines may wrap in your e-mail program
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DOS switch for Fujitsu V3 compiler

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T14:48:50+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4cc64c_10@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<newscache$vbbaqg$eqo$1@maggie.netlink.com.au> <to_28.2955$58.2431421@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <dh238.315319$WW.14343132@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

warren.simmons <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:dh238.315319$WW.14343132@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> Do any of you feel that "tips" like the batch file below belongs in some
> part of a Users Manual?
>
I think that if a Visual environment is available it should be used.

Batch files are better left on DOS machines...

Before you start sharpening the stakes let me say I have written hundreds of
them and still occasionally use one, but I PREFER not to. Why write a batch
file to compile and link, when there is an environment provided that will
organize my programs into projects, automatically keep track of which
modules I updated, select the correct compiler and linker options for me
(with the option for me to override), and compile and link the whole lot
when I click on "Build"?

The only reason I can see for anyone to use independent manual compile and
link steps is because they are used to working in a mainframe (or PC/DOS)
environment where this is the the only option, UNLESS you write a Batch file
or build JCL procedures.

The more "invisible" the process of compiling and linking is the more I like
it. A series of editing sessions, followed by a click on "Build", then a
click on "Debug" or "Execute" suits me down to the ground, and I would
encourage others to do likewise...

Fujitsu V3 comes with both PowerCOBOL and COBOL 85 Project Managers (in
addition to the Wincob, Link, facilities). Using a Visual Project Manager
makes  the job of maintenance easier for both yourself and any programmers
who follow you...

BOTTOM LINE:

Batch procedures do not belong in the User Manual (or anywhere other than in
DOS based systems...), as long as there is a Visual alternative provided.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS switch for Fujitsu V3 compiler

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

```
Jessop wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know the dos switch(es) to use so that I can compile source code
> straight from the DOS prompt using Fujitsu V30L10?
> 
> e.g. wincob -i {file}

I did set up version 4 to bulk compile a complete system using a
makefile.  The Compile that I used for .DLLs is:

    Cobol32 -ioptionfile -NM source.CBL

I recall that I determined this by creating a project for a couple of
programs and then seeing what the build process generated.
```

```
code

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs and then seeing what the build process generated.

I do both.  The project manager puts a batch file together as part of it's
process, and it is very handy to create a master compile of all projects
(the project manager will not allow one project to contain another, which
means you end up with one "system" being more than one "project"). I simply
create a batch file with one call statement per project, plus some copies to
library runtime libraries. That makes a nice easy way to re-compile
everything, and tuck the results into the right spot.

Donald
```

```
hehe.  I have some of that code myself.  The really humbling part is that I
wrote it myself about twenty, twenty-five years back.  The nasty part is
that it was not that bad, when I wrote it.  All these years of hacking it up
have degenerated it, though.  Now I am a bit afraid to try a complete
re-write, as the stuff actually works quite well.  It is just ugly, ugly
ugly.

Donald
```

```
programming and told us we would never use it.  Both of my mainframe gigs had
```

```
Susan A Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com> wrote in message > 
> I want to build a COBOL routine that extracts the required information
> from the IMS tables and insert it into a DB2 table.  Once tested I want
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> reading segments)

Hello Susan,

If you want to use IMS, you must have knowlegde of Program
Specification Blocks e.d. It's not easy for someone with a relational
mind!

I suggest: 

An IMS database is nothing more than a sequential file. You can read
it, sort it, etc.

So, define the IMS tables you need, ask for a copys of those tables(or
make a copy yourself) to be sure nothing can go wrong!.

Sort the files in such an order, Jackson would be delighted. Than make
one, or several cobol programs to read (balance-line) an insert the
records in your DB2 table.

Greetings,

M.
```

```
Ron,

One place to investigate is http://www.pdflib.com/pdflib/index.html.  PDFlib
is a library that is usable on Unix/Linux.  I built a PDFlib interface for
RM/COBOL which is available at http://www.liant.com/support/code/ (first
entry).  If you were to take a similar approach with AcuCOBOL. this solution
would require some recoding in your COBOL programs to use the CALL interface
rather that OPEN/WRITE/CLOSE.  However, you might want to change only one or
a few reports, which might make this option useful.

Another possibility is a subscription to http://createpdf.adobe.com.   I am
sure that one can hack the interface to make it usable from Unix, if you are
connected.

Happy hunting!

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
www.liant.com
```

```
Ron and Tom:

FormPrint can print to the Acrobat PDF Writer (Windows Printer Driver)
and produce Acrobat PDF files.

UNIX isn't a problem either, because the Flexus COBOL Thin Client
supports UNIX servers and FormPrint works very well with Flexus COBOL
Thin Client.  This allows you to run the COBOL application on the UNIX
server and print across a TCP/IP network to a Windows print server.
```

## Thread: SEQUENTIAL SEARCH WITH TWO CONDITION. — 3 msgs

### SEQUENTIAL SEARCH WITH TWO CONDITION.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T20:05:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0201212005.14a013a9@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
                         Read about sequential search, we have a
situation where the search is to be performed checking two condition
simultaneously. Do we have something similar to
EVALUATE...WHEN....ALSO....in SEARCH clause to check for two
conditions?
                         We have a ws-table predefined with value
clause.We have to perform a SEARCH on this, checking two condition
simultaneously.If SEARCH does not work, could we check the two
condition checked with IF and manipulate index with SET clause.Just
wondering how efficient this would be?
                        We are working on MVS platform.
thanks in advance.

Regards.
shyam
```

#### ↳ Re: SEQUENTIAL SEARCH WITH TWO CONDITION.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T22:19:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2ip9i$qc3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201212005.14a013a9@posting.google.com>`

```
Check out the online information on serial SEARCH at:\

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGYLR205/6.2.32.4

As it indicates "condition-1" in the WHEN phrase *may* be a compound
condition.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SEQUENTIAL SEARCH WITH TWO CONDITION.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T01:03:34+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4d56e9_15@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0201212005.14a013a9@posting.google.com> <a2ip9i$qc3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:a2ip9i$qc3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> Check out the online information on serial SEARCH at:\
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have also used multiple conditions specified in multiple WHEN clauses
(each of which can be a compound condition, as Bill noted, above), but I
can't remember whether this was valid on the mainframe platform (try it and
see...).

Like this...

set my-table-index to 1
SEARCH  my-table
     AT END
           set not-found to TRUE
     WHEN
           some-condition OR some-other-condition
           set found-it to TRUE
      WHEN
           some-lower-priority-condition OR
some-condition-indirectly-related
           set found-it to TRUE
END-SEARCH
if found-it
   perform process-as-found
else
   perform process-as-not-found
end-if
...

AT END will only be taken if ALL of the conditions in ALL of the WHEN
clauses, after ALL the increments of the table index, are found NOT to be
true. In this case the table index is technically undefined, but in
practice, usually points to the last entry + 1.

 If ONE condition in ANY WHEN clause is found to be true for ANY iteration,
then the Search stops and the action in the WHEN Clause is taken (Set
found-it to TRUE  in the above, but it could be anything you like.) In this
case the table index will be pointing at the entry which was being processed
when the condition was found to be true. Note that, while it is usual for
conditions to pertain to the data in the table, they don't HAVE to... I'm
not going into this here as it is a bit esoteric but take my word for it,
SEARCH can be used to do some VERY sexy things...(WARNING: One man's "sexy"
is another man's (read "maintenance programmer") nightmare...<G>)

As you can see, SEARCH is a very powerful and useful COBOL feature and
avoids the need to increment and check indexes (it is done for you "under
the covers"). A spin off is that f you change the definition of the table by
adding more entries to it you DON'T have to change the SEARCH verb; if you
searched the table manually with your own increment and test (say,
PERFORM... VARYING...) you would need to amend the boundary condition if
more entries were added to the table. (This was a frequent cause of error in
the days before SEARCH was available, and I for one, was very glad when we
could use SEARCH instead.)

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

<a id="t-ac5c07aab1"></a>
## Thread: Stack and Heap concept in COBOL — 7 msgs

### Stack and Heap concept in COBOL

- **From:** menonshyam@yahoo.com (shyam menon)
- **Date:** 2002-01-21T22:57:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,  
  I am new to this group.I am a bit confused about the memory
allocation in COBOL. Till now i was under the impression that there is
no dynamic allocation of memory in COBOL and the Working storage
variables are allocated memory when the load module is loaded.
  But i found the following information about Working storage
variables in an IBM manual:
Usage of Stack and Heap Storage by Language Environment-Conforming
Languages

Language  Stack                       Heap  
COBOL    Intrinsic functions          WORKING-STORAGE variables      
         Library routines                                            
         LOCAL-STORAGE variables(1)                                  
  

 STACK        Used by library routine stack frames that can reside
                 anywhere in storage
 HEAP         Allocates storage for user-controlled dynamically
                 allocated variables  

  Could anyone help me with understanding this?

Thanks in advance,
Shyam Menon.
```

#### ↳ Re: Stack and Heap concept in COBOL

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T12:57:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020122075724.12654.00000169@mb-fr.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com>`

```
Shyam Menon writes ...

>Hi,  
>  I am new to this group.I am a bit confused about the memory
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Could anyone help me with understanding this?

There is no _explicit_ dynamic allocation of memory in COBOL '85, even with the
'89 amendment. There may be some in COBOL 2002, but I haven't looked at the new
proposed standard yet.

But the IBM mainframe COBOL compiler uses dynamic allocation behind the scenes
as you described. In addition, all supported IBM compilers now require the use
of a facility called "Language Environment" (LE). LE includes callable
functions to dynamically allocate, resize, and free Heap storage.

Hope this helps.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Stack and Heap concept in COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T19:01:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2l20r$59i$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com> <20020122075724.12654.00000169@mb-fr.aol.com>`

```
FYI-
  The (opposed/draft) 2002 Standard *does* introduce the ALLOCATE and FREE
statements for "explicit" dynamic storage allocation.

It also distinguishes Working-Storage from Local-Storage.  In *most*
implementations (although this is NOT specified in the draft Standard), I
would expect Local-Storage to be allocated from "stack storage".  IBM (along
with Micro Focus - and possibly some others) already supports Local-Storage
as an EXTENSION to the '85/'89/'91 Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stack and Heap concept in COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T20:03:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2l5l3$8et$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com> <20020122075724.12654.00000169@mb-fr.aol.com> <a2l20r$59i$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Talk about a "typo"  <G> (aka Freudian slip)

That "(opposed/draft) 2002 Standard" was SUPPOSED to be "proposed/draft"
```

#### ↳ Re: Stack and Heap concept in COBOL

- **From:** daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch (Daniel Jacot)
- **Date:** 2002-01-22T06:15:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f3c351f.0201220615.57ffb089@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com>`

```
menonshyam@yahoo.com (shyam menon) wrote in message news:<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com>...
> Hi,  
>   I am new to this group.

Well, hello and welcome to CLC!

> I am a bit confused about the memory allocation in COBOL.

Don't mind, others are confused, too &-))

> Till now i was under the impression that there is
> no dynamic allocation of memory in COBOL and the Working storage
> variables are allocated memory when the load module is loaded.

Yes, you are right. WORKING-STORAGE is allocated when the program is
executed for the first time in a thread and remains allocated (and in
its last used state) if you call the program again. So , variables in
WORKING-STORAGE behave like they where static variables of a C or PL/I
program.

>   But i found the following information about Working storage
> variables in an IBM manual:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>          LOCAL-STORAGE variables(1) 

OK, now we are talking of LOCAL-STORAGE instead of WORKING-STORAGE.
Variables which are declared in the LOCAL-STORAGE Section are
allocated in the STACK rather than in the HEAP. They are intialized at
every entry of the program and freed as soon as the program reaches
its end.
You will not be too surprised if I tell you that this kind of
variables is called automatic in C and PL/I. You need LOCAL-STORAGE
variables if you need a COBOL program to be called recursive (and of
course you need at least one variable in WORKING-STORAGE to be able to
stop the recursion).

OK, and the library routines and the intrinsic functions use the STACK
too. Probably, these routines are written in C and use automatic
storage. No problem, let them use what they have to use!
                                 
>   
> 
>  STACK        Used by library routine stack frames that can reside
>                  anywhere in storage

I'd guess you are now in another chapter of the manual. In which
chapter?

>  HEAP         Allocates storage for user-controlled dynamically
>                  allocated variables 

If a user-program ALLOCATEs storage, it gets HEAP storage. In COBOL,
there is no verb for ALLOCATE, you have to CALL CEEGTST.
 
> 
>   Could anyone help me with understanding this?

OK, now exactly WHAT are you trying to understand? If you are aware of
the (writable) static and the automatic concept in C, you should be
happy to see that COBOL is able to handle these kind of storage too.
But, to be honest, most people know that in COBOL all variables are in
its last used state, so they forget about the VALUE clause and they
are just happy.

> 
> Thanks in advance,
> Shyam Menon.

Daniel
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Stack and Heap concept in COBOL

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T19:59:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0201241959.5182fbc9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com> <7f3c351f.0201220615.57ffb089@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
                Referring to your reply "WORKING-STORAGE is allocated
when the program is executed for the first time in a thread and
remains allocated (and in its last used state)".
                Now my concern  is if WORKING-STORAGE is defined at
run time then at compile time why I have to compile with REGION = 0M
When length of data defined in WORKING-STORAGE is too high?
               when I tried with REGION=6M I got MAXCC=16?These two
are contradicting each other.
              Please clear my doubts.
Thanks and Regards.
shyam
*******************************************************************************
daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch (Daniel Jacot) wrote in message news:<7f3c351f.0201220615.57ffb089@posting.google.com>...
> menonshyam@yahoo.com (shyam menon) wrote in message news:<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com>...
> > Hi,  
…[71 more quoted lines elided]…
> Daniel
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stack and Heap concept in COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T23:19:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2qptt$s9u$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<10986fae.0201212257.6f9e5ec2@posting.google.com> <7f3c351f.0201220615.57ffb089@posting.google.com> <c02fd744.0201241959.5182fbc9@posting.google.com>`

```
The amount of storage needed to compile a program has (close to) NOTHING to
do with the amount of storage allocated at run-time.  As you are using an
IBM mainframe compiler, check out your value for the SIZE *compiler-option".

For a RENT compiled program, the size of the load module *will* include the
size of all literals (both user-defined and compiler-defined) as well as (at
least one copy) of WS.  The amount of "virtual storage" needed to COMPILE a
program has LOTS to do with the "complexity" of the program, its source code
length, and many, MANY other items - with the size of WS being a "miniscule"
part of the equation.  The amount of virtual storage needed to RUN a COBOL
program (RENT, NORENT, with dynamic or static calls, whatever) *does* have a
lot to do with WS (and file section, and procedure division) and many, MANY
other things - most of which can be INFERRED from the load module size.

Shyam,
  I hate to keep coming back to this, but it really, REALLY sounds to me as
if you are approaching IBM mainframe COBOL development with

 A) inadequate training
 B) "misconceptions" based on different programming languages and operating
systems
```

```
wrote:
```

#### ↳ Re: number of the current record

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T22:40:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sZG38.646479$C8.45778107@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<6jll2a.mta.ln@gw1.rsw-orga.de>`

```

"Thomas H�rkens" <thoerkens@rsw-orga.de> wrote in message
news:6jll2a.mta.ln@gw1.rsw-orga.de...
> Hello,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> have someone a good idea? thanks for your help

One way to know the number of records in a file is to count them, stash the
count in a header record, and update this stashed count based on adds and
deletes during normal processing.

There is no 'number of the current record' for an ISAM file. There is a KEY
of the current record. Maybe you're thinking of relative files?
```

#### ↳ Re: number of the current record

## Thread: Acucobol Thin Client — 5 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Acucobol Thin Client

- **From:** aehodges@hotmail.com (Andrew Hodges)
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T02:04:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c692d8f3.0201230204.2319140d@posting.google.com>`

```
Have many people here used Acucobol's new thin client?

We have been testing the released version of the thin client and it
does seem to be reasonably quick as long as you have a beefed up
server to run multiple users.

During our testing we have experienced numerous "Illegal operation"
messages produced from Windows that kills the thin client on the PC.
These errors are coming up every now and then so we cannot reliably
reproduce the problem to give any evidence to Acucobol about these
errors. The trace file from Aculaunch and the runtime do not show any
information when the sessions are killed so we cannot determine if the
problem is with our application or the thin client itself. We have
been testing this running on Windows 95/98 PC's with a SCO Unix 5.05
server and also Windows PC's with a Windows NT 4 server. Both these
configurations are producing these errors.

We did not notice these errors during the beta testing cycle as their
was usually only 1 person using it, but since the released version has
come out everyone in the office (5-6 sessions at any one time) are
using it and all of us are getting these errors as many as 10 times a
day.

Once the "Illegal operation" message appears I can do an acurcl -info
and my session appears in the connectioned sessions list so it appears
as the thin client is still in communication with Aculaunch, but as
soon as I hit OK to accept the error message which kills the thin
client on the PC the process is gone from the acurcl -info display. We
initially thought they were occurring because we were running out of
memory on the server side, we have since tripled the memory on the
Unix server but these errors still persist.

I would appreciate any feedback from anyone using the thin client in a
multi user environment and any issues they have with it, especially
what do you think about the speed over a 56K modem and regular local
area networks.

Regards,

Andrew Hodges
```

```
-100% compiler independent, keep using your Acucobol compiler
-sold and supported since 1996, 5 years of experience
-minimal server requirements and extremely fast network execution
-have 1,000 concurrent user installations running without problems
-128 bit data encryption
-automated installation/version control system
-data compression feature to increase throughput speed
-download on demand feature to minimize client side software
-remote Windows printing and print preview
-client side OCX control support
-file transfer in both directions supported
-ability to launch remote programs in either direction
-2 tier and 3 tier architecture supported
-run standalone clients or display clients in web browser with plug-in
-servers support for Win32; UNIX & UNIX derivatives; VMS & Open VMS

Send an e-mail to rtwolfe@flexus.com for more information.
```

## Thread: Finding text in text — 16 this month / 18 total

### Finding text in text

- **From:** ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith)
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T03:32:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi

This is in an os/390 environment.

I need to parse each record of an input file (which contains IDCAMS
delete statements) and examine each record to see if the text contains
a particular string.  Basically I want:

If in-rec contains ".text."
   perform
end-if.

Haven't done too much string manipulation in COBOL so help would be
appreciated.
(also new to this group, so hi)

TIA
Simon Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: Finding text in text

- **From:** "Thierry Vindevogel" <caca.prout.boudin@bittepoilcul.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T13:09:31+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tvx38.247$Af2.6500@news.chello.be>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com>`

```
Use the "inspect" statement.
Example :

move 0 to W-NBR.
inspect in-rec tallying W-NBR for all "text".

W-NBR will content the number of instances of "text" in your string.
There are a lot of  inspect formats. Have a look in your manuals.


Vinde
Brussels.
```

```
ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith) wrote in message news:<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com>...
> Hi

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Simon Smith

Keep in mind that the IDCAMS output is fully formatted.  If you know
the line numbers (count them on a sample output!) where the data will
occur, and if you know the start/end positions of the containing text,
you will make your search simpler.

I once needed to get tape numbers from IDCAMS LISTCAT output: it was
easy, they were always is the same place.

Stephen J Spiro
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Finding text in text

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T13:00:46+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c4f513f_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com> <928495c6.0201230817.2568a2c4@posting.google.com>`

```
Sorry Stephen,

I strongly disagree on this and consider it is very bad advice.

INSPECT will meet his requirements admirably. REXX would do so even better.

What one should NEVER do when dealing with text is assume it will be in a
fixed location.

Having worked on COBOL compilers and knowing a thing or two about scanning
and parsing text, it is my firm opinion that relying on a fixed format input
is the path to ruin.(I accept that this is opinion, not fact, that it is
arguable, but it is informed opinion and I am about to give my arguments for
believing it.)

Having tried it on numerous occasions, I am persuaded that it is really not
much more difficult to write a parser that will find text in ANY location,
than it is to write one that finds it in a fixed location. The difference
with a general solution to this problem is that you do it once and the
problem is solved forever. If you tie it to fixed locations you are buying
maintenance in the future. What happens if a new release of whatever it is
that you are scanning the output from comes out and they have changed the
format?

I would not advise anyone considering any kind of parser or scanner to tie
it to fixed locations, other than starting at 1....

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Finding text in text

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T21:07:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0201242107.4ad2ff3e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com> <928495c6.0201230817.2568a2c4@posting.google.com> <3c4f513f_5@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3c4f513f_5@Usenet.com>...
> Sorry Stephen,
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>

Do you parse all your input files, Pete? Of course not. 
The Report output of IDCAMS is a fixed format file.  All the fields
have fixed locations and fixed lengths, and occur in particular
records, which are in a fixed sequence. It is not properly a "text"
file, except that it contains words and is frequently printed.  Your
advice is good for free-form text files, but unnecessary overhead for
formatted input.

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Finding text in text

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T14:00:35+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c520085_10@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com> <928495c6.0201230817.2568a2c4@posting.google.com> <3c4f513f_5@Usenet.com> <928495c6.0201242107.4ad2ff3e@posting.google.com>`

```

Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@mail.com> wrote in message
news:928495c6.0201242107.4ad2ff3e@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:<3c4f513f_5@Usenet.com>...
> Do you parse all your input files, Pete? Of course not.

Why would I do that? I don't understand what point you are trying to make
here.

If your point is that input files have known formats and can therefore be
referenced by a record definition so why not do the same with text, then I
stand by what I said. Looking for text (a given word/phrase/sentence) on the
assumption that it will occur in a particular location of a particular
record, is foolish. Text, by its very nature IS free format. Someone can
type an extra space and not even notice.

In the case of IDCAMS you are on firmer ground because it hasn't changed
output formats for many years. But that doesn't mean it won't...or that it
can't.

In cases where parsing is required my argument is that the solution should
be future proof. It is NOT difficult to make it so... Very effective parsing
can be done by Transition Diagramming and string slicing. The same solution
can be used in any case where parsing is required. Input a list of targets
(like a COBOL Reserved Word list f'rinstance...) let the TD engine do its
one-time analysis, and away you go... Next time the problem of parsing
something rears its head, repeat the process with a different target list
(it could be just one or two words...). My parsing engine returns the
location where the target is and indicates which target was found. It is up
to the user to decide what action to take... Writing parsers is good fun and
the one I use currently I wrote 25 years ago...so much for "general
solutions"...<G>

> The Report output of IDCAMS is a fixed format file.  All the fields
> have fixed locations and fixed lengths, and occur in particular
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I guess we will have to disagree on whether the overhead is "unnecessary" or
not...<G>

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Finding text in text

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T19:28:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l9E38.21946$hp6.17378@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com>`

```
Here's one of those instances where it pays to ask the question:  "What is
it you are >trying< to do?"

I recognize that one of the "parts" is to find the string, ".TEXT" - but
why?  What are you going to do next?

Did you run an IDCAMS step prior to the COBOL program?

If so, you could then have another step and use ISPF's built-in SUPER-C
utility to find the string.  It will produce a vastly smaller - yet still
formatted - report of just the lines you need.  You could then have your
COBOL program process that as an input file.

Hope that helps...
```

```

:>   perform
:>end-if.

While this can be done using INSPECT why would you use COBOL instead of REXX?
```

```
>From: Binyamin Dissen postingid@dissensoftware.com 
>Date: 1/23/02 3:29 PM Eastern Standard Time

>While this can be done using INSPECT why would you use COBOL instead of REXX?

Well......(I know I'm gonna get dumped on, I know it, I know it, I know it....)

I've been coding COBOL for over 20 years working exclusively on IBM mainframes
using either CMS or ROSCOE as the editor. I've had access to TSO (ISPF) at
several shops but never got very proficient with it.  I've worked for several
Fortune 100 companies - and I have never seen anyone in the applications staff
use REXX.  

I've heard of it, even seen a manual roaming around once - but use it?  Nope.

Perhaps he's in the same boat?

Eileen
```

```

YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020123211219.28605.00000089@mb-fn.aol.com...
> >From: Binyamin Dissen postingid@dissensoftware.com
> >Date: 1/23/02 3:29 PM Eastern Standard Time
>
>I've worked for several
> Fortune 100 companies - and I have never seen anyone in the applications
staff
> use REXX.
>
> I've heard of it, even seen a manual roaming around once - but use it?
Nope.
>
I have the same background of using ROSCOE (Loved it!) and ISPF but there is
really no comparison to REXX. (ROSCOE is close, but it has to be said that
REXX is more powerful).

I'm amazed that you haven't encountered a REXX wiz somewhere in your travels
Eileen. The ones I've met are usually bright eyed and bushy tailed and just
LOVE to demonstrate the power of REXX. And I have seen some VERY clever
things done with it... While not an expert in it myself (I can read it, but
I wouldn't pretend to be facile in it) there is no denying that REXX in the
hands of someone who knows what they're doing with it, is really quite
awesome. It renders JCL virtually redundant (and it really should be...<G>)
and gives access in a few statements to the facilities of IDCAMS,
manipulates system logs, and JCL procedures, and that's before we start with
the application files.

For parsing data streams it is extremely powerful and I believe it would be
useful in the context that was described.

However, your point is well taken that if there is no such expertise on
site, then he HAS to use COBOL.

(I would argue that there SHOULD be such expertise on site and if there
wasn't, then I'd be acquiring it myself...<G> It really is that good..)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Finding text in text

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T04:45:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1a55b$2d92ffe0$f831e641@chottel>`
- **References:** `<r57u4uospknh1temmu0t25k73bqgpqe47v@4ax.com> <20020123211219.28605.00000089@mb-fn.aol.com>`

```
IIRC he wants to search a dataset and find all lines that contain a string.
 This is easily gone with a Roscoe Rpf:

    <<fstring>>
    import dsn=dataset.name,asis
    deletex /string/

If you do this a lot you can parmeterize the dsn and/or the string:

    <<fstring>>
    +import dsn=+A1+,asis
    +deletex /+A2+/

You would invoke this with: fstring 'dataset.name'  'string.value'

YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in article
<20020123211219.28605.00000089@mb-fn.aol.com>...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Finding text in text

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T08:13:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0201260813.294e89e5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com> <r57u4uospknh1temmu0t25k73bqgpqe47v@4ax.com>`

```
COBOL string manipulation gets a bad rap.  Very undeserved.  Consider
this problem and the COBOL solution:

One has a string of data with elements of varying length.  The
elements are delimited by a /.  You want to put each element in a
separate table entry, losing the / and appending a low-value
delimiter.  When you get all done, you want to know how many entries
you have.

The data also has places where a / is required in the output - when
this is true it uses // in the data - that must be accounted for - and
result in // in the output table element.

 Oh - and there's a twist - you don't want to start at the front of
the string - you want to omit the first 20 characters.  (Real problem,
I recently coded for).

(Note - input string always has a trailing / - I check for it in other
code and insert it if it's not there).

  inspect USR-PR-value replacing all "//" by high-values
  move 21 to string-pointer
  move zeros to cntr

  perform with test after until s-field (cntr) not > spaces
     add 1 to cntr
     move zeros to chars
     unstring USR-PR-value
              delimited by "/"
              into s-field (cntr)
              count in chars
              pointer string-pointer
     end-unstring
     if s-field (cntr) > spaces
        add 1 to chars
        move low-values to s-field (cntr) (chars:1)
        inspect s-field (cntr) converting high-values to "/"
     end-if
  end-perform
  subtract 1 from cntr

Cntr has the number of table elements  


Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:<r57u4uospknh1temmu0t25k73bqgpqe47v@4ax.com>...
> On 23 Jan 2002 03:32:03 -0800 ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith) wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> While this can be done using INSPECT why would you use COBOL instead of REXX?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Finding text in text

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T14:04:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0201311404.391d6596@posting.google.com>`

```
For my purposes - the field CANNOT contain a null.  Not meaning that I
won't tolerate it - it just DOESN'T and cannot by virtue of some other
things that are happening.  The illustration was just to show the
power of the manipulation.
```

#### ↳ Re: Finding text in text

- **From:** Ed_Stevens@nospam.noway.nohow (Ed Stevens)
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T13:58:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c50103a.262616772@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0201230332.2dde6885@posting.google.com>`

```
To solve the general problem of parsing a text string out of a pic x field . . .


* to find 'needle' in 'haystack'
perform 
    varying x from 1 to function length(haystack) 
    by 1
     until haystack(x,function length(needle)) = needle
end-perform
if x = function length(haystack)
  perform not-found-routine
else
  perform found-it-routine
end-if.

That's off the top of my head, so it may need some tweaking.  It may also get
flamed for performance issues, but it seems to me the most compact and flexible
method.

On 23 Jan 2002 03:32:03 -0800, ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith) wrote:

>Hi
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Simon Smith
```

## Thread: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-23T20:07:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4F7A71.A1CD1CFC@sprynet.com>`

```
We are finally trying to make the move to the LE/VSE runtime environment
for out COBOL programs.  We currently have both DOS/VS COBOL programs
and VS COBOL II programs.  Our first step is to make sure these programs
will run with the LE/VSE runtime.

My first problem is opening SYSIPT as an input file.  See the following
program.  It opens SYS005-S-TFIL as input and SYS010-S-PRFIL as output. 
When I put a DLBL for TFIL as an actual file there's no problem.  But
when I assign SYS005 to SYSIPT and include 'punch' cards after the EXEC
statement I get the following error on SYSLST:

 IGZ0083W THE OPEN OF FILE WITH SYSTEM-NAME TFIL (ON SYS005) WAS
UNSUCCESSFUL. THE SYSTEM LOGICAL UNIT WAS ASSIGNED TO AN
          UNSUPPORTED DEVICE.
 IGZ0035S THERE WAS AN UNSUCCESSFUL OPEN OR CLOSE OF FILE TFIL IN
PROGRAM TEST1 AT RELATIVE LOCATION X'03AA'.  NEITHER FILE STATUS
          NOR AN ERROR DECLARATIVE WERE SPECIFIED. THE STATUS CODE WAS
90.
          FROM COMPILE UNIT TEST1 AT ENTRY POINT TEST1 AT COMPILE UNIT
OFFSET +000003AA AT ADDRESS 007E0422.
1CEE5DMP V1 R4.1: CONDITION PROCESSING RESULTED IN THE UNHANDLED
CONDITION.

[the rest of the dump deleted]

                 * ------------------------------ CONSOLE SUMMARY REPORT
------------------------------ *

FTEST                                                           
01/23/2002
  17.04.45         DATE 01/23/2002, CLOCK
17/04/45                                         01/23/2002
  17.04.45 F5 0005 1T20I  SYS005 HAS BEEN ASSIGNED TO X'04C'
(TEMP)                        01/23/2002

0001.                                                      01/23/2002

           F5 0005 EOJ FTEST

And here's my JCL for
1) Using SYSIPT:
* $$ JOB
JNM=FTEST,CLASS=5,USER=FJS,DISP=D                                     
* $$ LST
CLASS=F,LST=04E                                                       
// JOB
FTEST                                                                   
// ASSGN
SYS005,SYSIPT                                                         
// ASSGN
SYS010,SYSLST                                                         
// LIBDEF
PHASE,SEARCH=(USER.TEST,PRD2.SCEEBASE)                               
/* LIBDEF
PHASE,SEARCH=(USER.TEST)                                             
// EXEC
FSTEST1                                                                
THIS IS A TEST LINE
1                                                          
THIS IS A TEST LINE
2                                                          
/*                                                                             
/&                                                                             
* $$
EOJ                                                                       

2) Using a VSAM-managed SAM file:
* $$ JOB
JNM=FTEST,CLASS=5,USER=FJS,DISP=D                                     
* $$ LST
CLASS=F,LST=04E                                                       
// JOB
FTEST                                                                   
// DLBL
OFILE,'FJS.TEST',0,VSAM,RECORDS=50,RECSIZE=80,CAT=PGMRCAT              
// EXEC
IDCAMS,SIZE=AUTO                                                       
   REPRO INFILE(SYSIPT ENV(RECFM(FB) BLKSZ(80) RECSZ(80)))
-                   
         OUTFILE(OFILE ENV(RECFM(FB) BLKSZ(80) RECSZ(80)))
-                   
        
REUSE                                                                 
THIS IS A TEST LINE
1                                                          
THIS IS A TEST LINE
2                                                          
/*                                                                             
// ASSGN
SYS010,SYSLST                                                         
// DLBL
TFIL,'FJS.TEST',,VSAM,CAT=PGMRCAT                                      
// LIBDEF
PHASE,SEARCH=(USER.TEST,PRD2.SCEEBASE)                               
/* LIBDEF
PHASE,SEARCH=(USER.TEST)                                             
// EXEC
FSTEST                                                                 
/&                                                                             
* $$
EOJ                                                                       

Both will run when my LIBDEF does not have PRD2.SCEEBASE included (in
which case the COBOL II runtime is used).  The second one will run under
LE, but the first gets the error described above.

Any thoughts?
```

#### ↳ Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T08:05:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4FC010.323DA978@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3C4F7A71.A1CD1CFC@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> We are finally trying to make the move to the LE/VSE runtime environment
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> (snip)

It's been over 15 years since I worked in a VSE shop, but I have a lot
of experience with COBOL II under MVS and OS/390.  The thought occurs
to me that perhaps your ASSGN statements are incorrect.

I might be assuming too much based on COBOL II under MVS, but if your
COBOL program assigns your input file to SYS005-S-TFIL, shouldn't your
ASSGN JCL statement look like this:

// ASSGN TFIL,SYSIPT

Otherwise, I don't see how you can connect SYS005-S-TFIL to SYSIPT.

If you really want // ASSGN SYS005,SYSIPT to work in the JCL, then
shouldn't your COBOL II program say:
SELECT INPUT-FILE-1 ASSIGN TO SYS005.

At least, that's how it would work under MVS.

With kindest regards,
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T05:16:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4FFB05.66B91C9C@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C4F7A71.A1CD1CFC@sprynet.com> <3C4FC010.323DA978@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> At least, that's how it would work under MVS.

I did originally have just "ASSIGN TO SYS005", but I added the TFIL so I
could use the same source code for both the case where the input was
SYSIPT and where the input was a file.  There's no such thing as "//
ASSGN TFIL,SYSIPT".  With a VSAM managed SAM file having the DLBL w/o an
ASSGN is fine.  The SYS005 part in the COBOL program is ignored.  If you
ASSGN to a system CUU (SYSIPT, SYSOUT, virtual printer) then the file
name (TFIL) is ignored.

In other words:

SELECT INPUT-FILE-1 ASSIGN TO SYS005 works (should work) if SYS005 is
assigned to SYSIPT or SYSOUT (no DLBL required).
SELECT INPUT-FILE-1 ASSIGN TO TFIL works if TFIL is a DLBL pointing to a
VSAM file (no ASSGN required).
SELECT INPUT-FILE-1 ASSIGN TO SYS005-TFIL works for either of the above,
plus it also works for SAM files (both an ASSGN and a DLBL required) and
tape files (both an ASSGN and TLBL required).

Because of the latter I always like to include both the SYScuu and the
DLBL/TLBL name to make the program as flexible as possible without
requiring recompilation.
```

#### ↳ Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** Michael Rosinger <rosinger@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T06:29:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C4FF01D.76575004@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<3C4F7A71.A1CD1CFC@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank,

This is one of the ways we do it -- and we do this a lot in both VS/COBOL
and COBOL/VSE programs:

     SELECT SYSRDR   ASSIGN TO SYS019-UT-SYSRD-S-SYSRDR.
. . .

FD  SYSRDR
    RECORDING MODE IS F
    BLOCK CONTAINS 80    CHARACTERS
    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
    SKIP1
01  CD000-OPTION-CARD-SETTINGS.
    05 CD005-BLANK-LINE-FOR-REDEFIN        PIC X(80).
    05 CD010-INSERT-MODE-SETTING
        REDEFINES CD005-BLANK-LINE-FOR-REDEFIN.
     10 CD020-COMMAND-PREFIX               PIC X(6).
     10 CD030-COMMAND-ALPHA                PIC X(9).
     10 CD035-COMMAND-NUMBER REDEFINES CD030-COMMAND-ALPHA
                                           PIC 9(9).
. . .

// ASSGN SYS019,00C    (OOC is a RDR device)
. . .
// EXEC DMS415,SIZE=3M
* $$ SLI MEM=DMSSTSCD.R,S=(CCILIB.CONTROL)
/*
```

```
our virtual RDR.  Of course I don't have all of the "UT-SYSRD-" stuff,
```

```
Frank,

Something deep in my memory says that when SYSIPT and SYSRDR are assigned to
the same device it needs to be SYSIN.

If you are trying to read cards or a file on SYSIPT this may apply.

(As this is being dredged from 30 years ago, forgive me if it is not
apposite...<G>)

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T16:47:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C5080E4.2C1E98C5@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3C4F7A71.A1CD1CFC@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
<snip>
> And here's my JCL for
> 1) Using SYSIPT:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> EOJ
<snip>

I'm gonna take a wild guess that your SELECT/ASSIGN is not
quite right.

Basically the following is what you now have:

    SELECT INPUT-FILE-1 ASSIGN TO SYS005-S-TFIL. 

I think you may find that it works better if you do the
following:

    SELECT INPUT-FILE-1 ASSIGN TO SYS005-S-SYS005.

Then your JCL would look like the following:

// ASSGN SYS005,SYSIPT
 
OR

// DLBL SYS005,'FJS.TEST',,VSAM,CAT=PGMRCAT 

Then again, I haven't touched VSE since before VSE/ESA was
released. But I think this is what your problem is related
to.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

```

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

What system are you using?

In MVS systems, I used to keep samples of the SELECT/ASSIGN syntax and cut
and pasted them.   I was very glad when they changed it so that we could say
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T11:54:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C518DB7.22ED2474@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3C4F7A71.A1CD1CFC@sprynet.com> <3C5080E4.2C1E98C5@ix.netcom.com> <a2rs09$h5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
<snip>
> 
> What system are you using?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> You can't do that with your system?

Well, the question had to do with VSE, not MVS.

Originally in the DOS world, the ASSIGN part of the SELECT
had to be coded as follows:

..... ASSIGN  SYSnnn-c-d-o-x

Where:
c=class:
	DA for direct access, 
	UT for Utility
	UR for Unit Record
d=device (which had to match class):
	2501 (card reader)
	1442x (card reader/punch were x=R/P)
	3350 (disk drive)
	2314 (disk drive)
	1403 (printer)
	etc.
	etc.
o=Organization (which had to match the device):
	S for standard sequential
	A for direct (actual track adddressing)
	D for direct (relative track addressing)
	U for direct w/ REWRITE (actual track)
	W for direct w/ REWRITE (relative)
	I for ISAM
x=FNAME (if not given, then the SYSnnn becomes the FNAME)
	FNAME is treated as DDNAME for OS type systems (VS1, MVS,
etc.).

Once updates were done for VSAM, then things changed about
ye ol' ASSIGN clause. So the answer given must comply with
what is done for VSE and an MVS based answer may or may not
work as VSE handles VSAM differently than does MVS (in fact,
up until VSAM, DOS did not have a CATALOG - although one
could argue that the standard and alternate cylinders
provided such).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

```
> had to be coded as follows:
```

```
Hi Steve,

PATH is STEPLIB,JOBLIB,JOBCAT,STEPCAT,SYSABEND,SYSUDUMP,SYSMDUMP
.Following is the statements from the book:
**************************************************************************
Do not code a PATH parameter on the following DD statements:

JOBCAT
JOBLIB
STEPCAT
STEPLIB
SYSABEND
SYSMDUMP
SYSUDUMP

    *Coding the PATH parameter is useful only when one of the
following is true:
The job runs on a system with both MVS SP5.2.2 or later and DFSMS/MVS
1.3. At this level, programs can use standard MVS access methods to
work
with hierarchical files. See OS/390 DFSMS: Using Data Sets.
     *The program being run has been coded to recognize and process
the PATH
specification. Programs designed to use such DD statements must
either:
    &#8211; Use dynamic allocation information retrieval to obtain the
information
specified for PATH, PATHOPTS, and PATHMODE, and pass it to the
open() callable service. See OS/390 UNIX System Services User's Guide
for details on using open().
    &#8211; Use the C/370 fopen(//dd: ) function. fopen() handles the
differences
between DD statements with PATH and DSN specified. See OS/390 UNIX
System Services User's Guide for details on using fopen().
*******************************************************************************
                    But the explanation for not using path in above DD
statement is not given.I am unable to understand why we should not use
path in above DD statements?
Thanks and Regards.
shyam
************************************************************************
```

```
I'll make one suggestion though, in general terms. When you've got this
particular problem licked, a similar scenario is going to arise where you
want another 'set' of classes and properties. Certainly possible within
Net Express, and I'll take a bet it's also viable in Fujitsu - see if you
can clone a general class that holds the 'common' classes and properties
that you need.. Then invoke that Omnibus class from your Business
Program/Class for the repetitive routines/controls you need. (Maxim - done
once and proven, it is constantly reusable by creating 'new' instances).

Tony - who let you out of your cage ?

Jimmy
```
