<a id="t-ca04932472"></a>
## Thread: VIO or buffer — 15 msgs

### VIO or buffer

- **From:** kedarnj@hotmail.com (Kedar)
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T07:48:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com>`

```
At my workshop we are working on an application in COBOL on OS390
wherein the input file is to be split at intervals and this small
subset is to be written to a workfile. I need to close this workfile
as it would be handled by a C++ tool. Depending upon the return code
(in special register RETURN-CODE) I reopen the workfile in COBOL and
further process it. On completion of this cycle the workfile would be
overwritten and processed in the same way. This continues till
End-of-file of input master file.

	This process is to happen in batch and the above-explained cycle
would repeat at least 10000 times. Earlier we were using a DASD file
for workfile. Then we used VIO file and the performance was greatly
improved. However I have no idea as to whether it (use of VIO file) is
going to affect the operation in production region? The VIO file is
defined with SPACE=(TRK,(50,50,0)), and the record length is 200.

	Also if rather than using a VIO file if a WS area was used as a "work
area" that would be shared by COBOL and C++ (some sort of COMMAREA),
what would have been functional and operational difference in both
approaches? (This approach was tried earlier but because of some
difficulties was discarded later). Which approach would have been
efficacious? Can anyone kindly comment on this?

Thanks in advance,
Best regards,
Kedar
```

#### ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T11:00:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0201241100.698ee9bc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello Kedar,

If at all possible, store the data in working-storage and call the C
program to retrieve it.  You need to be sure that the amount of data
will not exceed the amount of working-storage that can be defined in a
COBOL program.

VIO in a production environment is an excellent idea, that's what IBM
produced it for.  It would be a good idea to discuss how it should
best be used with the people that set up production jobs and your
system programmers, who may have alternate ideas of how best to
organise the specific tasks for which it is being considered.

Regards

Robert

kedarnj@hotmail.com (Kedar) wrote in message news:<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com>...
> At my workshop we are working on an application in COBOL on OS390
> wherein the input file is to be split at intervals and this small
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Kedar
```

#### ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T16:53:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C50826F.D99DF384@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com>`

```
Kedar wrote:
<snip> 
>         Also if rather than using a VIO file if a WS area was used as a "work
> area" that would be shared by COBOL and C++ (some sort of COMMAREA),
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> efficacious? Can anyone kindly comment on this?
<snip>

First the VIO question: As I recall VIO actually is operated
in a page data set if it is small enough. So for small
amounts of data, this make it quite fast.

The possible problem that had been experienced before may
have to do with how much data can be kept within one "01"
level. This may have been the problem that they ran into.
The other thing is the old buffer overflow problem! If the
C++ program does not know the length of the data (or does
not properly track this), then it can over run the incore
buffer (the working storage area). Both of these things may
have come into play.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T00:15:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2q4hv$r1b$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com> <3C50826F.D99DF384@ix.netcom.com>`

```
"Steve Thompson" <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:3C50826F.D99DF384@ix.netcom.com...
> First the VIO question: As I recall VIO actually is operated
> in a page data set if it is small enough. So for small
> amounts of data, this make it quite fast.

AFAIK it is in-storage if small enough, backed by page space as necessary.

> The possible problem that had been experienced before may
> have to do with how much data can be kept within one "01"
> level. This may have been the problem that they ran into.

This problem will vanish as soon as one dismiss the old habit of defining
all work storage in WORKING-STORAGE.  Use linked lists defined in LINKAGE
SECTION and allocate storage with Language Environment service call.

> The other thing is the old buffer overflow problem! If the
> C++ program does not know the length of the data (or does
> not properly track this), then it can over run the incore
> buffer (the working storage area). Both of these things may
> have come into play.

This is just bad programming, or bugs, no excuse for that.

Regards,
Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T23:57:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2q3he$pmh$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Kedar,

VIO is fine for this, it will give you a performace improvement over real
file I/O, as you have observed.  If VIO is used extensively at an
installation, the system programmer might need to extend the page space.

You might benefit even more if you keep it in work storage (avoiding
simulated I/O overhead, especially open/close).  If ordinary working-storage
is not enough and you are running with Language Environment, you may use the
callable service Get/Free Heap Storage (CEEGTST/CEEFRST).  This may also
require page space.

Regards,
Gunnar.


"Kedar" <kedarnj@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com...
> At my workshop we are working on an application in COBOL on OS390
> wherein the input file is to be split at intervals and this small
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Kedar
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

- **From:** kedarnj@hotmail.com (Kedar)
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T21:11:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d5258c4e.0201242111.5a027b84@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com> <a2q3he$pmh$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`

```
> You might benefit even more if you keep it in work storage (avoiding
> simulated I/O overhead, especially open/close).  If ordinary working-storage
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Hi Gunnar and all,

	Thanks everyone for your help. 

	As Gunnar had suggested I would have loved to use the heap storage
facility CEEGTST. Let me explain the problem I am facing in doing so.
I read input file and the chunks I am writing to work-file do not have
fixed size. I check some condition after every read of the input file
and until that condition is satisfied I keep on writing that record to
workfile. Now I don't really know after how many records this
condition would be satisfied, that's how the workfile size (in terms
of number of records) is to vary.

	Gunnar, are you suggesting that the call to CEEGTST is to be used for
allocating every record so as to form a Linked list of records?? I am
a bit confused. Could you kindly elucidate?

	The way I had done it was like this: I didn't use the CEEGTST, rather
defined a WS table with ODO. As I said I check the condition and if
that's not satisfied I write that record to table incrementing the
count of number of records. The problem here   was for ODO you need to
specify the maximum possible occurances. Considering our requirement
here the max possible size was something like 99999. This is a huge
number and with record length 200 at compile time I got MXCC = 16.
	
	Is there any other way to do it? 


Thanks once again,
Cheers and have nice day (and a wonderful weekend too :-) ). 

Kedar
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T09:03:24+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2r3g7$eck$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com> <a2q3he$pmh$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <d5258c4e.0201242111.5a027b84@posting.google.com>`

```
> Gunnar, are you suggesting that the call to CEEGTST is to be used for
> allocating every record so as to form a Linked list of records?? I am
> a bit confused. Could you kindly elucidate?

Not necessarily a linked list of single records.  You may define an ODO
structure of a reasonable size, and allocate a number of them with CEEGTST
as necessary, chaining them together.  This will minimize overhead and
maximize flexibility.  You may keep the allocated structures for reuse for
the next group of records to further minimize CEEFRST/CEEGTST overhead.

The size of each structure does not have to be fixed, maybe a progressive
growth is good (the ODO definition should accomodate the maximum).  If the
job takes a considerable time, you may lessen the pressure on page space by
freeing an excessive allocation after use (maybe the need for large groups
of records are infrequent)

This technique requires a bit more programming, but manipulating chained
lists and pointer variables is cheep.  I have used this technique in online
programming (CICS) in a case where the need for space was unpredictable and
varied much, it was cheeper than Temporary Storage.

As an alternative, using VIO requires minimum programming, and will
automatically adapt to the needed space as the job progress.  Use large
blocksize on the VIO file for minimum overhead.

regards,
Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T12:15:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2s7cd$644$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com> <a2q3he$pmh$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <d5258c4e.0201242111.5a027b84@posting.google.com> <a2r3g7$eck$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`

```
Just so there is NO confusion, with IBM mainframe COBOL (and *probably* all
other COBOLs) the amount of storage that is allocated for an ODO is *always*
the maximum size (maximum occurrence).  Therefore, if you code

     Occurs 1 to 9999 times
           depending on NumField

and NumField *never* actually gets larger than 10, you will STILL get enough
storage allocated (in your WS) for 9999 occurrences.  (rules are a little
different in Linkage Section and File Section)

Besides going to CEEGTST, it is, however, also possible to do dynamic CALLs
to COBOL subprograms (and cancel them after use) and define/allocate space
in them.  The storage allocated in such programs would ONLY be "used"
between the first CALL and the CANCEL statement.  For example, you could
have

  Call  "Get-1000-Occurrences-1"

and have that subroutine allocate 1000-occurrence for your table - returning
the address of the table - which could be in the main program's linkage
section.  Then *if* you need more than 1000 occurrences, you could go ahead
and

  Call "Get-1000-Occurrences-2"

which would allocate another 1000 occurrences.

Once, you were "done" with this set of records, you would cancel all the
subprograms - and the storage would be freed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T18:53:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C51AB01.B5BE862F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com> <a2q3he$pmh$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <d5258c4e.0201242111.5a027b84@posting.google.com> <a2r3g7$eck$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <a2s7cd$644$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Just so there is NO confusion, with IBM mainframe COBOL (and *probably* all
> other COBOLs) the amount of storage that is allocated for an ODO is *always*
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> different in Linkage Section and File Section)

That's one of the things I find really appealing about OO
collections/dictionaries.
You *might* anticipate that your collection(table) will have 1,000
elements(entries) and you can initiate it for a 1,000. But if you only
*actually* have 10 elements(entries), that's all the area you take up.

This is parallel to a recommendation from Michael some years ago, (Compuserve
days), that a Relative file could be used as 'Temporary Working Storage".

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T01:07:03+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2ss2b$sv9$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com> <a2q3he$pmh$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <d5258c4e.0201242111.5a027b84@posting.google.com> <a2r3g7$eck$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <a2s7cd$644$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
> Just so there is NO confusion, with IBM mainframe COBOL (and *probably*
all
> other COBOLs) the amount of storage that is allocated for an ODO is
*always*
> the maximum size (maximum occurrence).  Therefore, if you code
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and NumField *never* actually gets larger than 10, you will STILL get
enough
> storage allocated (in your WS) for 9999 occurrences.  (rules are a little
> different in Linkage Section and File Section)

When you do the allocation with a call to CEEGTST Cobol knows nothing about
the size.  You then set the address of the ODO structure (defined in Linkage
Section of course) to the pointer returned.  Then set the ODO reference
according to the allocated amount.  Voila! An ODO structure with the
requested size, independent of the maximum defined.

> Besides going to CEEGTST, it is, however, also possible to do dynamic
CALLs
> to COBOL subprograms (and cancel them after use) and define/allocate space
> in them.  The storage allocated in such programs would ONLY be "used"
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and have that subroutine allocate 1000-occurrence for your table -
returning
> the address of the table - which could be in the main program's linkage
> section.  Then *if* you need more than 1000 occurrences, you could go
ahead
> and
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> subprograms - and the storage would be freed.

Gosh.  Wouldn't it be better to have an ALLOCATE verb in Cobol?  Now that
pointers and pointer-based storage (linkage section) is part of the
language, you might as well have proper functions to make use of it also.

Gunnar.
```

```
(mainframe) COBOL - before/whether or not IBM implements all of the draft
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: VIO or buffer

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T22:46:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0201252246.3899ad01@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d5258c4e.0201240748.68c05ece@posting.google.com> <a2q3he$pmh$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <d5258c4e.0201242111.5a027b84@posting.google.com> <a2r3g7$eck$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <a2s7cd$644$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <a2ss2b$sv9$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <a2st15$r0n$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <a2sv1m$30j$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no> <a2t462$i42$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hi Kedar,

I ran into a similar problem a while back. The pgm was designed to use
a work file to hold a customer phone bill then read it back and create
a paginated version of the bill. There were about 20k invoices
totaling 3m lines. The job ran in about 3+ hrs.

It was causing testing turnaround problemms for me, so I asked the PM
if I could redesign to eliminate the open/closes - 80k of them (20k
invoices x 4). The job ran in 10 minutes, a 95% reduction in run time!

It sounds like you have about half the open/close burden I had, so you
may be looking at a potential 50% reduction in run time (all other
things being equal).
Your problem is the qty of data you have to save (do you actually use
50+ tracks worth?). But it may be worth the effort.

From the COBOL Ref Appendix:

table size limit        16 meg
link sect size limit   134 meg
external size limit    134 meg

HTH, Jack.

…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Jack, 3m/20k is 150 i.e 150 number of open and closes.. correct me if
I am wrong. Well if that's the case I have much more number ( like I
mentioned earlier 10000 open-closes)
> 
> It was causing testing turnaround problemms for me, so I asked the PM
> if I could redesign to eliminate the open/closes - 80k of them (20k
> invoices x 4). The job ran in 10 minutes, a 95% reduction in run time!

Ooh gosh, that sounds magnificent. So you processed 4 invoices at a
time rather than 1 reducing the number of Open-close to 1/4th right?
Well unfortunately I cannot club them together to reduce the opens and
closes.


> It sounds like you have about half the open/close burden I had, so you
> may be looking at a potential 50% reduction in run time (all other
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I was successful in using buffer and the job stats were like this
With DASD file: 179 min
With VIO file: 122 min
With Buffer: 29 min
(these are results of test data, 5000 times OPEN/CLOSE)
Jack, Gunnar and others.. 
Thanks very  much. Jack, thanks for the hint of eliminating OPEN and
CLOSE statements. So even though VIO file is formed in memory system
takes time in "simulating" the file operations, thus  this does not
prove more efficient  than using the buffer directly.
We had to customize the C++ tool to handle buffer rather than file. 
Once again thanks everyone.
Best Regards,
Kedar
```

```
Hi:

Occasionally, when sending evaluation versions of my POS software to
prospects, it will crash when they try to run it.

I ask them to run MSCONFIG and to uncheck everything on the Startup
tab and then reboot.

This has never failed so far to fix the crash. There is obviously a
conflict with something else which causes the crash.

Anyway, it is worth a try.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

## Thread: programming - conversions (HIPAA) — 12 msgs

### programming - conversions (HIPAA)

- **From:** sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN)
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T00:28:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020124192835.20360.00000200@mb-bh.aol.com>`

```
Entire medical billing industry working on HIPAA conversion.HIPAA is the
acronym for the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996. In
plain language: entire insurance industry has to convert there present computer
systems to be HIPAA compliant. It is going to be another Y2K, but the real one.

Mayor players in this game are EDI and Sterling Commerce. Last one, has a new
development tool - GENTRAN. Does anybody has any idea about this product, or
maybe using it now?
```

#### ↳ Re: programming - conversions (HIPAA)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T12:13:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xZb48.18$i4.37710@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<20020124192835.20360.00000200@mb-bh.aol.com>`

```
SBRICKLIN <sbricklin@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020124192835.20360.00000200@mb-bh.aol.com...
> Entire medical billing industry working on HIPAA conversion.HIPAA is the
> acronym for the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of
1996. In
> plain language: entire insurance industry has to convert there present
computer
> systems to be HIPAA compliant. It is going to be another Y2K, but the real
one.
>
> Mayor players in this game are EDI and Sterling Commerce. Last one, has a
new
> development tool - GENTRAN. Does anybody has any idea about this product,
or
> maybe using it now?

Sterling's Gentran brand EDI translator/mapper has been around for about
fifteen years. It's available for MVS, AS/400, Unix, and PC. (The products
are NOT the same across platforms.) I've worked with Gentran/MVS and
Gentran/400 (which *are* quite similar) and found them excellent products
with good support.

Gentran is a general-purpose EDI translator/mapper and therefore is a lot of
horsepower (and expense) if you are only going to do claims (the 837) and
remittances (the 835). However, if you want to be ramped up for the whole
range of documents used in the health care industry and defined in the
implementing regulations for the 1996 HIPAA, it's not a bad choice.

Let me make a technical correction to something:
> entire insurance industry has to convert there [sic] present computer
systems to be HIPAA compliant.

It's not just insurers (payers), it's providers, DME vendors and
laboratories, too. And, a computer system is not HIPAA-compliant: only
'covered entities' are HIPAA-compliant. For details see CFR 45 parts 160 and
162. (Or engage a consultant to get you started). (Someone like, say, well,
me).

My latest project is related to the HIPAA-mandated ANSI EDI electronic
remittance advice . You can view info about the product at
www.providerpaymentpartner.com
```

```
Ms. P, on which platform was that? I worked with Gentran/AS400 and the Y2K
stuff was fine, except you had to upgrade to the "Server 1.x" version (or
was it 2.x?) to get the ANSI version 4 (e.g., 004010) support in the
editors. ( IIRC, the Y2K date support was already available in the
application defintions, where you define the format of date-time data).

MCM
```

```
> I am getting this error when I run a microfocus cobol

Which compiler ? I get the following from Net Express on-line help on
checksum :-

Runtime Errors :-
----------------------
109 Invalid checksum in run-time system Recoverable

The internal information in the run-time system has been altered. The
run-time system might have become corrupted, or you might have illegally
tried to change the internal run-time system information.
---------------------------------------
209 Network communication error Recoverable

This is normally given if an incorrect checksum has been received in a
communications packet.

Resolution:

Your program should continue to execute after you have received this
error but results might be undefined.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: shm index header checksum

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

```
J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C582FC1.43863AE4@shaw.ca...
> ---------------------------------------
> 209 Network communication error Recoverable
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> error but results might be undefined.

<rant>
Now talk about poor wording in a helpfile message.....How is it results
"might" be undefined? Either the results are defined, or they are not -
which makes them undefined. There is, and can be, no "might" about it. (A
"binary solution set").

Another example, courtesy of  *any* software vendor's sales department:

When a user or prospect asks, "Does the software do <thing>?" how come when
the answer is "no,"  the reply is always presented as, "not in the current
release"  regardless of plans to add such a feature in the future, or the
ability to even mention if such a feature is under consideration or
development?

 If the software does <thing>, the answer is "yes." If the software does not
do <thing>, the answer is "no."  (Another  "binary solution set").


</rant>

MCM
```

```
Michael Mattias wrote:

> J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3C582FC1.43863AE4@shaw.ca...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

HELPPPpppp ! Michael I was only trying to help. Those messages mean bugger all
to me - but perhaps they might just put him on the right track (And if it was
the first one, don't forget it said, "You failed, because you are trying to do
an unauthorised screw around with our software !").

In terms of 'help' sometimes it just can't be 'had', even if you contact the
vendor. Case in point, I had my application working with executables. I changed
something and reproduced the redistributable executables. It went belly up first
time I tried to to access a COBOL file - merely giving me my standard file
status messages. Asked in M/F's Answerlab - no joy. I even wrote to Mr. Coffey
(?) then at Newbury - suggestions but no solution.

Know what it was ? M/F allows me to do file compression. Sure I 'collected'  all
the support files second time around, to create the executables, but with the
EXCEPTION of their file compression dll. It was the absence of this particular
dll that caused the problem.

Screw it ! I don't use file compression anymore. (I think I also accidentally
tripped over a reference in their on-line help that compression also screws up
SORTS). Besides, I'm switching to ESQL.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: shm index header checksum

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T12:57:43+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c5889e3_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<sYy48.53$mR3.134299@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <20020127002049.26837.00000544@mb-mh.aol.com> <3C581E9B.9B56BD45@wright.edu>`

```
Terry Young <terry.young@wright.edu> wrote in message
news:3C581E9B.9B56BD45@wright.edu...
> I am getting this error when I run a microfocus cobol
> program
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I have a clue.

It means that the file you are accessing has become corrupted. Maybe there
was a power failure or the machine was switched off while it was being
accessed, or you haven't re-organised it for a 1000 years. (Or any other
reason why...<G>)

Your file consists of two parts:  A series of index entries and a series of
data entries. These parts are sometimes physically separate (.DAT and .IDX)
and sometimes combined into one physical file. (It depends on the particular
vendor). In EITHER case Indexed files require re-organisation every so
often. This is so that unused space can be reclaimed, the indexes can be
reorganised for better efficiency, and the user can feel a bit more secure
about his data ...<G>

You must run the MicroFocus REBUILD utility, (supplied with your MF COBOL
system), against the file giving the error.

Read (carefully) the chapter on using it in the MF Manuals. If you still
have a problem, send me the file (both components of it, if it has two...I
can't remember the format for old MF) and I have an old machine here that
has MF COBOL on it and will rebuild it for you then send it back.

> It compiles ok in microfocus.
>
You will have realised, after reading the above, that this is because the
problem is not with the PROGRAM, it is with the DATA.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com
```

<a id="t-a52a41fcbe"></a>
## Thread: .NET Programming or Teaching an Old Dinosaur New Tricks — 8 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### .NET Programming or Teaching an Old Dinosaur New Tricks

- **From:** "Lee Unterreiner" <flu@nospam.flu-ent.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T17:31:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2qcih$2r5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those interested in this exciting new environment:

.NET introduces a number of new terms and concepts.  Even with a solid
knowledge of OO programming, you can get lost VERY quickly if you lack the
proper foundation.  I recently completed "Bertrand Meyer's .NET Training
Course".  It's a video training course on 3 CD's along with a workbook.  You
can order it at Amazon for about $50.  I STRONGLY recommend it to all.
```

#### ↳ Re: .NET Programming or Teaching an Old Dinosaur New Tricks

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T03:09:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C50CD5B.520649A9@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a2qcih$2r5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


Lee Unterreiner wrote:

> For those interested in this exciting new environment:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> can order it at Amazon for about $50.  I STRONGLY recommend it to all.

"Even with a solid knowledge of OO programming, you can get lost VERY quickly
..........", and you aren't exactly a beginner, are you ?

So Lee, having put ".Net" aside, rather like many of us did with Windows,
(sticking with DOS until we 'had to'), what exactly is involved. I'm vague, so
some questions :-

    - is this .Net process like learning Windowing from scratch
    - you are of course using the Fujitsu new 'scripting' feature, (and I
believe,
        Net Express does provide something similar to get into .Net)
    -  what are the major things that really hit you as "pluses" when you use
.Net
    - Can you quickly summarize the advantages of .Net
    - How long can one ignore .Net,  (before you 'have to')
    - There's a price tag, (Microsoft)  for the .Net feature ?????? - and which
        Windows OS does it work with

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: .NET Programming or Teaching an Old Dinosaur New Tricks

- **From:** "Lee Unterreiner" <flu@nospam.flu-ent.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T00:14:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2rce6$7c7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a2qcih$2r5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3C50CD5B.520649A9@shaw.ca>`

```
I like to compare .NET to the Java Virtual Machine (JVM).  JVM is a
multi-platform/single language (Java) programming environment.  .NET is a
single platform/multi-language environment.  The single platform is Windows
XP/2000/CE for now.  .NET applications are always compiled to native code -
with JVM its optional.

>     - is this .Net process like learning Windowing from scratch

In many ways, yes.  It's really an OO layer on top of the OS.  No more
API's.  Everything is a method, property, or class.

>     - you are of course using the Fujitsu new 'scripting' feature, (and I
> believe,
>         Net Express does provide something similar to get into .Net)

No, I was not referring to Fujitsu's .NET offering in particular but to the
platform in general.  Many language vendors have 'ported' their compilers to
this new platform.  In addition, Microsoft allows for optional integration
into their Visual Studio (VS).  The only announced COBOL port (so far) is
from Fujitsu - they are also doing VS integration..  Interoperabilty with
.NET is easy and builtin, but you get virtually none of the benefits of .NET
unless the compiler vendor does a specific port to .NET.

Disclaimer:  For those who don't know me, I am an independent consultant
that has at one time or another has done work for most of the COBOL vendors.
Currently, Fujitsu is my biggest customer.  Those who do know me know I
shill for no one.  Much to the consternation of my past and previous
clients, I don't hestitate to recommend a competitor's product if I feel it
would be more appropriate.

>     -  what are the major things that really hit you as "pluses" when you
use
> .Net

1. Traditional, client server, and web server programming  - all in a
single, 'seamless', integrated, type safe environment, with a common set of
tools.
2. True multi-language support and binary transparency.  You can't tell what
language any given component is in, nor do you care.  Its really cool to run
a debug session that switches seamlessly between COBOL, C#, VB, Eiffel, etc.
3. Security to protect you from the outside world and vice versa.
4. Seamless components. Gone are COM and ActiveX.  .NET classes ARE
components.  Components are 'smart' objects. The runtime can do dynamic
binding - the debugger can interrogate them - the source code editor can
prevent coding mistakes - the IDE lets you 'drop' them on to forms.
5. Seamless communications. SOAP and XML are the protocols used.
6. The end of DLL hell.  The ONLY installer one needs is FTP or XCOPY.

I can't do this topic justice - thats why I recommend Meyer's course.

>     - Can you quickly summarize the advantages of .Net
>     - How long can one ignore .Net,  (before you 'have to')
>     - There's a price tag, (Microsoft)  for the .Net feature ?????? - and
which
>         Windows OS does it work with


The end user platform is free.  Its builtin to XP and beyond.  Its available
for 2000 and CE.  Yes, your favorite COBOL application running on a
hand-held or even a phone!

The development environment does not run on 9x.

The cheapest Visual Studio products (VB, C#, C++) from Microsoft are about
$100 US.    I have know idea of what any other language vendors are asking -
visit their websites.

As long as where you are is OK you can ignore .NET.  This is not a
replacement for Windows.  It's a programming model that sits on top of
Windows.  Microsoft no longer contain native code generators.  That function
is now performed by the Framework (on the end user's machine) .

Hope this gets you interested.

Lee
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .NET Programming or Teaching an Old Dinosaur New Tricks

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T18:38:37+00:00

```
> I like to compare .NET to the Java Virtual Machine (JVM).  JVM is a

> single platform/multi-language environment.  The single platform is Windows

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .NET Programming or Teaching an Old Dinosaur New Tricks

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T08:33:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C5269F5.BE5FB58B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a2qcih$2r5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3C50CD5B.520649A9@shaw.ca> <a2rce6$7c7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Lee Unterreiner wrote:
> 
> I like to compare .NET to the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). JVM is a
> multi-platform/single language (Java) programming environment.  

Not entirely true, for example JPython is Python that runs on the JVM.

> .NET is a single platform/multi-language environment.  

.NET is quite a bit more than just the run-time system.  It is primarily
a set of internet services which MS hope to charge for on a subscription
basis to supplement their current revenue model.

> The single platform is Windows XP/2000/CE for now.  .NET applications 
> are always compiled to native code - with JVM its optional.

Which may or may be advantageous.  It means that every handheld or
embedded device must have the compiler as well as the run-time, and must
cater for what all the languages may do for that device.  This is why
20Gb + 512Mb + 1MHz are no faster in doing actual work than a 486 in
1995.

As a comparison a subset Java, WABI, for the Palm runs with a 50Kb
interpreter.

> 3. Security to protect you from the outside world and vice versa.

That has yet to be demonstrated.  

> 4. Seamless components. Gone are COM and ActiveX.  

Are they 'gone' or just disguised by yet another layer of interfacing ? 
This has serious security (ActiveX are not secure) and performance
issues.

> 6. The end of DLL hell.  The ONLY installer one needs is FTP or XCOPY.

Only because they have a completely new set of .DLLs that replicate the
functions of the existing ones and both sets must be on the machine
leading to a bloat of many tens or hundreds of megabytes on disk and in
memory.

> The end user platform is free.  Its builtin to XP and beyond.  Its available
> for 2000 and CE.  Yes, your favorite COBOL application running on a
> hand-held or even a phone!

The services that will link the end-users and servers, will however, not
be free.
 
> The development environment does not run on 9x.

I doubt that there is any technical reason for this, it is just that MS
wants everyone to buy XP (see 'Revenue model').
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .NET Programming or Teaching an Old Dinosaur New Tricks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Gary Scott <scottg@flash.net>

```

problems.

Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Lee Unterreiner wrote:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> wants everyone to buy XP (see 'Revenue model').
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: .NET Programming or Teaching an Old Dinosaur New Tricks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Lee Unterreiner" <flu@nospam.flu-ent.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T16:48:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2suel$hvu$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a2qcih$2r5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3C50CD5B.520649A9@shaw.ca> <a2rce6$7c7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3C5269F5.BE5FB58B@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston  wrote:
> >
> > I like to compare .NET to the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). JVM is a
> > multi-platform/single language (Java) programming environment.
>
> Not entirely true, for example JPython is Python that runs on the JVM.

Agreed.  There are even COBOL to Java compilers

>
> > .NET is a single platform/multi-language environment.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> basis to supplement their current revenue model.

I don't know which services you are refering to.  Are they content
providers?  The services I am talking about areWeb Services (ASP+
essentially), Data Services (ADO+), WebForms, and WinForms.  These services
are integral to what MS calls the .NET Framework.  No connection to MS is
required.


>
> > The single platform is Windows XP/2000/CE for now.  .NET applications
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

All .NET languages compile what MS calls CLS or common language
specification.  The Framework for each platform come with one or more JITs
(just in time compilers).  The JITTER (I'm not making these terms up) for a
handheld device will have different properties than one used on a web
server.

I can't speak to your other points.  I don't even own a laptop. I like my
computers big (raised floor is optional).

> As a comparison a subset Java, WABI, for the Palm runs with a 50Kb
> interpreter.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That has yet to be demonstrated.

Granted.

>
> > 4. Seamless components. Gone are COM and ActiveX.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes they are truly gone - although there is interoperability.  They are gone
because ALL non-abstract classes ARE components.  This is transparent to the
class writer.  The compiler generates CLS code plus Metadata (an XML based
IDL).  After it is 'compiled' to Metadata and CLS it is impossible to tell
what the original language was without a debugger.

It is the idea of 'instant components' that has me most excited.  Components
are 'smart' classes.  Within Visual Studio they can be used immediately to
enhance;the Forms designers and tool boxes; the editor prompts for the
correct usage when referencing the class, the debugger and the runtime can
dynamically resolve references without requiring registration

> > 6. The end of DLL hell.  The ONLY installer one needs is FTP or XCOPY.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> memory.

I have no idea what the requirements are.  Also a second disclaimer: I am
not a fan of Microsoft in the same way I was not a fan of IBM in an earlier
era. But they are ubitiquous and I am a pragmatist.  Since all new computers
with an MS OS now come with it, I assume the hard disks can handle it.

>
> > The end user platform is free.  Its builtin to XP and beyond.  Its
available
> > for 2000 and CE.  Yes, your favorite COBOL application running on a
> > hand-held or even a phone!
>
> The services that will link the end-users and servers, will however, not
> be free.

Connectivity to content providers or to ISP may be chargeable.  But that
would be application specific.  Nothing in .NET Framework has anything
(inherently) to do with for-fee services.  You do not have to go through MS
or any of its cronies to use any of the features I've described.

>
> > The development environment does not run on 9x.
>
> I doubt that there is any technical reason for this, it is just that MS
> wants everyone to buy XP (see 'Revenue model').

You are probably right about the XP revenue model - Office will soon follow
suit.  But .NET is simply a programming model meant to make life easier for
those coding for MS platforms - and one that's open and available to COBOL
programmers.
```

<a id="t-3c0e94f3f7"></a>
## Thread: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-24T18:46:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C50B8EA.18802559@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<OFCC9EEC61.02A0B5C9-ON85256B4B.00520286@dapsco.com>`

```
This is a generic reply to all the people who've tried to help me.  I've
tried all of the following SELECT statements for SELECT INPUT-FILE-1:
         ASSIGN TO SYS005-UR-2540R-S-TFIL.
         ASSIGN TO SYS005-S-TFIL.
         ASSIGN TO SYS005-TFIL.
         ASSIGN TO SYS005.

Again, recall that I have // ASSGN SYS005,SYSIPT in my JCL.  My goal is
to read the RDR cards after the JCL statement.

The following statements are true:

The first assignment works when the program is compiled with DOS/VS
COBOL and run with the COBOL II runtime library.

The first assignment works when the program is compiled with DOS/VS
COBOL and run with the LE/VSE runtime library.

All assignments work when the program is compiled with VS COBOL II and
run with the COBOL II runtime library.

*None* of the assigments work when the program is compiled with VS COBOL
II and run with the LE/VSE runtime library.

I haven't tried one person's suggestion of "SYS005-S-SYS005", but I have
no reason to believe this will work either.

I *can* do "ACCEPT DATA-REC" instead of treating it as a file, and it
works with no problem.  The problem with that is that we have dozens of
programs out there using it as a sequential file.  No way we want to
make changes to all those programs.

I do have a couple other observations:

With the COBOL II run time if I use the following SELECT assignment:
ASSIGN TO SYS005
and the following JCL
// ASSGN SYS005,SYSIPT
// DLBL SYS005,'FJS.TEST',,VSAM

It will read from file FJS.TEST.

If I remove the DLBL it will read the cards from the RDR.  This is why I
don't think SYS005-S-SYS005 will make any difference.

It's also interesting to note that if I replace the DLBL above with
// DLBL SYS005

I will get the same error (90) with both runtime libraries, and it's the
same error I'm getting with no DLBL and the LE/VSE runtime.  It seems to
me that the LE runtime library is interpretting them in the same
manner...a DLBL to a non-existant file (or something like that).

Anyway...one of my systems programmers is going to check IBMLINK.  From
there, who knows...

Thanks all.
```

#### ↳ Re: Use of SYSIPT in COBOL II under LE/VSE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T12:07:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.vse-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2s6tv$k0f$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<OFCC9EEC61.02A0B5C9-ON85256B4B.00520286@dapsco.com> <3C50B8EA.18802559@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank,
 From one of my "usually reliable sources" at IBM,

"Bill,
That sure sounds like a bug! Unfortunately, it might be a bug in the VS
COBOL II compiler, which went out of service for VSE in 1997. He should
still call IBM Service."

When/If you *do* call IBM service, make certain that you explain that the
exact same program works under the VS COBOL II run-time, but not the LE
run-time.  This is *usually* an "aparable" situation.

 ***

The other thing that I noted while looking for an answer was the table at:


 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/FL2MGE00/3.3

which talks about SOME of the times that you *must* relink to run existing
VS COBOL II (or DOS/VS COBOL) programs under LE.  I don't think your
previous notes have talked about whether or not you did "re-link" before
trying to run under LE/VSE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric values

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T16:40:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2s1jg$3gc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a2rrk5$78$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <u530grriig6v48@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On 25-Jan-2002, "Kevin" <kmiller2001xx@charter.net> wrote:

> I have not tested it yet. I am reading a book, "Programming in Cobol/400"
> by
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> one used PIC S9(3). The book was just not real clear.

The way to make it clear is by testing.  Look for yourself and you will
learn better.

But your hypothesis seems odd - 
PIC S9(3) is a picture of a signed numeric.  If you move -12 to such a
location, wouldn't it store the sign?  And if it stores the sign, don't you
think it would know the difference between -12 and +12?

01   TEST-FIELDS.
       05     TEST-1        PIC 9(3).
       05     TEST-2        PIC S9(3)V99.
       05     TEST-3        PIC 9(3)V99.
       05     TEST-4        PIC S9(3).
01   DISPLAY-FIELD  PIC ----9.99.

...
TEST-PARAGRAPH.
       MOVE 12   TO TEST-1, TEST-2, TEST-3, TEST-4.
       PERFORM DISPLAY-VALUES.
       MOVE 012  TO TEST-1, TEST-2, TEST-3, TEST-4.
       PERFORM DISPLAY-VALUES.
      MOVE 12.00  TO TEST-1, TEST-2, TEST-3, TEST-4.
      PERFORM DISPLAY-VALUES.
      MOVE +12   TO TEST-1, TEST-2, TEST-3, TEST-4.
      PERFORM DISPLAY-VALUES.
      MOVE -12   TO TEST-1, TEST-2, TEST-3, TEST-4.
      PERFORM DISPLAY-VALUES.
DISPLAY-VALUES.
     DISPLAY ' '.
     MOVE TEST-1 TO DISPLAY-FIELD.
     DISPLAY 'TEST-1="'  DISPLAY-FIELD '"'.
      MOVE TEST-2  TO DISPLAY-FIELD'.
     DISPLAY 'TEST-2='" DISPLAY-FIELD '"'.
     MOVE TEST-3  TO DISPLAY-FIELD .
     DISPLAY 'TEST-3="' DISPLAY-FIELD '"'.
     MOVE  TEST-4 TO DISPLAY-FIELD.
     DISPLAY 'TEST-4="' DISPLAY-FIELD '"'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric values

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T07:44:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C525E74.9038FC6F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a2rrk5$78$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <u530grriig6v48@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Kevin wrote:
> 
> I have not tested it yet. I am reading a book, "Programming in Cobol/400" by
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> used PIC S9(3). The book was just not real clear.

Why would you think that +12 and -12 could have the same numeric value ?

        IF ( 12 = -12 )
            DISPLAY "I don't think so"
	END-IF

I think that you asked completely the wrong question.  What you _may_
have wanted to ask was:

    "What is the numeric value of:
          01    My-Number      PIC 9(3).
          MOVE -12      TO My-Number.

To be a succesful programmer you must be able to formulate the questions
in a way that gets the answer that you want, and vv, be able to
understand a question is phrased to obtain a specific answer.

No.  -12 does not have the same numeric value as +12.  After these have
been moved into numeric fields they may not have the same value due to
truncation or other effects.

But in other languages it may be that 12 does not = 12.0, and 012 does
not = 12:

         if ( 12 != 12.0 )
            puts("12 not 12.0, I told you so");

         if ( 012 != 12 )
            puts("012 not = 12, are you surprised ?");

         if ( 012 == 10 )
            puts("How can 012 equal 10 ?");

which is why the point was being made.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric values

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T13:19:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FB9F749D3C7FCA91.6DB124A9E068730E.0487B800F4A873AF@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a2rrk5$78$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <u530grriig6v48@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Fri, 25 Jan 2002 10:59:15 -0500, "Kevin"
<kmiller2001xx@charter.net> enlightened us:

>I have not tested it yet. I am reading a book, "Programming in Cobol/400" by
>James Cooper. In a section in the book, it talks about compairing numeric
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Kevin

If you had stated the above in your first post, you may have gotten
the responses you were looking for.  It looked like a homework
question to me as well and I thought Doc's standard answer was
appropriate.  

Most here in CLC will help with homework if the poster shows what
they've done to date and what they are having a problem with.  It
serves them no benefit to just have answers given to them without
their having first tried to learn the subject.  You may want to read
the CLC FAQ which goes into more detail about homework.

Now, as to your problem.  Remember that computers and computer
languages must operate on the basic principles of mathematics.
Therefore, 5th grade math tells us that 012, 12.00, 12 and +12 are all
equivalent.  You may have had to wait until algebra in 8th grade to
know that -12 is not equivalent to the others.  The computer must
operate on the same principle.  However, the computer language or how
the data is described within it, can present the number to the
computer differently, thus possibly causing them to not be equivalent.

For example, and sticking with Cobol examples here, if you have a data
field described as Pic S9(3) and you placed within that field 012, or
12.00, or 12 or +12, they would all be held in storage as +012.  In
the case of 12.00, the decimal portion would be ignored.  In all
cases, the field is right justified and zero filled.  The field could
accurately hold -12 as -012 and, according to the rules of
mathematics, would not be equivalent to the other data.

Now, if your field was defined as Pic 9(3), then all the
aforementioned values would be equivalent because the sign is dropped.

If the field is defined as Pic S9(3)V99, then the equivalency in this
case is the same as if there were no decimals.  Pic 9(3)V99 in this
case gives the same results as Pic 9(3).

Finally, if your field was defined as Pic X(3), then none would be
equivalent.

>"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
>news:a2rrk5$78$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ HOMEWORK (yet again) (was: Numeric values

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T12:23:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2s7s0$hps$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <L%f48.5985$RD5.14195@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <a2s3r5$881$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
From the COBOL FAQ - here is what it (unlike DD) says about Homework
questions,

"18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?
Yes, you can get help with your homework via these newsgroups. HOWEVER, that
does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT find many participants
who will be very happy if you ask them to do your homework FOR YOU. Some
hints that you should consider when drafting your post for assistance with
homework include:

�         Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking for homework
help. (Most of the newsgroup participants are very good at sniffing out
those who try and pose homework questions as "business need" questions - and
they are not very polite in replying to such questions).

�         Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating system
your homework is targeted at. Solutions may vary significantly based on
this. (You might also want to include what text book you are using.)

�         The more information that you can give that demonstrates that you
really have tried to solve the problem yourself (using your text book, class
material/presentations, lab assistance, etc), the more likely it is that you
will get useful and friendly responses. If you let us know what you have
found on your own and why you are still uncertain or confused, you will
usually get helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very pointed
replies.

NOTE: if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't want to see the "Do
your own homework" notes that occur in the COBOL newsgroups), you might want
to try,

http://www.tutoraid.org/

"



The full FAQ can be read at,

    http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

It should also be noted, that comp.lang.cobol is a NON-moderated newsgroup.
Therefore, DD is "free" to post his usual response to any/all "homework
appearing" questions, and others are free to post "nasty" comments about his
usual response.   I don't personally find either of these particularly
"useful" - but they do continue to occur.

I *do* strongly support DD's view that *students* should "show us their
work" and *try* to find the answers via normal "course resources" *before*
asking us questions.  OTOH, there is NOTHING to force them to do so.
```

```
A thought. We need to get at the students so that they *know* - but
unfortunately we have no idea where they first start looking, (certainly not
your COBOL FAQ <G>). Chances are, many instructors DO read cobolreport.com. Howz
about drafting a short entry for Ed that will catch instructors' eyes - so that
they can tell their students to read the COBOL FAQ ?

have  any comment on above suggestion ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HOMEWORK (yet again) (was: Numeric values

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T04:12:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2tabh$i46$1@ins22.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <L%f48.5985$RD5.14195@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <a2s3r5$881$1@panix1.panix.com> <a2s7s0$hps$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3C51ACED.D6F7F189@shaw.ca>`

```
Adding an instructor "oriented" entry to the faq is a good idea.  A link 
to the faq and to comp.lang.cobol in google (or some other publically 
available source) might be helpful. 

Each semester that I teach COBOL I let students know about 
comp.lang.cobol - though I don't think I have seen any evidence that they 
have used it directly.  I also include a link the group (i.e. via google) 
on the course INTRANET page.  I occasionally use a question as a case 
study when it relates to something we cover in our "file structures" 
course.  

A number of years ago (8 ?), I did have a pascal student make use of the 
pascal groups - but it is the only time that I have know it to occur 
legitmately. That does not include the student who copied a paper off the 
net - it was so well written, that I had to check.  I found four copies of 
it at different locations around the web.  He got a ZERO on the assignment 
and was kicked off the schools basketball team for a semester! [:)]

I know that I have spoken about Thane's help in the past; but over the 
four years that I have been teaching COBOL, the group has done a lot to 
make my course a better course.  Thanks.

Floyd Johnson

===================================================================


This could probably be a modification the faq's 
comments aobut homework.
```

```
a**holes

instructive way

onus on the question poser to do some work and not nearly as "nasty" as the
DD 'usual' response
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HOMEWORK (yet again) (was: Numeric values

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T09:00:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0201260900.64c95e4b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a2s3r5$881$1@panix1.panix.com> <a2s7s0$hps$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <M4i48.529$Om2.666@rwcrnsc53> <a2sebc$bm9$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
I get a lot of questions.  Over time some specific individuals ask a
LOT of questions.  If I have to "work" a bit to answer the question -
then I consider it very legitimate.  If, however, it's very simple - I
usually point them to the documentation.

Now - what I get even more of is Analytical questions.  It bothers me
that some people just can't work through a problem to find a solution
- one minor stumbling block and they give up.  I'm not above asking a
question MYSELF when I can't figure something out.  However, you can
rest assured I've exhausted my experience and resources in finding the
answer.  That's not to say it doesn't have a simple solution - that's
obvious to someone else!

At any rate, what I've found is that it's a personality thing.  Some
people want the easy answer and some are willing to dig to learn. 
"Encouraging" individuals to do their own home work and not grab the
easy answer is doing them a service that they will (or should) thank
you for later.


docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<a2sebc$bm9$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> In article <M4i48.529$Om2.666@rwcrnsc53>,
> Rational One <noreligion@nogod.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

```
>

I accept what you say, but a slightly different take on the topic. I feel
particularly sympathetic towards students confronted with learning programming,
particularly if they are thrown into those 'other' OO languages; COBOL lends
itself more immediately to business problems.

One immediate problem, that Floyd has already pointed out, is the limited
timespan at a learning centre to get a programming course across. I
particularly latched on to your word 'Analytical'. Just like systems analysis
can't be taught, (you can only guide), the same applies to being analytical -
you either are or aren't - and if the latter, perhaps you shouldn't be in EDP.
(This ties in with your comment about being a 'personality thing').. Some here
love to get into the bits and bytes. For myself, having read the theory
(Language Reference Manuals), how do I apply that in the real world.

Having 'attempted' to learn machine code and Assembler back in '63, I breathed
a sigh of relief that I was to be an analyst rather than a programmer; and that
'program learning' merely gave a sense of how the problem was to be handled
when we arrived at our systems design. (It was at a BEA Training Centre - the
best part was looking at the young trainee stewardesses - no 'grannies' around
in those days <G>).

Note that when I did get into COBOL programming, (preceded by Datapoint's
DATABUS and Wang WP macros), I didn't find it any big deal, because of the
systems analysis background. Took three shots at reading the LRMs (1) Skim, (2)
A second Skim and (3) Pause and read in detail on specific topics that I *felt*
I would use. Note that the three 'reads' also gave me my own 'memory index' -
if something came up, the memory box triggered that I had read about it
somewhere.

Consider something like merchandise ordering for a retail group. Aeons ago,
there just weren't too many models to work from, so you were faced with a blank
whiteboard on which you made many scribblings with felt pens, and did a lot of
wiping out and re-drawing, until perhaps six months later, you can up with a
viable model.

Students just don't get near this approach at all - never seeing or visualizing
the 'grand scheme of things'. And this I think, is their major problem. Give
them a task, read in a file, put into a table, accumulate and write out a new
file or report - they may or may not comprehend, but they are left wondering
where this particular task might fit as a segment into a real world
application. Plus of course, "Why doesn't 12 equal -012. Hell ! They've both
got a '1' and a '2' in them !".

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT meanspirited insulting fellow travellers was: Re: Numeric values

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T13:57:12+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <L%f48.5985$RD5.14195@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <3c525066_2@Usenet.com> <07f58.8487$y57.29966@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net>`

```

Rational One <noreligion@nogod.com> wrote in message
news:07f58.8487$y57.29966@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net...
> If you have seen no "help" from me then I have two things to tell you
>
> you are as nasty as Docdwarf

Oh! NOOOOOooooooooooo!!!!

That was definitely meant to wound...<G>
(Actually I could never aspire to reach the dizzy heights of ascerbity that
the Doc attains...But I'll keep trying, if you push me...<G>)

> why do you need to enquote "help" - are you being meanspirited and
> attempting to insult me ?
>
"Help" was enquoted because sometimes "help" isn't any help at all... If you
give the wrong kind of help at the wrong moment, you can actually do more
damage than good. You can even rob someone of the exquisite pleasure of
solving a problem for themselves, and the satisfaction and growth that
follows from this. If you give "help" just to show how much you know about
the subject or to impress your friends, you might as well not come here at
all.

As for attempting to insult you, two things:

1. Why would I do that? There is no gain for me in insulting someone I have
never met. (I try not to do it with people I HAVE met...don't always succeed
though.) Sometimes you can imagine you are being attacked when, in fact, you
are not.

2. Should I decide to insult you, (for whatever unimaginable reason...), it
will not be an "attempt", you will KNOW you were insulted...<G>

As for being meanspirited, well, that is a matter of  public record...<G>

Here's a story for you...

Are you sitting comfortably? Good. Then I'll begin....

A traveller approaches the Gates of a great city.

He sees an old man, dressed in rags, sitting in the dust, warming his
ancient bones in the hot sun.

The traveller addresses the old man...

"What are the people in this City like?"

"What were the people in the City you came from like?"

The traveller shudders at the memory.

"They were awful. Mean, money-grubbing, unkind, impolite, unfriendly,
unhelpful, a real bunch of nasty no-hopers. That's why I left..."

The old man nods gently and says:

"I'm afraid they're just the same here..."

The traveller decides to move on and try his luck at the next city.

The old man dozes...

Presently, the sound of a tired horse approaching makes him raise his head.
Another traveller stands before him and asks the same question:

"What are the people in this City like?"

The old man responds...

"What were the people in the City you came from like?"

"Ah, they were marvellous! Good, decent, generous folk. Always a smile and a
helping hand where needed, truly the salt of the Earth. In fact I'm
wondering if the Wander Lust that brought me here was such a good thing... I
had many good and genuine friends back there..."

The old man stands up and opens the gate.

"You will find the same decent people here. Welcome to our City..."

They go into the City together.


> and you are wrong - I "helped" with an answer about picture clauses about
a
> week ago
> when many others were nasty with their answers I and a few others actually
> "tried" to offer some helpful advice
>

See my comments about "help" above. If your heart was in the right place,
well, that's a start.

Pete.
```

```
outskirts of a village in the Old Country (Brooklyn?), begins his answers 
with 'You know... I've lived outside of this village all my life' and told 
the second lad (both lads were described as 'a young man with a pack on 
his back') to go down the street and talk to (tradesman), saying (old 
man's name) sent him and maybe there was some work.  After that Mrs Old 
Man came out and upbraided her husband for telling one A and the other Z, 
the first black and the second white and what sense did it make?

... and the Old Man smiles and says 'You know, I've lived outside of this 
village all my life and I've found that young men often carry their old 
villages in the packs on their backs.'
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T09:13:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C56AE05.444A21C9@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <3c525066_2@Usenet.com> <07f58.8487$y57.29966@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <3c55f7a0_10@Usenet.com> <a3567n$q2f$1@panix1.panix.com> <EJp58.4788$LH1.111173@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>`

```
Rational One wrote:
> 
> So I take it that this is a game to you and Doc..........
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> whether they "deserve" it or not
<snip>

This thread has gone on entirely too long.

1) If you get answers from someone in a news group that you
get tired of, use your reader's tools to filter out any
postings from that poster (PLONK!). That's how a rational
person takes care of trolls and the like.

2) If you think this is bad, don't go asking questions in
any of the Linux news groups. You must become thicker
skinned. Those who worked hard at gaining their knowledge
sometimes think that they must make sport of those who do
not have that knowledge. A rational one will come to
recognize this behavior and live with it.

3) If you post in certain other news groups, and ask a
simple question, you may get reported for spamming if that
simple question is Off Topic. Which could cause you to lose
your email account. A tad irrational, but a rational person
would get the point and move on.

So be the rational one in a non rational world and quit
whining!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T12:25:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c57327f_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <3c525066_2@Usenet.com> <07f58.8487$y57.29966@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <3c55f7a0_10@Usenet.com> <a3567n$q2f$1@panix1.panix.com> <EJp58.4788$LH1.111173@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <3C56AE05.444A21C9@ix.netcom.com>`

```
A couple of points which I really can't let go, Steve.

Comments below.

Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:3C56AE05.444A21C9@ix.netcom.com...
> Rational One wrote:
> >
> > So I take it that this is a game to you and Doc..........
> >
> > seems to me that neither of you seek to offer any help to any user of
this
> > post group
> > you just want to sit back and poke fun at people who need want help
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Simple solution: Don't read it.

> 1) If you get answers from someone in a news group that you
> get tired of, use your reader's tools to filter out any
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> skinned.

Good point, and fair.

>>>>
> Those who worked hard at gaining their knowledge
> sometimes think that they must make sport of those who do
> not have that knowledge.
<<<<
As one who has worked for a lifetime gaining knowledge, I must protest.

I have NEVER thought it was OK to make sport of anyone who is pursuing
knowledge in a genuine spirit of enquiry. On the contrary, I have spent
thousands of hours working with people to pass on the knowledge gained, just
as the people who gave it to me did (and out of respect for many of them,
who are now dead.) Yes, I believe humour has a very important place in both
the Workplace and the Classroom, but there is a big difference between
gentle (or even savage) humour and making sport of people.

Rational One is carrying a chip the size of a California redwood. He
imagines he is injured whether the injury is real or not, has posted
intensely provocative stuff just for the sake of trolling, and then cries
like a baby when he is unable to achieve the desired result.

On the whole, I believe my responses to him have been restrained and much
less savage than he deserves. (Of course, I can't speak for the Doc <G>) I
have in fact, tried to get through to him and point out what giving help is
all about.

What would your reaction be to someone who says you do NOT "seek to offer
any help to any user of this
 post group", when that is precisely what you have been doing for a number
of years here, and for a lifetime in the Workplace?

I am old enough and wise enough to not take it to heart, but I would be less
than human if I didn't respond with some degree of tartness.....

Pete.




 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T11:43:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0635261D6E7B2FA6.48CC56CDA101C08C.E778CA4DD25BC965@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <3c525066_2@Usenet.com> <07f58.8487$y57.29966@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <3c55f7a0_10@Usenet.com> <a3567n$q2f$1@panix1.panix.com> <EJp58.4788$LH1.111173@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <3C56AE05.444A21C9@ix.netcom.com> <3c57327f_8@Usenet.com> <z5L58.4926$Tu1.15261@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net>`

```
On Wed, 30 Jan 2002 05:00:15 GMT, "Rational One"
<noreligion@nogod.com> enlightened us:

>Peter,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If that's what you think, then you've missed the whole point that
Peter and others have been trying to make about whether or not they
should share knowledge.  Simply put, everyone who participates in this
newsgroup are more than willing to share knowledge so long as the
requestor has shown they've tried solving whatever problem it is on
their own first.  In the case of homework, this means showing some
coding and explaining what platform they are on and what problem they
are seeing.  In the case of a fellow programmer, just an explanation
of the problem is usually enough.

For us, who have got our degrees, who have worked for many years in
the industry to just out and out do someone homework for them does a
disservice to that person (they're not going to learn it if someone
else does it) and could eventually weaken the industry itself, i.e.
put far too many people in programming situations who have no problem
solving skills or lack knowledge of the syntax of the language they
are to program in.


As for you comment about Doc not helping anyone, well you haven't been
in the newsgroup long enough to make that determination.  I've seen
Doc contribute positive responses to legitimate requests for help more
than I've ever seen you contribute anything useful.  Continual ranting
and whining about and berating of the participants of this newsgroup
does not count.

>
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[81 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I am a nobody, nobody is perfect, therefore I am perfect.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
You seem to use the claim of "childish" for everyone that disagrees with
you.  Seeing as Warren has been coding for longer than you have likely lived
(as have I, and Pete, and half the people in here), perhaps you should learn
a new adjective.  I am a relative newbie at 35 years of programming compared
to both of them.  I believe all three of us are grandparents, and are well
versed at handling obstreberous children, of all ages.  Unniversity students
are nothing new.

As for you claims that we should all behave as you do, no.  I do not think
we should, quite frankly.  I also see no good reason for doing someone elses
work, free of charge, regardless of whether they think themselves adult,
programmer, consultant, technician, guru, or "childish".

Steve gave you good advice, about 2000 years old.  When in Rome, do as the
Romans do. If you do not care to follow it, then expect flack.  If
conversing with certain people gets your shorts in a knot, then do not
converse with them.  Expecting them to convert to your way of thinking, and
calling them names because they will not is just testosterone.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T06:13:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1a955$3ac9c2e0$0434e641@chottel>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <3c525066_2@Usenet.com> <07f58.8487$y57.29966@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <3c55f7a0_10@Usenet.com> <a3567n$q2f$1@panix1.panix.com> <EJp58.4788$LH1.111173@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <QpB58.292$0J6.20699@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <MRB58.4715$9U4.9438@rwcrnsc54> <iSC58.12508$9y2.1722859@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Studies show that in face to face communication the majority of information
is conveyed on a non-verbal level.  As we all know there is plenty of
miscommunication even so.  In a newsgroup posting all we have is a text
representation of what we might say verbally.  Typing is harder than
speaking so we tend to abbreviate even more.  My point is that the chances
for miscommunication keep increasing as more and more information is
omitted.  Many people asking for help do not give enough details and
sometimes it takes several exchanges to determine what problem they are
trying to solve.  Sometimes they are trying to solve the wrong problem. 
While all of this is taking place, some people respond to the lack of
information by what I call "mindreading".  They assume that they know the
intentions or motivations of the poster in the absence of complete
information.  I do not hold out much hope that this will change in the
short term.  Perhaps when we are all using sound and video it will be
greatly reduced.

So what to do?  As a poster you post hoping for some kind of response.  If
you get the response that you want, then unless you want to post a "thank
you", you are done.  You have succeeded in your communication.  There may
have been many responses that fall outside of the range of what you were
seeking.  If and how you respond to them is up to you.  It is your time to
do with as you will.  All of that is irrelevant to your original purpose in
posting, because you received response(s) that you were seeking.  Of course
it may affect how others feel about helping you in the future.

What if none of the responses are what you wanted or there is no response. 
Some people post the exact same thing to another newsgroup and somtimes
that is appropriate.  Often it is not, but many people keep trying the same
thing over and over, even when the feedback is not what they want. Maybe it
is human nature.  My point is if at first you do not succeed then try
something else, anything else, until you get the response you are seeking.
If being thin skinned or defensive or calling potential helpers names, etc.
works, then fine.  If not then perhaps being thicker skinned or less
defensive or simply clarifying might work better.  Keep your original goal
in mind.

One of the strengths of newsgroups is the variety of viewpoints available. 
Choose what you find helpful and ignore what you find unhelpful.  Exchange
ideas to get as many viewpoints as possible to choose from.  My experience
is that if you do that without attacking or trying to change the other
persons strongly held opinions you will be more likely to succeed.

<snip>
```

```
admit, I should have been more detailed with my question. Also, I certainly
understand if one chooses to not offer help to another. What I don't
understand is what useful purpose it serves to respond with rude and
negative remarks. Wouldn't it be better to just not respond at all if one
chooses not to offer help?  I guess there are some that just get some
intrinsic reward by just stirring conflict.


Thanks again to all that did offer support

KEvin
```

```
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Well.. I wasn't going to but..

When posting a question like this, you will normally get at least once
reply (from DD).

If DD does not give his most clarifying reply (which some of us think
is appropriate), then it is possible that no reply is given at all.

This leads to some complains from whoever is posting the question that
they are ignored, that no one helps them, that they are being censored
and so on.

I think that the last is far worst than DD's.

The only thing I would eventually add to DD's reply is a line such as
"See COBOL faq at xxxyyy for further info" just to avoid some of the
mindless comments this thread had but it is obviously up to DD to
decide what his "Homework" reply is.

FF
```

```
Two things:

1. Doc has a right to word his message however he thinks fit (just like the
rest of us...). This is an unmoderated NG.

2. I agree with Frederico and Howard that a pointer to the FAQ, appended to
Doc's standard response, would make it much more useful. (This in no way
implies that it is not useful in its current form).

The problem is that if you don't change it, Doc, then the rest of us will
need to post a "modifier" pointing to the FAQ. It would be much more
convenient if your message included it.

Think on't.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

```
On 30-Jan-2002, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >The only thing I would eventually add to DD's reply is a line such as
> >"See COBOL faq at xxxyyy for further info" just to avoid some of the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the newsgroup to get its 'mood' is also... one's own homework.


Someone starting to learn CoBOL might have no idea how to find the FAQ, and
it takes time to monitor a newsgroup for its mood.  I agree with Fredrico
100%.  Such a link would enable a newbie to learn what was expected, and
doesn't hurt the industry.

I really don't know how to find FAQs to newsgroups.  I have directions saved
off somewhere, but the only FAQ I care about have web pages linked to my
browser.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Finding FAQs (in general) (was: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T17:21:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a39v65$gqd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <EJp58.4788$LH1.111173@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <u5gc15juucp0ff@corp.supernews.com> <udkg5u8ipe1b5ud8t6tcrstr9hea3npo60@4ax.com> <a39lor$r03$1@panix1.panix.com> <a39pb6$2pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
to find which "comp." newsgroups have FAQs, look at the news group

  comp.answers


There are many other "xyz.answers" newsgroups that also contain FAQs for a
variety of newsgroups.
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T21:14:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3a9b2$1b8$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <udkg5u8ipe1b5ud8t6tcrstr9hea3npo60@4ax.com> <a39lor$r03$1@panix1.panix.com> <a39pb6$2pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
