In article <a39pb6$2pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
>On 30-Jan-2002, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>100%.

I am reminded of the Old Saw: 'A lack of planning and foresight on your 
part does not constitute an emergency on my part'; someone who waits until 
the last minute to ask someone else to do their homework for them is not a 
someone who arouses in me much pity.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T09:48:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3blga$mhi$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a39pb6$2pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <a3a9b2$1b8$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c595109_4@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3c595109_4@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a3a9b2$1b8$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>have to eat them.'

Notice the use of 'please'.

>
>Suppose...just suppose... that you got it wrong.

This has happened before... nothing new there.

>Suppose that SOMEONE you
>THOUGHT was looking for Homework assistance (without having actually done
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Couldn't happen?

The likelihood of it happening diminishes with one's doing things like 
reading FAQs and watching the traffic for a while before posting... you 
know, stuff that has already been called 'homework'.

>OK, then I agree that the short shrift meted out to
>recalcitrants who come here looking for a free ride is justifiable.

Trying to use a tool (UseNet) without learning about it in advance and 
expecting All To Work Out Well seems to be... looking for a *very* 
inexpensive ride, to say the least.

>(Although there is still an argument that says no real good purpose is
>served by it...)

There are arguments which say that, if asked, one should do someone else's 
homework for them, too... I am not moved by them.

>
>But, IF there was a case where you got it wrong, then adding a pointer to
>the FAQ would at least be of some assistance.
>(It would point a genuine knowledge seeker in the right direction and at
>least inform him as to what the general rules are...)

This seems a matter of definition... a 'genuine knowledge seeker' can be 
said to be one who learns about the tool before using it.

>
>And even in the case of the REAL bad guys it wouldn't do them any harm to go
>off to the COBOL FAQ and gain some knowledge, would it?

I've never encountered a 'REAL bad guy' in comp.lang.cobol.  Should I 
start reading some of the linux groups?

>
>I completely share your distaste for freeloaders, but I honestly believe
>that pointing people at the FAQ is a "no lose" situation. If you disagree,
>I'd be interested to hear your arguments.

Stated above... in essence, learning the tool is part of one's homework.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T15:11:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3bmkq$4q1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <udkg5u8ipe1b5ud8t6tcrstr9hea3npo60@4ax.com> <a39lor$r03$1@panix1.panix.com> <a39pb6$2pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <a3a9b2$1b8$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

On 30-Jan-2002, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> I am reminded of the Old Saw: 'A lack of planning and foresight on your
> part does not constitute an emergency on my part'; someone who waits until
> the last minute to ask someone else to do their homework for them is not a
> someone who arouses in me much pity.

My hope is that the person didn't "wait" until the last minute.  That he
worked like a dog trying to solve his problem on his own, is running out of
time, and then someone mentioned this newsgroup.  He's not familiar with
FAQs, went to this newsgroup and asked a question without showing his work.

Admittedly I doubt this happens very much.  But for that rare case,
facilitating his process is worth while.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T11:59:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3bt5r$j0d$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a39pb6$2pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <a3a9b2$1b8$1@panix1.panix.com> <a3bmkq$4q1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <a3bmkq$4q1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
>On 30-Jan-2002, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>FAQs, went to this newsgroup and asked a question without showing his work.

Hmmmmm... so (in language appropriate to the group):

IF DIDNT-WAIT-UNTIL-LASTMIN
    IF WORKED-LIKE-DOG
        IF RUNNING-OUT-OF-TIME
           IF SOMEONE-MENTIONED-NEWSGROUP
               IF NOT-FAMILIAR-WITH-FAQS
                   IF ASKED-QUESTION
                       IF NO-WORK-SHOWN

... then data are to be displayed at all times?  For a condition 
predicated by seven levels of condition this does not seem very 
desireable.

>
>Admittedly I doubt this happens very much.

I do not recall seeing any evidence that it happens at all.

>But for that rare case,
>facilitating his process is worth while.

'Worth while' is not a label I would normally apply to a situation which 
requires universally displaying data in the event that a seventh-level 
conditional for which no evidence of existence has yet been given 
occurs... but that's just me, I guess.

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a39pb6$2pl$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <a3a9b2$1b8$1@panix1.panix.com> <a3bmkq$4q1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <a3bt5r$j0d$1@panix1.panix.com>`

On 31-Jan-2002, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> 'Worth while' is not a label I would normally apply to a situation which
> requires universally displaying data in the event that a seventh-level
> conditional for which no evidence of existence has yet been given
> occurs... but that's just me, I guess.

Fine, that's your choice.  But if you have an automated or cut 'n paste
response it is less work to add a reference to the FAQ than to respond to
messages all the time.    Heck, you could even add an opinion to the canned
response that varies from the FAQ opinion.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T17:46:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3bvnn$9le$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a3bmkq$4q1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <a3bt5r$j0d$1@panix1.panix.com> <a3bu9s$8sk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <a3buu2$pob$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

On 31-Jan-2002, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> Nope... each 'Please do your own homework' (and most 'When posting to
> compl.and.cobol...') posting is hand-crafted.

That does make it harder - having to remember where the FAQ is and spell it
correctly and all.  Without a macro or cut and paste, available, then it is
a matter of just how much work you're willing to do to indicate that the
message was received, but was insufficient.  Most of us skip replying
altogether.  Then when you go half-way (according to our standards - not
necessarily yours), we wish you went the whole distance - when we were too
lazy to do it the way we prefer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T13:39:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3c31s$akc$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a3bu9s$8sk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <a3buu2$pob$1@panix1.panix.com> <a3bvnn$9le$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <a3bvnn$9le$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
>On 31-Jan-2002, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>lazy to do it the way we prefer.   

Plural majestatus est and all that... but have, rather delicately, pointed 
out what I was trying to avoid, the old 'Well, *I* certainly never take 
the time to reply... but when *you* do you should *really* do it the way 
*I* want'.

(similar to 'The food here is utter poison... and such small portions, 
too!')

I do as I see fit, of course; others are welcome to the same as I take for 
myself... but saying that from the start might have denied folks the 
chance to see how the Older Tymers in this group disagree with each other.  
Really, it has been frighteningly civil and rife with Lofty Concepts - the 
UseNet as a tool which one should learn to use, how does one differentiate 
between The Truly Shiftless and the Unfortunately Caught Short, 
conditionals falling seven levels deep - and refreshingly bereft of 
name-calling, obscenities, tawdry vulgarisms and suggestions of anatomical 
impossibilities.

Oh, I *cannot* resist... and this was not easy to do, either, as all I had 
to work with was a bunch of poopie-heads!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: User Group is a game to some meanspirited posters

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T13:53:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3c3rq$d38$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <a3a9b2$1b8$1@panix1.panix.com> <a3bmkq$4q1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C5A45AA.D01A3862@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In article <3C5A45AA.D01A3862@Azonic.co.nz>,
Richard Plinston  <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

[snippage]

>DD puts his opinion out in a message, you can nullify that by providing
>a complete answer if you wish, or a reference to the FAQ (findable on
>Yahoo as 'Cobol FAQ' and 3 clicks), or just a message "Please do not do
>your own homework" to counter DDs.

Point of information, Mr Plinston: it is not my intention to post an 
opinion (other than, of course, the fact of *any* posting can be said to 
be evidence of 'It is my opinion that this posting should be made'); I 
intend only the creation of a paradigmatic, perfunctory, politely-posted, 
practical pentalogism.

(That alliteration might be a bit hard to swallow... but if one washes it 
down with sizeable swigs of aceto balsamico one might soon be...

... full of p's and vinegar.)

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric values

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T21:04:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0201252104.b76606@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<u51g1a7b85ks75@corp.supernews.com> <8v335u00fvev22m5flupjp87rrv182s1mq@4ax.com>`

```
Hi Kevin,

I see you've already met Doc. I've always thought he should've chosen
Grumpy as his Nom de Guerre (I know I'm inviting some sort of "Dopey" 
retort from that Poopee Head).

Anyway, data representation and related topics are probably the most 
important and confusing aspects of pgmming for a beginner. 

The data types, A/N, num packed, num display, num binary and the way
they're represented in memory, how they're defined in the pgm, what
happens when one type is moved to another (sometimes the format of 
the data changes, sometimes the pgm abends), you'll learn all of this 
and more (and the sooner the better - you can't be a good pgmr w/o 
knowing this stuff).

Don't let Doc discourage you from asking questions. He's an OK guy; 
just lacks the social graces <G>. It's always a good idea to let us 
know what you tried, what results you got, where you looked, etc.

Good Luck with your new adventure.

Jack   


Opus <yo_dog40@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<8v335u00fvev22m5flupjp87rrv182s1mq@4ax.com>...
> On Thu, 24 Jan 2002 21:11:49 -0500, "Kevin"
> <kmiller2001xx@charter.net> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff
```

<a id="t-39188b850d"></a>
## Thread: More numeric values. — 81 this month / 86 total

### More numeric values.

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T13:50:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`

```
If a data record contains  x'0123456C'
  and is READ INTO working-storage where this
  field is defined in a record as

 05  INFIELD  PIC 9(5)V99  COMP-3.

and in the procedure division the following
statement is executed

 IF INFIELD IS NUMERIC
    PERFORM A
 ELSE
    PERFORM B.

Which paragraph will be performed?

Not homework...spent all night on this once 20 years ago.
(IBM mainframe).
```

#### ↳ Re: More numeric values.

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T15:05:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C51BA84.F74F1719@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`

```
Ron wrote:
> 
> If a data record contains  x'0123456C'
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> (IBM mainframe).

Back on a VS1 system when I first learned COBOL, someone did
this trying to avoid a S0C7 ABEND. When INFIELD wasn't
numeric, it still got the S0C7 (in the middle of the "IF").
So, in an attempt to avoid this problem, basically they did
the following:

05 INFIELD....
05 INFIELD-X REDFINES INFIELD PIC X(4).
   .
   .
   .
  IF INFIELD-X IS NUMERIC
     PERFORM A
  ELSE 
     PERFORM B.

Can you guess which paragraph was performed no matter what
the test data was?

I wrote a QUIKJOB routine to verify the data in the file
(how it got mangled was another story).
```

#### ↳ Re: More numeric values.

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T15:18:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2seil$c6i$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`

```
In article <a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>,
Ron <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com> wrote:
>If a data record contains  x'0123456C'
>  and is READ INTO working-storage where this
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Which paragraph will be performed?

Low-order Charlie's a sign-nibble.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: More numeric values.

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T17:15:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2sl4g$ba1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`

```
Ron,

        It will fail because of the "C" sign-nibble as the NUMERIC test is
expecting an "F" and the translate-table built for the "TRT" doesn't include
a "C" sign-nibble test for packed-decimal with a "neutral" sign-nibble. I
too have had this same problem several times in the past. You'd be better
off re-defining this as signed packed-decimal and then it will pass.

Go Figure,

Bill

"Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com> wrote in message
news:a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com...
> If a data record contains  x'0123456C'
>   and is READ INTO working-storage where this
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Sheesh, you actually expect programmers to remember all that arcana [valid
sign nibble values]? You get pleasure out of "stumping" programmers with
chickenshit questions like that?

Give me a programmer who would answer that question, " I know there's a set
of allowable values for that, and it's in the reference manual and I'l look
it up for you if you need it."

I encounter lots of programmers who know all this kind of trivia.
Unfortunately, it seems retention of this information takes up all the core
storage space in the brain normally used for other things, like remembering
how to tie their shoes.

MCM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T13:32:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D327123D7D0A16E4.3A36C34F0E712FDA.DF769DA66D129C0B@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2sl4g$ba1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <a2sp4j$ofl$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com> <tYy48.54$mR3.134508@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <a2um9j$fbv$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
On 26 Jan 2002 11:42:27 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:

>In article <tYy48.54$mR3.134508@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>,
>Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

I'd have to say knowing how to test a numeric field for valid numerics
and knowing that packed-decimal can cause problems is not arcane
trivia but knowledge that should be known so that you too can be a
good programmer.

In the old DOS/VSE days it didn't take me long to figure out that the
only correct way to do a proper numeric test on a PD field was to move
it to a PIC X field so that it would get unpacked and it signs would
be "fixed".  Then you would get proper results from If the PIC X field
is Numeric...etc. etc.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

The secret of a happy marriage remains a secret.
                                -- Henny Youngman
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T05:15:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020127001540.26837.00000543@mb-mh.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<D327123D7D0A16E4.3A36C34F0E712FDA.DF769DA66D129C0B@lp.airnews.net>`

```
>Subject: Re: More numeric values.
>From: SkippyPB swiegand@neo.rr.com 
>Date: 1/26/02 1:32 PM Eastern Standard Time

>In the old DOS/VSE days it didn't take me long to figure out that the
>only correct way to do a proper numeric test on a PD field was to move
>it to a PIC X field so that it would get unpacked and it signs would
>be "fixed".  Then you would get proper results from If the PIC X field
>is Numeric...etc. etc.

Ok - I'm confused (so what else is new). I remember back in the old DOS days
(before VSE) that if you moved a PD field (which if memory serves is COMP-3 on
a mainframe :D) to a PIC X it does NOT unpack and therefore your numeric test
would fail.  Again if memory serves this is true for OS, MVS, XA, and now z/OS
(which of course has nothing to do with how your particular version of COBOL is
going to do anything for the most part).

If you took said field and moved it to a PIC 9 field it would unpack, dropping
the sign and would pass, but if moved to a PIC S9 field the sign would remain
and the PIC S9 field would pass but if redefined as a PIC X would fail.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T10:45:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5D826D2F421A16EF.81C7821F74688806.AEACB9A8AF5CD222@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<D327123D7D0A16E4.3A36C34F0E712FDA.DF769DA66D129C0B@lp.airnews.net> <20020127001540.26837.00000543@mb-mh.aol.com>`

```
On 27 Jan 2002 05:15:40 GMT, yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) enlightened
us:

>>Subject: Re: More numeric values.
>>From: SkippyPB swiegand@neo.rr.com 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>and the PIC S9 field would pass but if redefined as a PIC X would fail.

Well maybe I'm the one who is confused!!  It has only been 20+ years
since I programmed in DOS mainframe, but I'm sure I remember in both
versions of Cobol that I worked with then that if you moved a packed
(COMP-3) field to an alphanumeric field (Pic X), the assembler that
was generated was an unpack to a double word field and then a move
character command for the length of the receiving field and then an or
immediate to fix the sign in the last byte.  I'm almost certain Cobol
did it this was in the VSE systems I worked on as well.  I know for
sure it does it now on MVS.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

The secret of a happy marriage remains a secret.
                                -- Henny Youngman
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T14:23:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a31nkj$mdf$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<D327123D7D0A16E4.3A36C34F0E712FDA.DF769DA66D129C0B@lp.airnews.net> <20020127001540.26837.00000543@mb-mh.aol.com> <5D826D2F421A16EF.81C7821F74688806.AEACB9A8AF5CD222@lp.airnews.net>`

```
That is true. Moving a Pic 9 comp-3 field to a Pic x field does cause
the field to be unpacked. This is true on VSE, MVS or whatever. If
the field is signed, you lose the sign.
If you move a Pic 9 comp-3 field to a GROUP item it will NOT be
unpacked however.

Ron.

> > Mr Mattias, leaving aside the fact that there are many work-site where

> > Manuals are not available (and web-access is denied a Non-Manager or
Mere
> > Consultant)...
>
> Real Consultants invest in their own manuals.
>
Yes, they do. (or, occasionally write the manual themselves...<G>)

But having said that...

How can the Company stop you accessing the Internet in your own time?

Or maybe  you get an empowered Manager to do so for you and look up the
problem solution on the spot.

Sites that don't make manuals available to their employees (contract and/or
permanent) are just incredibly short sighted and stupid. It's like saying
they want something done but there are no tools or resources available. If
they can provide a computer, how much more difficult is it to provide the
manuals/software for it?

Employees who accept these conditions are also short-sighted and stupid.
(Yes, I know a job is a job and a career is important and there are
commitments to be met, but tolerating this situation simply perpetuates it.
Tell them the job starts when the tools are available. If everyone in the
department presents the same line then they HAVE to get the tools (or fire
the lot of you...in that event you start your own company and, with the
collective knowledge of their applications you have locked up in your heads,
you sell the service back to them for more than they were paying you
originally... Can't be done? Oh, yes it can...been there done it. <G> (OK,
it wasn't over tools, but it might just as well have been...))).

Employment is a symbiotic arrangement. Neither side should dictate to the
other. Both sides should work together with good will to achieve a common
good. (Wise Managers will see their teams have everything necessary to
achieve success (it means one less excuse for failure, if nothing
else...<G>)). Withholding tools is not a demonstration of goodwill...

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

company to say 'You can bill hourly but need to do this on your own time' 
(which, to me, is the same kind of theft as a Consultant billing for 
non-worked hours) there might be a bit of a difficulty in responding to 
'You get the Big Bucks, you gotta do this now Now NOW!' with 'I'll get 
back to you on that as soon as I can look that up on my own time.'

>
>Or maybe  you get an empowered Manager to do so for you and look up the
>problem solution on the spot.

Sorry... one is *not* allowed to speak with Other Managers and one's own 
is a *Real* Mnager... you know, one who is proud about being 'Not 
Technical'?
 
>
>Sites that don't make manuals available to their employees (contract and/or
>permanent) are just incredibly short sighted and stupid. It's like saying
>they want something done but there are no tools or resources available.

Mr Dashwood, you are preaching to the choir here... but I am relating 
conditions under which I have been asked to work and which I do not 
believe are unique to my circumstances.

How about a shop that says 'You cannot use SEARCH... or temp VSAM files... 
or SYNCSORT'?

>If
>they can provide a computer, how much more difficult is it to provide the
>manuals/software for it?

[snippage]

>Employment is a symbiotic arrangement. Neither side should dictate to the

>good.

of times I have heard a manager tell a subordinate 'Hey, you're lucky we 
gave you a job'... and I, personally, am the only one I know who has 
leaned close and said, quietly, 'No more lucky than *you* are that *I* 
have chosen to work with you... shall we get the job done, now, please?'

>(Wise Managers will see their teams have everything necessary to
>achieve success (it means one less excuse for failure, if nothing
>else...<G>)).

Compare this to 'Wise Managers always have a ready source of blame for 
their own failures'.

>Withholding tools is not a demonstration of goodwill...

at least to me.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T17:03:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2um9j$fbv$1@panix1.panix.com> <k0G48.1258$3Y3.476033@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3c53fce5_3@Usenet.com> <a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com...
> >
> >How can the Company stop you accessing the Internet in your own time?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> non-worked hours)...

You know, you may consider this semantic anal-retentiveness, but this is the
kind of  'cheapening' of the word, "consultant" which has been one of my hot
buttons for about twenty years.

I believe the word "consultant" should only be applied to professionals who
think, analyze, propose and dispose; and the word "consultant" should never
be applied to a technician.

Nothing against technicians, mind you, but technicians are not the same as
consultants. A technician is a narrow-niche expert - someone who, say, can
produce the engineering drawing, repair the furnace or create COBOL code.
The consultant is the one who can decide what goes on the drawing, analyze
facts and propose which repair should be attempted on the furnace, or design
the computer applications system which may utilize COBOL components:
components which will be created - or, rather, "crafted" - by a COBOL
technician.

Sign Nibbles? Might be a good thing for a COBOL technician to know; but it
seems a bit out-of-scope for a consultant.

As for looking up the needed info on your own time? Ain't no consultant
worthy of that title I've ever met who did not invest personal time in
enhancing his/her skills and knowledge. Technicians, sure: but not
consultants. One last thing: the true professional consulant is not afraid
to tell the client or anyone else that he does not know as much about some
specific subject (e.g., sign nibbles) as does the technician.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T13:00:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a31f7r$o6g$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <3c53fce5_3@Usenet.com> <a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com> <0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`

```
In article <0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com...
>> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>buttons for about twenty years.

Now why would anyone do that... expecially when it comes from someone who 
so readily categorises knowledge into Worthwhile and Trivial?

>
>I believe the word "consultant" should only be applied to professionals who
>think, analyze, propose and dispose; and the word "consultant" should never
>be applied to a technician.

I have seen the word 'consultant' applied to a variety of folks at a 
variety of times; what is considered 'good consulting' at one organisation 
will get one terminated for 'overstepping bounds' at another.

>
>Nothing against technicians, mind you, but technicians are not the same as
>consultants.

Says who?... outside of you, of course.  Remember, The Customer is Always 
Right... and if the Customer wants to use a torque-wrench as a hammer it 
is Their business... as long as they keep paying for torque-wrenches.

What is more valuable to an organisation... a Right Solution or not 
offending a good, 20-year Company Man?

A question to the group... has anyone run into a situation where a Right 
Solution offered by a consultant makes a 20-year Company Man look like 
an idiot?  If so, what have you seen as the result?

Mr Mattias, you are, of course, perfectly entitled to your opinions and 
the expressions thereof... and who knows, maybe you have a *perfect* grasp 
of How Things Should Be...

... but how you see Things As They Should Be does not, it seems, coincide 
with my experiences of What Things Are (or, more precisely, perhaps, How I 
Have Interpreted What I Believe I have Seen Happen).  If it comes to a 
toss-up as to what should be given more weight when it comes to 
establishing definitions or making decisions - What Mr Mattias Thinks 
Should Be or What I Have Experienced - then I guess I'll just have to live 
with my narrow, petty, small-minded choice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** akent29@attbi.com (Arthur M. Kent)
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T18:40:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akent29-2701021039040001@12-229-192-120.client.attbi.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <3c53fce5_3@Usenet.com> <a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com> <0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <a31f7r$o6g$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
> >I believe the word "consultant" should only be applied to professionals who
> >think, analyze, propose and dispose; and the word "consultant" should never
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> will get one terminated for 'overstepping bounds' at another.

I have been working as a COBOL consultant at a large bank in Seattle for
over 3 1/2 years, and using the terms in this thread, I have functioned as
both a consultant and a technician.  In fact, for the two critical
applications I support (including being on-call every day of the year), I
have "done it all", so to speak.  That is, I have analyzed the user
requirements, developed a design to address these requirements, developed
the COBOL code (and any other "pieces", ie, JCL, Procs, Tables, Copy
books, etc., etc.), performed complete testing, and implemented all the
necessary modules into the production environment.

I have also gained an excellent reputation with numerous individuals at
the bank, both client and systems personnel.  In fact, I am basically
treated like a full time employee.

As for making a 20 year company person "look like an idiot", I have never
done that.  I work together on a team consisting of all employees, and
there is none of this "look like an idiot" present.

In article <a31f7r$o6g$1@panix1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> with my narrow, petty, small-minded choice.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT Consulant or Insultant? was:Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T11:05:10+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c547b18_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <3c53fce5_3@Usenet.com> <a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com> <0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <a31f7r$o6g$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a31f7r$o6g$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>,
> Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> ><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message
news:a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com...
> >> >
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Many times. And not necessarily because the man WAS an idiot. Sometimes they
just don't have the breadth of experience and imagination to see innovative
solutions. That's why they employ a consultant. (BTW, in my book a
"consultant" is someone who gets consulted. In other words, gives advice.
This can be technical or it can be strategic; I have worked in both
capacities).

Coming back to your question, Doc. It would be foolish for a consultant (or
any decent Human Being, for that matter) to make people look like idiots
(even when they WERE acting foolishly...).

There is no point in being right, if your right solution never gets
implemented.

On the occasions where I have had this situation, I usually approach the
person concerned (maybe over lunch) and go through the broad outline of the
proposed solution. Make it very clear that the only thing of interest is
WHAT's right and not WHO's right. If he has reservations about the new idea,
discuss them openly and  resolve the issues without rancour. Ensure that he
and his department get any credit for the new idea. (I'm not interested in
kudos, just give me the money...<G>). Try and create a positive atmosphere
where time is not wasted in asking "Why didn't we do this before?" but
rather in "When can we start to get this off the ground?". Get him to buy in
to the idea and when it is presented to the team make sure he gets credit
for backing it.

Strangely enough, the above is the way I do it with Companies and Managers I
don't know very well. People who I feel really comfortable with (maybe even
friends with) don't get this. They get the lunch, but the talk is simple,
direct, and to the point. I say exactly what I think (well, I always do
that, but sometimes I am "gentle" about it...<G>) and I trust the fact that
they know me, so they won't take my comments personally or be offended,
realising I only have their interests at heart and there is no malice in
what I say. In other words, it takes less time with people you know and
trust and who know and trust you.

I guess the point that comes out of this is that for effective consultancy
you have to ensure that consulting you is not an unpleasant experience for
the Manager concerned. Maybe that's an added skill that is necessary for a
good consultant.


Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Consulant or Insultant? was:Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T17:28:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a31uu7$mvp$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <a31f7r$o6g$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c547b18_7@Usenet.com>`

```
In article <3c547b18_7@Usenet.com>,
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a31f7r$o6g$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>capacities).

Bingo, Mr Dashwood... a consultant gets consulted.  Whether the 
consultant's words have any effect is another matter, of course.

>
>Coming back to your question, Doc. It would be foolish for a consultant (or
>any decent Human Being, for that matter) to make people look like idiots
>(even when they WERE acting foolishly...).

It would be, indeed... and yet, at times, is accidental.

'Hey, what's with job MDD10082?  It's running in half the time it usually 
does!'

'Oh, that?  I was in it, changing the headers, and noticed a loop that 
could be made more efficient.'

'Whaddaya *mean*, 'could be made more efficient'?  *I* wrote that code 
when I got here, back in 1521, and *nothing* in it could be made more 
efficient!'

'Well, here's a listing of the original code... and my mods... and the 
LIST... errr, excuse me, the PMAP of the two for comparison... and as you 
can see...'

... and suddenly someone looks like an idiot.

>
>There is no point in being right, if your right solution never gets
>implemented.

As my Sainted Mother, may she sleep with the angels, told me, when I 
started at my Very First Job:

'When it comes to work you should remember two things: you can be wrong 
about something... and be fired for it... and you can be right about 
something... and be fired for it.'

I've been fired for both, and, speaking strictly from personal experience, 
I prefer being fired for being right.  I'd rather not be fired, of course, 
and I'd rather have the right solution implemented... but that, in my 
experience, again, has not always been possible; there are a few My Way Or 
The Highway types I have met.

>
>On the occasions where I have had this situation, I usually approach the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>for backing it.

There are times, Mr Dashwood, that I have been able to make this approach 
work... and there are times when I've gotten 'Look, you Just Don't 
Understand How Things Work Here... do it *my* way or clean out your desk.'

It might have been a deficiency in my powers of suasion, granted... or it 
might have been that the fellows who responded this way were idiots... or 
perhaps something else.

>
>Strangely enough, the above is the way I do it with Companies and Managers I
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>good consultant.

In the words of Old Lode Skins, from the final scene of the film 'Little 
Big Man' (a scene which, by the bye, was *not* in the book): 'Sometimes 
the magic works... sometimes it doesn't.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Consulant or Insultant? was:Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T12:08:30+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c55e440_9@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <a31f7r$o6g$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c547b18_7@Usenet.com> <a31uu7$mvp$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a31uu7$mvp$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <3c547b18_7@Usenet.com>,
> >
> >Coming back to your question, Doc. It would be foolish for a consultant
(or
> >any decent Human Being, for that matter) to make people look like idiots
> >(even when they WERE acting foolishly...).
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, we've all heard this conversation or variations on it...

This is an example of someone who IS an idiot. (The ultimate stupidity may
be to believe there is nothing left to learn...<G>)

Personally, I'd simply smile and walk away... I have better things to do
with my time than engage in a battle of wits with someone who is so totally
unarmed...

I MIGHT indulge myself to the point of saying:"Oops. Guess I got it wrong.
Maybe you better change my code back to how you like it...." THEN smile and
walk away. (The most important thing here is to smile... there really is
humour in this situation.)

The LAST thing I would do is sit down and show him exactly what I changed
and why.

On the other hand, if the reaction had been that of a wiser person ...
'Really?! You managed to cut the execution time in half? Wow! Can you show
me how you did that?'

I would re-schedule my day (or arrange another specific time) and ensure
that the concepts and details behind the change were explained and walked
through so that an effective skills transfer could take place. (That is part
of what I'm paid for...) And it is always a pleasure to work with people who
"want to know".

>
> 'When it comes to work you should remember two things: you can be wrong
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, sadly there are a few Managers who fit this category.

I am very happy to hit the Highway under these circumstances and walk down
the road to a place where I CAN do some good...
> >
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I would be gone so fast he would have my footprints all over him...But then
I probably would never have taken the job in the first place... There is
little point in taking a job where you can be of no use.

Fortunately, I only encountered a completely tyrannical and immovable
Manager once in my career, and I was quite young at the time. I was glad to
leave.

>'Sometimes
> the magic works... sometimes it doesn't.'
>

If it doesn't, I consider it my failure not his. You can't call yourself a
Magician unless you can make the Magic work <G>.

Pete.

What's the old joke about the difference between Heaven and Hell like 
being the difference between being Interviewed and being On Board?  It 
didn't look like it would be this way coming in the door, no, but when it 
became obvious that Things Weren't Working Out then I got on the telephone 
with my pimp and told him to start working on filling the slot with

(This, of course, lead to the laughable 'Whaddaya mean, you're gonna

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Consulant or Insultant? was:Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T00:30:05+13:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Consulant or Insultant? was:Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T14:40:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a36c34$5hp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <3c547b18_7@Usenet.com> <a31uu7$mvp$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c55e440_9@Usenet.com> <a3558f$pgt$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
I have been a contractor much of my career.  In my present job, we hired a
contractor who was once a co-worker of mine at E.D.S.  He has been
contracting for a while now - partially because he wanted a few months off
to hunt and work on his ranch - but partially because he found it easier to
simply do what the boss wanted and not do things "right", if he was a
contractor.

When he was an employee, he felt the need to do things the "right" way,
arguing about how things should be done.  His jobs would end when he got
frustrated with this, when promises weren't kept, or when they kept bugging
him about going into management.  (rewarding him by making him one of them).

Interesting that he could have the same mindset about owning his work as an
employee, but he didn't.
```

> Many times. And not necessarily because the man WAS an idiot. Sometimes
they
> just don't have the breadth of experience and imagination to see
innovative
> solutions. That's why they employ a consultant. (BTW, in my book a
> "consultant" is someone who gets consulted. In other words, gives advice.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Coming back to your question, Doc. It would be foolish for a consultant
(or
> any decent Human Being, for that matter) to make people look like idiots
> (even when they WERE acting foolishly...).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> person concerned (maybe over lunch) and go through the broad outline of
the
> proposed solution. Make it very clear that the only thing of interest is
> WHAT's right and not WHO's right. If he has reservations about the new
idea,
> discuss them openly and  resolve the issues without rancour. Ensure that
he
> and his department get any credit for the new idea. (I'm not interested in
> kudos, just give me the money...<G>). Try and create a positive atmosphere
> where time is not wasted in asking "Why didn't we do this before?" but
> rather in "When can we start to get this off the ground?". Get him to buy
in
> to the idea and when it is presented to the team make sure he gets credit
> for backing it.
>
> Strangely enough, the above is the way I do it with Companies and Managers
I
> don't know very well. People who I feel really comfortable with (maybe
even
> friends with) don't get this. They get the lunch, but the talk is simple,
> direct, and to the point. I say exactly what I think (well, I always do
> that, but sometimes I am "gentle" about it...<G>) and I trust the fact
that
> they know me, so they won't take my comments personally or be offended,
> realising I only have their interests at heart and there is no malice in
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T21:17:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C546FCB.9EA1C477@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2um9j$fbv$1@panix1.panix.com> <k0G48.1258$3Y3.476033@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3c53fce5_3@Usenet.com> <a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com> <0qW48.99$pZ.298754@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`

```


Michael Mattias wrote:

> <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a314cr$69u$1@panix1.panix.com...
> > >
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Michael,

Interesting. I think I buy into your dissection between technicians and
consultants - in the specific context that you use the word 'consultant'.

Generally 'consultant' is a word that raises my hackles, having 'suffered'
consultants twice, dairy industry and retail. Yep I'm thinking specifically of
Management Consultants, (Hello Arthur Andersen and 'inventive' accounting). The
ones I dealt with contained team members who were from many disciplines,
including engineering. Because they are paid big bucks, the boys on the Board of
Directors listen - and what the consultants say must be gospel, given all that
'expertise'

Here's a classic. The consultants, having looked over our shoulders, and seen
that we were proposing printing a computerized catalogue to be used by retail
departments, stock-taking followed up by ordering, (that's where our ICL OMR
document reader came in), thought this was an hell of an idea. Similarly we
weren't aghast at their interest as their clout meant that our proposal might at
last leave the marine building yard..

Well of course they did their own thing. Can't wait to specify it so that it
gets computerized, so let's do it manually first. They drew together the
accounting and computer people to test our reaction, prior to submitting it to
London for the merchandisers and directors. They took our catalogue concept,
typed up in London by the merchandisers, for reprinting by admin and
distribution to the stores.. They designed some nifty pre-printed transparent
overlays containing Purchase Order info, and these were laid over the catalogue
pages, showing descriptions, quantities ordered etc. Then you photo-copied the
requisite number of copies.

Just one snag - this is mid '70s. Although each store is in a town of some size,
'Xerox' was still a magic word - and the MAJORITY of businesses did not have
photo-copiers, which had to be at least 80% of our stores. When we pointed this
out, they responded, "No problem. Just take your documentation and waltz down
the street to the nearest Co-Operative grocery store and do your photo-copying
there". That also ASSUMED each Co-op had a photo-copier. Plus of course it also
ignored English weather and an image of some rain-soaked, wind-swept wretch
shuffling down the street with a mass of soggy papers.

They were really taken aback when we commented, "You must be out of your minds".
They pursued their proposal in London, when they then understood our comment,
getting the same reaction and losing the consultancy contract !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T03:59:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C561F48.70BF577B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a33ooo$lvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com>`

```


YukonMama wrote:

> >From: "Howard Brazee" howard.brazee@cusys.edu
> >Date: 1/28/02 9:58 AM Eastern Standard Time
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> thinking.  I'm not sure what it is that allows her to keep her job.

No Eileen, she is not the problem. What would be more germane is if you were to
introduce us to your mangers !

A while back there was a topic on leadership, with many offering their take on
defining it  I'll sum it up in one word - ENTHUSIASM. It starts with each
individual. You have to enjoy what you are doing, resulting in enthusiasm - and
if that doesn't happen, get the hell out of there to another job. As you rise in
the hierarchy, you should be taking your enthusiasm with you, and without making
a hard sell, that same enthusiasm should permeate from you and induct your
subordinates.

Coupled with that is management style, and this differs tremendously dependent
upon our individual personalities. But one thing for sure, your management style
MUST include feedback and measuring staff performance. I've only done it twice,
and HATED it - but 'gracefully' fired two people who didn't measure up. As
individuals, I liked them both very much.

You sure you still want that job, motoring in (what is it, some sixty miles ?).
Perhaps you'd be happier reducing the family income and staying at home with the
kids. You could still keep your hand in, doing work on a PC at home.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T12:25:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9xw58.405$cg1.57369@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<a33ooo$lvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com...
> >From: "Howard Brazee" howard.brazee@cusys.edu
> >Date: 1/28/02 9:58 AM Eastern Standard Time
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >So what kind of programmer does not "think, analyze, propose, and
dispose"?
> >
>
> Allow me to introduce you to the gal that sits 2 cubicles down from
me.......
>
> And no, she isn't blonde, she isn't built, she isn't whatever it is you're
> thinking.  I'm not sure what it is that allows her to keep her job.

As I said some posts ago, this is a semantic issue: is one a consultant or a
technician?

That this unblonde unbuilt lady does not fit one's definition of a
consultant does not make here either a bad person or non-asset. She may in
fact be a valuable technician.

I just do not like the word "consultant" applied to anyone who happens to
work on contract rather than a traditional employer-employee arrangement.

It's as though I, a short dumpy nearsighted balding middle-aged dweeb were
to insist on being classified a "stud" simply because I have one small
physical thing females do not have.

(A 'Y' chromosome. What did you think I meant?)

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T08:04:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y4x58.9831$9y2.1539355@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<a33ooo$lvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com> <9xw58.405$cg1.57369@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:9xw58.405
>
> As I said some posts ago, this is a semantic issue: is one a consultant or
a
> technician?
>

Personally, I am neither.  I am a programmer.  A consultant talks.  A
technician makes the hardware work.  A programmer slides around the
consultant's bullshit, tests the technicians work, makes the software work,
then gets stuck with the maintenance for thirty years.

The only consulting I have ever done consists of answering two questions.
Can it be done?  How much will it cost?  I answer both free, it is called
"quoting a job".

The other stuff is just office-overload staffing propoganda, and haggling
over salaries. Words only have meanings after they are assigned to a
particular variable, in a particular program.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T19:03:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xKG58.13392$9y2.1892590@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<a33ooo$lvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com> <9xw58.405$cg1.57369@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <a367oa$agn$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c572b71_4@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3c572b71_4@Usenet.com...
>
> <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message
news:a367oa$agn$1@panix1.panix.com...
> > In article <9xw58.405$cg1.57369@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>,
> > Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > repeating... I'll stick with Doing My Job and leave being concerned
about
> > titles for the employees.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'll bet he's been called a "systems analyst" more than once.  I have, and I
always flatly deny it. Why?  Because if you are a programmer, then a system
design consists of working code.

Anything specified well enough to work on a computer, I can write a compiler
to compile and run.  If it is not designed to that stage, then the job is
only partially completed.  I have seen all sorts of formal ways of
specifying what a "system" is suppose to do.  Not one of them, with the
exception of heritage systems, specified a task completely enough to work on
a computer.  The heritage systems that did were specified in Cobol, or some
other language that a computer actually understood.  Anything less is
preliminary programming notes.

That is what programming *is*.  Analysing a "system", then specifying what
is to be done to the point where even a computer can understand it.  In
writing.  Anything else is talking a good line, then leaving the job half
done, and expecting a real programmer to finish it.

As for all those "systems analysts", "consultants", etc. who do not/can not
program ... pfooee.  That is like a literary critic that prides themselves
on the fact they are illiterate, or never read books. I'll grant that there
are literary critics that have never *written* a book, but then I never met
a systems analyst that read a program a day instead of writing programs
either.  Maybe when I do, I will re-consider.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T00:50:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C5744B7.F820ED4D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a33ooo$lvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com> <9xw58.405$cg1.57369@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <a367oa$agn$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c572b71_4@Usenet.com> <xKG58.13392$9y2.1892590@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Monsieur Donald <G>,

As you said in your other message you were about to get into 'rant mode' - and
how <G> ! You aren't going to buy into it, and apart from which the title
'Systems Analyst' title has joined the dinosaurs.

But I do sincerely believe there is a difference. Something like the size of a
Stock Market application. You just cannot think of it solely in technical
(computer) terms - the computer is merely a processing device. Apart from the
objectives, what are government and commercial regulations covering the topic.
What are the undocumented actions that are by tradition, commonly used in this
particular commercial environment

While it certainly is an asset to have a programming background, it represents a
wastage of programing proficiency to involve programmers at the early stages,
e.g. sweet talking the end users, when you are faced with a blank wall chart. OK
arrive at a general consensus of the system's total flow - now you need to talk
DB and what each file/table will contain. Got a bit confused about the rules -
slip in Decision Tables.

Now granted, as independents, (I've avoided using  analyst, consultant or
programmer), we fulfill that role of designer-cum-programmer. BUT - given that
Stock Exchange situation above, you would be on a hiding-to-nothing, if you
approached it purely from a programming background. In your case, you'd have to
drop the programming, assuming here you are the team leader, or whatever, and
concentrate on the BROAD design. Coupled with that you'd have to delegate off to
a subordinate, (or several, for an application this size),  to act as DBA.

Sure your programming expertise comes into play as a project leader - but the
bottom line in the above scenario is that you are the Project Boss - an
administrator NOT a programming technician. (Think in terms or
aircraft/auto/ship builders. You can have the best qualified staff bashing
hammers or turning wrenches - but it doesn't come together until somebody has
produced a 'commercial' art work, which in turn is handed over to draughtsmen,
before it ever gets near the factory floor).

And I still say it - not every proficient auto mechanic has the savvy to become
an auto designer. (Before somebody jumps in - not every auto designer makes a
good technician <G>).

Jimmy

Donald Tees wrote:

> I'll bet he's been called a "systems analyst" more than once.  I have, and I
> always flatly deny it. Why?  Because if you are a programmer, then a system
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald
```

represents a
> wastage of programing proficiency to involve programmers at the early
stages,
> e.g. sweet talking the end users, when you are faced with a blank wall
chart. OK
> arrive at a general consensus of the system's total flow - now you need to
talk
> DB and what each file/table will contain. Got a bit confused about the
rules -
> slip in Decision Tables.
>
> Now granted, as independents, (I've avoided using  analyst, consultant or
> programmer), we fulfill that role of designer-cum-programmer. BUT - given
that
> Stock Exchange situation above, you would be on a hiding-to-nothing, if
you
> approached it purely from a programming background. In your case, you'd
have to
> drop the programming, assuming here you are the team leader, or whatever,
and
> concentrate on the BROAD design. Coupled with that you'd have to delegate
off to
> a subordinate, (or several, for an application this size),  to act as DBA.
>
> Sure your programming expertise comes into play as a project leader - but
the
> bottom line in the above scenario is that you are the Project Boss - an
> administrator NOT a programming technician. (Think in terms or
> aircraft/auto/ship builders. You can have the best qualified staff bashing
> hammers or turning wrenches - but it doesn't come together until somebody
has
> produced a 'commercial' art work, which in turn is handed over to
draughtsmen,
> before it ever gets near the factory floor).
>
> And I still say it - not every proficient auto mechanic has the savvy to
become
> an auto designer. (Before somebody jumps in - not every auto designer
makes a
> good technician <G>).
>
> Jimmy

Jimmy, you have juniour engineers and senior engineers.  You do not have
junior engineers that get so good at it that they cease to being engineers.

I have been head of a forty man programming team.  My job title was
vice-president of software.  My profession was programmer.  That fact that
we used a whole bunch of juniors to actually whack out the code is quite
irrelevant.  We told them what to write, and read what they each wrote, and
I worked with the team leaders to edited that into a whole. Sure we analysed
the systems.  We also wrote the systems, and maintained the systems. We were
programmers.  The only non-programmers were writer, artist, secretaries or
clerks.

> And I still say it - not every proficient auto mechanic has the savvy to
become
> an auto designer. (Before somebody jumps in - not every auto designer
makes a
> good technician <G>).

I disagree.  Designers that do not understand the technical end of
production extremely well cannot design the most cost effective product or
method by a long shot.  An automotive engineer that did not understand both
construction of a car, or the mechanics of repairing a car, could not design
a car.  Allowing non-programmers to design computer systems has resulted in
hundreds of fiasco's beyond belief. Thousands(probably millions) have only
worked because programmers ignored the spec, and debugged with the actual
users. So which is more important?  Being a senior enough programmer to be
able to design, or being an designer that doesn't have the skills to program
anymore because they have been too senior to program for at least 15 years?

You have all degrees of engineers, from fresh grads to the president of
general motors.  You have all degrees of programmer, from new grads thru
senior designers to VP's of software.  But if they are not programmers, they
cannot do the job. I'm a f*cking programmer, not a consultant, not an
analyst, not a designer, not any of them.  If they great unwashed masses of
human service technicians cannot understand that, then I am way too
expensive for them anyway.

Donald<having fun ranting>

P.S.  I know you code constantly.  Could you design a good piece of modern
software with none of the programming skills you have gained in the last ten
years, let alone the last thirty?  I think being branded "analyst" is the
first step of being put out to pasture. Screw that.

Donald
```

ten

>
My brother-in-law tells this tale about an engineer.

His grandfather worked in the steel mills as an engineer.  Upon retirement,
he designed and built a house trailer to live in while in retirement. It
included
a bath tub. He and his wife traveled all over the country looking for a
place
to stay.

Meanwhile, a problem arose in the part of the mill he was working in before
retirement, and the mill convinced him to come back to find the problem.

He roamed around for about a day, and recommended that someone use a sledge
to hit a spot he identified.  He submitted his bill for $99999, and when
there were
objections to the price already agreed upon, he told them $99 was the cost
of
coming back and finding the problem, the remainder was knowing where to hit
the machinery. BTW, he lived into his 90's, and married a second time.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T04:59:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C577EF1.DE6EBD03@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a33ooo$lvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com> <9xw58.405$cg1.57369@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <a367oa$agn$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c572b71_4@Usenet.com> <xKG58.13392$9y2.1892590@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C5744B7.F820ED4D@shaw.ca> <50J58.15333$9y2.2018674@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

>
> Jimmy, you have juniour engineers and senior engineers.  You do not have
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

I'm glad you are having fun ranting, and you make a good case which is difficult
to refute, but being of Irish descent we are just as bloody stubborn as you
Scots. I know, I know, we've covered that one before, your ancestors were Yanks
who became Empire Loyalists <G>.

Try this one for size. When I left the RAF I started at the bottom of the
accounting tree, plain simple bean-counter first class. I got the job because I
had been taking correspondence courses for Chartered Institute of Secretaries.
Duration in bean-counting, Sep 61 - Aug 63  I made it to Deputy Accountant
before switching to EDP (SHhhhhh! As an analyst). Pretty quick progression in a
large firm - but no magic formula; then as now I was always asking myself WHY do
we do it this way ....? So apart from the modern Treasury stuff where you wheel
and deal with money as though it was printed by Monopoly, I had a fair handle on
accounting which equipped me for EDP. (As regards the 'WHY', even now when I
think I've got something licked in OO and feel pleased with myself - I still
pause and say, "Could I have done this simpler, more easily, more efficiently
.....?" I don't accept that I have found the right way, believing there is
always room for improvement).

Take a different route. Had my parents been able to afford it, I could have
articled to become a CA, (rather than piss away 12 years in the RAF). That
articling, particularly *now* in the 21st century, would have introduced me to a
gamut of accounting systems in very diverse industries. As either a student or
CA I could start out knowing absolutely zero about day-to-day accounting
methods, but could be a whiz on accounting theory/practice and law. Using my
savvy as an articling student, each time I visited a new outfit on an audit, I
would note new, intriguing approaches as to how book-keeping functioned in
different industries - play it smart and I keep a mental note of these in my
memory box, even taking away sample documentation, where appropriate.

So who sez, had I been a qualified CA, I *should* have started at the bottom,
laboriously whiling my days away with a quill pen doing double entries, hoping
some day to get to the top of the tree.

Now back to the sixties, when DT and PECD were still youngsters, wasn't it great
<G>. I had a clear idea about machine code and Assembler - they were
ABS0-bloomin-LUTELY confusing, but even the intro did give us an insight into
the programming problem, albeit I couldn't write three lines of code. What we
did have, in GREAT detail, supplied by ICL, was a understanding of storage, both
mag tapes, including three tape sorts,  and in our case the RCA MCF(Magnetic
Card File) Unit, with a comprehension of how indexed records and indices worked,
how files could be blocked on a combination of 'bucket' sizes, and how 'tags'
worked when the index buckets overflowed. This knowledge was an absolute MINIMUM
that we required in order to use paper/mag-tape and the indexed cards for our
applications.
Note that we hadn't the slightest comprehension of how programmers actually
coded to interrogate the MCF unit.

It follows that if DBs had been around then, then part of our training would
have been in usage of DBs.

Now when it comes to 'designers' I guess one of your main beefs would be the
airy-fairy way in which 'analysts' communicated the problem to you in the early
days.
(I can understand that happening then, just as I'm quite sure it still happens
to-day).
That's an area I spent a lot of time at. Few books around, but I got my hands on
an excellent big hefty tome from the US (no idea what the title was). I spent
weeks combing this book for clues on how to present my design specification to a
programmer. I no longer have a copy, but from memory my text went something like
this :-

- Intro - describing the problem (THIS program) and where it fitted into the
whole, prior and succeeding programs
- A block diagram showing inputs and outputs
- Record layouts for input and output - currently our pic x's or pic 9's
- Layouts if there were Error Lists or Reports
- Procedures - step-by-step from beginning to end, with a list of validity
checks and error message to be generated.

Now I'll definitely concede one thing to you - those validity checks above. As
an analyst I did my damnedest to list all known errors; however as we know as
programmers, when you start coding there are errors which the analyst wont pick
up on.

My standard document, (program Specification) worked then, and many copied the
format. Certainly the ICL sales boys, tech people never offered a better ICL
alternative. Forgive me for preening a little, but I reckon my then Program Spec
would stand the test of time and be just as valid to-day.

Keep ranting - "Lay on McDuff" <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T11:53:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zxV58.33332$f53.2197277@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<a33ooo$lvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com> <9xw58.405$cg1.57369@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <a367oa$agn$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c572b71_4@Usenet.com> <xKG58.13392$9y2.1892590@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C5744B7.F820ED4D@shaw.ca> <50J58.15333$9y2.2018674@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C577EF1.DE6EBD03@shaw.ca>`

```
"J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C577EF1.DE6EBD03@shaw.ca...
>
> I'm glad you are having fun ranting, and you make a good case which is
difficult
> to refute, but being of Irish descent we are just as bloody stubborn as
you
> Scots. I know, I know, we've covered that one before, your ancestors were
Yanks
> who became Empire Loyalists <G>.

Jimmy, my mom was born and raised in the Curraugh, county Kildare.  She was
a war bride, and is as Irish as a shamrock.  In fact, when I visit, I still
catch myself speaking with an Irish lilt after a few hours. <G>.

I am not about to deny that there are all sort of people working in all
sorts of capacities in the IT industries.  Lots of them are not programmers,
and are quite good at what they do.

What I am saying is that you cannot design a good computer system without
the ability and knowledge to program it.  I'll also say that you cannot
design a good computer system without extensive knowledge about the
bussiness/application that you want to design a system arround. Both are
required.

When no one person has both sets of knowledge, you have to combine fields of
expertise, and work as a team of specialist to get the job done. The problem
arises is when the programer is taken out of the equation.

All too often, instead of a team that includes a programmer, the design team
is done on the cheap.

The industry, and the pimps, go out and sell the idea that there is this
thing called an "analyst" that cannot program, but is out of work, and
available because they have been rendered obsolete, but "they really know
the bussiness well", and that they can design the system without a clue in
the world about what works on a computer and what does not.  Said person
starts by gathering the advertising, and deciding on a data base and
machine. They then list ever neat term they can find and do not understand,
and write it all down.  The programer is handed a 200 page PR/motherhood
statement that is far closer to advertizing than a spec, and told to code
it.

I have government requests for quotes that are obviously designed to
discourage quotations.  They have been set up on the buddy system to sell a
particular package that some manager wants, and the entire specification,
request for quotation, and everything associated with it is no more than an
elaborate smokescreen.

In my experience, specifications of the above type *never* come from someone
that claims to be a programmer.  Most programmers are too professional to
play those games.  They would be ashamed to play those games.  I've seen
hundreds from "consultants" and accounting firms though. The bottom line is
that if I am looking for decent work to be done, I go to a programming firm,
not a consulting firm or an accounting firm. Secondly, I would never hire a
person that said something like "I am not programmer, I am a systems
designer", anymore than I would deal with someone that said "I am not
qualified as a doctor, I am a brain surgeon".

In the former case, I would assume that they want a lot of money for a sales
call, and at the end I will have a nice peice of paper that does nothing.
In the second case I would assume I was interviewing a has-been or a
bullshit artist.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T19:41:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C584DAE.C4851B5F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a33ooo$lvo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20020128220926.26833.00000903@mb-mh.aol.com> <9xw58.405$cg1.57369@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <a367oa$agn$1@panix1.panix.com> <3c572b71_4@Usenet.com> <xKG58.13392$9y2.1892590@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C5744B7.F820ED4D@shaw.ca> <50J58.15333$9y2.2018674@news20.bellglobal.com> <3C577EF1.DE6EBD03@shaw.ca> <zxV58.33332$f53.2197277@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Dear Ranter <G>,

Now we are getting closer to the hub with your latest barrage. But let me first
answer your Postscript. I had to think on it before trying to give an honest
answer :-

> Donald<having fun ranting>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Given background in data processing, the overall answer would have to be 'Yes',
with qualification.

While not necessarily having the ability to code GUIs, an "analyst" who was
acquainted with the general principles of Windowing and the application of the
various controls, could specify what he wanted a Dialog to look like. (Given the
tools, no reason why he shouldn't actually design the Dialogs). Don't know how
it is with Fujitsu on-line, but it would be a hellava challenge with M/F
on-line. Perhaps SP2 and Screen IO on-line might be more helpful.

COBOL file system - that really is no big deal, (the concepts of relative,
indexing etc.). Like any design system, it is dependent upon the "designer's"
ability to see the necessary linkage between file layouts.  Given that the COBOL
file system is replaced by (R)DBMS - then without being language specific, it is
possible to grasp the concepts. To my mind, slightly more difficult where Pete
mentions Normalization and 3NF (is that correct ?). The latter even causes me a
problem in coding ESQL. I can see what it is trying(does) do, but for the life
of me, how do I lay out my ESQL code to take advantage of table linking ?

Now here's one area where an "analyst" would be absolutely screwed - OO. If he
sits down and tries to read examples, say from C++, then he might just as well
be a programmer ! COBOL - problem here, is that there aren't as yet, any wealth
of examples. Will's and Ed's books are excellent, but attack the initial stage
of 'concepts', although as I've previously said, Ed's book, as an appendix, does
offer a simplified application. Until such time as there are examples of 'OO
Units', (some might prefer the word 'Libraries'), the "analyst" isn't going to
make it happen. Still, the same applies with programmers - with a lack of
working code, each programmer is virtually starting from scratch. A good example
in this area is Collections/Dictionaries. As and when J4 make up their minds,
either J4, (and I hope it *will be* J4), or a third party, must provide coded
examples. (I don't think at this stage you are too turned on about
collections/dictionaries, but believe me, they are one hell of an asset to the
language).
--------------------

So now let's move on to your latest 'tirade' <G>

Donald Tees wrote:

> "J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3C577EF1.DE6EBD03@shaw.ca...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'll repeat. At the initial stages it is still a waste of  programming
expertise, to involve programmers, while the application is still at the
'committee' stage. It serves no useful purpose to involve programmers in the
haggling. When things get concrete, and you talk about introducing COBOL files,
a DB, perhaps using UPC codes or a document reader - Yes - then they are very
much into the action. (I'm not saying exclude programmers, your 'team leaders'
may well have a programming background - and it would be excellent if they
have).

>
> When no one person has both sets of knowledge, you have to combine fields of
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Right, now your paragraphs above make the real point, you are royally PISSED off
with CON-sultants, prostitutes who represent themselves as experts. They are
nothing more than the snake oil salesmen of the old Wild West. I'm with you
100%.

Now back to those Unigate systems in '63.

First our Chief Programmer, I think he had a Masters in Mathematics, nice guy,
somewhat aloof and not easily approachable I mention him deliberately <G>.
The applications, in order of introduction :-

- # 1 - Milk Collection (Unigate Creameries) - manual system computerised. The
MMB(UK Milk Marketing Board) designated that certain manufacturers, Unigate,
Express Dairies, Co-Op etc. could collect milk from specified dairy farmers
across UK. One ex-accountant as the analyst. Print route sheets for each day of
the week, and the truckers would collect various sorts of milk from different
farms, in route order. Truckers return to dairy where milk churns are
categorised by butterfat content and go on a weighing scale, not dissimilar to
your Inco system. Keypunch this (paper tape), and once a month (week ?), send a
detailed list to the MMB along with a cheque for the milk we had collected. MMB
then pays the farmer his cut, based on this report. Nothing too complex about
this.

- #2 - Advanced and Van Selling (Unigate Diary Sales) - three of us as analysts.
A hellava lot of haggling up front, because we had to work through the Secretary
and the Managing Director to get the sales boys to accept a number of rules
before we could get off the ground. Sure we had minor problems, but it worked
out quite well.

- #3 - Cream Sales (Unigate Creameries, again). This was fast delivery of
perishables, cream and yogurt. Didn't go through Unigate Dairy Sales, being
delivered direct to retailers from manufacturing points. My mind is an absolute
blank on this one, but I think it eventually merged into # 2 above.

As you can see #3 stuck out a bit like a sore thumb, so the Management
Consultants hired a two-team software house  to handle #3  when we were about
half way through designing #2. Two extremely nice and COMPETENT people. They
needed much of what we had in #2 - like the Product Catalogue and the Customer
File. We had well defined our requirements for these features, and these two
guys, wanting to get up and running, suggested they should code both the
Catalogue and Customer files. We jumped with enthusiasm at the suggestion !

I gotta rub it in <G>. One of those systems failed miserably. Which one ? #1.
And the reason - the Chief Programmer was the backup support to the analyst. For
whatever obscure reason, the CP INSISTED that paper tape should be read in,  in
EXACTLY the same sequence that Collection Sheets had been printed. So process
some 10 reels of paper tape, get to tape # 8 and the system goes belly-up ! Take
the offending tapes, cut them and re-splice them, and resubmit to the Edit
program. The application lasted less than six months <G>

Jimmy
```

on
> a computer.  The heritage systems that did were specified in Cobol, or
some
> other language that a computer actually understood.  Anything less is
> preliminary programming notes.

there
> are literary critics that have never *written* a book, but then I never
met
> a systems analyst that read a program a day instead of writing programs
> either.  Maybe when I do, I will re-consider.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:<tYy48.54$mR3.134508@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>...
> Ron <NoSoy@swbell.net> wrote in message
> news:a2sp4j$ofl$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
><snip>

Those of us who are professionals got that way because we remember all
that arcana.  We don't usually set out to learn things like sign
nibble values, but when we have an experience where the knowledge is
required, we tend to remember it.  Similarly, there are people who
will tell you they can find it in the manual, but who wouldn't have
even thought of it unless you brought it to their attention.  A
familiarity with the obscure ("arcana") is what makes a good trouble
shooter/bug-killer/fireman.

Here's one for you:
The value of FIELD-A is 845.
Procedure Division code:
     IF FIELD-A IS LESS THAN 9
          PERFORM OPTION-1
     ELSE 
          PERFORM OPTION-2.

OPTION-1 alwyas gets performed. Why?

Stephen J Spiro
```
