```
"ThatGuy" <thatguy@thatguy.net> wrote in message news:<lIJ48.3$iw.5143@typhoon.jacksonville.mediaone.net>...
> My guess is the fields are alpha, which would result in a left-justified
> comparison of bytes.
> 
<snip> > > > Here's one for you:
> > > The value of FIELD-A is 845.
> > > Procedure Division code:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

This is exactly correct.  This was real-world code on a system with
which I once worked...  ALL of the valid values for FIELD-A were
numeric, but this field was defined as PIC XXX.

*sigh*
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T11:33:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ouo75u05cobdv7tn2s14ag8s3i46o68k30@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2sl4g$ba1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <a2sp4j$ofl$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com> <tYy48.54$mR3.134508@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <928495c6.0201261434.1be81112@posting.google.com>`

```
Skip..
>
>Here's one for you:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>OPTION-1 alwyas gets performed. Why?
Maybe the field is defined
as a PIC X(3)
or a PIC V999
or a PIC 9.
or as a Group

Or (and this is can happen in AS/400 COBOL/400 depending on the
compilation options)
FIELD-A PIC 999 value "845". --> note the quotes.

This may result in the compiler detection a error on a particular
error level group, and it will issue a WARNING on the listing saying
that this value has been converted to ZERO.


If there are more please let's hear of them as they can be
interesting.

Either of them would yield the first IF option.

Frederico Fonseca
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT Professionalism. was "Re: More numeric values".

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T02:45:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c540581_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2sl4g$ba1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <a2sp4j$ofl$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com> <tYy48.54$mR3.134508@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <928495c6.0201261434.1be81112@posting.google.com>`

```

Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@mail.com> wrote in message
news:928495c6.0201261434.1be81112@posting.google.com...
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:<tYy48.54$mR3.134508@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>...
>
> Those of us who are professionals got that way because we remember all
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This sounds like pompous dribble, Stephen.

Not only that, but it isn't true.

I am a professional, have been for (nearly) all my working life, and I share
Michael's dislike for anal retentive idiots who believe that the more
obscure trivia they memorize, the more potent they are.

I learned long ago that every time I attempted to memorize the syntax of the
COBOL TRANSFORM verb, I forgot a VSAM reply code... Fortunately, IBM came to
the rescue by dropping the TRANSFORM verb from the language and I never used
it much anyway.

They did the same thing with EXAMINE... there it was doing its job quite
nicely but it didn't sound important enough...ANYONE can do Examinations,
but to be an INSPECTOR, now there's a job with power...(Not only that but
Inspectors are "official" and they get to wear peaked caps...) couple it
with some really intricate syntax that only the nerds would WANT to memorize
and it is no wonder the INSPECT verb is such a success...

Now, ALTER was really brilliant because it had such a simple syntax and was
so blazingly efficient...(Why  would you set a "first time thru" flag then
test it again on every subsequent iteration, when you could simply ALTER the
start paragraph to go somewhere else after the first time thru...? I never
understood what all the fuss was about over ALTER and I was sorry to see it
go... See, that's a case in point: nice simple syntax and really useful and
efficient verb like ALTER and they go and drop it... But  it was too simple
for all the "Professionals" who need complexity to make themselves feel
important...

What I am gently suggesting here (and only some of the above is not
serious... I'll leave you to figure which bits are which...) is that
complexity for its own sake is no guarantee of professionalism.

Neither are feats of memory a guarantee either. In my youth I had an almost
photographic memory  which enabled me to sail through exams by simply
visualizing the pages of the textbook I needed. Today it is not so
effective...(what was I on about...? Oh, yes...) the point is that I am more
"professional" today than I was then.

> Similarly, there are people who
> will tell you they can find it in the manual, but who wouldn't have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Nope. Removing the  "obscure ("arcana") " completely and bringing everything
to light, is what makes a Professional. Converting the complex arcana into
simple everyday terms so people can deal with it is professional in my book.

For example, do I need to know the hex codes in PFS which enable the
mainframe to print graphics (Yes folks, mainframes really can put signatures
on letters...<G>) or do I simply need to know that the facility exists, so
when the fortress COBOL/PL/1 people say it can't be done, I can just say,
"Here's a manual on PFS. Read the section on Hex Control codes for the
buffer to accommodate graphic files. If you get stuck, come and see me and
I'll help you code it...".

Or do I need to know every single EPSON control code so I can put Swahili
Superscripts into my Doctoral Thesis?

I think not.

> Here's one for you:

Oh Goody! I like puzzles...

> The value of FIELD-A is 845.
> Procedure Division code:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I reckon it is because FIELD-A is defined as CLASS ALPHANUMERIC or "pic xxx"
to the professionals...

This causes the literal to be padded with blanks and compared from left to
right (even though it isn't coded as an alphanumeric literal... see my
discussion on CLASSES elsewhere in this NG). As '8' is less than '9'
OPTION-1 will always be performed.

Reciprocating....

Two trains are coming towards each other at 100 MPH. One is coming from the
East and the Other (obviously) is coming from the West. They are
accidentally running on the same track. This is occurring in the same time
frame (same time on the same day...Respondents who wish to consider the
Relativistic effects should code their calculations on a separate piece of
paper and place it carefully in the nearest trash can...).

When they are exactly 500 yards apart, the Engineers realise the problem and
apply the brakes on BOTH trains.

Sadly, the brakes fail on both trains.

Is there a crash? If you answer YES or NO (and these are the only acceptable
answers; wishy washy "MAYBES" will receive zero marks...) you must elucidate
your reasoning. This is to test how good you are at uncovering the "obscure
arcana" and delivering a solution that is undoubtedly professional.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
I agree it is good to have a general idea of what can be done and looking
up the details is fine with me, as we can't all remember everthing in in
all of its gory detail.

One guys obscure arcana is another guys essential fact.   I am thinking
especially of signs in packed decimal/comp-3 fields.  For anyone who has
experience with coding in assembler language this kind of thing soon
becomes hard to forget!
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T21:47:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0201312147.3e5c698d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2sl4g$ba1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <a2sp4j$ofl$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com> <tYy48.54$mR3.134508@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <928495c6.0201261434.1be81112@posting.google.com> <3c540581_7@Usenet.com> <3c595214_2@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3c595214_2@Usenet.com>...
> Well, Stephen, given that I played YOUR game and answered YOUR question, I
> thought you might've at least attempted my problem with the trains...<G>

I'll tell ya, Pete, I thought seriously about the question, and YES
seemed the only reasonable answer.  Therefore, I figgered there was
some really weird physical/mathematical/logical answer and decided to
wait to see what got posted.   I will confess I had not thought of a
punny solution, but I did grin when I saw it.

It DID remind me of an old Texas folk song, which is short enough for
me to quote in it's entirety:
"There's a train coming down the track at eighty miles an hour!
 There's another train coming down the same track in the opposite
direction at ninety miles an hour!
 That's a hell of a way to run a Rail Road!"

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T15:03:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a33p15$m3b$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2sl4g$ba1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <a2sp4j$ofl$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com> <tYy48.54$mR3.134508@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <928495c6.0201261434.1be81112@posting.google.com>`

```

On 26-Jan-2002, stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro) wrote:

> Here's one for you:
> The value of FIELD-A is 845.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> OPTION-1 always gets performed. Why?

A couple of different possibilities come to mind - which will be answered by
looking at the definition of FIELD-A, and possibly the method of placing the
value of 845 into it.

I can even think of reasons that are not the obvious - for instance it is
possible with subscript overflow to create such a result.
```

#### ↳ Re: More numeric values.

- **From:** "Tom Hasselbach" <thasselb@usol.com>

```
I think it will be non-numeric and do perform B.

If you defined INFIELD as PIC S9(5)V99, then I'm not sure if it would be
numeric or not.  I can't remember if "C" if a valid sign. I think it is, but
I'm not sure.

news:a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com...

>   and is READ INTO working-storage where this
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **Date:** 2002-01-26T09:10:48+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2toah$13u3to$1@news.hansenet.net>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`

```

"Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com> schrieb im Newsbeitrag
news:a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com...
> If a data record contains  x'0123456C'
>   and is READ INTO working-storage where this
>   field is defined in a record as
>
>  05  INFIELD  PIC 9(5)V99  COMP-3.

I remember that I did something like
COMPUTE INFIELD = INFIELD * 1
once before testing for NUMERIC. I believe that helped...

Alex.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T13:35:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<797D355CE99D765C.E80CEDF900F66E51.A5F135C0220384FB@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2toah$13u3to$1@news.hansenet.net>`

```
On Sat, 26 Jan 2002 09:10:48 +0100, "Alexander Meins"
<usenet.am0001@blue-seal.com> enlightened us:

>
>"Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com> schrieb im Newsbeitrag
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I believe that would cause you a SOC7 (or data exception in VSE) if
INFIELD is not numeric.  If it is numeric, multiplying it by 1 does
not change the sign (hex C).

Regards,

         (o o)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T08:26:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C53B9C7.3EDBCEF8@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <a2toah$13u3to$1@news.hansenet.net>`

```
Alexander Meins wrote:
> 
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> once before testing for NUMERIC. I believe that helped...

It seems to me that the whole point of doing an IF NUMERIC is to avoid
the program ABENDing on an 'Invalid numeric field'.  In order to achive
this it is necessary to do the test _before_ doing any arithmetic
operations, or even a move.

If your code gets as far as the IF NUMERIC then it is redundant.
```

#### ↳ Re: More numeric values.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T14:52:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C52C2A4.50C41DA9@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`

```
Ron wrote:
> 
>  05  INFIELD  PIC 9(5)V99  COMP-3.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     PERFORM B.

For a conforming ANS-85 implementation the answer should be 'B'
regardless of what the sign nibble value is, unless it accidentally has
a byte valid that is equivalent to 4 decimal digits.

      "Indentifier-1 must refence a data item whose usage is
       explicitly or implicitly DISPLAY."

NUMERIC tests on COMP~ or PACKED-DECIMAL are extensions provided by
various implementations and the actual results will depend on how it has
been implemented.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T20:21:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2t3s6$r5j$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <3C52C2A4.50C41DA9@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Actually, Richard ...
  From the very rule that you mention, the answer (for an '85 Standard
compiler) is NOT that "perform b" will occur, but that AT COMPILE-TIME, a
"message" (either syntax error, or extension) will be issued.

Support is added in the draft Standard for such syntax.
```

```
to check a PIC 9 COMP-3 for numeric?  Seems to me like you can check
anything for numeric in COBOL.  And I know that compile doesn't know what
kind of data you plan on reading?  Can you elaborate on why you would get a
message at compile time?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-25T21:26:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2t7va$ffq$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <3C52C2A4.50C41DA9@Azonic.co.nz> <a2t3s6$r5j$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <u5483qqh3q1t2f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Richard has given the exact rule from the '85 Standard,

"Identifier-1 must references a data item whose usage is explicitly or
implicitly DISPLAY."

I know that there are vendors that already have extensions "relaxing" this
rule - and that the next Standard will ALSO remove this rule, but it IS the
current rule in the ANSI/ISO Standard.  (Obviously, COMP-3 is already an
extension, so the actual "sample" would always be non-conforming.  However,
even if you changed COMP-3 to Packed-Decimal, the IF NUMERIC test would be
non-conforming).

FYI, (and I have raised this issue before), in the draft Standard, there is
support for IF NUMERIC on non-DISPLAY items, but I think that MANY people
will be "surprised" by how it works in some cases.

Assume

  01  Group-1.
        05   NumBinary Pic S(04)  Binary.
   ...
 Move all X"FF" to Group-1
 If NumBinary Numeric
     Display "not according to the draft Standard"
 Else
    Display "All X'f's does not match the PICTURE, so this will be the path
taken"
End-If

  ***

It has been "argued" that *all* values are "numeric" in a Binary item.
However, the draft Standard *explicitly* says that it is ONLY valid numeric
if the contents match the explicit Picture clause.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More numeric values.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T19:24:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c525063_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a2scu9$qjp$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com> <3C52C2A4.50C41DA9@Azonic.co.nz> <a2t3s6$r5j$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <u5483qqh3q1t2f@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Tom Hasselbach <thasselb@usol.com> wrote in message
news:u5483qqh3q1t2f@corp.supernews.com...
> I don't understand why you would get a compile error.  Are you not allowed
> to check a PIC 9 COMP-3 for numeric?  Seems to me like you can check
> anything for numeric in COBOL.  And I know that compile doesn't know what
> kind of data you plan on reading?  Can you elaborate on why you would get
a
> message at compile time?
>
I believe I can...<G>

The IF NUMERIC test is part of what's called a CLASS test (NOT "Class" in
the OO sense; this was formulated 40 years ago when there was no such thing
as Object Orientation...)

Before PICTURE was available, the CLASS of data was very important and we
used to describe data by its CLASS, SIZE, and USAGE (SIZE 5, CLASS NUMERIC,
USAGE DISPLAY would be pic 9(5), for example.)

Data could be one of 3 CLASSES:

1. NUMERIC.
2. ALPHABETIC
3. ALPHANUMERIC

Even after PICTURE was admitted (sometime in the early 60s if I remember
correctly) the CLASS tests were retained because it was sometimes useful to
see if something in a pic x string was actually NUMERIC, or ALPHABETIC, or
ALPHANUMERIC.

However, as the fancy new PICTURE clause already implied certain classes,
there was no point in applying a CLASS test to something you already knew
the class of.

F'rinstance, if you have something defined as pic 999, it MUST be NUMERIC
because the NUMERIC Class is implicit by the character 9 in the picture. Any
attempt to store a non-numeric character here will result in a compiler
diagnostic...
Because the compiler knows the CLASS of the field from your Data Division
entry, it can check what you are moving to it and rap you over the knuckles
of you take liberties...

The early standards therefore said that CLASS tests could ONLY be applied to
data held in ALPHANUMERIC strings... So you could check if something that
was technically ALPHANUMERIC (could hold literally ANYTHING) was in fact one
of the 3 CLASSES mentioned above.

It is the same logic as if you defined something like this...

01 alpha-field  pic A(12).

It is nonsense to code:    if alpha-field is NUMERIC.... it cannot be. It
has been defined to hold letters of the Alphabet and space. (It's CLASS is
ALPHABETIC). Similarly, it would be nonsense to code: if alpha-field is
ALPHANUMERIC... for the same reasons.

The same logic applies to coding: if NUMERIC.. for a field which is defined
with 9s in the picture and is therefore implicitly of CLASS NUMERIC... It
HAS to be NUMERIC...

Now of course, in reality, there is a "hole" in this reasoning in that
something which is NOT NUMERIC COULD be moved to a CLASS NUMERIC field if it
is done at the group level... or read in as part of a record moved at the
group level.

Non-IBM mainframes would simply process whatever was in the field and give
wrong results (it was common practice to make ALL input fields ALPHANUMERIC
and check them with the CLASS test before moving the numeric fields to
numeric pictures so they could be used in arithmetic...); IBM gave the
infamous S0c7 and dumped. As dumps are fairly tiresome, there was a lot of
grizzling and the IBM implementation was Extended to allow people to check
even non-ALPHANUMERIC fields for being NUMERIC. So, you can be forgiven for
believing that ANYTHING in COBOL can be checked for NUMERIC.(Especially if
you have only been exposed to IBM COBOL...)

But realize that this is a VENDOR EXTENSION to make life easier on a
particular platform. It is NOT "Standard" (whatever the Hell that is these
days..<G>) COBOL.

Whether we agree or not with the above reasoning, in the light of current
practice, that's how we got to where we are...

Pete.


> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
> news:a2t3s6$r5j$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...
> > Actually, Richard ...
> >   From the very rule that you mention, the answer (for an '85 Standard
> > compiler) is NOT that "perform b" will occur, but that AT COMPILE-TIME,
a
> > "message" (either syntax error, or extension) will be issued.
> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > regardless of what the sign nibble value is, unless it accidentally
has
> > > a byte valid that is equivalent to 4 decimal digits.
> > >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > various implementations and the actual results will depend on how it
has
> > > been implemented.
> >

>
```

```
This was the characteristic of CoBOL that bothered me the most.   With
display fields, a simple redefines handled the problem, but with packed and
binary data we were hosed back then.
```

```
Young kids - overcomplicate things

make the field a table - make another field just like it -
read the first field going backward on the first subscript until you get a
non blank - load the other field with the non blank characters.  But you
should know how to handle tables - which I find the new breed does not -
they only like GUI.......
```

```
... but what if it comes as file-input?

FD  A7102285-CUST-MAST-D600-FILE
    ORGANIZATION      IS  SEQUENTIAL
    ACCESS MODE       IS  SEQUENTIAL
    RECORD CONTAINS  600  CHARACTERS
    BLOCK  CONTAINS    0  RECORDS
    DATA RECORD       IS  A7102285-CUST-MAST-D600-REC.
01  A7102285-CUST-MAST-D600-REC.
    05  A7102285-CUST-MAST-D600-KEY.
        10  A7102285-CUST-MAST-D600-FOO      PIC  X(015).
        10  A7102285-CUST-MAST-D600-FILE-REDEF01
             REDEFINES A7102285-CUST-MAST-D600-FOO.
            15  A7102285-CUST-MAST-D600-BAR  PIC  9(015).
ETC... errrr, etc.

(now, folks, just for a moment... think of the response of a programmer 
used to dealing with lower-case source code who is exposed to this kind of 
thing; it might help explain why an initial, aesthetic response is 'Ugh!  
Yuck!  Nasty ol' archaic, blocky stuff!')

DD
```

```
> I have seen a lot of Programming Languages (and teach said course on a
> semi-annual basis), but "foo", "bar", etc, still mean nothing to me -
> especially when I coach my students to use meaningful variable names.
> [:)].

"foobar" is a sanitized rendition of "FUBAR," a venerable military
expression standing for "Fucked Up Beyond All Recognition."

As such, "foobar" is entirely appropriate for many data processing
applications.
```

```
Mark Dodel wrote:

<snip>

> And how could I forget Bird-in-Hand, PA as well.  People travel from
> long distances to mail letters to themselves from these towns just to
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

Seeing that quote from FDR - seems it's about time there was an update to
legislation, not just in US, but Canada, Europe etc.. Did any of you watch PBS
Frontline 'Dot CON' last week ? OK the Enro disaster is a biggie, but just as bad,
if not worse, are the bunch of crooks manipulating shares. Basic story :-

- A & B decide to start up a dot.com flogging anything from fluorescent condoms to
cruise liners.
- Middlemen jump in and offer to put A & B in touch with bankers.
- Sure bankers are interested, they make SEVEN PERCENT COMMISSION on share
transactions
- with a guarantee of money AandB.com sets up business, with a share offering BEFORE
they have actually produced anything.
- Knowing the game,  IPOs (forgotten what it stands for) are 'advised' by their
buddies in banking that the shares are coming up for sale. The IPOs represent a
restricted and preferred list of buyers - but you and I don't get a look-in at this
stage.
- The IPOs commit to the shares, say for $9 each and 'flip them'  in SOME 90 MINUTES
for $100 bucks each.
- Then we dopey buggers, seeing the shares rising, plonk out our money at $100 and
*might* see them go to $130.
- Poohf ! Somebody realizes AandB.com is just a mirage, no product, no profit -
AandB.com go belly up just like the other dot.coms did. (Our share holdings look
really enticing at this stage !).

Meanwhile, back at the ranch, the former chairman of the SEC who had already sent
out a dire warning about Enron type fiascoes, ALSO STRESSED some while back, that
the IPO involvement in share offerings was highly questionable. Nobody listened,
they are all making money out of the racket.

To paraphrase Regan, "Business requires less government involvement........." Ain't
democracy great ? (I suppose that's another way of saying greed is a virtue).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: PA Dutch town names [Was: "foo"]

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T00:25:25+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c56877e_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C53422E.50BB5E31@shaw.ca> <20020126234606.26837.00000527@mb-mh.aol.com> <yRNWO1cJ1gUw-pn2-mM3loknW9rb0@localhost> <yRNWO1cJ1gUw-pn2-COBxCPodh0d9@localhost> <3C561940.9BC24B68@shaw.ca>`

```
Comments interspersed.

Pete.

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C561940.9BC24B68@shaw.ca...
> > Mark Dodel wrote:
> >
>
> Seeing that quote from FDR - seems it's about time there was an update to
> legislation, not just in US, but Canada, Europe etc.. Did any of you watch
PBS
> Frontline 'Dot CON' last week ? OK the Enro disaster is a biggie, but just
as bad,
> if not worse, are the bunch of crooks manipulating shares. Basic story :-
>
> - A & B decide to start up a dot.com flogging anything from fluorescent
condoms to
> cruise liners.
> - Middlemen jump in and offer to put A & B in touch with bankers.
> - Sure bankers are interested, they make SEVEN PERCENT COMMISSION on share
> transactions
> - with a guarantee of money AandB.com sets up business, with a share
offering BEFORE
> they have actually produced anything.

With you up to this point, but then it goes a bit awry...

> - Knowing the game,  IPOs (forgotten what it stands for) are 'advised' by
their
> buddies in banking that the shares are coming up for sale. The IPOs
represent a
> restricted and preferred list of buyers - but you and I don't get a
look-in at this
> stage.

This bit is just plain wrong, Jimmy. (at least it is in the UK and here in
New Zealand.)

IPO stands for "Initial Public Offering". ANYBODY, including you and me (I
have been in on several such...) can participate in an IPO. It is PUBLIC
(That's what the "P" stands for...). If you have a Share Broker or
Investment Adviser or Financial Consultant, he will almost certainly advise
you of Companies about to launch and offer you shares at the IPO price. Now,
there ARE cons that may be attached to IPOs (like certain Brokers getting
huge commissions for moving large blocks of these shares to their
clients...) but this is illegal and is treated like "insider dealing" in the
countries I mentioned. (I don't know what the US position is, but I would
expect it to be VERY similar. The Stock Market cannot function properly
UNLESS it is seen to be fair. There are violations but that is why we have
an SEC.)


> - The IPOs commit to the shares, say for $9 each and 'flip them'  in SOME
90 MINUTES
> for $100 bucks each.

There are often restrictions preventing the trading of IPO shares within a
given period, but not always, and, if a "feeding frenzy" develops, then the
prices can become ridiculous. But if you are a thinking adult you would be
unlikely to be persuaded by a commodity that has just had its value rise by
over1000%, especially when the underlying asset is so thin (particularly
thinking of fluorescent condoms here...<G>).

> - Then we dopey buggers, seeing the shares rising, plonk out our money at
$100 and
> *might* see them go to $130.

The fault here is not with the Market but with the greed of individuals.
Anyone who is a serious investor in Stocks and Shares will NOT do this. The
underlying value of the assets is always paramount. So NTA and P/E ratios
are the prime consideration before parting with the readies. I have managed
my own investments for the past 20 years, after having a significant chunk
taken out of my life savings by so called "professionals" who couldn't see a
good investment if it leapt up and bit them on the arse. I have never been
burned with the failure of the Dot COMs or with IPOs. I DID lose some cash
in 1987, when I simply couldn't get a "stop loss" sell order executed, due
to being in Holland on my boat and all lines to the LSE busy... But I
learned from  it and within two years had recovered the cash. (Which I then
sank into my ex-wife, a very bad investment which I totally failed to
see...<G>)

> - Poohf ! Somebody realizes AandB.com is just a mirage, no product, no
profit -
> AandB.com go belly up just like the other dot.coms did. (Our share
holdings look
> really enticing at this stage !).
>
> Meanwhile, back at the ranch, the former chairman of the SEC who had
already sent
> out a dire warning about Enron type fiascoes, ALSO STRESSED some while
back, that
> the IPO involvement in share offerings was highly questionable. Nobody
listened,
> they are all making money out of the racket.
>

This is pretty accurate and certainly a lot of people make money out of IPOs
(I have been one...) Whether it qualifies as a "racket" is questionable.
Like I said before, these are PUBLIC offerings and ANYONE can participate.

> To paraphrase Regan, "Business requires less government
involvement........." Ain't
> democracy great ? (I suppose that's another way of saying greed is a
virtue).
>

Well the image of Michael Douglas in Wall Street is not necessarily accurate
("Greed is Good").

Greed is actually a very good way to lose your assets. People driven by
greed are not thinking straight, and in investing, as in most endeavours,
crooked thinking can be very expensive.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3C56F283.E72A2984@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3C53422E.50BB5E31@shaw.ca> <20020126234606.26837.00000527@mb-mh.aol.com> <yRNWO1cJ1gUw-pn2-mM3loknW9rb0@localhost> <yRNWO1cJ1gUw-pn2-COBxCPodh0d9@localhost> <3C561940.9BC24B68@shaw.ca> <3c56877e_7@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Comments interspersed.

Pete,

Before I respond, let me interject with a salutary lesson I learned a long time
ago. Back when I was finishing in the RAF (situated in that same Hillingdon
House that Keith Park had his office in during WWII),  I had an adjutant who
used to dabble modestly in stocks and shares. His comment, "Don't dabble in
stocks and shares unless you can afford to loose the money". I listened, besides
which I'd be hard put to think when I had spare cash to dabble <G>.

>
> > - Knowing the game,  IPOs (forgotten what it stands for) are 'advised' by
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Can't disagree with what you say, but the significant thing is that a former SEC
chairman, (now retired from business ?), feeling he is no longer under a
constraint to 'play the game',  has spoken out and criticised what is going on
in the US.  The rules undoubtedly are as you outline them, but they are being
circumnavigated.

>
> > - The IPOs commit to the shares, say for $9 each and 'flip them'  in SOME
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not knowing the specifics, it certainly appears that the insiders were able to
bend the rule on this one.

>
> > - Then we dopey buggers, seeing the shares rising, plonk out our money at
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm glad you gave the reference for that. I was on the edge of being shocked by
Jerry's response <G>

>
> Greed is actually a very good way to lose your assets. People driven by
> greed are not thinking straight, and in investing, as in most endeavours,
> crooked thinking can be very expensive.

Agreed, and a theme which Michael reiterated. There is always RISK in business.
'English' Canada exists because of such a risk, when Charles II granted a
charter to the Hudson Bay Company back in the 1650s(?). Mind you, they would
have been bloody silly not to have made a buck out of collecting
bear/fox/beaver  pelts etc. (Now we just know it as 'The Bay' department store
group).

On the theme of greed, sure there are many in the game hoping to make a quick
easy buck. Many were naive to think they could do it with the dot coms., but at
the time 'tech stocks' were the darling of the Stock Exchanges. A year or so
ago, Time Magazine did an article on the California yuppies, complete with
Porsche, Mercedes Benz etc., who played the game, even outstripping their income
by relying on the valuation of their own holdings in the dot coms. Though note,
the latter part didn't become apparent until the dot coms went belly up.

My problem is when, with cynicism, investment is treated as 'greed' exercised by
the  suckers. Pete Dashwood and others, work in a business environment, and
accumulate sufficient savvy to know the rules. They gamble, and as you
indicated, may sometimes lose. Now move over to those many retirees, or close to
retirement, (and this is not the dot.coms), who with modest savings, see a
glossy promising them rich returns. You'll know what I mean when I say the ads
are very sophisticated in their wording. Not really knowing what is going on,
and with no appreciation of the contents of a Balance Sheet, they see only one
phrase "This 'respectable' company can guarantee you a return on your
investment". It happened here in Alberta some twenty years ago with an outfit
called Abacus Cities. Just like the poor sods, (employees), at Enron, these
folks lost their lifetime savings.

Yes there are rules - but perhaps the current set should be put under a
microscope.
The Michael Douglas character you quoted, would have hated the word "ethics".
I think (?) this has become a sufficiently hot topic, that Ethics is now
mandatory on many university curricula. Whether one thinks Moses waltzing down
Mount Sinai with two bits of granite, was a fairy tale or not, those ten rules
certainly present any human with a daunting task to measure themselves against..

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C53422E.50BB5E31@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It's my understanding that FUBAR is the aggravated case of SNAFU. Beyond
that, in intensity, I'm advised is "CLUSTER F'KED" (no acronym available).
The penultimate is "DAISY F'KED" (again, no acronym, sorry) named after the
results of the "Daisy Cutter" bombs.

Aside: The "Daisy Cutter" bomb is a VERY LARGE container of mostly fuel that
(sort of) explodes into a large fog about 100 meters above the ground. After
dispersal, this fuel vapor is ignited - no, detonated - and everything below
the exploding mist ceases to exist. This cloud of devastation concusses the
target up to 600 meters in diameter. Originally designed to clear mine
fields, the "Daisy Cutter" has been found to turn jungles into circles of
desert and, in the case of Afghanistan for example, to turn desert into
proto-desert, a sort-of primordial fused sand littered with very hard things
(the tempered steel of some weapons). The bomb derives it's name from the
imprint of the destruction, roughly resembling a cookie-cutter imprint of a
cute little flower.

The ultimate disaster, would be something along the lines of "NUCLEAR
F'KED," though I've never seen that applied to a computer condition, at
least no application in which I've been involved. Yet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "foo"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Tim Scrivens" <tim.scrivens@nz.eds.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T08:18:03+13:00

```
"JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com> wrote in message
news:cKX48.144825$QB1.10742812@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > I have seen a lot of Programming Languages (and teach said course on
a
> > > > semi-annual basis), but "foo", "bar", etc, still mean nothing to
me -
> > > > especially when I coach my students to use meaningful variable
names.
> > > > [:)].
> > >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> The penultimate is "DAISY F'KED" (again, no acronym, sorry) named after
the
> results of the "Daisy Cutter" bombs.
>
> Aside: The "Daisy Cutter" bomb is a VERY LARGE container of mostly fuel
that
> (sort of) explodes into a large fog about 100 meters above the ground.
After
> dispersal, this fuel vapor is ignited - no, detonated - and everything
below
> the exploding mist ceases to exist. This cloud of devastation concusses
the
> target up to 600 meters in diameter. Originally designed to clear mine
> fields, the "Daisy Cutter" has been found to turn jungles into circles of
> desert and, in the case of Afghanistan for example, to turn desert into
> proto-desert, a sort-of primordial fused sand littered with very hard
things
> (the tempered steel of some weapons). The bomb derives it's name from the
> imprint of the destruction, roughly resembling a cookie-cutter imprint of
a
> cute little flower.
>
> The ultimate disaster, would be something along the lines of "NUCLEAR
> F'KED," though I've never seen that applied to a computer condition, at
> least no application in which I've been involved. Yet.

Bowing, of course, to your superior knowledge, but I thought a "Mongolian
Cluster F*ck" was a totally scripted, news free press conference, as
perfected by the US military in the 60s?

I have also always been fond of FUBB ("Beyond Belief").
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T21:24:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0201282124.22d0b41b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20020126021505.26792.00000516@mb-mh.aol.com> <rYy48.52$mR3.134139@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <a2uvsh$heg$1@ins22.netins.net> <g2H48.47822$%b.2889154@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C53422E.50BB5E31@shaw.ca> <cKX48.144825$QB1.10742812@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <a31jpc$nlv$1@hermes.nz.eds.com> <15_48.3079$yi5.376942@news20.bellglobal.com> <a34dda$n1k$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>`

```
Around 1946, I remember the New York Daily News ran an article
explaining SNAFU.  Apparently, some official had used the term in a
speech or an interview, and civilians had no idea of what he was
speaking.  They went into a bunch of related terms, all of which were
explained using "fouled up" instead of "fucked up".  I remember SNAFU,
and FUBAR (which also turned up extensively in a Sylvester Stallone
movie), and, vaguely, FUBB. There were more than that, but I can't
remember the others now.

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "foo"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T05:17:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B9558.6437$mU4.408948@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20020126021505.26792.00000516@mb-mh.aol.com> <rYy48.52$mR3.134139@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <a2uvsh$heg$1@ins22.netins.net> <g2H48.47822$%b.2889154@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C53422E.50BB5E31@shaw.ca> <cKX48.144825$QB1.10742812@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
    I heard a 2cd hand story about a problem that may have
warranted the "Cluster F'ked" bit.  As best as I can remember
this happened at a very large group of Mainframes at Prince of
Something or other in Pennsylvania, possibly with Boeing.

    The story was that they were formatting a new disk pack,
when the format utility went haywire, possibly due to
another program that wrote outside of it's memory space.

    I thought that mainframes would not let you do that, but this
was a long time ago.  Instead of formatting all cylinders on that one
disk pack, it formatted cylinder zero on all disk packs.

    This crashed many mainframes, since they were apparently
connected in some sort of early network.

    Do you suppose that anyone with a large site in the World Trade
Center, and without a good disaster policy might qualify for Nuclear.
Especially if they were near the top of the building.
```

```
According to Mark G. Dolnick, the term cluster fuck first appears in
our common usage during the 60ï¿½s, almost simultaneously as a term for
group sex and as military slang for a complex, screwed-up operation.
The non-military citation appears a scant year before the other.

http://pw1.netcom.com/~mrlucky/amlbkgd_query.html

Regards,

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "foo"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ed_Stevens@nospam.noway.nohow (Ed Stevens)
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T19:03:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c559ed6.21749554@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<20020126021505.26792.00000516@mb-mh.aol.com> <rYy48.52$mR3.134139@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <a2uvsh$heg$1@ins22.netins.net> <g2H48.47822$%b.2889154@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C53422E.50BB5E31@shaw.ca> <cKX48.144825$QB1.10742812@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
On Sun, 27 Jan 2002 18:33:44 GMT, "JerryMouse" <jerrymouse@invalid.com> wrote:

<snip>

>It's my understanding that FUBAR is the aggravated case of SNAFU. Beyond
>that, in intensity, I'm advised is "CLUSTER F'KED" (no acronym available).

<snip>

Actually, there is -- or more precisely, an acronym morphed back to another
phrase.  The "acronym" for "Cluster F****d" is "CF", to which one then applies
the phonetic alphabet to yeild a "Charlie Foxtrot."  I think "Charlie Foxtrot"
is a rather recent development on military slang.  I don't recall hearing it
when I was on active duty in the mid-70's (back then we just had "Cluster F*")
but have detected it in the National Guard sometime since the mid-80's.
```

<a id="t-23bbc11622"></a>
## Thread: Language stats — 2 msgs

### Language stats

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-26T15:20:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C532BB2.5FE42A36@brazee.net>`

```
Does anybody have a link to a study that shows how much CoBOL is being
done compared to other languages?

Of course, it is hard to compare since most CoBOL isn't OO, and thus
fewer programs per application.   And it's hard to decide what is a
program or an application with HTML, XML, & Excel.

Maybe a stat showing how many CoBOL jobs compared to other languages.
```

#### ↳ Re: Language stats

- **From:** Susan A Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T18:48:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C58400D.408F6EF4@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<3C532BB2.5FE42A36@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Does anybody have a link to a study that shows how much CoBOL is being
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe a stat showing how many CoBOL jobs compared to other languages.


seems like a stat measuring productivity in lines of code

	I think I have heard of applications studies (insurance, banks etc.)
but not absolute modules or jobs counts - 

	how would you count job frequency for a beginning question?
a job that runs weekly on millions of records or every few minutes on
one record - is that still 2 jobs or some other number?

	Susan
```

## Thread: Create unique ID from Employee Numbers — 1 msgs

### Re: Create unique ID from Employee Numbers

- **From:** "Edwin Earl Ross" <EdwinEarl@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T11:39:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zFR48.2113$Fd6.732167306@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3c533cf8$1_1@nopics.sjc>`

```
Store your employee numbers in a relative table. The key to each table entry
is a relative record number that exactly meets your requirement for employee
number.

"Ralph Noble" <ralph_noble@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3c533cf8$1_1@nopics.sjc...
> I am a newbie and could use some help ... I have a master database table
> called "EMPLOYEE" that includes employee numbers in the "EMPNO" field (a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have to add another field called "ID" to the master and child tables,
then
> create a query that will assign a unique 9-digit number for each of the
> "EMPNO" entries that will maintain the integrity across all the child
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-445861d645"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 .dat question — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 .dat question

- **From:** xun0@xrs.net (goober)
- **Date:** 2002-01-27T18:46:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f95938e7.0201271846.2da37689@posting.google.com>`

```
ok.. here's the thing

this semester i'm taking a COBOL class and i just got the class book
'COBOL Mircro to Mainframe'... i code and compile the programs in the
book and all seems fine, until i enter the SYSIN properties... it
finds the .dat files but i dont get the results i expect... it works
fine with the .dat files i downloaded off of the book's website... i
then open the .dat file i made and the one i got from the 'fujitsu
data disk' i downloaded from the book's website.. i compare them and
they're identical...

i think i might be going about the wrong way when creating the .dat
files.. here's what i do....

open notepad
enter the stats
save with .txt extention

open 'COBOL project manager'
go to 'tool > file utility'

in file utility i go to 'commands > convert'
under 'input name' i put the .txt location
under 'output name' i write something like 'blah.dat'
under 'convert method', 'text->seq' is chosen
under 'data format', 'all char' is chosen
click 'ok' then i get the message '12 records have been processed'
the .dat file is now created


i'd really like to get this resolved as soon as possible so if anyone
can help out, i'd really appreciate it
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Obc58.81933$%b.4915120@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<f95938e7.0201271846.2da37689@posting.google.com>`

```

"goober" <xun0@xrs.net> wrote in message
news:f95938e7.0201271846.2da37689@posting.google.com...
> ok.. here's the thing
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> can help out, i'd really appreciate it

A 'text' file has records delimited with carriage-returns or
carrige-return/line-feeds. A 'sequential' file does not have a record
delimiter. So, a 'text' file (line sequential) record of 80 bytes is really
81 or 82 bytes in length. A similar 'sequential' record is only 80 bytes
long.

So, COBOL reading a SEQUENTIAL file in the LINE-SEQUENTIAL mode reads only
ONE record irrespective of the size of the the file (the read passes data
until the computer detects a CR). Conversely, reading a LINE-SEQUENTIAL file
in the SEQUENTIAL mode generates records progressively off by 1 (or 2)
bytes.

Looks like you took a LINE SEQUENTIAL file (created with Notepad) and
created a SEQUENTIAL file (deleting the CR/LF at the end of each record).
That's OK, but not if your program is expecting a LINE SEQUENTIAL file.

Why are you running the File Utility program if you created a file in the
proper format anyway? (Hint: file extensions are irrelevant.)

To be sure whether the CR/LF (X'0D0A') bytes do or do not exist, you must
view the file with a program that displays the file in hex.
```

```
:>I have to add another field called "ID" to the master and child tables, then
```

<a id="t-11b0018e41"></a>
## Thread: Help requested re learning cobol on MVS, using ISPF, JCL, TSO, etc — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help requested re learning cobol on MVS, using ISPF, JCL, TSO, etc

- **From:** not_a_pack_animal@yahoo.com (Cato)
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T09:55:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8e5b6c2b.0201280955.61d7f63@posting.google.com>`

```
I am trying to get a job with a state govt.

I have a BSCS, but no real programming experience (though I currently
work as a SW architect of sorts).

I have a COBOL compiler on my PC, and have picked up the basics of
COBOL.

Can someone tell me what computer/OS the above acronyms (MVS, ISPF,
JCL, TSO)are typically associated with?

How could I learn such environments/tools? A PC emulator perhaps?

Any help is appreciated....
```

#### ↳ Re: Help requested re learning cobol on MVS, using ISPF, JCL, TSO, etc

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T14:04:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C55A0B9.36FFAAE2@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<8e5b6c2b.0201280955.61d7f63@posting.google.com>`

```
Cato wrote:
> 
> I am trying to get a job with a state govt.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any help is appreciated....

MVS = Multiple Virtual Storage
ISPF = Interactive System Productivity Facility
JCL = Job Control Language
TSO = Time Share Option

These are all IBM mainframes (in historical order: S/360,
S/370, S/370-XA, S/370-ESA, S/390 and finally,
z/Architecture). The operating system in historical order:
OS/MVS, OS/MVS SE(1), OS/MVS SE(2), OS/MVS SP, MVS/SP2
(a/k/a, MVS/XA), MVS/SP3/4/5 (a/k/a, MVS/ESA), OS/390 V1 &
V2 (just "OS/390"), z/OS. Typically all referred to as "MVS"
except possibly for z/OS.

There is a PC emulation of S/370 & S/390. Look for Hercules.
However, unless you know how to be a systems programmer and
all the underlying things that have to be done, I seriously
doubt you will ever get OS/MVS to IPL (IPL= Initial Program
Load, BOOT to you PC and *nix types).

ISPF is also used by another "IBM" mainframe operating
system, called VM (Virtual Machine). JCL is also used with
another "IBM" mainframe operating system called DOS/VSE/ESA
(Disk Operating System / Virtual Storage Extension|Extended,
Enterprise System Architecture).

TSO and ISPF are typically married to each other. Most TSO
users today do not know line commands, or enough line
commands to be able to get by with native TSO. Think of JCL
as the equivalent of BAT for DOS or Windoze. However, BAT is
more like CLIST (Command List) under TSO.

What you need to do is get hired as an intern with a company
that uses mainframes, or find some educational facility that
has a mainframe and take their JCL course, or a COBOL class.
The things that you have learned via a PC do not transfer to
a mainframe until you recognize the actual concepts vs the
implementation idiosyncrasies. Then things will click and
the mainframe implementation will become intuitively
obvious.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help requested re learning cobol on MVS, using ISPF, JCL, TSO, etc

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-01-28T19:14:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Mqh58.169916$TC1.11790787@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<8e5b6c2b.0201280955.61d7f63@posting.google.com> <3C55A0B9.36FFAAE2@ix.netcom.com>`

```

On 28-Jan-2002, Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> MVS = Multiple Virtual Storage
> ISPF = Interactive System Productivity Facility
> JCL = Job Control Language
> TSO = Time Share Option

Of course, what the letters stand for doesn't always have much meaning. 
Where's the "customer" in CICS?  Nobody talks about getting some
Productivity Facility emulating software.  Those are just words.

PDF and SPF will get us into the ISPF menu from TSO, but lots of people
refer to the TSO menu.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help requested re learning cobol on MVS, using ISPF, JCL, TSO, etc

- **From:** Guy Lonne <glonne@club-internet.fr>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T22:17:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2002129-231759-599770@foorum.com>`
- **References:** `<8e5b6c2b.0201280955.61d7f63@posting.google.com>`

```

Hi Cato,

You are talking of IBM mainframe environment (IBM 39XX); MVS is the operating 
system (Virual Memory, please), JCL is the command language (just as .bat for 
the good ol' PC)IFPS are tools for the developper etc. You would need quite a 
load of money to afford such an environment! But, I use it for "free", since it 
is my working environement. If you try Amazon.com (no strings attached), you 
will find a looot of litterature about all this. Good luck!
Guy
PS: think of the COBOL web ring; lots of gold nuggets...
```

<a id="t-fef063a1b6"></a>
## Thread: TinyCOBOL release 0.56 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### TinyCOBOL release 0.56

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T12:57:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C56E28F.E765EF54@arvotek.net>`

```
Announce: TinyCOBOL release 0.56

The TinyCOBOL compiler version 0.56 is available at the TinyCOBOL home
page (1) and iBiblio (2).

0.56:
This release contains mainly bugs fixes, and some enhancements.  
It includes updates to the main compiler, run-time, the regression 
test suite.

Support for the CHAIN, GOBACK statements, BLANK WHEN ZERO, 
INITIAL (preliminary), SPECIAL-NAMES (preliminary) clauses, 
COMPUTATIONAL-X type, have been added to the compiler.

The compiler front end has been enhanced to enable TC to create 
an executable directly using the command line.

The command line options have been changed to more closely reflect 
the GCC options.

Preliminary support for the Win32 (native, no emulation layer required)
platform using the MinGW (Mingw32) tool chain. 

A set of utilities have been added to enable TC testing 
for COBOL standard compatibility using the NIST test suite.

General notes:
Bison is the default parser, Berkeley's YACC (byacc) version 1.9.3
is now an option.
The configure script will now handle multiple versions library DB and
C compilers depending on the options selected.

Important note:
Effective with version 0.56, the command line options have been changed
to more closely reflect the GCC options.

The default action when no appropriate command line option is given,
defaults to 'x', create an executable (preprocess, compile, assemble and
link). 

Previous version defaulted to 'S', generate assembler code (preprocess,
compile).


Please report any bugs and/or problems to the TinyCOBOL mailing list. 


1) TinyCOBOL Home Page
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/

2) iBiblio (formerly Metalab)
   http://www.ibiblio.org/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/
```

<a id="t-77fa711473"></a>
## Thread: Calling a C routine from Fujitsu cobol 3.0 many times causes app to crash — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Calling a C routine from Fujitsu cobol 3.0 many times causes app to crash

- **From:** st946tbf@drexel.edu (VD)
- **Date:** 2002-01-29T12:22:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f4aa172.0201291222.5b93c186@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all,

I created a small cobol app using Fujitsu 3.0.  This app calls a
function in C.
I use VC++ 6.0 to link the object code.  Everything goes well except
that:

1) There is no problem if the function has no parameter, and call many
times.
2) There is no problem if the function has parameter, but only couple
calls is made
   (2 or three calls)
3) The application crash if there are 100 of calls made in a loop.

To make things simple, the app is as simple as hello world.  The
function from C does not do anything.

Note that calling 1 time will work.  If return any value, it show it's
a correct value.

If any of you have any advice, please help.

C code:

__declspec(dllexport) int __stdcall SAYHELLO(char * something);
__declspec(dllexport) int __stdcall SAYHELLO(char * something){
         return 0;
}

Cobol code:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.   HelloWorld
 AUTHOR.       asdf
 DATE-WRITTEN. Jan 23 2002
 DATE-COMPILED.

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.

 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.

 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION. 
 
 01  FUNCTIONCALL.
     03  MYFUNC-RETURN-CODE  PIC S999.
     03  NAME                PIC X(50).
     
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      MOVE LOW-VALUES TO NAME.
      PERFORM 120 TIMES
          CALL 'SAYHELLO@4' USING BY REFERENCE NAME
      END-PERFORM.
      STOP RUN.

 END PROGRAM HelloWorld.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling a C routine from Fujitsu cobol 3.0 many times causes app to crash

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T12:32:39+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c573284_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<f4aa172.0201291222.5b93c186@posting.google.com>`

```
This looks like a stack problem caused by neither the Calling nor the Called
program clearing the stack down
after the call.

You need to check the Calling Conventions in Fujitsu and see which one
matches what you are doing in the C program.

COBOL (When calling BY REFERENCE) pushes the parameter addresses onto the
stack. Whether they get popped off again depends on the calling convention
used.

I seem to recall something about the PASCAL calling convention being
required when using Fujitsu V3 but it is years ago and I'm not sure exactly
how it is implemented.

Try the manual and if you are still stuck, mail me privately and I'll look
up some old code.

Pete.


VD <st946tbf@drexel.edu> wrote in message
news:f4aa172.0201291222.5b93c186@posting.google.com...
> Hi all,
>
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>  END PROGRAM HelloWorld.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Calling a C routine from Fujitsu cobol 3.0 many times causes app to crash

- **From:** st946tbf@drexel.edu (VD)
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T10:50:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f4aa172.0201301050.4fad9325@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<f4aa172.0201291222.5b93c186@posting.google.com> <3c573284_8@Usenet.com>`

```
Thank you very much, Peter.

I got this to work now.  I used C LINKAGE.  It turns out that I should
use
STDCALL linkage.  This solves the problem, and also, I don't have to
use
dumpbin to find out what is my function's name is.  Again, thank you
for the thoughful and prompt reply.

VD



"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3c573284_8@Usenet.com>...
> This looks like a stack problem caused by neither the Calling nor the Called
> program clearing the stack down
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com
```

## Thread: IBM withdraws (from marketing) IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM withdraws (from marketing) IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T12:42:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a39f52$eln$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those who may have missed it, from

 http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_902-010

"Effective December 31, 2002, IBM withdraws from marketing COBOL for OS/390�
and VM (OS/390 Features Only) V2 (5648-A25), licensed under the ICA.
    ...
The OS/390 features of COBOL for OS/390 and VM V2 (5648-A25) are replaced by
IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OST and OS/390 (5655-G53). Refer to Software
Announcement 201-343 , dated November 27, 2001."

 ***

NOTE well:
  This is not (YET) an announcement of withdrawal of service, on the other
hand, it is a pretty clear "clue" of what will be happening sooner than
later.

NOTE 2:
  For the "vast" number of you running COBOL under VM, this product is not
yet being withdrawn from that environment (which probably has a LOT to do
with the fact that Enterprise COBOL doesn't run there - yet).
```

<a id="t-c5e08bb845"></a>
## Thread: Calling PL/I procedures from COBOL? — 2 this month / 3 total

### Calling PL/I procedures from COBOL?

- **From:** manojb@mailcity.com (Manoj)
- **Date:** 2002-01-30T19:41:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ec0f6848.0201301941.61d9a43a@posting.google.com>`

```
Does anybody know about how to go about calling PL/I procedures from COBOL.
It's an OS/390 mainframe and I have to write a program which has to call 
several pre-existing PL/I procedures.  I don't know anything about PL/I, so
instead of learning it, I was thinking of writing a COBOL program which calls
those procedures.  Can I just use ordinary CALL statements with the parameters
the same size as the PL/I data structures or do I have to do anything special?
Any specific compiler options that I have to use?
Thanks for any replies.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling PL/I procedures from COBOL?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T13:38:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020131083839.14478.00000355@mb-ck.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0201301941.61d9a43a@posting.google.com>`

```
Manoj writes ...

>Does anybody know about how to go about calling PL/I procedures from COBOL.
>It's an OS/390 mainframe and I have to write a program which has to call 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for any replies.

The problems lie on the PL/I side of things. What kind of data is it expecting?
If you are simply passing elementary data items (comp, comp-3, display, for
example) there should be no problems. But if you need to pass an array (table)
or structure (like a record layout), then PL/I normally expects to be passed a
descriptor block that describes the data and points to where it is. The
simplest approach is to compile the PL/I programs with the parms declared as
having the COBOL attribute. Of course, if the routine is being called by PL/I
programs, then you have some difficulties; two possible solutions: maintain a
call-from-COBOL version and a call-from-PL/I version, or establish alternate
entry points in a single module, one for being called from PL/I and one for
being called from COBOL.

<ad>
All this, and a lot more, is discussed in our course "Inter-Language
Communication in OS/390" (3 days). Details available at:
http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220descr.htm
</ad>

Hope this helps.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-d9062636aa"></a>
## Thread: The compiler in "The Revolutionary Guide to Cobol" — 4 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### The compiler in "The Revolutionary Guide to Cobol"

- **From:** "Fim W���stberg" <fim.wastberg@fimator.se>
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T17:22:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z3f68.15275$n4.2328263@newsc.telia.net>`

```
Does anybody know how the ACCEPT and DISPAY works ??
In the book they (Y. Handel and B. Degtyar) are talking about
special format of ACCEPT and DISPLAY, "The Cobol/B compiler
implements an original extension of these features". But there is
no further information in the book as far as I can see. I have
tested with DISPLAY ETWAS AT LINE  12 COLUMN 20 and
it works. But there must be more.

fim.wastberg@fimator.se
```

#### ↳ Re: The compiler in "The Revolutionary Guide to Cobol"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T11:37:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3bvgr$kc$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<z3f68.15275$n4.2328263@newsc.telia.net>`

```
What COBOL compiler are you using?

Extensions that include "AT LINE ... COLUMN ..." are available in *some* but
not all COBOL compilers.

Depending on which COBOL compiler you are using ("shareware" vs "purchased")
you should check out the LRM (Language Reference Manual).   You should also
check to see if your compiler supports the "Screen Section" - another
medium-common extension.

The quote that you include mentions "Cobol/B" - Is that a compiler that
comes along with the book?  If so, I haven't heard of that compiler, but
others in the NG may have.
```

#### ↳ Re: The compiler in "The Revolutionary Guide to Cobol"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T18:03:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C59884E.7EA9212C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<z3f68.15275$n4.2328263@newsc.telia.net>`

```


"Fim Wï¿½stberg" wrote:

> Does anybody know how the ACCEPT and DISPAY works ??
> In the book they (Y. Handel and B. Degtyar) are talking about
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> fim.wastberg@fimator.se

I'm not familiar with the COBOL/B compiler that you mention, but ACCEPT
and DISPLAY depend upon the actual compiler you will be using. There are
two forms :-

- Standard ANSI ACCEPT and DISPLAY :-

    accept anumber
    display NumericEditedNumber

Both those forms just appear on the next line on your DOS text screen.

- and what you've indicated in your message, some varying flavours of
SCREEN SECTION which allow you do do what you illustrated :-

    ACCEPT MYNUM LINE 12 COLUMN 20

and I think some compilers might have, as an alternative to the above :-

    ACCEPT MYNUM COLUMN 20 LINE 12, (perhaps even POSITION instead of
COLUMN)

For your given compiler, either from the Reference Manuals or on-line
help look up ACCEPT, DISPLAY and SCREEN SECTION (if it has the latter).
```

#### ↳ Re: The compiler in "The Revolutionary Guide to Cobol"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T18:31:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C598EF4.D57FBDD4@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<z3f68.15275$n4.2328263@newsc.telia.net>`

```


"Fim Wï¿½stberg" wrote:

> Does anybody know how the ACCEPT and DISPAY works ??
> In the book they (Y. Handel and B. Degtyar) are talking about
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> fim.wastberg@fimator.se

Fim,

Further to my previous message, as you are at the learning stage, you
might be interested in looking at the following examples at the
University of Limerick :-

http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples/

The site only has a simple ACCEPT/DISPLAY, but for learning, you may
find the other examples extremely useful. (When looking at the examples
REMEMBER these are written using a Micro Focus compiler).

If you do a google.com or WebFerret search, you will also find some US
university sites with examples. Try using 'COBOL EXAMPLES' in your
search.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-2ca25dbc91"></a>
## Thread: fix for RTS load failure error — 1 msgs

### fix for RTS load failure error

- **From:** ant_eater_98@yahoo.com (somthing)
- **Date:** 2002-01-31T09:31:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95ace044.0201310931.5df29917@posting.google.com>`

```
What Operating System are you using?
If it is Windows 2000 or Windows XP then use the following procedures:

To install Personal COBOL under Windows XP and Windows 2000 do the
following:

1. Place the Personal COBOL CD-ROM into the drive.
2. Select Start Menu and Run.
3. Select Browse from the Run menu and select the CD-ROM drive (D on my
system) and select D:\SETUP.EXE,
4. Run Setup.
5. The Setup Welcome screen is displayed - Press OK to continue.
6. Enter your name and company at the next screen and Press OK.
7. Press Yes at the next screen when prompted to check your name.
8. At the next screen accept the default installation directory name of
C:\PCOBWIN and press OK.
9. You will then be prompted to select the programs to install. Check 
both
Personal COBOL for Windows and Personal Dialog System and Press OK.
10. You will be prompted for the name of the Program Group. Accept the
default of Personal COBOL.
11. A screen will then be displayed allowing you to select if you would 
like
to modify your autoexec.bat file.

Select "Leave" so that it will not modify your settings.

12. Personal COBOL will then be installed. You will get a message that 
your
environment variables need to set. Click OK and Exit out of the 
Personal
COBOL group.

To modify your system settings do the following:

1. From the Start Menu select Control Panel. Then select Performance 
and
Maintenance and then the System icon at the bottom.
2. From the System Properties menu select the Advanced Tab and press 
the
Environment Variables button.
3. Under the User Variables selection highlight PATH and select Edit. 
To the
end of the current PATH add the following:
;C:\PCOBWIN and press OK.
4. Select New for each of the remaining options to create a new 
environment
variable and setting.

Name Settings

COBDIR C:\PCOBWIN
COBCPY C:\PCOBWIN\CLASSLIB
COBHNF C:\PCOBWIN
DSGDIR C:\PCOBWIN
COBSW +p3/s14000

When you are done Press OK and then OK again to exit.

Then download the patch for Windows 2000 at:
http://www.merant.com/services/aprograms/aprograms_support.asp
Personal COBOL should then start.
```
